Toto very specifically to be clear said this is not a feeder team play. His quote is no and I'm telling you in big capital letters no we do not want to create a feeder team. Well but surely it will be.

Look, there's a very good reason why in football, not only you not allowed to own um two clubs in the same domestic ecosystem, even at UEFA level, as became clear with the whole controversy over Crystal Palace last summer, where the fact that John Texter had owned 40% of Palace but also owned Olympic Leon, that was enough basically to push um uh Palace out of the Europa League.

Okay. So that even though they were in totally different countries, even though the likelihood of them actually ending up facing each other was very very small, they were palace were pushed down and out of the Europa League because UEFA couldn't accept it. It's partially to do with reality. So let's just imagine that there was a situation where you were coming to the last race of the season and it was a pitch battle as you would want it to be between Mercedes and McLaren and there was a situation that happened out on the track where Toto Wolf being desperate for Mercedes to win that was in a position to influence how his other team acted during that race. No one can tell me that there wouldn't be a a more than a temptation, a major serious obvious incentive to try and control control things in that way as you mentioned with Ricardo um in the Red Bull group.

But then there's also perception as well. And perception is really important because ultimately it's all about the punters. It's all about that, the audience that drives the commercials and drives the TV audience. So even if Toto could put his hand on his heart and say, "No, I would never do that." the questions would be out there every race. Surely he must be directing what's going on. He controls the sporting operation there. He controls the sporting operation here that and that that is a major perception problem. You just can't get around it. It just is.

So I think you know you and I agreed on this 20 years ago. It was a question of needs must and that's when you had Red Bull and Toro Russo as it was then called and Bernie who was running the sport at the time needed to fill out the grid. Okay, if you guys want to have two teams, have two teams because I need to make sure we've got enough cars racing. Otherwise, we got a serious problem. They don't need that anymore. If there is a spare place on the grid for an expansion franchise, let's say, imagine what that would go for. It would go for absolutely vast amounts of money. So, there's no need to bend to somebody's desire to start monopolizing different parts of the grid. And I agree with Zack Brown. I think there should be a three-year um period when Red Bull are told right over the next three years you need you need to divest that team right also it's a sellers market why wouldn't you I mean make a lot of money out of it going to make a lot of money out of it but from the sporting perspective the more competitive teams you have on the grid the better

completely agree

let's move on

National League 史诗一战：York vs Rochdale

football finally quickly because we because we had the most incredible scenario at the weekend happen

now this is very domestic I think we have to say again to our foreign audiences we've been discussing F1 we've been discussing World Athletics. Those are genuine global things. This is quite domestic.

Strap in because it's amazing, right? It's domestic, but it's an incredible uh story.

Sporting contest.

Yeah. So, I lay it up and then you're going to smash it home for all the beliefs that that you have on this, which are genuinely different to anything I've heard. So, I cannot wait kind of wait for you to make me look silly. Right. So, this season

I I could never do that, Charles.

Only you do it yourself.

Very good.

Uh so the National League uh the the the top football league outside of um the well the EFL which is Championship League One League Two has just had the most incredible season. York City and Rodale have both had the most incredible run getting over 100 points a piece and this was neck and neck throughout the whole season. It was teed up perfectly because what was the final game of the season? The final game was Rodale versus York at Rodale.

And my god, it it it didn't disappoint. So going into the game, uh York run 107 points, Rodale run 105. Rodale win and they're up. York draw or win and they're up. Rodale scored a 95th minute goal and the stadium goes mental like pitch invasion.

The whole thing even though there's still time in the game left. Pitch invasion.

They think they're up. Of course they do. Um now there's only one team that goes up automatically which is what the root what's going to be grounded in this um in this conversation. One team goes up automatically and then there's a playoff underneath. So watchell score 105th pitch invasion. Finally they get the fans off etc. You know everyone calms down a bit. Game restarts 103rd minute. So 13 minutes into stoppage time York City score and York City go up.

And actually the most amazing thing about this, go and watch the game, guys. So literally go and take a look because I'm the the the linesman, the referee to give this goal is brilliant because it's a it's a line J. It doesn't hit the net. It's cleared just behind the line and it would no goal line technology.

No goal line technology. It would be so easy for that to have been missed and it wasn't in the most high pressure environment. Anyway, York City score

to equalize

to equalize 1-1. York City end on 108 points. Rodale end on 106. Now I'm looking at that going that is incredible. I'm also looking at it going that is ridiculous. How can a team get 106 points and not get promoted? What a joke. National league sorted out two automatic places. Well, how can the National League sort it out? Sorry. What you're asking National League, EFL, like whoever football governance, just sort that out. Two teams up automatically. That can't happen.

You asking for my opinion?

Yeah.

You You're actually looking like you got a slightly thick look on your face as if you're actually waiting to be slapped.

"Three Up" 之争与英国足球升降级历史

I know. I want you to So So Charlie is jumping on board a bandwagon which has been created by the National League. It's called Three Up.

Yeah.

Okay. So bandwagon. So at the well it's the National League as a whole but Jeff Selling is a very prominent pusher of this because his club Hartool is a National League club. Okay. So the National League just to reiterate what Charlie says is the fifth tier of English football. So it sits just outside the top four divisions and is the top tier of what we call the non-league game right the non-league pyramid. And the non-league pyramid starts at very very low levels almost at kind of village football level. and you can get promoted all the way up to the National League. And if you come either in first place in the National League or win the next six team playoff, as Charlie said, those two teams then progress to the bottom division of the football league and two teams from the football league get relegated back into the National League and they become what is called a non-league club. So the National League is the top of non-league and then you get the football league which is the the three division pyramid running up to the of the of the Premier League.

Okay. So, the National League earlier this year under the leadership of their chief executive Phil Alexander, who is a long-standing friend of mine, the former chief executive of Crystal Palace and a great man who I have nothing but respect for, but genuinely genuinely and I totally respect Phil's campaign on behalf of his clubs to get three promotion places to go up to the football league and therefore three relegation places from the football league to the National League. When sorry, can I just be clear? When you say three places, do you mean so one more automatic place plus the playoff place?

Exactly that. So two automatic positions plus the playoff places.

Um, now let me explain why I think this isn't a very good idea. And to understand this, we have to go back into history. So the National League was formed in 1979. Back in those days, it was called the conference. And those of my generation will grow up knowing it was sponsored by Voxil. So was known as the Vauxhall Conference. And the two leagues it was formed from were the Northern League and the Southern League. And those two that that that part of the game was regionalized effectively, as you can imagine, the Northern League and the Southern League. And the Northern League and the Southern League then have various divisions that ran below them which were also regionalized that fed into them. And at this point there was no automatic promotion of any kind between the non-league and the league.

Now I've got sort of personal knowledge of this albeit before I was born but I grew up with the history of it which is that the dominant southern league team the dominant non-league team in fact of the 1950s was a team called Hadington United. Hedington United were captained by a man who went on to become very famous called Ron Atkinson and their leading goal scorer was his brother Graeme Atinson and Hadington United was in the suburbs of Oxford but it's where Oxford Brooks University is right now. Um, and they were basically winning the Southern League every year during the 1950s. But there was no way of getting promoted to the football league unless you got elected. And fundamentally, you got elected when the football league clubs decided that one of their member clubs was no longer fit to be in the football league. And that was normally a function of bankruptcy. One of the football league clubs would go bankrupt. They would get chucked out of the football league. They lose their shareholding in the football league. And then the football league clubs would elect a non-league club to take their place. And that election would not necessarily just be who's currently the top performing non-league team. It would be who is the current top performing non-league team who would actually make a good football league club, one of our partners, one of our business partners, one of our co-shareholders.

So what happened was um that Hennington United applied for election when Aington Stanley went bust in 1961. and uh their directors had to go on a tour of the whole of the country trying to persuade the directors of football league clubs to elect them and eventually by a narrow margin they did elect Hennington United but they made a condition they said you have to change your name to Oxford United you can't be a shareholder and member of the football league whilst fundamentally being a village team you've got to make yourself into a proper city team you are now going to be a member of the football league that comes with responsibilities that comes with you know You have to be fully professional, previously not semi-professional. You have to be named after your city, not after your suburb, and so on and so forth.

Now, hold that thought that there were conditions attached. You had to be a certain type of club. You had to be ready for it. You had to be a responsible future shareholder of the EFL,

similar to what Prem Rugy's just done now.

Exactly that. Right now, automatic promotion first got introduced in 1987. That was when the first team got automatically promoted from what was then the Conference, now the National League, into the Football League. And it was Scarra who got promoted and Lincoln were the team who finished bottom of the whole football league who got relegated. Funny enough, just been promoted to the championship. Okay. So, the idea was in those days, if you finished bottom of the pile, 92nd out of 92nd, you've kind of stunk the place out. you kind of need to go and put a cold tow around your head. Sort yourself out because we don't really want to have teams stinking the place out in the football league. So, let's make it automatic. Let's not make it about an election. Let's make let's make sure that our worst member, our worst shareholder, the one who stinks the whole place out knows we're actually going automatically. We can't hang on just because we happen not to have gone bankrupt. And it also then gave a a pathway as it were to non-league clubs to say it's now automatic. We don't have to seek election. If we are the top non-league club, we will get the privilege and the right to become a shareholder in the football league. This is what it is. It's not just being promoted into a competition. You become a shareholder in the football league when you get promoted.

Go to 2003 and they say this is the football league saying right we think that the National League is stronger now. is better now and we want to see a bit. We're going to offer a second place. Okay. Now, the National League decided that they wanted that second place to be determined through a playoff. Now, this is a commercial move on behalf of the National League. Okay. Because what it means is is it means that more teams that are in the National League think that they've got a chance.

Makes sense,

right? As it does in other divisions. Okay? Now, if we're talking strictly about what your challenge is, which is, well, it's absolutely disgraceful that a team that gets 106 points shouldn't get promoted. The only reason why Rodell were not promoted this season was because of the National League, not the football league. Because the football league says you've got two spaces. And if the National League decided that actually first and second should go up, then they would go up. It's the National League that decided first of all to have a 14 playoff and then even more controversially to extend it to a six team playoff. So actually the jeopardy we're looking at here which is that Rodale who've got 106 points might not get promoted and a team who only got 80 85 points might get promoted is entirely because the National League have decided that that's how they want to operate things.

So what they're now saying is nice bit of cake that

I really enjoy that cake and I'm going to eat it. I want to take some cake as well.

半职业到职业的"相变"：为什么三升三降不合适

Three to four up in some going like through the leagues above. That's correct. There's four four up go from league to league one,

right? That that's consistent across those leagues. So if you're saying okay from two because it's two down from league two, two up from from National League. Really are we saying here that there would be a concern that the teams that could come up if it was three up three down wouldn't be able to survive well wouldn't wouldn't be able to sustain a football league

fundamental set of requirements

fundamentally yes that is my argument but I'm going to explain what I mean which is that um the jump from league 2 to league one within one sporting competition which is the football league is a very small jump right so the requirements the structural requirements for league two club are the same as the structural requirements for a league one club. You don't need to do anything different. You you need the same type of pitch. You need to have the same type of safety standards around your stadium. You need to have a community trust. That's a club charity. You're very likely to have a proper women's team. You're very likely within the academy system to have a proper academy that that brings all the young people from your area through. These are fundamentally requirements or if not requirements very close to being requirements of league clubs. The National League, not so. The National League doesn't have any of these requirements. There are clubs in the National League. There are competitive clubs in the National League. There are clubs in the top half of the National League that are semipro whose players are not full-time.

This is a totally different environment. Club charities, well, if you want, go for it. If you don't want, don't worry about it.

And a football league club has to have a foundation.

Yep. Exactly. Academy. M if you want one great if you don't want one not a problem right so this is really fundamentally different so what the challenge is here is when a national league club gets promoted to the football league it's going to have to quite fundamentally change itself in a number of different ways okay so if you look at Brmley for instance who recently got promoted from league 2 to league one going back 10 years Brmley is a semi-pro club operating with an all-weather pitch in a community club kind of Okay, they then start to do better on a consistent basis and they start to gear themselves up for we might get promoted to the football league. What does that mean? That means we'd have to dig our pitch up, get a grass pitch because you don't have all weather pitches in the football league. What does that mean? What that means is we can't rent out that pitch during the week and that's a massive part of our revenues. Okay. Okay. So, we're going to have to start thinking about our revenue base. If we don't have the revenues from the plastic pitch, then we we've got potential revenue problems. Okay. Are we eventually going to have an academy? Okay, we have to start having a look at that. Are we going to need to institute a Brmley charitable trust? Okay, we have to start looking at that so that by the time that they actually get into the football league, they are ready to be a football league club because the last thing you want is what I would call yo-yo clubs in this particular area. Okay? A club that gets on a nice little run in the National League, gets promoted to League Two, is in no sense ready to be in League Two, doesn't have an academy, has a plastic pitch, doesn't have the right safety requirements around the ground and so on so forth, and then struggles in League Two and comes straight back down, but then has dug up its plastic pitch, no longer has the revenues that it concerned, and so on and so forth.

So, my point is is I think that two up is adequate and correct. I think it is generous of the football league to allow this overall. Historically, it wasn't the case. Historically, it was a closed shop. It's now no longer a closed shop, and they've in fact even doubled the entry during my lifetime. They've doubled the number of clubs that can come in. But I don't see why if the National League is genuinely concerned from a sporting perspective about the problem that you posed, which I do have sympathy with, which is a team could have an outstanding season and only just miss out. Fine. Get rid of your playoff. Yeah,

get rid of your playoffs and just put two up.

Now, of course, what Phil Alexander would say to me is, "But those playoffs are crucial because they give hope to the mid-table and and they're also good from a TV perspective." I go, "Okay, Phil, that's great, right? Totally get it, but you're not talking about sporting integrity there, are you? You're asking the EFL to pay for you to have the luxury of having a playoff system when you don't need to have one."

That and the yo-yo club points, you've you've got me there. Okay, I get that. And and I completely agree. And it's easy to look at this just from the

how can that happen? But then also you had a team, you know, oh the poor old Liverpool, you know, give us a trophy for finishing second. You had a team that got like 97 98 points and didn't win the Premier League and some just like

Yeah,

that can happen. So

look, I I like this. Sorry, can I really like this with you putting that across because you know the point that is continually drilled into everyone is this is wrong and we need to have more place. we need to have that extra place. So to hear that side of it is great because that's grounded in rationale and that's grounded in the broader conversations we have in football which is sustainable product improvement. You know developing the right structures in the system that actually supports all of the participants in it. That sounds like a very sensible explanation of why this is actually in place.

It's a big jump, Charlie. There are fundamental differences and changes that come from moving from a semi-professional league into a professional league. Um and those need to be recognized and if at the end of it it's decided structurally do you know what actually there are three spaces here because the depth in the National League is now so deep of the clubs who could automatically translate there. So we're not talking about four or five clubs we're talking about 8 9 10 11 12 clubs who are league ready properly league ready with their foundations with themies with all this type of stuff. Okay have a proper look at it. Okay. But do make sure that's the case cuz what I'm seeing at the moment is in the upper mid table of the National League, you've got semi-pro teams. Whe Tamworth, you got semi-pro teams. And great for them by the way, but that's an awful long way away from league football.

Charlie, excellent as ever. Thank you. That was fascinating.

Thanks, CHZ.

item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的观察：体育的"金字塔顶端悖论"
这期节目暴露了一个几乎没人指出的结构性怪象——**一项运动越接近"人类极限的纯粹证明"，运动员反而越穷**。Sebastian Sawe 跑出史上最快马拉松，奖金 4 万英镑；一个无人知晓的 Challenger 网球巡回赛冠军也能拿到这个数。这不是市场的偶然失灵，而是一个系统性的"价值蒸发"机制：
田径越是"纯粹"（谁最快、谁最远、谁最高），它就越被吸入奥运会这个四年一度的引力黑洞——而 IOC 每年从田径上获得的约 10 亿美元收入，**没有一分钱按比例下行到日常职业巡回赛**。换句话说：田径的"纯粹性"恰恰是它的诅咒——它太容易被国家、被奥运、被业余主义传统所征用，反而失去了讨价还价的杠杆。NBA、NFL 50% 的收入分给球员的模式之所以可能，正是因为它们"不纯粹"——它们是商业 IP，不是民族图腾。
## 二、跨领域连接：Chelsea、LIV Golf、F1 共享同一个"叙事免疫系统"
表面上这是三件不相干的事，但 Charlie 无意间揭示了一个深层机制——**一旦某个组织在公众心智中固化成某种叙事，相同事实会得

item.title: [投资TALK君] ✨【投资TALK君1428期】吐血总结：5家大科技财报！泪崩，鲍威尔的最后一舞✨20260430#CPI #nvda #美股 #投资 #英伟达 #ai #特斯拉
item.dc:creator: 投资TALK君
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的观察：微软「毛利率下降」其实是AI落地最强信号

市场对微软盘后下跌的解读集中在「毛利率被AI拖累」，但这正是被忽略的悖论——只有当用户疯狂烧token、把"按席位制"的合同烧穿了，企业才会被迫切换为"席位+消耗量"的混合定价。换句话说，毛利下降不是需求疲软的症状，而是需求过载的症状。这在SaaS史上极少见：上一次类似的"被迫定价升级"是2008年AWS将免费层缩水——之后是云计算的真正起飞。

把这个逻辑外推一下：未来一年，谁的财报里出现"我们正在从席位制转向混合计费"，谁就是真正跑通了AI落地。 这是一个比"DAU/MAU"更狠的真实信号——因为它意味着客户用得停不下来，而不是被你硬塞功能。

二、跨领域连接：鲍威尔的「最后一舞」与亚马逊的「估值模型重写」共享同一种结构性变化

表面看，美联储换帅和亚马逊自研芯片业务是两件毫无关联的事。但底层都是"隐性资产被迫定价"的过程：

  • 鲍威尔时代，美联储的"独立性"是隐形的、不需要被定价的。换成沃什后，市场必须开始显性地为"美联储独立性溢价"定价——这部分以前免费包含在美债里。
  • 亚马逊的自研芯片以前是AWS的"内置成本节省"，藏在AWS业务里。一旦开始外销，就必须把芯片业务从AWS中"拆解出来单独定价"——这部分以前是免费包含在估值模型里的。

两个事件都揭示了一个更深的规律：当一个系统的"内嵌价值"开始外溢，市场就被迫重写定价模型，而重写过程本身会制造波动。 这也是为什么我认为接下来的不确定性，并非来自"AI泡沫"，而是来自估值框架的换挡期——旧框架装不下新东西。

三、隐藏的系统性真相：管理层的「政治正确话语」与「行动数据」的剪刀差，正在变成最稳定的alpha来源

小扎说"AI不替代人"、安迪说"AI不会减少岗位"、库克说"我们在加大投入"——但同时三家都在裁员或冻结招聘。这不是矛盾，这是一种结构性的"语言通胀"：当一件事在政治上不能直说，CEO的语言会越来越温和，而行动会越来越激进，二者剪刀差会越来越大。

更出人意料的连接：这其实和美联储「降息倾向→中性倾向」的措辞调整是同一种现象。鲍威尔不会直接说"我们怕通胀回来不敢降"，他只会让"措辞悄悄变中性"。在劳动力市场，CEO们也不会直接说"AI正在替代人"，他们只会让"员工总数悄悄不增长"。

所以真正的投资框架，不应该是"听言论，看数据"，而应该是"测量言论与数据的偏差幅度"——偏差越大，趋势越确定。 这一点对就业市场尤其关键：当美联储还在用"失业率后视镜"判断软着陆时，微观财报已经显示了硬变化——这个时间差，可能就是2026年下半年宏观叙事剧烈反转的根源。

📝 转录文本

开场与今日要点

各位观众大家好，欢迎来到一个不单只聊新闻的专业美股频道，我是市场分选小陶，欢迎大家来到周四的更新。今天的内容就非常的多了，5家大科技的财报，因为走的非常好的谷歌我就稍微往边上放一放，其他4家大科技我会稍微去聊一下。

那如果大家觉得今天的内容不够、性致不够的话，大家可以去看一下我在周三下午在APP上晚上所做的这个直播。在昨天那个直播里面，我前面画了一个小时的时间去聊了一下周三盘后发布杂文的大科技，所大家感兴趣的话，可以去听一个非常详细的一个讲解。当然在这期视频的最后，我会把我APP的一个二维码提供在画面里面供大家去下载。

那今天除了大科技的财报以外，讲一下周四的一些宏观数据，然后讲一下周三下午的一个美联储会议鲍威尔的一个最后义务，我们去做一下点评。所以今天内容非常的多了，我就不多说废话了。在开始之前拜托大家记得留言点赞加转发。

一、周四市场走势回顾

我们先回顾一下周四市场的一个走势。美元指数下跌，下跌的幅度不小，然后美债收率下跌，主要是因为日本央行那边又干预汇市了。日元兑美元、美元对日元是1比160，所以日本干预汇市之后，日元出现了上涨，美元出现了下跌。

那三大指数也出现了反弹。周三的盘、周四的一个盘，周四上半场，其实三大指数是出现下跌，特别是钠指相当的弱，因为对冲基金的这个再平衡。但是最后最终的一个收盘，你看到三大指数还是出现了比较大幅度的一个上涨。

所以如果在昨天把自己仓位全部清掉，觉得自己"哎，今天好像time对了这个市场"，下半场以后你会发现"哎，怎么又回去了"，那就完美踏过了，那就完完美踏空了。所以如果抱着这种心态去度过整个4月份的话，那可能就是一路踏空了。

我个人建议也不要把这些单一的一个事件作为自己去探这个市场的一个理由，因为长期之后你会发现经常会卖飞，因为你会发现这个事件本身其实没有办法帮助你去判断明天股市，或者不能连续的帮我们去判断明天的股市到底是涨还是跌。

二、过去一个月的反弹回顾

我们回顾一下过去一个月市场的一个走势吧。从3月31日到现在为止： - 标普上涨了14% - 钠指20% - 道指相对弱些10% - 黄金2.1%也相对弱一些 - 原油上涨2.1%

在原油上涨的情况下，三大指数的反弹非常的大。芯片板块上涨50%，我没有去回过回溯过历史，那起码我认为整个4月份芯片板块的这种涨幅在历史上单月的涨幅一定是排在上号——单月指数上涨50%，非常的夸张。前期大科技股的涨幅也非常的好20%，等权指数8.2%。

所以大家如果有听进去，我在3月份的时候我说现在大科技非常的便宜，那如果有听进去的，包括我当时聊了，我当时基本上含不喉咙的，我说英伟达是便宜的，博通是便宜的，微软是便宜的，包括整个纳指都是便宜的。所以大家如果有听进去，并且有赶加仓的，并且在4月份没有非常明显的这个卖空，一直拿到现在的话，起码恭喜大家。

虽然整一个4月份或者3月份只是我们整个投资周期里面非常短的这两个月，但是起码是一个事件。当时在3月份的时候我就说过，每当有市场的下跌，都是考验我们自己投资策略的一个非常好的一个检验。

当时我一再强调，我劝大家不要把焦点去放在战争的这个事件的一个判断上面。我相信当时市场里面，包括社交媒体上，我知道很多人说"哎，这次川普一个人的他扣是没办法的，你必须要伊朗那边也他ack扣，你必须要以色列那边也他ack扣"，包括以色列的革命卫队、伊朗的革命卫队、伊朗那边革命卫的领袖的名字、伊朗的领导人，所有的名字我们都了解的清清楚楚，包括所有的历史背景。

但是最后怎么样？最后即使你对战争的这方向的一个判断正确，你也没办法判断正确川普是不是他扣，更没有办法想到一个月的时间里面那指可以上涨20%、价值破金高、标普破金高，且我们还在战争里面，且油价还上涨了2.4%。站在一个月之前，从任何的一个我们当时认为非常合乎逻辑的这些思考、这些角度去出发的话，能够想象到现在吗？

所以我希望在下一次如果再有类似于战争这些事件的话，我们起码可以有一个比较冷静的一个判断呢，知道到底从哪一个视角去出发可能会对于我们是有帮助的，比如说PE，比如说当下市场的一个估值——那时候的市场估值就是在一个相对比较低的位置。

三、市场参与度变化

经过这一波的上涨，我们来看一下市场的参与度。其实在两周之前、还是一周之前我和大家分享过的，我说当时罗素2000的参与度非常高了，已经92%了20日均线。然后纳指相对是最低的78%，标普的参与度也非常的高。

但是经过这段时间的一个横盘或者小横盘之后的一个小幅上涨，纳指的参与度都下来了： - 纳指的参与度50%，完全没有前段时间80%要高了 - 罗素63% - 标普59%

昨天在直播里面还有人问我，他说市场里面有一种声音说现在市场的参与度下降了，只有几只个股带领着市场往前走，这是不是一种不健康的上涨，代表接下去市场要下跌？

我说在市场里面反弹的时候，你可以是一部分的一些板块先走出来，然后带着其他的一些板块再继续上涨，那么也可以是类似于这种。如果前期大科技股，其实通常来说，科技股能够带出来的一个反弹都是非常健康的反弹，你可以回过头去看，每一次都是一样的。

因为接下去——昨天直播的时候我就说——我说接下去是不是有一种可能性，资金可以从前期大科技股、可以从科技股里面轮动出来，去到其他一些板块、去到其他一些标的，然后把整个市场的参与度再次带回来，可不可以出现这种情况？如果可以的话，那为什么现在由科技股所带领的这一波上涨就是不健康的呢？

四、关于"90度上涨"的警示

当然，我承认就芯片板块的上涨，包括像我自己手里持有的英特尔的上涨，已经非常的夸张，就90度的这种上涨。我一直不认为任何一个标的短时间之内90度的上涨一定是可持续的——没有一个标的可以比值的90度上涨永远不带回调。而且通常来说，你现在上涨的越激烈，接下去的回调幅度就会越大，基本上就是怎么上去怎么回来。

当然，很多标的如果有基本面，比如说有EPS、有盈利的这个支撑的话，那接下去下跌这个幅度可能不会那么的深，但是基本上都会看。因为我知道90度的一个上涨，它不单单一定是基本面的触发，不会出现90度。90度一般都是情绪面的累积，而情绪面的累积at one point未来某一个时间点是一定会崩塌的。任何一个标题都会崩塌。

一旦这个崩塌的话，你有基本面的这个支撑，情绪面散去之后，那股价下跌一定会下跌嘛，只是它下跌的幅度可能不会那么的大，可能不会怎么腰斩、百分之六七十这种下跌幅度，但是百分之二三十从顶部的下跌幅度非常的正常。

所以我说最好的一种情况就是你可以去左侧自己非常主动的去进行止盈，或者说被动嘛。你很多标的就设一个移动止盈，比如说最高点回调10%我止盈多少仓位，最高点回调20%我止盈多少仓位。在这种情况下，你前段时间那么大的这个涨幅就不会最终完全消失了。所以这也是一个保护自己浮盈的一种方法。

五、纳指当前估值

看一下现在纳指的一个估值吧。可能和市场、可能和我们每一个人的感受非常不一样的是，现在纳指的一个估值，截止到周四收盘25.4倍： - 最低的时候在3月底是22倍 - 2025年4月份的时候也是22倍 - 2022年12月份的时候是在21倍左右

所以其实这波的下跌，从估值这个角度去看，已经非常接近于22年的一个低点，包括2025年4月份的这个低点。经过这一波反弹之后，现在是在25.4倍。

我并不认为现在纳指是贵的，所以我说你从估值这个角度去看，其实你并得不出一个结论现在美股市场里面的估值非常的贵，甚至于有泡沫——这种结论根本就得不出来。

六、周四宏观数据：ECI才是关键

接下去我们来聊一下周四公布的一些宏观数据。我今天在X上做了一个测试，我说我平时在视频里面一直聊到底哪一组数据非常的重要，那大家可以摁一个暂停，在今天周四所公布的那么多的数据里边，你们认为哪一组数据最重要？

在那么多的数据里面，比如说： - PCE的数据 - 核心PCE的数据 - Initial jobless claim（失业人数申请） - Employment Cost Index（ECI，工资上涨数据） - GDP的数据

那么多的数据里面到底哪一组数据是最重要的？Employment Cost Index ECI数据，因为这一组是工资的一个增速上涨数据。

还记不记得在2021年的11月份，当时美联储转向的一个时间点，一个trigger就是ECI Employment Cost Index工资增速的这一组数据超出了他们的这个预期，使得他们出现转向。所以在那么多的公布的数据里面，最重要的不是GDP数据、不是PCE数据，而是ECI这组数据。

市场预期0.8，最后的结果是0.9。从这个结果去看，是不是一组非常好的数据？并不是。但是如果你拆开来去细看的话： - ECI这组数据它其实包括了普通的一个工资增速，还包括了政府的这个补贴 - 在一季度里面，政府的补贴的一个增速非常的高1.3% - 如果把政府的补贴给去掉，我们单纯的去看企业的这个工资增速的话，0.7%——季度环比增速0.7%和四季度是一样的

所以从这个角度去看，工资增速并没有出现一个加速。虽然我们看到这个结果是0.9，看似令人恐惧令人担心的，但是如果去细看里面那些数据的话，就还好，美联储并不会对于这组数据表现出过分的担忧。

七、鲍威尔的"最后一舞"——一段感情的回顾

既然讲到宏观，我们来聊一下周三的美联储会议——鲍威尔的一个最后义务。

从我个人来说，从鲍威尔2018年当时刚刚上位的时候，我没有去追他的一些会议等等。但是从2018年年底的时候，那时候股市逐渐下跌，2019年报歪的一个pay，然后到2020年疫情的一个QE、撒钱、降息，到了2022年的一个加息，到2021年加息加晚了——那么呢爸Y就说"inflation is temporary"通胀是暂时的——到2021年底的一个非常明显的pivot，然后到2022年的一个加息周期，一下子加了75个基点，导致市场的这个下跌，到2023年的SVB事件，到2024年9月份的一个降息。

2024年9月份记不记得第一次市场猜测是25个基点、50个基点都不知道，没人知道。市场当时猜测如果直接降50个基点的话，可能美联储知道一些什么，市场要下跌。但那时候我说了，我猜测我认为美联储第一次会降50个基点，且市场会做出一个正面的一个解读。结果也和我的预期完全一样：美联储做出了这样的一个决定，第一次降息就降50个基点，也是历史上没有看到过。

因为当时是因为7月份的劳动力市场数据不是那么的好看，所以他们担心了，担心劳动力市场恶化。所以从24年9月份开始，11月、12月连续降息3个月，然后25年再次降25个基点、25个基点，又降了75个基点。24年降息，25年降息，到了26年——26年的4月份，鲍威尔这个最后义务。

整个你会发现其实都是历历在目。所以很多时候我也是静下来了之后，意识到这是鲍威尔最后一次美联储会议，下一次我们看美联储会议就换一个人了，可能也产生了一定的一个感情。因为对于一个人，你毕竟了解了他8年了，你跟那个人相处了8年，他讲的每一句话、他讲什么你就知道。很多时候美联储的讲话，鲍威尔的讲话，他不是那么的直接的，但是你就知道他在讲什么。这就是对一个人的一个了解吧，长时间相处下来，确实有了一定的感情。

那接下去要换一个人，你要去了解一个人是需要时间的，每一个人讲话的这些语境用词其实都是有个人的一个特色在里边的。所以接下去作为我自封自己是美联储的一个观察员，所以接下去可能还需要更加的一些学习，先学习一个凯文·沃什到底是什么样的人，他的一个讲话的一个语境到底是什么。所以又是一个适应的一个过程吧。万一接下去我对于凯文·沃什这个判断有些出入的话，大家必须要原谅我，因为我对于他还在一个互相了解这个过程之中。

八、美联储会议关键点解读

讲回周三的美联储会议。首先从公告里面，我们看到有三位美联储反对了降息的这个倾向。什么叫降息的倾向？就是对于美联储来说，有所谓有降息的倾向，就是接下去下一次的美联储会以更多的这种可能性和概率还是在降息，并不是加息或者保持利率不变。

我以前和大家形容过，美联储是大海里边所航行的一艘船，他们要转向的话是非常的慢，而且他们的转向一定会提前给市场这一个信号。所以在当下这个公告里面，起码在这一次会议里面，整体的美联储大多数人认为美联储还是要保持一个降息的一个倾向，包括鲍威尔。然后有另外一个人是反对的，他希望要加息，所以这三个反对降息倾向的人是偏鹰派的，另外一个反对不降息，他是偏鸽派的。

我听了鲍威尔的演讲，包括他的Q&A，在他的Q&A里边，鲍威尔其实有透露这个信号——他说最早是在下一次的会议里面，就有可能会改变这一个降息的倾向。什么意思？就是接下去可能从降息的一个倾向变成holding period，变成保持利率不变的一个倾向，那么接下去可能一直保持利率不变。

直到什么时候？ - 直到他们认为关税是一次性的吗 - 那关税一次性的影响体现在通胀的数据里边 - 直到油价开始出现下降 - 直到我们看到油价下降的这个右侧

才可能使得他们再比较舒适的去打开这一个降息的倾向。所以下一次的美联储会议，如果他们宣布接下去降息这个倾向变成保持利率不变的这个倾向的话，那对于从美联储态度上的一个转变来说，它是一个比较明显的一个转变，是一个比较明显往相对比较鹰派的一个方向转变。

九、鲍威尔不离任的政治背景

第二点，他说他不会离开理事的这个职位，直到DOJ对于他的一个调查彻底结束。但是他说接下去我会保持非常的一个低调。

所以鲍威尔不会离开，主要是因为他认为BOG（川普对于他的这个调查、故意整他的这个调查）没有结案。在他的理解里面，这个事情没有结束，在没有结束之前他也不想去退休。但是他说的非常明白：他说我就是想退休了，你一定要搞我的话，那我就留在理事的这个位置上。

那对于下一任的凯文·沃什到底会有什么的一些影响呢？我个人的判断，我认为鲍威尔起码还是一个非常专业的人，他起码还是把一个自己的理性和专业放在第一位的。当然他留在那边，主要是因为你既然用政治的手段对付我，那我起码也要用一定的政治的手段去做出一个回应。所以某种程度被迫待在理事的这个位置，他其实是被动的。

但是从接下去会不会影响到凯文·沃什的一些决定方面的话，我并不认为对于凯文·沃什会有非常大的这个影响。但是我后来思考了一下，因为华尔街的一个大词多头马克·汤姆尼，他认为每一次美联储的主席换届之后，接下去对于市场都会造成一个负面的一个影响。当时我基本上会dismiss、会忽略这些观点，但是昨天我有仔细的思考了一下，我觉得存在一种可能性，我们不应该完全忽略这种可能性。

因为你想一下，即使在鲍威尔当下，因为鲍威尔起码8年了嘛，大家对于他所有的美联储选人基本上都是对于他有尊重的。但是即使在这种情况下，我们很少看到一次美联储会议的这个决定有4位美联储成员是反对的，就是美联储内部的声音、现在的噪音其实非常的多。

那想象一下，换了凯文·沃什上来之后，这内部的分歧会变得更加的严重还是会变得更加的缓和？那么一定会变得更加的严重。所以从这个角度去看的话，我认为市场有可能会担心美联储内部的这个分歧使得美联储的这个独立性没有那么的体现出来，或者市场怀疑美联储的这个独立性，那有可能会影响到美债市场，那通过美债市场再反馈到股市这边。所以这个风险是存在的，并不应该完全把它无视掉。所以这个风险我会起码会记在心上，或者留一个心眼在那边，并不会完全无视掉。

十、谷歌财报：满分财报与2027资本开支指引

OK接下去我们就来聊财报。那首先是微软。在聊微软之前，我先稍微聊一下谷歌，因为谷歌在周四走的也非常的好，然后我看现在盘前又再次出现了一个上涨。

谷歌我稍微聊一下，他首先基本上财报是无懈可击的，我觉得有点类似于亚马逊百分百、有点类似于英特尔百分百的这种财报，就是满分财报。

但是在他们的财报里面，他们有聊到非常有意思——他们说2027年的资本开支，我们现在才刚刚2026年的第一季度，他说2027年的资本开支我们会在2026年的基础上继续大幅度增长。

很少有公司会在一个季度之后就去给2027年资本开支这个指引，这就非常有意思。明明市场现在在担心大科技的资本开支影响到了现金流，那么谷歌的现金流也影响到、所有大科技现金流都影响到，但是他们还能够给出2027年的资本开支，说明什么问题？

说明这些大科技包括谷歌在内对于未来AI的这个需求是有多么的bullish。而他们的这个看多的一个情绪并不是他们简简单单判断主观的一个判断，而是因为他们看到了背后的这个订单——他们的资本开支某种程度是为了去满足他们现在已经看到的这个订单。

所以我觉得这是谷歌财报里面的一个重点，就是在刚刚26年过去了一个季度，他们就给了27年资本开支这个指引，就说明他们对于AI是有多么的看好。

十一、微软财报：Copilot 250%增速与"被迫定价升级"

然后我们来看一下微软。微软的话有几个重点：

第一个重点是Copilot的一个同比增速250%。 这就体现了什么？这就体现了微软内部的一个分发——就是大科技、起码像微软这一类的大科技，他们分发渠道的这个护城河。管理层有聊到Copilot这个周使用率现在已经和Outlook是一样了。Outlook你知道基本上你用办公用具的话，你基本上你用微软的话，Outlook基本上是每天都在用的。

然后第二点： 因为市场担心Copilot的没人用嘛，那现在首先第一点，Copilot现在已经不是免费使用，Copilot现在都是收费的，都是席位制加上consumption。然后席位制就现在已经收费，以前是负担的送给你，然后你自己去用。而且因为微软的这个派发，导致Copilot的同比增速250%，这种增速你是很难看到。当然现在体量没有那么的大。

然后收费的模式从原来的席位制变成了席位制加上consumption——就加上token based on token。你token消耗了多少，然后你该付多少钱。

为什么他们出现了这种转变？是因为他们这一个季度的毛利润率稍微有些下降，同比增速和上年居作对比的话有些下降。为什么下降？这个下降的理由非常的重要：

  1. 第一点是因为AI的投资，这个先不讲
  2. 第二点，管理层他们提到了GitHub Copilot的一个使用——原来GitHub里边的Copilot它是席位制的，席位制给你token一个credit。结果使用者觉得这个产品非常的好用，然后所以再去疯狂的烧token。你token烧了多了之后，因为你是席位制的，你付一个月费、你付一个年费，你就可以无限的使用。然后微软不干了，因为你烧掉微软太多的token，微软的成本上升了，所以微软开始从席位制变成了一个席位制加上consumption。

有些人对于这一点的看法可能是认为是悲观的，因为你没办法、你必须要改变你的一个收费的一个模式。但其实我认为我的理解完全是反过来。

为什么完全反过来？你一个产品的推出，你AI产品的一个推出，即使你是免费的也不见得有人用。反而是如果你是免费的，因为一开始你一定会让别人去试用嘛，让你的客户去试用，反而当你看到了客户试用的时候，客户试用的这个反馈非常的好、疯狂的去烧token，说明你推出的这个AI附加的一个功能本身它是有价值的，所以客户才会用、客户才会疯狂的用。

那在这种前提下，如果接下去我们看到任何AI软件公司这一块的AI产品都是一样的，如果他们有这个能力从席位制变成席位制加上consumption，反而是一个利好——为什么？这代表原本席位制背后的需求、席位制已经加上AI的这个需求非常的大，所以他们才被迫要从席位制变成席位制加上consumption。

所以这一个季度GitHub里边Copilot的使用——大家疯狂的使用导致毛利润的这个下降，我反而把它看作是一个AI落地非常明显的这个信号。那么所以他们马上改变了这种收费模式，对第二季度这个毛利润应该会有一个正面的影响。

然后下面一点RPO（未来订单）增长至6270亿，同比增速100%。 所以这个订单增速也非常的快，三家云厂都是一样的。这就是所以我们能够理解，因为这个订单进来之后，他们必须要花那么大的资本开支——你不花这个资本开支，你没有办法满足现在所下的这个订单。

十二、需求从哪里来？AI替代人的真相

然后最后一点是在Q&A里边，这个问题其实我觉得问的挺好的。分析师问：他说你们说需求非常的强劲，那长期到底是谁在买单？我们看到很多公司里面，他们的IT spending的这一个未来的一个期待并没有上涨，GDP的增速也没有上涨，那这个钱到底是哪里来的？

这个问题其实问的非常的好。然后萨提亚的一个回答，他说：你考虑到IT的这个——简单公司必须要从自己考虑这个钱到底是哪里来的。有些时候如果你公司因为你IT这一块的钱花了多了之后，你营收上去之后，你自然会愿意花更多的钱。但是另外一方面，很多时候是你用了现在AI这些工具之后，反而给你去增效了，反而给你可以省出什么？可以在OpEx这一块（在运营花费这一块）可以省出来。

所以对于很多公司来说，你总共的人工这一块、你总共运营花费可能是一样的，但是你人工的这个成本可能下降了，就是AI替代了一部分的一个人工成本。

所以我们不要去听这些大科技的管理层或者CEO在接受采访的时候说"哎，AI对于岗位不会造成负面的这些影响"。你去看他们怎么做嘛，包括你看他们回答问题的这个回答里面他们到底是怎么回答的、他们怎么做的： - 员工的同比数量、员工数量的同比出现了减少 - Meta在裁员 - 亚马逊的裁员，亚马逊的裁员告诉你"我们要往一个初创公司这个思路去"——那其实就是用任何的一些借口来进行裁员、来进行人员上节省

大科技相对来说对于传统企业，它的效率是偏高的。如果大科技在相对比较效率偏高的一些大科技上场都能够裁员的话，你说传统公司有没有裁员的空间？我认为一定有裁员的空间。

所以这就是为什么如果我们去看这一次的美联储会议，他们甚至认为劳动力市场是非常的坚挺的，所以接下去我们可能从降息的这一个bias变成中性的一个倾向。但是美联储看的什么？美联储看的是失业率、看的是大非农数据。大非农数据或者失业率永远是一个后视镜，而现在他们看到的都是一些宏观数据。

那现在我们通过这些大科技的财报，我们通过一个微观的一个视角，把很多个微观的视角联系在一起，我们其实就出现了一个宏观的一个视角。那从微观的这个视角去出发的话，我没办法对于未来的就业市场感到乐观——你看大科技在做什么，你看管理层在回答这一类问题的时候，他们那个答案是什么。

即使我认为微软的财报是OK的，但是即使在这种财报的一个情况下，好像并没有说服市场，所以微软在周四出现了下跌。下跌完之后，微软现在这个PE是在21.6倍。3月份的时候是20倍都不到，将近19倍——19.3倍、19.4倍左右。现在微软的估值仍然是大幅低于25年4月份的水平，甚至于低于22年的这种水平，所以微软现在的估值仍然是相当的便宜。

十三、亚马逊财报：自研芯片的估值重写与"一生难求"的机会

然后我们来看亚马逊。安迪说自研芯片业务这一块，如果我们芯片这一块的业务作为一家卖芯片的公司，我们年化的营收是500亿，也就是一个季度是125亿。

那我稍微做了一些简单的算术：你500亿的营收，我们按照英伟达的operating margin是64%、63到65左右，我们假设亚马逊的这个operating margin 50%，那你年化的这个operating income就是250亿。给他20倍的这个估值不算贵的，因为凭亚马逊芯片这一块的增速，20倍的估值绝对不贵。给他20倍的估值，光就芯片业务这一块就是5000亿（500 billion）。那现在亚马逊是差不多3万亿不到这个市值，就是亚马逊现在六分之一的这个市值的体量是来自于芯片业务这一块。

我们知道现在无论是买方市场买方的分析师还是卖方的分析师，都是看亚马逊都是电商业务，对电商业务分为北美业务、国际业务，然后就是AWS。那未来有一种可能性，因为安迪有聊到，他说未来两年有一种可能性，我们会把自己的自研的芯片往外去卖。一旦像谷歌一样把自研芯片开始往外去卖的话，那在估值的这个模型里面它就要变了——它就变成AWS、电商分为美国这边和国际业务，那最后一块就是芯片业务，因为他们要把芯片业务这一块的margin也全部算进去。但是你三个业务全部放在一起的话，他们会有一个operating leverage在那边。所以未来像亚马逊这类、包括像谷歌，它的一个估值的一个模型可能会出现改变。

然后下面一点我想聊的是安迪说，"我们认为这是一次一生难求的机会"——他讲的是AI几乎所有的应用都会被重新打造，还会有很多的应用我们从来都没有见过，所以我们不会放过这次机会。

我希望大家可以去听一下亚马逊这一次财报会，你听完之后，如果管理层CEO都表现出那么的bullish，我认为除非我认为我自己对市场的判断比他要好，不然的话我应该和他一样的bullish。

那很多时候我不用去判断市场是不是有泡沫、我不用去判断宏观有什么风险、我不用去判断战争的风险到底在哪里，我只需要非常笃定的把这个钱交给他、让他帮我去打理就可以了。所以很多时候其实投资说简单也简单，说难其实也难。难就难在你要放弃自己的这个判断，把这个判断交给公司的管理层。

十四、Meta财报：AI不替代人？小扎的"自相矛盾"

然后我们来聊一下Meta。市场对于Meta的财报的一个反馈相对是最负面的，主要是两点：

第一点就是很有意思，就是它也是和AI对于劳动力市场是相关的。 小扎说到，他说AI会使得人们更加的厉害，要"empower people not replace people"，并不会替代人。

我这边后面三个"哈哈哈"是我自己打上去的，是我自己的内心那个独白：你一边在裁员，然后你一边告诉我AI不会替代人？这完全是自相矛盾的。这就像亚马逊在接受采访的时候，他说"你们不要看AI会替代人员，然后你看我们还在招1万个软件工程师"。但你去看一下亚马逊的一个员工数量的增长，你去看一下微软的员工数量的增长，你去看一下谷歌员工数量的增长——几乎都没有了。他们自己都不招人了，员工数量都不增了，甚至于还在裁员，你告诉我AI不会replace people？

所以很多时候不要信他们说什么，只要看他们做什么就可以。因为从政治这个角度去看，他不可能告诉你AI会去替代劳动力。但是我认为接下去从政治这个角度，AI这一块可能会受到一定的一个阻力在那边，因为确实对于劳动力上可能会造成破坏。但是我们一定要先看到数据才有可能会看到这个pushback。

我们继续看下去。小扎说到，分析员问的是"10个月你们把Llama 4弄出来——就是他们大模型给弄出来——非常的不错，但是帮我们理解一下如何去解锁新产品，接下去的9个月对于现有业务这个提升到底是什么"。

其实我可以理解市场对于Meta的这个悲观。你去看Meta现在的股价，其实在2024年的10、11月份是一年半的时间，它的股价其实基本没有动的。那为什么没有动？是因为Meta本身的营收增速非常的好，margin这个季度的margin其实非常的好。那为什么还是盘后出现了下跌？

就是因为在投资周期里边，资本开支刚刚砸下去，到他最后的这个回报，中间的这一段真空期市场看不到。中间的这一段真空期对于市场来说就是一个不确定性，那这个不确定性市场就会以一个股价下跌的方式来体现。

十五、AI落地的"三个梯队"框架

如果我们把三家云厂——比如说微软、谷歌、亚马逊——他们在起码在云这一块的一个资本开支和他们的一个回报放在一起的话，这三家云厂其实是非常确定的——就是你的资本开支已经体现在你的云营收里边了。所以从投资回报的这个角度、从投资周期、包括已经看到回报的这个角度去看，它其实非常匹配——市场认为OK的，"我已经看到这个回报了，所以你现在投更多的话，我也可以理解"。

第二梯队我认为就是Meta。 为什么Meta在第二梯队？因为Meta起码现在已经把大模型弄出来。我认为他们既然已经把大模型弄出来——虽然这个大模型好像又没有什么声音了，但是我认为接下去还会更新迭代。接下去更新迭代完之后，我认为Meta在AI这一块的产品只是一个时间问题。加上Meta在社交软件这一块的一个派发，它是有绝对的这个护城河在那边的。然后Meta现在还有硬件，这个眼镜现在卖的也非常的好。我认为未来Meta在AI这一块的落地是可以期待的，只是它确实中间需要一个时间。

那什么时候出来？我不知道。我们只需要知道你相不相信小扎能够在AI这一块做出来、你相不相信小扎这个执行力。我认为最终你会发现还是回到管理层那个execution执行力这一块。如果今天Meta并没有把他们这个模型在4月初的时候弄出来的话，那我并不会把它排在第二梯队，他可能和特斯拉一样在第三梯队。

特斯拉也是一样的一个道理，在第三梯队。 在今年年初的时候，在发布上一个第四季度财报的时候，管理层自己说过，接下去特斯拉再次变成一家初创公司——体量非常大的初创公司——什么意思？就是不确定性增加了，接下去我们要增加这个资本开支。

所以我认为可能在时间点上，我上次有聊过了，可能在28年的下半年，我们可能能够真正的看到在美国、起码在北美这一块多个城市有非常迅速robot的这个量产。但是这个前提可能是建立在2026年的资本开支这一块，包括未来AI5的这个上限。

所以特斯拉为什么是第三梯队？不是说第三梯队的不好，而是第三梯队从现在的这个投资到未来这个回报中间的这个时间可能会比较的长，或者这个不确定性的一个跨度时间跨度会比较的长。

那么所以前三大科技股、前三大云厂派第一梯队，Meta在第二梯队，特斯拉可能就在第三梯队。所以我认为无论是Meta也好、特斯拉也好，并不是在AI这块掉队了，只是现在市场在定价这个不确定性——投资的周期和回报这个周期的一个不确定性。

那反而我认为在现在不确定性的时候，确确实实其实是一个增加自己仓位的时候，是一个比较好的实际去积累仓位的时候。

Meta的PE周四收盘是17.2倍，在一个月之前的话是在15.5倍、16倍左右。现在这个估值明显是低于2025年的4月份。当然比22年当时收候要好很多——22年当时是不谈烂卖、它是过街老鼠的10倍以下，8倍9倍多的这个PE，当时市场情绪面是远远比现在悲观的多得多了。但是即使从过去3年的这个PE这个角度去看的话，现在Meta已经是在一个相对的低位了。

十六、苹果财报：净现金中性策略的终结

最后是苹果。我前面去听了一下苹果的财报会议，没有非常多的信息，只是说主要就是在聊Tim Cook聊的"我们第一季度是受到了产能的这个限制，然后第二季度继续会受到产能的这个限制，接下去的两个季度基本上都有这个产能的限制在那边"。所以第二季度的财报，因为他们给了指引，今年第二季度财报也会非常的好。

某种程度，第一个季度之所以增速没有想预期的那么的高、或者高出市场预期那么的多，是因为产能受限制了，很多的订单、很多的销量都要推到第二季度去执行。

但这其实不是我关心的，因为这次是一个iPhone 17的一个refresh cycle，所以反馈也非常的好，产品也非常的好。我关心的是什么？

我认为这次财报里边有一点非常的重要：苹果他们宣布结束了自2017年开始的这个策略，然后2018年开始执行2017年以来的这个净现金流中性的策略，结束了。

什么意思？就是以前中国的这个政策——就是我有多余的这个现金流，我就会用来去派发或者派股息。从公司的一个角度，我们叫corporate finance，从公司这个角度去看，股东他其实就多出来这个现金，它有很多种方法：

一种就是投资自己——投资自己的话又有几种方法： - 一种就是OpEx，你可以增加员工对吧，在员工数量这一块可以增加 - 另外一种就是CapEx，资本开支的话你可以去建厂、可以去做科研，这些都算是类似于资本开支吧。科研一部分的科研是放在OpEx里面的

另外一种就是反馈股东，反馈股东的话又有两种方法： - 一种是派股息，我们最常见的派股息 - 另外一种就是回购

所以基本上是三种方法。那现在苹果这一次结束了以前的一个将近要10年的一个净现金策略，就是接下去我们不会无脑的把多余的现金全部用来进行回购和派股息，我们会留一部分的现金给自己，用来干嘛？很简单，就是用来资本开支或者投资自己。

所以我引用了2月25日我发了这条消息：我说"未来有一天年底或者明年——这个时间点其实比我要早，当然他们这个政策到最后他们那个执行你还不知道什么时候开始要留很多的现金嘛——年底或者明年苹果加入AI的这个推理，到时候看看是不是还有人担心没有人买算力、没有人买芯片，世界上口袋最深的那一位厚积薄发还在场外观望"。我认为接下去苹果会在资本开支这一块会砸钱下去。

我认为这是苹果今天财报会议里面最重要的一点。然后关于AI这一块并没有任何的更新，我觉得我们的一个期待就是6月份Siri的这个推出。

最后是关于苹果的这个估值，那截止到周四收盘是30倍。30倍的苹果的估值是不便宜的，其实是在一个中性偏高的一个位置： - 如果我们把上区间，那么是在2025年的年初是34倍以上 - 然后下区间的话是2025年4月份当时的一个估值是在22倍、23倍左右 - 上区间和下区间我们画一个中线的话差不多是28倍

28倍就是一个过去三年的一个中性的一个位置。那现在在什么位置？30倍。所以现在苹果是在一个中性偏高的一个位置。

十七、APP与活动信息

那最后就是我视频一开始说的APP。我自己的这个APP我在昨天的话APP上面做了一个非常长3小时这个直播，Q&A有2个小时，然后前面画了一小时。那这边二维码是一个下载的一个方式，然后下一次直播是在5月5日。

APP里面最新上线的一个功能是AI的一个bot 24小时——基本上这个AI的bot，你去问他我以前对于任何标的的一些看法、什么时候买入、有没有买入，他都会给你一个非常精准的一个答案。包括某一个标的占我自己的一个整体的一个比重占了多少，大家都可以去问。

然后其实在一个多月之前，在APP里面我还加入了一个研报的一个功能，我们现在已经有三份研报上线了，大家如果感兴趣的话也可以去看一下。

然后现在正在举办的这一个活动，这个活动的截止日期是5月14号，所以大家感兴趣的话可以去看里面其实有很多免费的一些功能，贝塔计算机大家都是可以去试一下，我认为对于大家在控制风险这一块都会有非常大的一些帮助。

那今天就进录到这边，我们下期见。

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的观察：微软「毛利率下降」其实是AI落地最强信号
市场对微软盘后下跌的解读集中在「毛利率被AI拖累」，但这正是被忽略的悖论——**只有当用户疯狂烧token、把"按席位制"的合同烧穿了，企业才会被迫切换为"席位+消耗量"的混合定价**。换句话说，毛利下降不是需求疲软的症状，而是需求过载的症状。这在SaaS史上极少见：上一次类似的"被迫定价升级"是2008年AWS将免费层缩水——之后是云计算的真正起飞。
把这个逻辑外推一下：**未来一年，谁的财报里出现"我们正在从席位制转向混合计费"，谁就是真正跑通了AI落地。** 这是一个比"DAU/MAU"更狠的真实信号——因为它意味着客户用得停不下来，而不是被你硬塞功能。
## 二、跨领域连接：鲍威尔的「最后一舞」与亚马逊的「估值模型重写」共享同一种结构性变化
表面看，美联储换帅和亚马逊自研芯片业务是两件毫无关联的事。但底层都是**"隐性资产被迫定价"**的过程：
- 鲍威尔时代，美联储的"独立性"是隐形的、不需要被定价的。换成沃什后，市场必须开始**显性地为"美联储独立性溢价"定价**——这部分以前免费包含在美债里。

item.title: [Dwarkesh Clips] How a 29-Year-Old Machiavelli Rose to Power – Ada Palmer
item.dc:creator: Dwarkesh Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、被剥夺公民权的爱国者——一个反直觉的政治悖论

最令人震撼的细节藏在最不起眼的地方：马基雅维利终其一生为一个并不完全承认他公民身份的政府服务。因为祖父欠税，他没资格抽中那些"抽签产生的官职"。我们习惯把爱国主义解释为"国家给了我什么所以我回报"，但这里出现了一种逆向爱国主义——正因为被半排除在外，他反而看得更清、爱得更深。

这与现代心理学中的"局外人优势"（outsider advantage）形成奇妙呼应：人类学家列维-斯特劳斯、社会学家齐美尔都指出，真正能描述一个系统的人，往往是那个站在系统门槛上、半进半出的人。马基雅维利之所以能写出《君主论》这种"冷酷得不像内部人"的作品，可能恰恰因为他永远不是完全的内部人。他爱共和国的姿势，更像一个被拒之门外却依然贴着窗户向内张望的人。

二、"5% 寡头制 vs 1 人独裁"——人类对"自由梯度"的非线性敏感

Palmer 提出的观察极其锋利：从我们现代人看，5% 精英统治和 1 人独裁都不是自由，差别可以忽略。但当时的佛罗伦萨人却愿意为这"一英寸的自由"上街、举旗、流血。

这揭示了一个被现代政治学严重低估的真相：人类对自由的感知不是线性的，而是对"梯度"极度敏感。0 到 1 的差距，远大于 1 到 100 的差距。这与神经科学中的韦伯-费希纳定律（感知与刺激强度的对数成正比）惊人一致——人不是在测量自由的绝对值，而是在测量自由的对数。

跨领域的连接更有趣：这正是为什么硅谷里"从被微管理到拥有少许自主权"的员工幸福感跃升，远大于"从中等自主到完全自主"的跃升；也是为什么朝鲜逃北者描述的最大震撼往往不是首尔的繁华，而是"可以自己决定今天吃什么"这种微小选择。马基雅维利时代人们为之死战的"libertas"，本质上和今天打工人为远程办公一天而欣喜若狂，是同一种神经机制。

三、500 年未断的"影子权力网络"——制度死了，网络还活着

最让我起鸡皮疙瘩的是结尾那句：昨天，鲁切莱家族刚刚向佛罗伦萨市长递了一封信。500 年前从抽签袋里被抽出来掌权的那批家族，今天还在用"反对开发商、要求建花园"的方式继续影响这座城市。

这撕开了我们对"制度更替"的天真理解：共和国倒了，王朝倒了，意大利统一了，墨索里尼来了又走了——但家族网络从未真正断裂。这暗示了一个被政治学忽视的法则：正式制度（formal institutions）的寿命远短于非正式网络（informal networks）的寿命。

跨领域看，这与生物学里"基因 vs 表型"的关系如出一辙：政体是表型，会死；家族网络是基因，会延续。也与哈佛社会学家 Robert Putnam 在《使民主运转起来》中的核心发现暗合——一个地区今天的治理质量，最佳预测变量不是它现在的制度，而是它 800 年前是否有公民社会的雏形。

由此推出一个让人不安的预测：今天硅谷形成的那个由 PayPal 黑帮、Founders Fund、a16z 编织的精英网络，很可能在 2500 年的旧金山仍以某种形式存在——哪怕届时美国早已不复存在。马基雅维利如果活到今天，他研究的对象不会是华盛顿的政客，而是这些跨越政体生灭的家族图谱。

📝 转录文本

一、马基雅维利成长的世界：一个"半公民"的爱国者

This is the system into which Machiavelli is born, in which his family has worked for the Medici family for generations. He grows up expecting to work for the Medici family. But the problem with heredity is that sometimes you get a weak link.

And in the moment that Machiavelli is in his early 20s, coming of age about to work in government for the first time, a government in which he himself is not, in fact, even fully enfranchised. That's one of the fascinating things about the degree of his patriotism.

You weren't allowed to serve in government office fully — the elected lottery offices — if your family was deep in debt. And his grandfather had a lot of unpaid tax debt. So he worked his whole life for a government of which he was not even quite a full citizen. Which is, again, deep love of your country, but also shows: even people who could not be in office deeply loved and cared about this republic, and that important liberty that they felt they had — being ruled by the 5% instead of being ruled by one dictator.

二、"一英寸的自由"：为什么人们愿意为它流血

Yeah. To us, that isn't a very big difference, right? They're still both not democracy. We would say they're both not liberty in the sense that we want liberty. But it's an inch more liberty than monarchy.

And even that small amount of liberty, people loved it. People were willing to fight for it. People were willing to go to the streets and wave their banners and say, libertas for the republic. And because they were invested in it, Machiavelli observes, they sustained it.

三、年轻的美第奇、入侵的法国人，与共和国的回归

But eventually, one particular Medici — I'm not saying the name because they all have the same names over and over and it's really confusing, so it's easier without names — one particular Medici comes to power quite young and weak. He's basically 20 when he's suddenly in charge of a very particular and precarious republic.

And right then, the French are invading Italy, and he's scared, and he botches the diplomacy with France and falls into disrepute. And the city takes the opportunity to kick him out. The subsequent regimes, which are an independent republic again, are the ones for which Machiavelli works.

四、29 岁掌外交：一个"被吓大、被书喂大"的孩子

And so he's 29 when in the subsequent… — 21. Or sorry, who's he? Is Machiavelli…? — Machiavelli. Yeah. Machiavelli's 29 when, in the next, you know, theocratic republic, he's made in charge of diplomacy.

And how does that happen? How is that a 29-year-old with a minor family?

For what — I think, remember — this man saw his country nearly fall six times by the time he was 12. Right. He has grown up fast, and he has grown up scared. Right. And he has grown up bookish and ready to work hard at this.

五、印刷术之外：一个"小家族"少年如何读到那么多书

— So can I ask another question? Yeah. How do you…? This is… I guess, sorry, the printing press has been around for 50 years, but the books are still expensive, right? How is a guy from a minor family read so much?

So it's a minor family in one sense. It's still a very wealthy family. He's not in the top 5%, but he's definitely in the top 10%. And he's had — who have been in the Senate — in the family. So he's still a wealthy elite kind of family. The kind of family that in the 19th century would have gone to Oxford but would not have been a duke.

And also in Florence, there are the libraries of other wealthier families, and your family works for theirs. So he has access to the Medici's library because he is from a Medici client family. And any smart bookish kid who is being well educated in that direction, the family will work hard and make sure that he has access to those collections as well.

So a number of his manuscripts that we know he looked at or copied from are in the Medici collections. Others were other important families — like the Rucellai family, which had an important library. We know that he visited there, gardens and so on.

六、500 年仍在运作的家族网络

And when I say these are important families — like, yesterday, the Rucellai family filed a letter with the mayor of Florence demanding that they not let a slumlord redevelop some land outside the city, and that it needs to be turned into gardens and art centers, co-signed by a dozen other old noble families who were in the bag that lotteries were drawn out of 500 years ago.

These families are still there, and they're still powerful. And the Rucellai have Medici blood in their veins, and they're still exerting this influence over Florence, even now that Florence is part of the larger Italian Republic, right?

So these are very old institutions with a lot of staying power.

If you enjoyed this clip, you can watch the full episode here and subscribe for more clips. Thanks.

观看原视频 →

item.title: [Dwarkesh Clips] How the Medici Bought Florence – Ada Palmer
item.dc:creator: Dwarkesh Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、被忽视的系统真相：美第奇不是"银行家"，而是"私营税务局"

大多数人以为美第奇靠"放贷收利息"致富——这是用现代金融业的滤镜误读了文艺复兴。真正的机制更接近于他们承包了整个基督教世界的现金流。每一个朝圣者投入功德箱的硬币，每一个教区上缴的什一税，都要经过美第奇的账簿才能到达罗马。

这里隐藏着一个让人脊背一凉的洞察：Cosimo 的真正资产不是金币，而是"时间差"（float）。在帆船需要数月才能跨越地中海的时代，谁掌握了资金从收取到送达之间的那段空窗期，谁就拥有一笔无限滚动的、零成本的、合法的"影子资本"。这与今天 Stripe、支付宝、PayPal 的商业模式在结构上完全同构——支付公司真正赚钱的从来不是手续费，而是浮存金。Cosimo 是 15 世纪的支付中介之王，只不过他的"API"是教皇的权威。

二、跨领域连接：抽签民主的"统计学漏洞"，与今日推荐算法是同一种病

视频中最反直觉的一段是关于 sortition（抽签制）。我们通常被告知抽签是"最难腐败的民主形式"——古雅典用过，看似纯粹随机就等于纯粹公平。但 Cosimo 揭穿了一个概率层面的结构性漏洞：当你雇佣了城里三分之一的人，你不需要操纵任何一次抽签，大数定律会自动替你统治城市。9 个随机抽出的人里必然有 3 个是你的人，而在一个无人占多数的会议里，"最大的少数派 (plurality)"就是事实上的多数派。

这个机制和今天的社交媒体平台惊人相似：TikTok、X、YouTube 不需要审查任何一条具体内容，只要让自己的内容生态占据"足够大的随机基数"，算法的统计学结果就会自然倾斜。Cosimo 没有作弊，他只是让整副牌都是他的。 这是一种比贿赂更高级的腐败——它是概率结构本身的腐败，而不是程序的腐败。值得思考的是：现代抽签式公民议会（爱尔兰、法国都在试）是否复刻了同样的漏洞？

三、最让人意外的洞察：Cosimo 在监狱里发现的，是"贿赂的拉弗曲线"

最容易被忽略的，是那段关于狱卒的轶事。Cosimo 写道，那两个狱卒是他这辈子见过最蠢的人——不是因为他们收了贿赂，而是因为他们要价太低。他本可以付几千万，他们却只敢开口要几十万。

这里藏着一个超越中世纪的、关于权力心理学的普遍法则：底层执行者无法在心理上为顶层资源定价。这种"想象力的天花板"是一种结构性的认知枷锁——一个月薪 3000 弗罗林的狱卒，根本无法在大脑中生成"3000 万弗罗林"这个数字作为可谈判选项。

这和现代谈判学中的"锚定效应"、以及行为经济学里的"参考点依赖"是同一现象。更深一层，它解释了为什么反腐败往往不是从打击受贿者开始的——因为受贿者天然会自我设限地低价出卖系统。真正危险的腐败者，是那些敢于按系统真实价值开价的人。Cosimo 的牢狱之灾，反而让他领悟到：与其贿赂底层执行者，不如直接收购选举的设计者——这就是他后来"我要不再满足于控制plurality，而是去贿赂真正决定选举的人"那句宣言的真正起源。

而他那句名言"Rich without power is dangerous（富而无权，危如累卵）"，本质上是文艺复兴版的"资本必须政治化才能自我保护"——这正是后来汉密尔顿、洛克菲勒乃至今日硅谷大公司游说华盛顿的同一逻辑。Cosimo 在 1433 年的牢房里，独自发明了现代政商关系的基础公理。

📝 转录文本

引子：美第奇为何成为教廷的银行家？

Dwarkesh: I think you should discuss the fact that the Medici are the bankers for the papacy. What does that mean? Why is that necessary? And how they're able to make money off of that from the interest on the float.

一、前现代的征税系统：层层分包的"包税制"

Ada Palmer: You know, in the premodern world, collecting taxes is very difficult and complicated system. It is generally done by the centralizing power that has the right to tax delegating somebody local who knows.

So if you're in a town, there will be a local tax collector. It's his job to go around to everybody and collect taxes, and then send a portion of those taxes home to the central power and keep a remainder to pay himself.

The central power will say: "We expect x amount of taxes from this area." And when you hear about "wicked tax collectors" — wah-hah — uh, it's because if you are told "we want 10,000 florins worth of tax from this town," but you extract 15, you can keep the other five — because the 10 is what you need to send to the central. So the more you extract, the more you get paid.

This delegate system, in which there's a local tax collector and even a more local tax collector below him who might collect tax from a particular village, means that you depend a lot upon the person whose job it is to collect your taxes.

二、Cosimo 作为教皇的银行家：掌控整个基督教世界的现金流

So when Cosimo is papal banker, he is the person who is collecting and channeling the money from every church in Christendom. When everybody puts a coin into a collection box, or pilgrims come and put money — all of the wealth that's supposed to flow back to the papacy is actually flowing to Cosimo. Cosimo is passing it on to the papacy after taking a cut.

So that is a lot of money moving quickly. It is also a lot of ability to make contracts and contacts. We all know how important networking is.

And he rises in prominence from a banker to somebody who has enough money to effectively take over his state via manipulating the "guys out of a bag" system.

三、抽签制（Sortition）：一种古老却可被腐化的制度

And so to discuss that again briefly: if you have a system where you lottery people — sort is the technical term for it — this is a very old form of government. Ancient Athens uses it. It actually works really well.

But, like any institution, it is corruptible. And in the same way that you can corrupt voting by bribing people or manipulating the machines or manipulating voters, you can also corrupt sort — uh, by bribing the people who pull names out of the bag, or by the simpler mechanism which Cosimo uses first:

If you're a giant big-wig in the city and you employ a third of the people in the city, and a third of the people in the city are on your payroll, and nine guys at random are chosen out of a bag, three of them are going to be your guys — just statistically.

And so if you tell all your guys: "I want this policy, this policy, and this policy, and if you have questions, send for me and I'll tell you what to do" — when the plurality on a random council all have a plan, and it's your plan, you effectively control the city.

And so in that way, the Medici effectively control this lottery system, because they've guaranteed that the plurality in a situation that doesn't have a majority will always be them.

四、1433 年的危机：抽签的反噬与监狱中的贿赂

Dwarkesh: Yeah. But of course there's a chance to that.

Ada Palmer: And in 1432 — sorry, in 1430 and 1432 — Cosimo has bad luck, and the lottery draws a lot of people who dislike him and doesn't draw any of his guys. And they immediately declare him a traitor to the state and arrest him and lock him in a tower.

And he bribes his way out: he offers the equivalent of about $300,000 to the guard outside the cell, and $700,000 to the captain of the guard to smuggle him out of the tower.

And he wrote in a letter later that they were the two most foolish men he'd ever met — because he was Cosimo. He would happily have paid them tens of millions of dollars to let him out of there, but they weren't ambitious enough to think to ask for more than a few hundred thousand.

五、凯旋归来与权力的升级：从"控制多数派"到"贿赂选举本身"

So he escapes, and then the next election — by gum — happened to elect entirely people who just loved Cosimo. And they invited him back to the city in triumph, and they declared him Father of the Fatherland, and they arrested and persecuted all of his enemies, who turned out to be guilty of tax evasion and all sorts of other things.

And that was the moment that his grip tightened. And he's like: "I'm going to stop simply controlling a plurality, and I'm going to start bribing the people who actually run the elections."

And his famous quote about this is: "It is dangerous to be rich and not powerful."

结语

Dwarkesh: If you enjoyed this clip, you can watch the full episode here and subscribe for more clips. Thanks.

观看原视频 →

item.title: [GoldRepublic Global] Peter Schiff: Gold Is Signaling a Historic Reset
item.dc:creator: GoldRepublic Global
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

隐藏的镜像：1971 与 2025 的对称性

最让人意外的观察不是 Schiff 说的"美元末日"——而是他无意中描绘了一个完美的 54 年镜像结构。1971 年美国对世界单方面违约（关闭黄金兑换窗口），是"美国对外部的金融暴力"；而 2022 年冻结俄罗斯储备、2025 年的全球关税战，本质上是同一动作的延迟回弹——只不过这一次，美国不是在切断与黄金的链接，而是在亲手切断世界与美元的链接。

换句话说：美国 1971 年通过单方面行动摧毁了"美元-黄金"的契约；2025 年又通过单方面行动正在摧毁"世界-美元"的契约。同一种政治反射弧，时隔半个世纪打在了自己身上。 Schiff 没有明说，但这是他叙事中最深的对称——黄金不是在"回归"，而是在完成一个被推迟了 54 年的清算回路。

跨领域连接：免疫学视角下的"储备资产"

这里有一个出乎意料的跨领域类比——把全球货币体系当作一个免疫系统来看。美元作为储备货币的特权，类似于一种"免疫耐受"：世界各国把自己的免疫细胞（储备）训练成不攻击美元这个"自体抗原"。但 SWIFT 制裁、关税武器化，相当于美元这个"自体抗原"突然开始主动攻击宿主——免疫耐受被打破，全球开始把美元识别为外来威胁。

而黄金恰恰是免疫学里的"无抗原性物质"——它没有发行人、没有政治意志、没有被冻结的可能性。这解释了一个 Schiff 自己都没完全展开的悖论：为什么央行在零售投资者抛售黄金时疯狂买入？ 因为央行不是在做投资决策，它们是在做免疫决策——在重建一种不依赖于"对方善意"的自我保护机制。这与 Bitcoin 试图扮演的角色形成了讽刺性对照：Bitcoin 的"无国籍"是技术性的，黄金的"无国籍"是物理性的——而央行选择了后者。

被忽视的盲点：Schiff 自己的"时间观"是错的

最反直觉的一点——Schiff 警告了一辈子的"危机"，但他从未真正解释过为什么系统能延续这么久。从他 2006 年开始警告美元崩溃，到今天将近 20 年。这不是预测失误，而是揭示了一个他没意识到的事实：信用货币系统的崩溃不是一个事件，而是一个相变过程。

类似于水从液态到气态——99°C 时水还是水，100°C 时仍然像水，只有所有分子都获得足够能量后才会沸腾。Schiff 描述的"金融末日"实际上是一个慢动作熔化：美国制造业的流失、债务的累积、储备地位的侵蚀——每一项都已经"沸腾"了几十年，只是肉眼看不到气泡。

真正值得警觉的不是"危机什么时候来"，而是 Schiff 在演讲中无意透露的一个细节：白银今年首次领先黄金，金矿股首次跑赢黄金本身。在金属市场的技术语言里，这种"末端资产先于核心资产爆发"的形态，与生态系统崩溃前的"杂草先于乔木枯萎"是同构的——系统的边缘往往比中心更早感知相变。如果他这次说对了一件事，那不是"美元会死"这个老生常谈，而是"白银的领涨"——这个连他自己都只用一句话带过的细节，可能才是真正的信号。

📝 转录文本

引言：一个被嘲笑过的预言者

For years, he had been warning about a housing crisis, but they laughed at him.

It's a fantasy. People can't sell their house. The inventories are exploding all over the country. Price is going to fall through the floor. You guys are looting yourself. We heard it.

After the great financial crisis hit, he went to the streets to educate the angry crowd about the real culprits. And I've come down here to Occupy Wall Street to represent the 1% and see if I can have a dialogue with the other 99%.

As a convinced libertarian, he fights the Federal Reserve, but he is also one of Bitcoin's biggest critics.

Bitcoin is Ponzi scheme. This isn't digital gold. This is fool's gold. Bitcoin doesn't have a future.

He is the chief economist at Eurobeck, chairman of Shiv Gold, host of Shiv Radio, and the best-selling author. And now he has another warning.

I think the next crisis that could happen before Trump leaves office will be much worse than 2008.

He's here to show why gold is the ultimate futureproof asset. Welcome, Peter Schiff.

开场：黄金牛市仍处于早期

Thank you. I'll try to live up to the introduction. You know, if I'm the number one gold analyst, that just gives you an indication of how early we are in the gold bull market because there's not enough people who pay attention to it.

And, you know, I know there's a lot of people out there. It's hard to see with all the lights, but this is a hell of an event to get this kind of a turnout for a gold conference.

You know, I just came from Dubai. I was there a few days ago and I spoke at the blockchain week, and I think we have more people here, which is very rare now. I think they probably have more people now in Abu Dhabi at Bitcoin Mena because I spoke at their conference in May, and there were like 35,000 people in total attendance. But I think at the lecture hall, well, there was more people there.

But that was an indication that we probably hit the peak of the market. In fact, Bitcoin is lower today than it was back in May, especially if you price it in gold. In fact, Bitcoin is 40% lower priced in gold than it was four years ago. So it gives you an idea of what's going on.

黄金的过去就是黄金的未来

But anyway, I'm going to talk about the future of gold. And I think the future of gold looks a lot like the past of gold. I think just modernized for the 21st century, but gold has been the cornerstone of the global monetary system. And that's where we're headed again.

We kind of took a detour back in 1971 when the US suspended the convertibility of Federal Reserve notes into gold, which legally are dollars in the United States. Dollars are actually defined as a weight of gold or as a weight of silver.

Oh, and by the way, just before I came down here, I checked the price. Silver is now over $60.60. So, it's an all-time record high. So silver, the future of silver looks very bright too.

1971 之后：法币实验与 70 年代的滞胀

So in '71, when we suspended the convertibility of US Federal Reserve notes, otherwise known as dollars, into legitimate money into gold, right? We went on this 50 plus year experiment in just fiat money where everybody's currency is simply backed by another currency, the dollar, which ultimately is backed by nothing.

And when we went off the gold standard 1971, you know, that ushered in the decade of the 70s. It's not an accident that we had the stagflation of the 1970s. It's a direct consequence of that bad decision.

Now, yes, the dollar lost a tremendous percentage of its value relative to gold when the price of gold went from 35 up to 850, but the dollar did go down against other fiat currencies. The dollar lost about 2/3 of its value relative to other major currencies like the Deutsche Mark, the Swiss Franc, the Japanese yen.

So, it wasn't just gold. The dollar was going down against everything. And of course, prices went up. Oil prices went from $3 a barrel to $40 a barrel. But it wasn't that oil was becoming more expensive. It's just that the dollar was becoming less valuable.

You know, we like to blame OPEC for raising oil prices. Well, they didn't raise their prices. We started paying them in paper instead of gold. And when we started to pay in paper, we needed more of it. So they weren't raising their prices. We were debasing our money. And how could we expect them to keep the price the same?

Volcker–Reagan 改革与美元的延寿

And so the dollar went down a lot during that decade, but it was marked down, but it wasn't, you know, counted out. The world did not go off the dollar standard that had been established in Bretton Woods despite the fact that it was no longer redeemable in gold. And of course, had the dollar not been redeemable in gold, the world never would have agreed to go along with that.

But after 1980, Paul Volcker, Ronald Reagan came in, interest rates went up to 20% short-term, and Reagan came in, and there were some substantial reforms. Tax system was reformed. And a lot of confidence was reinstilled in the US economy in the US dollar that was rapidly deteriorating throughout the 1970s.

And so as a result of what we did, we were able to maintain the dollar's reserve currency status, and that really allowed the United States to evolve its economy in a very unsustainable way that has gotten to the point where today we have almost $40 trillion in debt.

从最大债权国到最大债务国

But not just that debt. In 1980, the United States was still the world's largest creditor nation. The world owed the United States a lot of money because Americans had made investments all around the world. And we also ran large trade surpluses with the rest of the world. Not just in services, but we had manufactured trade surpluses. We made everything and we exported it to the rest of the world. And we took the money that we earned exporting manufactured goods and we accumulated assets overseas and we became the world's wealthiest creditor.

Well, now the United States is not only the world's largest debtor, but we owe more money than all the other debtor nations of the world combined. And we no longer have trade surpluses. We have trillion dollar plus per year trade deficits.

And all of this has been made possible by the fact that the dollar stayed as the world's reserve currency. And now the world needed dollars. The world needed to earn dollars. Well, the only source of dollars is the United States, but the United States creates dollars out of thin air. Doesn't cost us anything to just conjure up dollars. And we're able to use the dollars that we conjure out of thin air and we exchange them for the goods that the rest of the world produces.

But those goods require real resources in order to create. You need to have factories. You need to have supply chains. You need to have resources. You need to have workers. You need to do all this hard stuff. And the world does that and then America just creates money and gets it.

寄生式繁荣：美国的两大短期红利

And so we're able to live beyond our means, but only because the rest of the world is content to live beneath its means to finance it. And then the dollars, the paper dollars that the world earns selling us all this stuff that we didn't produce and that we can't afford, they then take those paper dollars and they buy our assets.

Which is one of the reasons now we're the world's biggest debtor nation because we've sold off a lot of our real estate, a lot of our stocks to the rest of the world so that we can consume what they produce. And they also buy a lot of our bonds. They buy US Treasury bonds, they buy mortgage-backed securities, they buy corporate bonds.

But as a result of this process, America gets two short-term benefits that we have been enjoying. One is that consumer prices are much lower than they otherwise would be because the supply of goods that Americans can buy is much higher because of all the goods that are coming into the country from the rest of the world. If we simply had to consume what we produced ourselves, prices would be much higher because we don't have the supply of goods and we keep creating money. But what happens is we create money and we send the money abroad and the goods come back. So we create inflation money, the expansion of the money supply, but the money isn't bidding up domestic prices. We send it to Asia. We send it all around the world and the goods come in. So that keeps a lid on prices in the United States.

At the same time, when the money comes back, it's coming back into financial assets, bidding up our stock prices, bidding up real estate prices, bidding up bond prices. So not only do we get lower consumer good prices, we get lower interest rates because we get to borrow all this money from the rest of the world.

Now, we're not actually borrowing it because borrowing connotes repayment, but we have absolutely no ability to repay any of the debt. All we can do is borrow more money to pay the interest on the money we've already borrowed. And if any of the bonds mature, well, we have to find new lenders so we can borrow that so we can pay off the old lenders, right? We run this giant global Ponzi scheme in debt.

系统终局：世界正在脱离美元本位

But I think what we are close to now is this whole system coming to an end. The US took the world off the gold standard in 1971. I think the world is now going off the dollar standard, and there's a lot of indications now that this day is coming. Right? It's long overdue. We've been kicking the can down the road for a long time.

But here is what makes me think that we're a lot closer to this. And a lot of people take for granted, too. They say, "Look, you know, the dollar will always be the reserve currency by default because there's nothing to take its place. I mean, who's going to trust the euro? How you going to trust the Japanese yen or the Chinese RMB?" Right? They say the dollar is the cleanest, dirty shirt in the hamper, right?

But they overlook the fact that gold is not in that hamper. Gold is clean and gold is real money. Gold is not currency. It's money.

触发器一：拜登时代的对俄制裁——美元的武器化

And what's happened recently, I think, is moving the world from the dollar to gold.

First started with Biden, which were the sanctions on Russia, which at the time they were first imposed, I specifically said it was a very bad idea for this precise reason. We had never really weaponized the dollar to that degree, and we told Russia, "You have been holding all of these US dollars as reserve, which is, you know, a gift to us." Right? We get the benefit from all those dollars that are just held by Russia in reserve, and we basically took them away from them, saying to them, "You don't have access to them. You lose your dollars, you lose your treasuries, because we don't like what you did, right? You invaded Ukraine."

I don't want to get into that, but whatever, the United States says, "We don't like what you did and this is how we're punishing you." Now, obviously, Russia did not like that, and that was a big incentive for Russia to start accumulating more gold.

But the rest of the world, you know, looked at that and said, "I could be next, right? It is not a good position to be in to give the United States that much power." I mean, think about China. Look at all the dollars China has. And we're keep talking about how China's our enemy, right? Well, if China's our enemy, why are they going to put themselves in this vulnerable position where we could yank their reserves out from under them?

So, I think this was a wakeup call politically for the entire world that they need to dedollarize, they need to lessen the dependence on the US dollar, on the SWIFT system, and just wean themselves off of this.

触发器二：特朗普第二任期的财政失控

But then Donald Trump has now given the world, I think, three more reasons to get out of the dollar.

So one of them, right, is the deficits. Right, so when Biden was president, right, he basically continued the Trump policies of during COVID, which was, you know, printing massive amounts of money, running huge deficits, right, the worst monetary and fiscal combination in history, which is why we had the big increase in consumer prices that we did under Biden that started under Trump. You know, the CPI in the US, which is still a flawed way to measure prices, but it really started to go up in the final six months of the Trump administration, but all that inflation had its roots in the COVID stimulus.

And so it continued under Biden, and that's why the highest year of CPI increase under Biden was his first year because it was mostly left over from Trump. But then Biden continued and expanded the COVID-era stimulus, and we took deficits that were 7 to 800 billion a year and turned them into 2 to 3 trillion dollar a year deficits running now as far as the eye can see, except when we have the next recession, official recession, the deficits are going to skyrocket from there.

But the Republicans ran a campaign of, "We're going to have to roll back government. We're going to slash government spending. We're going to bring Elon Musk in here and the Department of Government Efficiency. We're going to cut trillions of dollars in spending because spending is the source of inflation, and Biden spent too much money."

And so this gave the world some hope. Okay, the US is going to get its fiscal house in order, right? Well, what did the Republicans do, you know, with Trump leading the charge? They took all of the Biden deficits and not only preserved them, but expanded them, right? The big beautiful bill increased government spending, cut taxes, and increased the deficits, right? Didn't take an opportunity to reduce them. We made government even bigger than it was before.

And so this is a message that the United States is never going to get its fiscal house in order. We know the Democrats won't do it because they love government spending. And if even these Republicans that ran a campaign on cutting government, if even they can't do it, then there's no hope, right?

And the debt is now spiraling out of control. The interest on the national debt alone is over $1.3 trillion a year. In the United States, we're spending more on interest than we do on national defense. It is the second largest line item in the federal budget, exceeded only by Social Security. And this is just temporary because rates are still low. The government is still borrowing at 4%. That is a low interest rate. Remember, I mentioned that rates got to 20% in 1980. I mean, if they got to half of that today, imagine that. And we were in much better shape fiscally in 1980 than we are today.

触发器三：美联储独立性的崩塌

So, you have the fiscal train wreck, but you also have what's happening with the Fed. You know, one of the reasons that there's confidence in the dollar is because there's confidence in the independence of the Fed. Now, I've thought the Fed independence was a pretense, but we're now dropping that pretense because Donald Trump is now stacking the Fed the way Roosevelt tried to stack the Supreme Court during the New Deal.

He kicked off Lisa Cook, or he's trying to, because, you know, he came up with a reason — mortgage fraud, whatever. But the real reason is he wants to put his own person on the Fed, and he's already done that by putting one of his lackeys from, you know, economic advisors in the Fed who's there just to cut rates.

But the next Fed chairman is going to be Trump's puppet, and everybody pretty much knows that that's the case because Trump is demanding that the Fed slash interest rates when they're already too low.

Again, the biggest problem underlying the US economy is that interest rates have been too low for too long. That's what's screwed up the economy. That's why we're so dependent on the rest of the world. That's why we don't have the factories. That's why we have to import everything that we consume, because nobody saves. And if we don't have savings, we don't have capital investment. We just spend and spend and borrow money because interest rates are too low.

We got a housing bubble. That's why no one can afford to buy a house because the prices are too high. But all this is a function of low interest rates. We need high interest rates, but we can't afford high interest rates because we have so much debt.

But the world now sees what we're doing. We want to cut interest rates at a time when inflation is already rising. It's well above the Fed's so-called 2% target and headed higher. Even the way the government measures it. So, I think the world is going to lose confidence in the Fed and therefore lose confidence in the dollar.

触发器四：关税战与"解放日"的反讽

So, you've got the fiscal train wreck coming. You've got a loss of confidence in the Fed. And then lastly, you've got the tariffs, right?

Trump has now declared war on the entire world, right? He said, "Everybody is screwing us over. Everybody is taking advantage of us. So, everybody's got to pay, right? Everybody's got to pay tariffs for the privilege of selling American stuff." Right? But meanwhile, we're the ones that enjoy the privilege. We get to consume the stuff and we didn't have to produce it.

You know, when he gave his big speech on Liberation Day, he was right about one thing. There was a liberation. But he was wrong about who was going to get liberated. Actually, it's going to be the rest of the world that's going to be liberated of the burden of having to support the American consumer.

And so but now that we've imposed these tariffs, right, what have we done? We now made it harder for Americans to afford to buy the imported products because now all the imported products come with a big tariff. And initially some of the world's exporters may eat some of the tariff just because they already produce the stuff and they're not really sure where they're going to sell it. But the world now has another reason to try to change its whole system of distribution of goods.

I think what's going to happen is more of the goods that are produced outside the United States will be consumed outside the United States, and our trading partners will trade more amongst themselves than with us. And all of this, I think, is going to be a major win for the global economy, in particular for the emerging markets, which I think have borne the lion's share of the subsidy that the United States has enjoyed all of these years with the exorbitant privilege of printing the world's reserve currency.

黄金的技术信号：13 年盘整后的突破

And, you know, running out of time, they're moving into gold. And so the last piece of the puzzle that confirms that I think the end is near is the fact that gold finally broke out of this 13-year consolidation.

You know, gold started this bull market under $300 an ounce. It rose to $1,900 an ounce in 2011, and then it went sideways for 13 years. It went down to $1,000 after going to $1,100, and then, you know, got back up $1,500, $1,600, $1,700, really stayed there, only peaked above $2,000 in the early days of COVID, but then came back down again.

But it wasn't until the beginning of 2024 that gold started to move, and nobody really cared. Nobody paid attention to it. In fact, all during 2024 and for most of 2025, American investors and European were net sellers of gold. Gold went from 2,000 to 3,000 in 2024 and Americans were selling all the way up. And not only were they selling their gold, they were selling their gold mining stocks. And when Donald Trump won in November 2024, gold stocks collapsed and gold went down — not nearly as much as stocks, but analysts were writing it off. Everybody just assumed that, you know, this was it for gold.

But this was a significant move and it was powered by the central banks. So even though retail investors were selling their gold and buying Bitcoin, right, the smart money, the real money, right, the central banks were buying gold. Why are they buying gold? To get out of dollars, right? They need to do something with their dollars. They didn't want to buy euros. They didn't want to buy yen. Gold is what you buy. If you want to get out of the dollar, you're getting into gold.

And so the fact that you now have seen this happen, and then in 2025 this year gold goes up another thousand or now it's over 4,000. The one missing piece of the puzzle was that silver wasn't going along for the ride. And now silver is leading the charge. Silver keeps making new highs before gold. And this is the first year, 2025, where the gold stocks have actually done better than gold. Right? And those are two of the ingredients. Silver was not confirming gold and the gold stocks were not confirming gold. Now both are confirming gold. I think gold is in this new bull market.

结论：黄金作为"经济煤矿里的金丝雀"

But gold is the canary in the economic coal mine.

To me, you know, when I was warning about the problems in the housing market and forecasting the financial crisis 2008, which hit in 2008 — what happened in 2007 was the blow up of subprime. And when that happened, which was something I had been forecasting would happen years before it did, when that happened, I was like, "Aha, the end is near. We're close." Right? This is a warning sign that I had been waiting for. But the powers that be, the mainstream, the Federal Reserve, they weren't worried at all about what happened with subprime. In fact, they said, "It's nothing. It's contained. Don't pay any attention to it." Right? But I knew what that meant. And then we had the crisis.

Well, I think what just happened to the price of gold moving like that is the sign that the dollar's days are coming to an end as a reserve currency, and that it is in the process of being replaced by gold. And I think that that has major implications for the global economy.

I think on balance for the rest of the world, it's going to end up being a big plus. For the United States, it's obviously a major negative in the short run. I don't know how it's going to play out in the long run if we're going to do what is needed to right the economic shift, but it is a major monetary reset.

And I think the crisis that it is going to usher in is going to be significantly bigger than the 2008 financial crisis in the United States, because it's not just about subprime or mortgages. It is a US dollar and a sovereign debt crisis.

Anyway, I'm already past my time. So, thank you everybody.

item.description:
## 隐藏的镜像：1971 与 2025 的对称性
最让人意外的观察不是 Schiff 说的"美元末日"——而是他无意中描绘了一个**完美的 54 年镜像结构**。1971 年美国对世界单方面违约（关闭黄金兑换窗口），是"美国对外部的金融暴力"；而 2022 年冻结俄罗斯储备、2025 年的全球关税战，本质上是同一动作的**延迟回弹**——只不过这一次，美国不是在切断与黄金的链接，而是在**亲手切断世界与美元的链接**。
换句话说：美国 1971 年通过单方面行动摧毁了"美元-黄金"的契约；2025 年又通过单方面行动正在摧毁"世界-美元"的契约。**同一种政治反射弧，时隔半个世纪打在了自己身上。** Schiff 没有明说，但这是他叙事中最深的对称——黄金不是在"回归"，而是在完成一个被推迟了 54 年的清算回路。
## 跨领域连接：免疫学视角下的"储备资产"
这里有一个出乎意料的跨领域类比——把全球货币体系当作一个**免疫系统**来看。美元作为储备货币的特权，类似于一种"免疫耐受"：世界各国把自己的免疫细胞（储备）训练成不攻击美元这个"自体抗原"。但 SWIFT 制裁、关税

item.title: [Bits + Bips] How Microsoft Won in Its Revised Deal With OpenAI
item.dc:creator: Bits + Bips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、表面是"重谈合同"，实质是一次反身性的权力倒挂

大多数解读把这件事看成 Microsoft 与 OpenAI 的"友好分手"，但真正的隐藏结构是：违约本身成了 Microsoft 重新议价的杠杆。

OpenAI 当初为了拿 Amazon 的算力和资本，主动踩进了与 Microsoft 协议的灰色地带——这在合同法里叫 anticipatory breach。一旦你成了"违约方"，你在重新谈判桌上的所有筹码都会被压缩成一句话："你想要我不主张损害赔偿，那拿东西来换。"

意外的观察是：Sam Altman 用算力焦虑换来了短期 Amazon 的资金，但代价是把 2030 年之前所有 IP 的"看涨期权"无偿转让给了 Microsoft。 这是一种典型的"用未来的非线性收益，去对冲今天的线性现金流缺口"——在金融工程语言里，他卖出了一个深度价外的看涨期权，价格却按平价收的。

二、跨领域连接：这是 AI 版的"袁世凯式契约"，也是好莱坞净利润会计的翻版

把 Microsoft 拿到的条款拆开看： - 收入分成继续流向 Microsoft 到 2030 年，与 OpenAI 的技术进展脱钩； - Microsoft 不再向 OpenAI 反向支付分成； - IP 许可延长到 2032 年，但不再独占。

这种结构和好莱坞经典的 "Hollywood Accounting" 几乎同构：制片厂（Microsoft）通过合约把上行收益锁住，把下行风险（巨额训练成本）外推给创作者（OpenAI）。OpenAI 越成功、训练越烧钱，Microsoft 的"免费看涨期权"就越值钱；OpenAI 越失败，Microsoft 也已经把现金流先收走了。这是一种"无论你赢还是输，我都赢"的 convex payoff——在期权定价里这叫 long gamma, short vega 的对手方。

更出人意料的连接：这种合约形态，本质上和 19 世纪英国对印度棉花产业的"特许权安排"是同一个模板——让对方拥有创新的义务，但不拥有创新的果实。 算力成了新的殖民资源，IP 协议成了新的关税。

三、被所有人忽略的真正信号："算力即主权"的拐点已经到来

这件事最反直觉的洞见是——OpenAI 不是输给了 Microsoft，是输给了"算力的物理学"。

LLM 训练的边际成本曲线还在以幂律上升，而任何一个前沿模型公司一旦在算力上慢半步，模型质量就会以复利速度落后。Sam 之所以宁可冒违约风险也要拿 Amazon 的合同，是因为他比任何人都清楚：在 AI 行业，"现金流问题"会在 90 天内变成"模型质量问题"，再在 180 天内变成"生存问题"。

这里有个反共识的预测：未来 18 个月，所有"前沿模型公司 + 超大云厂商"的合约都会朝同一个方向漂移——云厂商拿走 IP 的看涨期权，模型公司拿走算力的看跌保护。 这意味着真正的"AI 公司"不再是 OpenAI、Anthropic 这些做模型的，而是 Microsoft、Amazon、Google 这些控制算力分配权的主权级实体。

而对投资者最有用的一层洞察是嘉宾顺嘴提到却没展开的一句："It's mispriced." —— 市场把 Microsoft 当作 AI 的"参与者"在定价，但合约结构表明它实际上是 AI 的"地主"。参与者赚周期的钱，地主赚永续的钱。 这就是为什么这位嘉宾把它放进 Top 3 持仓——他赌的不是 AI 涨，而是市场迟早要把估值模型从 P/E 切换到 P/Rent。

📝 转录文本

引子：OpenAI 与 Microsoft 重新议约

I want to unpack a little bit what's going on with OpenAI. This news just recently broke.

So, Microsoft is going to remain OpenAI's primary cloud partner, and OpenAI is going to sh— is going to— products will ship first on Azure. Microsoft is going to continue to have a license to OpenAI IP for models and products through 2032, but Microsoft's license is no longer going to be non-exclusive. Microsoft will no longer pay a revenue share to OpenAI, and the revenue share payments from OpenAI to Microsoft will continue to 2030 independent of OpenAI's technical progress.

So this — this seems like, you know, what's your take on all this? Is this friendly — like, "hey, we're both grow"— like, "we agree to disagree" — uh, you know, how do you think about whatever you call it?

Winners and Losers：为什么 Microsoft 是赢家

Yeah. Yeah. Let's do winners and losers on this. Like, overall Microsoft's a winner here.

Yeah. You know, as you know, when a startup cuts a deal with a big corporate, the corporates bring their M&A and corp dev team, and they ink an airtight contract that protects them many ways to Sunday. And Microsoft was the purse funding OpenAI for such a long time, and they win because the Microsoft cloud would benefit from all of OpenAI's development.

Now, here's a couple things:

  1. Microsoft doesn't have to spend money and do a re-gister back to OpenAI. So their expenses linked to OpenAI are going to drop.
  2. They're going to enjoy revenue from OpenAI through 2030. So the upside on that.
  3. They have rights to the OpenAI IP through then as well.

The R&D for these LLMs is significant — you're going to spend tens and tens of billions of dollars to do model training runs. Microsoft gets a free call option on all of that.

背景：Amazon 入局与违约疑云

So, the context was, as we talked about — I think maybe a month ago — OpenAI cut a deal with Amazon, and Amazon invested in OpenAI. OpenAI needed the capital to keep up with Claude. OpenAI is saying, "Hey, we are growth constrained because we don't have compute." Amazon, as a result, got OpenAI to do a deal.

There was a report in the Wall Street Journal that OpenAI violated their agreement with Microsoft, and we all wanted to see how this would play out. It looks like Sam cut a deal that was illegal under contract law and was going to play— figured out later. Well, the "later" just happened, and they reworked terms with Microsoft.

合同法的逻辑：违约方没有筹码

But, you know, if you're in breach in a contract — if you're the breaching party — you don't have the negotiating leverage, because the party that claims breach can allege damages.

So, Microsoft, it seems to me, ironed out a great deal: where they get more revenue, less expense, and a call option on the IP. So, I think it's a big win for Microsoft. It's one of my top three positions right now, too.

I think— interesting. It's mispriced.

节目预告：Bits + Bips 频道迁移

If you've been loving Bits and Bips, don't forget that the show is transitioning to its own feeds on X, YouTube, and your favorite podcast player. If you're not already subscribed to Bits and Bips on its own channels, go there now and hit that subscribe button so you can keep up with our twice-weekly live streams and macro-meets-crypto breakdowns.

Bits and Bips will only be on the Unchained feed for a few more weeks. So, subscribe today to be ready for launch. You can get all the links at unchainedcrypto.com/bitsandbips.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、表面是"重谈合同"，实质是一次**反身性的权力倒挂**
大多数解读把这件事看成 Microsoft 与 OpenAI 的"友好分手"，但真正的隐藏结构是：**违约本身成了 Microsoft 重新议价的杠杆**。
