OpenAI 当初为了拿 Amazon 的算力和资本，主动踩进了与 Microsoft 协议的灰色地带——这在合同法里叫 *anticipatory breach*。一旦你成了"违约方"，你在重新谈判桌上的所有筹码都会被压缩成一句话："你想要我不主张损害赔偿，那拿东西来换。"
意外的观察是：**Sam Altman 用算力焦虑换来了短期 Amazon 的资金，但代价是把 2030 年之前所有 IP 的"看涨期权"无偿转让给了 Microsoft。** 这是一种典型的"用未来的非线性收益，去对冲今天的线性现金流缺口"——在金融工程语言里，他卖出了一个深度价外的看涨期权，价格却按平价收的。
## 二、跨领域连接：这是 **AI 版的"袁世凯式契约"**，也是好莱坞**净利润会计**的翻版
把 Microsoft 拿到的条款拆开看： - 收入分成继续流向 Mi

item.title: [All-In Podcast] CA Governor Candidate Steve Hilton on Why California is Destroying Itself & How a Republican Can Win
item.dc:creator: All-In Podcast
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的发现：加州的"环保叙事"正在亚马逊雨林砍伐森林

整段访谈中最令人脊背发凉的一句话不是税收数据，而是 Hilton 顺口提到的一个细节：加州为了"climate dogma"限制本土钻油，结果重炼油厂被迫匹配"重质原油"的进口源——伊拉克和南美。为了喂饱加州的炼油厂，亚马逊雨林正在被扩张性钻探。

这是典型的"道德外包"（moral outsourcing）现象——和时尚业把污染外包给孟加拉、科技业把锂矿外包给刚果是同一个结构性病灶。加州人享受着"我们很环保"的心理舒适，是因为污染和碳排放被推到了12海里之外（加州空气资源委员会真的只从海岸12英里外开始计算碳足迹）。这是会计学上的"碳殖民主义"。

更深的讽刺：油轮烧的是bunker fuel，是地球上最脏的燃料形式之一。所以加州的"climate policy"实际净效果可能是负的——它没有减排，只是把排放转移到了无法投票反对它的人头上。

二、跨领域连接：加州正在重演1970年代英国"被工会绞杀"的剧本——但这次没有撒切尔的解药

Hilton 的个人轨迹本身就是一个隐藏的预测模型。他亲历过1979年英国的"不满之冬"（垃圾堆积、死人无法下葬、98%边际税率），又是撒切尔改革的同情者，现在他在加州看到了完全相同的结构性病理：

  • 工会对立法的垄断（加州民主党捐款人前三名：政府工会、诉讼律师、非政府工会）
  • 滞胀型经济
  • 监管使任何建设都不可能完成
  • 道德优越感掩盖经济失败

但这里有个Hilton 自己都没说出口的悖论：撒切尔之所以能赢，是因为英国在1979年濒临破产、IMF 已经介入、中产阶级真的体感到危机。加州还没到那一步——它还有AI、半导体、好莱坞这层"经济缓冲垫"在持续输血。这意味着加州的痛点不够深，改革窗口可能要等到一次真正的财政崩溃才会打开。换句话说：Hilton 可能来早了五年。

三、最隐藏的系统性真相：加州的"反垄断州"本身建立在三重垄断之上

加州自我形象是"反垄断、反大企业"的进步州，但 Hilton 揭示了一个未被命名的结构：加州治理建立在三个互锁的垄断之上—— 1. 工会对立法的垄断（CEQA私权诉讼是工会的勒索杠杆） 2. 诉讼律师对司法的垄断（环境诉讼70%被用来阻止住房） 3. 气候教条对监管的垄断（每个住房单位30,000美元的费用，建筑规范强制EV充电桩）

这三者形成了一个"自我强化的腐败回路"：工会用环境法起诉→开发商被迫签订工会专属协议→工资上涨2-3倍→住房成本上涨→低收入者更需要福利→工会会员靠政府合同生存→工会向民主党捐款→民主党立法保护工会。

这与中世纪的行会制度（guild system）几乎完全同构——表面是"保护工人"，实际是通过法律壁垒制造稀缺，让圈内人收租。这也解释了一个看似矛盾的现象：为什么"最进步的州"有最高的贫困率、最严重的住房短缺、最不平等的财富分配。因为进步主义的修辞掩盖了一个本质上前现代的、寻租型的经济结构。

最 unexpected 的洞见：加州的真正问题不是"太左"，而是太"行会化"。一个真正的左翼州会建公共住房、降低准入门槛、削弱寻租。加州做的恰恰相反。它是穿着进步主义外衣的封建主义。

📝 转录文本

嘉宾介绍与背景

主持人：All right, everybody. Welcome back to the All-In Interview Show. We're very lucky today to have a candidate for the governor of California who is extremely unique in a number of ways. First of all, he's a Republican. And second, he's a Brit. Welcome to the program, Steve Hilton.

You've decided to increase the degree of difficulty in two ways, but you're polling fantastic. You've got five or six people in the polls.

He's leading the field.

You're leading the field. Obviously, it's going to get narrowed a bit when the Democrats shiv a couple more people and get them out of the race and then pick their eventual winner in their cobble um whenever that happens when Nancy Pelosi picks who's running. But, uh Steve, maybe you could start by — sorry guys, I got jokes. But, Steve, maybe you could introduce yourself a bit and tell us why you're running.

Well, hang on. Can I just say just after that great intro where you just tried to kill my chances in just a couple of words. Thanks a lot, Jason. Really appreciate it.

主持人：Let me actually tee this up. I've known Steve since 2012-2013 when he and his wife Rachel Wetstone moved to Silicon Valley. Rachel worked at Facebook initially and then she worked with you Jason at Uber and then has had a great run, and Steve similarly. And you said it in a funny way, but ultimately this is an incredible land of immigrants and Steve has a really compelling story.

So before we jump into the questions, I know your background, Steve, but I do think it's important — go back to your parents, your mom, how you grew up, and just set the stage for how you made it out from the way you started, because I think that's important, and then how you got to the United States and why.

匈牙利移民家庭：从布莱顿到牛津

Hilton：Thank you. Thank you. I appreciate that. And you're right. We've known each other a long time now. And it's a great joy to be here. By the way, just want to say it's a great joy to be on a show where I don't have to wear the suit and shirt. And you know, that's one of the things about running for governor that I'm loving most of it, but dressing up is not the favorite part for me. So, it's great to be with you.

I thought for this show, we got to get it right. The more I think about my background, the more I think it is really important in terms of how I see things and what I want to get done.

My parents are Hungarian. They were refugees from communism, and I grew up in England in a town called Brighton on the south coast. And you know, we just had a regular working-class immigrant aspirational family story I guess. My parents actually split up when I was young. My stepfather's also a Hungarian. He had an amazing story. He was a refugee as well, but literally ran across the border. He grew up in a small village on the west side of Hungary.

And in 1956 when you had the Soviet invasion, he tells this amazing story. They heard on the radio, "The Russians are coming." And he and his brother and some friends from his school — he was 14 years old, like one year younger than my youngest son right now — and they just ran. They literally ran. They said, "Right, we want our freedom." And they ran to the border, barbed wire fences, minefields, got shot at by the guards. All that — half of them were killed — and he ended up in a refugee camp in Austria, and from then to England.

So all of that I guess just gives you that sense of real appreciation for freedom and opportunity. And I grew up in England, worked very hard, ended up at Oxford University, but my first job was project manager for a construction company. I just wanted to earn money. I just wanted to get out. I think that's exactly the right phrase that you used.

After Oxford, I went to work for a little bit of the Conservative Party in England. Then I worked for a big ad agency, worked all around the world, started my own business, couple of offshoots of that, including a couple of restaurants. Then went back into politics when my friend David Cameron, who I'd met many years before, had gone into politics, got elected to parliament. I helped run his campaign for the leadership of the British Conservative Party, won that election, and then worked with him to get the Conservatives elected when he became prime minister in 2010.

I joined him in 10 Downing Street. I was senior adviser to the prime minister. Most of my job was really focused on trying to implement our reform program. And then in 2012, that's when we met. We moved here because Rachel — actually before Facebook, she was at Google. She had this big global job at Google, running comms and public policy for Google worldwide. I had my job in number 10. It was actually when our second son was born. There was a lot — the travel for her and the time difference. So that's why we moved here.

主持人：Well, you're also notably — you became naturalized. You're a citizen of the United States now. So you have dual citizenship. Less people are confused by the accent. You're running for governor and you're a citizen of —

政治觉醒：撒切尔时代与"工人 vs 懒汉"

主持人：Let's talk about your political setup. So, being a child of Hungarian immigrants escaping communism, you're going to hear a certain version of what the role of the state is versus what the role of the family or the individual is. Then, growing up in the UK, I'm sure your attitudes either get cemented or change. Give us the setup. What is the political evolution of Steve Hilton? What did he believe and then what does he believe now, and what has shaped these beliefs?

Hilton：It really goes back to just around when I first really started thinking about it all — it was just as Margaret Thatcher was coming to power. The 70s in England were a disaster, a decade where the economy was completely stagnant and sclerotic. Unions ran everything. There was this period called the winter of discontent in 1979 when you had massive strikes. Famously, the dead went unburied and trash was piled up in the street — just real collapse of everything. And that's what Thatcher came in to fix.

I really did identify with that, as well as with the very clear stand against communism. So she was — funny enough, when I was thinking about the video that I made to launch my campaign about a year ago now, we ended up putting that in there. I thought, well, actually that was the thing that got me going. I was totally inspired by her, but also the focus that she had on business and enterprise and hard work.

And remember my stepfather — they weren't at all political, by the way. It wasn't like some household where we talked about politics. It really wasn't. But he had this thing that stuck in my mind. In England you've got the Conservative Party equivalent of the Republicans and for the Democrats it's the Labor Party. And I remember he just used to say, "Mrs. Thatcher's for the workers, and Labor are for the layabouts." This phrase stuck in my mind about the importance of work and hustle. And I think about that all the time.

加州 = 1970年代的英国？

主持人：Where do you think California is, if you contrast?

Hilton：Well, this is the point I was just about to get to. We really are there. There are so many things I see in California today that are exactly like the UK in the 70s. You've got the massive dominance of the unions in policymaking. You've got a sclerotic economy. You've got massively high taxation. I mean, it was higher then — at one point the top rate when you add in the wealth taxes in the UK was literally 98%. You had that confiscatory taxation and top rate of 60% and so on. So very, very similar.

And actually, funny enough, Mike Moritz actually sent me a report that someone had done about the UK today. And again, there's just these eerie parallels with how impossible it is to do anything in the UK, to build anything — the over-regulation. When I read this report, it's just exactly like California today.

By the way, one thing, Jason, just to be clear: I am a proud American now, but I actually renounced my UK citizenship. I did that because I just wanted to be clear that — just to borrow the title of the show —

主持人：You're all in.

Hilton：All in. Literally. I think it's really important everyone knows that. And I am.

税收政策：10万以下免税，10万以上7.5%统一税

主持人：Thanks for the background there. You have some unique policy positions. Taxes, I think, is the most unique and dare I say pretty populist. You want to have no state tax in California for people with under $100,000 in income, and then a flat tax for everybody over 100K of 7.5%. How is that possible? And is that something you've studied? And where did this come from?

Hilton：The tax plan that I put out there — that was the first day of my campaign — I think of it as pro-worker and pro-growth. And I think we need both of those things, because if you look at what's going on in California today, big picture, obviously you can look at the data — that's a real economic disaster.

I'm not sure people appreciate just how bad things are because hiding behind that data point of having the fourth largest economy in the world, which is true — and obviously I'm proud of that, I want California to be big and successful and growing — but underneath that fourth biggest economy data point, you've got the state with the highest unemployment rate in the country and the highest poverty rate in the country, tied with Louisiana.

There's a United Way report from about a year ago. They do it every two years — sort of an assessment of living conditions in California. And they found that over a third of Californians cannot afford to meet basic needs.

So the starting point for my tax plan is: what can we do quickly to help people who are really struggling? If you think about it, the working poor who aren't particularly being taken care of by the welfare system — they're working incredibly hard, but they're being squeezed by all these costs. We have the highest gas prices in the country, the highest electric bills everywhere except for Hawaii, housing costs the highest in the country, insurance — all these costs are so high.

So what can you do to help working people quickly? The starting point was: what's affordable? The $100,000 mark. I remember when we — I was just playing around with numbers. I did it with some economists from the Hoover Institution, where I was a fellow the first couple of years that we moved to. I taught at Stanford, including in the public policy department, also the D school at Stanford. But I was also a fellow at Hoover. So we did the math on the tax plan there about a year ago.

That first part — first 100 grand tax-free — actually, in many counties in California today, the official definition for low income is 100,000. That number may sound very high to people in other parts of the country. It's actually the definition in a lot of counties of low income. So you've got people earning 70 grand, 80 grand, 90 grand in California. They are paying 9.3% state income tax. That rate is higher than the top rate in most states in America. So to me that's ridiculous when you've also got all these other taxes that those exact people are paying — sales tax, property tax, gas tax, all of those are the highest in the country.

主持人：So cutting taxes this significantly means you have to then also cut spending.

Hilton：Yes. But can I just do the other part of it? The 7.5% flat tax. I just thought, when you look at the facts about economic performance — for example, Chief Executive Magazine ranks us, and has done for the last 10 years or so, 50th out of 50 for business climate. A big driver of that is tax. And I'm sure we'll get into the insane proposed billionaires tax and all these things that are driving wealth creation out of our state and business investment out of our state.

So it's not enough just to give some relief to people who are on the lower end of the scale. You've got to actually have a pro-investment, pro-growth tax framework. Apart from anything else, the complexity is ridiculous of our tax system. These endless different rates is ridiculously complicated. And that itself is a cost — the bureaucracy and hassle associated with that. That's why I think a flat tax makes sense.

Remember, this is in the context of federal taxes, all these other taxes. It's not the only component, but the cost — to get to that cost, you've got to reduce spending exactly as you say. The cost of that in total is about an 18.5% reduction in revenue, which takes us down about 60 billion, something like that — which is not even going back to what the budget was just before the pandemic.

If you look at the budget of the state of California, it's nearly doubled in the last 10 years. In the last 5 years it's gone up something like 75%. So this is just bringing the budget back to where it was roughly before the pandemic.

主持人：Let me just summarize. So if you make between 0 and $100,000 a year as a California resident under your plan, no tax —

Hilton：No state income tax.

主持人：No state income tax. If you make $100,000 and a dollar and above, you pay 7.5% flat tax.

Hilton：Yes, that's the concept.

主持人：Okay. How many Californians does that impact? What percentage of the population gets that affordance?

Hilton：The tax numbers usually only count households, and so it's about 7 million households would benefit from the under-100,000.

主持人：Do you know how many that is as a percentage?

Hilton：Working households — we got 40 million people. I think that's probably just over a third, something like that.

主持人：Okay. So a third of homes now essentially go to zero tax.

Hilton：State income tax. Yeah, there's all these other taxes.

削减开支：CalDOGE 与欺诈调查

主持人：Now the pushback would be, if we then take it dollar for dollar from the operating budget, programs will suffer. To your point — I'm putting words in your mouth, but you filled them in — well, not really, because we're just going to go back to 2019-2020 budgets. The difference was: we spent a dollar in 2020, we now spend $2, and nothing has changed. So go from $2 back to $1.50 and everything should be fine, is your point.

Hilton：Yes. And I'd actually go further than that. First of all, what we've seen happen to the budget is basically the expansion that we saw in the pandemic and afterwards is gone — baked into the baseline, which is totally unsustainable. Even without tax cuts, I would argue you've got to get back to a more reasonable growth in spending because we'll go bankrupt — as we're seeing with these deficits that we're getting even when we're not in recession, taking money out of the reserves, out of the rainy day fund to plug the gaps, which is what they're doing. Totally irresponsible fiscally.

But actually, it's more than that. Even if you don't change anything in the composition of the spending and just get back to where we were, that gives you scope for a major reduction in tax. But the other part of it is what we're discovering in terms of where the money is actually going.

Obviously, the whole fraud story has exploded as a national political and economic story ever since Nick Shirley's first investigation in Minnesota just around the time of Thanksgiving last year. We've been making our own contribution to that. A few months ago, I set up — I literally called it CalDOGE: California Department of Government Efficiency. I know that's a controversial brand, but the idea of efficient government is something I think everyone would support. So I thought why not use that, because everyone knows what it is.

We've been looking at the published data on spending to find examples and to make an estimate of the total amount of fraud, waste and abuse in the system. We've now published four separate fraud reports out of CalDOGE.

When I say "we" — this is a longer story we can get into, but one of the ways I think I'm running this campaign differently is that I'm actually putting together a team before the election in terms of others who will run with me for statewide office. You've got some very important positions alongside the governor that are going to be crucial in putting us back on track. In this instance, the state controller is very important, because the state controller is an elected position that has the legal power to audit any organization receiving state money and to stop the flow of money if there's any suspicion of improper spending.

There's a guy running with me called Herb Morgan, and we've been doing this work together. We've published four reports — three of them on individual examples of fraud, and the fourth one was an estimate of the total. We just went through published data from the state auditor, from Medicaid error rates and so on, to make an estimate.

欺诈案例

主持人：What did you find? Give us a couple of examples.

Hilton：Some specific examples. The second fraud report — it's a classic. $1 billion over the last 10 years, 100 million every year since 2015. This is from the climate change mitigation fund, which is part of the cap and trade system. This is actually gas taxes and surcharges on electric bills. 100 million a year was allocated to be spent on climate change mitigation. In this case, it was solar panels for low-income apartment buildings.

We tracked that money, and with an AI partner that can get all the reports — of that 1 billion total in 10 years, the actual amount spent on the purported benefit (solar panel installation) was 72 million. 928 million actually went to nonprofits doing all the usual Democrat-associated stuff frankly — voter registration, environmental justice campaigns, all that kind of stuff. The actual thing was barely spent on. That's $1 billion.

The first one was the cannabis tax, Proposition 64, legalizing cannabis. There's a tax associated with that supposed to be spent on substance abuse prevention. We found $350 million that was supposed to be spent on substance abuse prevention — again, going to this network of nonprofits, over 500 of them, and small individual grants. When you look at what each of those organizations does, it's all the usual stuff: voter registration, activism.

The third one was Project Homekey, which was the homelessness thing that they set up after the pandemic — buying up property for homeless people, sometimes building new property for homeless people or converting hotels. $3.8 billion that we found on that one. Most of which went into the pockets of developers without any real result.

主持人：The California budget, if I'm not mistaken, $350 odd billion?

Hilton：350 billion. 349 this year.

主持人：What percentage of it, in your best estimation, do you think is inefficient, fraudulent, wasted?

Hilton：Our number over the last five years total, our estimate was 425 billion. So averaged over the years, it's about 80 billion a year. Around 20% or so.

主持人：That's unbelievable.

与立法机关博弈：跨党派合作的可能性

主持人：Now, just to bring some reality to the situation, you would have to get through the legislature, which is controlled by Democrats. You can't unilaterally as the governor just say, "Hey, we're cutting these services." We had a Governor Schwarzenegger who tried this very thing — he had to move to the center. You of course have a line item veto in California, so you have some balance there.

But this is fantastic for people to maybe get a reprieve from taxes. You're going to get a major fight with Democrats to cut any spending. What's your plan there if you were to win?

Hilton：Jason, a couple of things. You're right about that. I'm very thoughtful about the realities of these things, and I always make clear — certainly on the tax plan — that you can't do that without the legislature. I think actually we'll get a... there's a possibility of a consensus around some of these items where we can actually work together with the legislature to make it happen.

One indicator of that is actually one of my Democrat opponents in the governor's race, Katie Porter. We were doing a debate the other week in Fresno, and we were talking about affordability or whatever it was, and she said, "Well, I've decided I'm stealing Steve Hilton's tax plan. I agree with him. First 100 grand tax-free, and I think we should take good ideas where we find them." So this is an interesting example that I think part of it we may be able to actually persuade the legislature to do.

主持人：And then I noticed she yelled at you and said, "Get the hell out of my shot."

Hilton：Except a stronger word than "hell."

主持人：Exactly. Than hell.

Hilton：The attitude that I've got on the question of the legislature is that when I'm elected — and I'm sure your eyebrows are raised, saying, "What are you talking about? It's impossible for Republicans to win" (and we'll get into that) — but I'm doing this on the basis that I will, and I'm preparing to actually start implementing the big changes we need to make in a thoughtful manner on day one. Because otherwise what's the point of doing this?

主持人：Steve, do you think that there's legislative agreement or momentum to give you the win? Even though, to your point, I think it's quite significant that the Democrats would signal that it's a legitimate policy proposal — but do you think that if you win, people would see the forest from the trees and realize how important it would be to take salaries under 100,000 to no state income tax?

Hilton：Look, I've seen the Democrat arguments now upfront many, many times. We've done a lot of events together — some of the televised debates, many more that aren't televised. We're literally all saying the same thing in terms of the diagnosis of the problem. It's incredibly expensive to live here. People can't — people are really struggling. The business climate is a disaster. We're massively over-regulated. We can't build anything. Everything takes too long, everything's too complicated. There's a real consensus about diagnosing the problem among all the candidates.

That doesn't mean that we agree, of course, on the solutions. I would argue that the Democrats all, in some version, are more of the same, actually, despite what they say about the problems. But I think that there are certain things where we will be able to get agreement.

I also think that when you have a situation where you have the first Republican governor elected for 20 years, that really will change the dynamic in Sacramento. It actually may loosen things up a little bit, because I think there are people there in the legislature who really understand that things have gone too far. Some of them have said it to me personally — Democrats there — but they feel constrained by the current political situation, the machine being in control. They can't really move, and I think that'll shake things up a little bit.

Secondly, I really do have experience working across party lines like this. I mentioned earlier I worked in 10 Downing Street, senior adviser to the prime minister. He was a conservative prime minister, but it was a coalition government. I literally shared an office in 10 Downing Street with my opposite number from another party, and we would hash things out and argue. We were part of the team that negotiated a coalition agreement and then tried to implement it. I think those skills of actually putting something together where you don't agree about everything but you can make some things happen — it'll be useful in this situation.

主持人：And it's not farcical to think a Republican can't win here. Pete Wilson did two terms. Schwarzenegger did two terms. That's 16 of the last 36 years. It is completely conceivable that a Republican could win.

You and Katie Porter have the same plan. I think Chad Bianco has the same plan, which is under 100,000, all of you agree, no taxes. You're all attacking affordability. They don't believe in cutting services though. They want to increase taxes on businesses, if I'm correct. Why is that plan not as good as yours? Which one do you think would be more appealing to the voters? Paying less taxes, fantastic — makes you more competitive with Florida and Texas. But if they had their druthers, they're probably going to want to see Google and Apple pay more in taxes and not lose their services.

Hilton：Yeah, but we're losing jobs. And I think that that's the consequence of squeezing businesses and high earners more and more. You're seeing it right now — the business exodus. If the billionaire tax proposal goes through, that absolutely puts — I think that's a complete disaster for the tech ecosystem and what we've built in Silicon Valley over the years and all the job creation and wealth creation that comes with that.

Everywhere you go in the state, there are so many conversations — you sit down with business people: "We are on the brink of leaving." I don't think people realize quite how near the cliff edge we are. I'll give you another example. I was just in Pomona the other day, down in Southern California — fantastic company, sheet metal, HVAC duct manufacturing. It's exactly the kind of thing you'd want here. They're union jobs actually — a great manufacturing facility. They are making these HVAC systems, the air conditioning, incredibly important for TSMC and these semiconductor factories and all the high-end manufacturing that's happening in other states. Massive amounts of investment in the AI economy and tech more broadly — but none of it's happening in California.

We just published our policy report on that today — how we can get some of that full stack of those jobs in California. But that company said to me: "Since the facilities are all now being built in other states, we're on the brink of moving our facility to be closer. Because what's the point of making this stuff in California? It's not going to be used because nothing's happening here." So you have to stop this squeeze on business. You really do.

住房危机：三大结构性病灶

主持人：Let me ask about the broader cost of living for a second. Probably the most impactful cost to people's lived experience is the cost of housing. Double click into that for a second. For the 40 million residents of California, what is going on? Why are rents so high? Why are homes so expensive? And what can actually be done to make the cost of living and rent cheaper?

Hilton：This particular issue I think almost captures better than anything else the underlying structural reasons why everything is so difficult in California and so expensive. You've got these three structural forces that I think underpin the problem and show why a Democrat can't fix it:

  1. Union power
  2. Litigation
  3. Climate dogma

And they all come together in the housing story.

The first part of the story is that we're just not building enough homes for the number of jobs that we're creating and the size of our population. It's a classic supply and demand situation. Within that, there are certain wrinkles. You could point out that because of rent control, which has gotten completely out of control, there are a lot of empty properties in California that could be used to house people, but they're not because landlords don't want to do it, because the rights have swung so far in favor of tenants. But I don't think that's the major driver. The major driver is the fact that we just haven't built enough housing of different kinds.

If you go through the reasons for that and why it's so expensive, it brings into play these three factors. First of all, it just costs more to build anything in California. The same exact floor plan — house, apartment building, industrial building, whatever it is — costs two or three times more to build in California than in neighboring states.

第一重病灶：气候教条下的建筑规范

The first reason is the building codes, the actual requirements for construction, which is way more onerous, driven by climate dogma that actually doesn't really provide much specific.

主持人：What does that mean, "climate dogma"? Because Nevada's hot and drought-ridden and Arizona has issues. So what is it that we say that those states don't say?

Hilton：When you build apartments or when you build parking, you have to put in EV charging. The scale of what's required for the EV charging just makes it more expensive. When you do a parking structure, they have to reinforce the floors. The bays have to be wider. You have fewer bays per structure. There's the specific cost associated with that. Solar panels — we talked about that earlier in terms of low-income apartments that the taxes are paying for. Developers have to pay for that as well. Insulation, energy efficiency — all these things are good. And I think that's pretty much the story of California: things that start with good intentions actually end up being taken to an extreme where it just makes it too expensive to build at a rate that people can afford to buy the properties.

第二与第三重：CEQA 私权诉讼 + 工会勒索

The other two are really CEQA, where anybody can sue —

主持人：Exactly — the private right of action under CEQA.

Hilton：Let's unpack that, because that brings together the three things: climate, litigation and unions. The California Environmental Quality Act itself is a nightmare in terms of the amount of regulation you have to comply with. The private right of action means anyone can sue. 70% of CEQA lawsuits are used to block housing. Most of those lawsuits are filed by unions. They're used as leverage to negotiate what they call project labor agreements, where you have an agreement for the site, and usually they have one or both of these components — both of which sound great:

  • Skilled and trained workforce — which means union only. So it's a closed shop.
  • Prevailing wage — again sounds very good, but it's two or three times market rate wages.

Both of those things inflate the cost. I've spoken to many developers — there aren't enough union workers in the area to actually do the job. So they have to sometimes fly them in from other states to do the job, and the cost of travel and accommodation —

主持人：This is the key. There's no equivalent to CEQA in Texas, where I now reside after 20 years in California. The other thing is the fees. It's $30,000 per door in fees to the government to build a door in California. It's under a thousand in Texas. And Texas has three times the new units per capita than California. So every year we produce three times as many new homes per capita.

一个简单的反诘：CEQA 真的让加州空气更好了吗？

Chamath：Just a simple question though, guys — put this into ChatGPT or whatever. California's mandate with CEQA is to protect the air, protect the water, protect the land. By some measures, Texas doesn't have it. Is it the case that Texas's air is worse, the water is worse, and the land is worse?

Hilton：No, definitely not.

Chamath：So is it roughly the same? Meaning the particulate count, the pollen count — is the air quality the same? Because if it is, then what is CEQA doing other than just slowing down and retarding the progress of housing? Why hasn't that been studied? Because all of this, guys, comes back to: when the data is presented in a way that's factual, there's very little room for people on both sides to argue it, because they're all relatively smart. It's when it's presented either in a partisan way or by somebody who reeks of partisanship that I think people attack the messenger versus the message.

So I'm just trying to understand: why hasn't the California government confronted this? It has the highest rents in America. It has the highest poverty rate in America. And it also has the highest regulation that has the lowest and the slowest unit housing growth. Steve, I guess what I'm asking you is: how does that not get to the legislators more?

系统性腐败：当议员告诉你"工会管这地方"

Hilton：Okay. Well, I'll tell you — I'm afraid the answer is the corruption within the system and the interest groups that have taken over the system.

I'll tell you a story. I know a lot about housing policy because the first area of policy I studied when I decided that I wanted to get into the whole world of policy and politics in California — I actually tried to get a ballot initiative qualified for the ballot that would have two elements:

  1. What Jason just mentioned — capping impact fees, which are now up to about 20% of the cost of housing. I wanted to do a statewide cap of 3% of construction cost.
  2. Eliminating the private right of action under CEQA.

I didn't succeed in getting it on the ballot. Didn't raise enough money in time. So then I tried to pursue it through the legislature. I went to Sacramento, I took meetings with legislators, started to engage with Sacramento. There was one meeting I had with a legislator who was described to me as "good on housing — this is a person you need to talk to." We had a great meeting. They said, "This would be transformational."

I said, "Great, let's work on it together — bipartisan. You're Democrat, I'm a Republican. That'd be great. People like that."

"Oh, I couldn't support you publicly."

"Why not?"

"Well, the unions would hate it."

"Why?"

"Because if you take away the private right of action, you take away the union's leverage."

I said, "Yeah, but you just told me it would be transformational." We were sitting in an office. You could see the state capitol down below, high up. They just waved their arm around like this and said, "Yeah, the unions run this place."

And that's the real reason. If you look at, for example, Newsom touted these two bills last year — AB130, AB131 — that were going to solve the housing crisis. He said, "This is the moment where we are embracing abundance and all the rest of it" — big CEQA exemptions for certain types of housing. But if you look at the fine print tucked away in it, you only get the exemptions if you have these project labor agreements and union closed shop and prevailing wage. So you're just writing back in exactly the things that CEQA is causing the cost increases from.

Let's follow it all the way through. If you look at Gavin Newsom's political donations over the 16 years he's been running statewide — just as a proxy for Democrat politicians — by category:

  1. Government unions
  2. Trial lawyers
  3. Non-government unions

So these are — that's why nothing changes. Because the interests that benefit from this system are funding the politicians that make the decisions.

主持人：And Chamath, to your other question of "Is the environment better since 1970, when this regulation came into pass?" — California still has the worst air quality in the country, largely because of the addiction to cars and traffic. And then Texas, as a comparison, just has industrial waste problems because we have a lot of chemicals here, or chemical processing done here.

Chamath：So we have a car-loving culture in California — to your point, Jason. It's part of our cultural fabric, driving down Highway 1. It's just a very iconic thing that's embedded in this state.

能源政策：为什么加州汽油 7-8 美元一加仑？

Chamath：Steve, I have two questions. What has all of the incremental regulations done with respect to climate quality, whether it's EV mandates or the ICE engine requirements? And then separately, just as a more general way to explain it: why is gas in California $7-8 a gallon, and why is it $3 everywhere else? Why is ours more than 2x what it costs everywhere else, including other states that are also quite expensive to live in?

Hilton：We have the highest gas in the country, including Hawaii in the middle of the Pacific Ocean — even though we have abundant oil reserves here. So we have way higher gas prices than states that don't have oil reserves. We actually have very significant oil reserves in California.

The fundamental reason that gas prices are so high is because, again, in the name of climate — but without actually, in this case, it's counterproductive to climate. Instead of using the production that we have here in California (I've been to the oil fields in Kern County, mainly near Bakersfield), we are now importing nearly 80% of the oil that we use.

Over the period since this all started in 2006 with the passage of the Global Warming Solutions Act — that was the foundational climate legislation in California — over that period, our use of fossil fuels has declined by not that much. The proportion of our energy that's coming from fossil fuels is about 80% still. The rest of the country, it's about 81%. So it's barely any different. But the difference is: we used to produce most of what we use in-state. Now we are importing nearly 80%. And that has driven up — of course, you have to ship it from halfway around the world. Our number one provider is Iraq right now. That's the number one source of oil.

Chamath：Sorry, sorry. The state of California — we are wholly dependent on Iraqi oil to sustain our economy?

Hilton：Not wholly — it's the number one provider. If you look at the sources of oil: number one, Iraq; number two, I think it's Ecuador and Brazil.

重质原油、亚马逊雨林与"碳排放欺骗"

The broader point is: we used to have a really strong energy industry and infrastructure in California where we produced most of the oil and gas that we use, and we had refineries — about 40 of them around the state, mostly in the Bay Area, down near LA — that refined and turned it into products that we use, gasoline and so on.

Now we're down to seven refineries. One of the main reasons for that is that we're not producing what we could be refining. We're shipping it in instead, because there are no oil pipelines into California. Whatever we don't — if we don't use our own, we have to bring it in by tanker.

Because of this, and because the refineries were built to refine California crude, which is known as heavy crude — there are different types around the world, you got to have a good match. Iraqi oil is a good match. The other place whose oil is a good match for our refineries is South America. And so as a result of Democrat climate policy, we are now expanding oil drilling in the Amazon rainforest in order to provide the right kind of oil for California's refineries.

It's just so utterly insane and incoherent. And of course, in the process, we're spewing out carbon emissions because the tankers run on what's called bunker fuel, which is the most polluting form of transportation there is. And just to make the whole insane scheme work — CARB, the California Air Resources Board, which is obsessed with having all other businesses account for their carbon emissions right down the supply chain — miraculously, the carbon emissions for the oil imports are only counted from when they're 12 miles off the coast of California. It's just so crazy.

主持人：Yeah — change the finish line.

Hilton：Yeah.

主持人：The taxes add like a dollar a gallon, and then there's this CARB standard.

Hilton：It's about 60. It's more like 61 or 65, I can't remember exactly. It's just going up again.

主持人：Most of the $2 premium for California is regulatory, not taxes.

Hilton：Yes.

主持人：And most of the oil that's been pulled out of the ground in California — we got the easy stuff out. What's left is generally dirtier or thicker —

Hilton：No, it's not right. I've had lots of conversations with the industry on this, and the problem is that you've got fields that could be producing. Actually, it's a good example of what you can do as governor without the legislature, because the way that they've been shutting down production is not legislatively — it's through an agency of the state government called CalGEM (the California Department of Geologic and Energy Management). It's simply a question of refusing to issue permits for the various stages of production, including maintaining existing wells or expanding.

There's a process called sidetracking, where you can take a well that's doing five barrels a day and increase it to 100. And then drilling new wells in existing fields — they're denying permits for all of that. Actually, you can pretty much turn that around overnight by appointing people who are pro-energy who will issue permits.

Because I think there's a simple common-sense rule here, which is: as long as we're using oil and gas in California, let's use our oil and gas rather than importing it. My conversations with the industry: I said, "Look, what could we do if we had a kind of green light from a governor and a regulatory framework that just says, 'Let's do what we can. Let's produce what we can'?" The estimate that I've got from them is that we can double production every two years in California.

EV 真的改善了空气吗？

主持人：If we're already one of the big gas-burning states with the worst air, or previously — my state, you know — then you're going to get into the circular conversation with the public of "Do we want the air quality to decrease?" And most people would say the EV credits were actually a good thing because we had 20 years of smog going down. Even though we're still worse, it's gotten a lot better.

Hilton：So that smog is not to do with carbon — there's a real misunderstanding. I completely agree on air quality, and one of the major advances that has been made is on smog. Obviously I wasn't here then, but people say, "You know, it's really bad" — and now it's not. You have clean skies and you can see Mount Baldy or whatever. It's like a really different world. But that's nothing to do with carbon emissions.

The main driver of the air quality improvements in California is actually car technology. And if you look at EVs — EV penetration even with all the subsidies and so on, it's incredibly low in California. It's about four or 5%, something like that — tiny. So actually, the dramatic improvements in air quality that you saw in LA were nothing to do with EVs.

教育：每年花 27,000 美元，结果却是全国最差之一

Chamath：Steve, I want to switch topics to education. This is a thing that we on the pod talk about a lot. We're all the byproduct of a pretty fantastic education system, affordable education, frankly, at every level. We had options to pay for it. We all had access to things like AP to really distinguish ourselves —

主持人：Even Jason.

Chamath：Yeah. That's true.

What's happening in the California education system? Why are we stripping away things like AP? And how do we tie compensation to outcomes? Because a lot of us would want to pay teachers triple, but we'd want to tie it to something that says, "Wow, the test scores are going up. Our kids can read. Our kids can write. Our kids can compete on the global stage." And it just feels like we are moving backwards.

Hilton：We really are. And the numbers are horrific. We spend nearly the most of any state per student right now. This year, it's about 27,000 — just over $27,000 per student per year in California. If you take the average of the money — and we get some of the worst results in the country. 47% meet basic standards in English and reading. So less than half meet basic standards. For math, it's 35%. So two-thirds do not meet the standards. It's just an insane level of failure considering we spend nearly the most.

You've got to look at this in a practical way. There's a long-term structural reform that I think we need, because the driver of this is really the grip on the government school monopoly of the teacher unions, who increasingly have been driven by ideological factors. You saw that, for example, in the pandemic when you saw the longest and most destructive school closures in the country.

I was always struck by LA — the teacher union in LA, when they put out their demands for reopening schools, it was just a list of: a wealth tax, Medicare for All, something about Palestine. They've completely become an organized political interest group that's about their members and broader political goals rather than anything to do with the interests of students and kids in school.

So you've got this monopoly of the public school system controlled by the unions. They in turn control the politicians, as I mentioned earlier — the number one donor to Democrat politicians are these government unions, including the teacher unions. And so you've got to break that grip.

长期方案：择校；短期方案：学习密西西比

Long-term, the answer is to move in the direction of school choice, which I've always been a strong advocate of. You're seeing that school choice revolution across the country now. Many states moving very rapidly in the direction with really good results. It's not a panacea, but I think that is the long-term structural change you need. But that takes a long time, and it's going to be very, very hard to get that moving in California given the fact that the teacher unions basically control the legislature through the Democrat politicians they put there.

So there are some practical things that we've got to do immediately to improve these basic standards. Here we've got to look at what works elsewhere. You see a lot of attention now on Mississippi — rightly so, because for one-third of their spend per student than California, their results are spectacularly better. And it's really happened in the last 10 years. There's some simple practical things that they do.

Number one is how you teach kids to read. There's a technique of reading instruction. This was a debate I remember having back in the day in England in the '90s, and it's pretty much settled then, which is: there's a technique called phonics. It's a way to teach kids to read, and it's totally clearly established as the most effective. It's barely used in California schools at all — like a very small proportion of public schools. So that's something that the governor can drive forward through the State Board of Education, where you appoint all the members.

Secondly, in Mississippi, they introduced something very common-sense. As everyone in education says, up to about third grade, you're learning to read, and then from fourth grade, you're reading to learn. And if you can't read, you can't learn. So there's widespread consensus that reading by third grade — by the end of third grade — is incredibly important benchmark. In Mississippi, if you don't pass the basic reading test by end of third grade, they give you a bit of help over the summer, and if you still don't make it, you repeat the year. They don't let you go forward. That single change has transformed their results.

And then your point about accountability — also happens there. This is something else that we could implement here, which is taking the publicly available test scores and data and really assigning it in a very visible way to individual teachers and individual schools. One of the proposals I've got in my campaign is a grade for every school and a grade for every teacher, so we can reward the good ones and remove the bad ones.

犯罪问题：通过法律容易，执行困难

主持人：Two more topics that Californians are very passionate about and have a lot of opinions about. One is pretty challenging, and the other one seems pretty easy and other states have handled it where it's easier. Crime, and then homelessness.

Crime — obviously, as a society, we've seen violent crime go down over the long arc of our lifetimes in the last 40 or 50 years, but California still 30% more violent than the rest of the country. So we definitely have a violence problem specific to California. And if you live in the major cities — San Francisco, Los Angeles — they let people out for petty crimes under $850. There seems to be — and we see going to a drugstore everything's locked up — there is a feeling, and a lot of debates over the numbers, that there's a lot more property crime. Some people claim people don't report it anymore. That was my lived experience in California. What is your take on crime, and then we'll go to homelessness?

Hilton：It's just this classic thing in California where they seem to be brilliant at passing laws, right? Every year more and more laws, more and more bloat and bureaucracy, more nanny-state nonsense. Last session, for example, they passed 1,118 bills. One session. We did a thing outside the state capitol — I'm not very tall — we printed them all out. It's like double my height. Just ridiculous.

The point I'm making is: really good at passing laws, but not very good at enforcing them. There's just something missing in terms of the willingness to just enforce the law. That's going to be one of the main points I make in terms of homelessness.

But when you get to crime, there's just this attitude — there's something off about how the left has seen this issue. Just when you think the worst excesses of "defund the police" and all that have receded, you've now got them popping up. What is it — this new thing? Microlooting, right? Oh, microlooting — isn't the New York Times and Slate podcast going on about: "Oh, it's fine because it's just social justice, and we're allowed to basically steal things because it's okay." It's just unbelievable subversion of basic values and morality.

监狱关停 = 抓了又放

On crime, it's very decentralized. There are some state things that need to be done. Remember the law that you're talking about that legalized theft up to $950 a day — that part has been overturned. That was Prop 47, which was a few years ago, has been overturned by Prop 36, which was overwhelmingly passed in 2024 by about 70%. But of course, it's not being properly implemented. Gavin Newsom was against it, and so were most Democrats in the state. The people passed it anyway, but now there's real resistance to enforcing it, which is ridiculous.

In terms of the overall picture, it is very localized. You've got local police forces and sheriff's department and so on. So my focus has been: well, what can you do as governor? One of the biggest drivers I think that's caused the problem is — really started with Jerry Brown before Gavin Newsom; Gavin Newsom's accelerated it — which is the prison closure program.

They've basically — also this is classic California — they've reduced the number of prison places by half. Guess what happened to the budget? It doubled.

主持人：Not quite that bad, but like —

Hilton：It's a classic. They cut the numbers in half, doubled the budget. But the serious point is that you've had tens of thousands of really dangerous violent criminals either released directly into the community, or — more destructively for the system — transferred to county jails, which are now completely overcrowded.

And therefore, at the local level, the whole system is aware that you've had all these transfers from state prison. The system is full, and so there's no capacity, and that really undermines the kind of accountability that judges and prosecutors would want to seek at the local level, because they know the jails are full. And so that in turn undermines law enforcement, because they say, "What's the point?"

I hear this term all the time from law enforcement around the state — I'm traveling the state the whole time — they talk about "catch and release" as the basic operating rule for local law enforcement. For the kinds of crimes you're talking about, you catch them, they just release. Nothing happens. And so that undermines law enforcement. They think, "Why bother if we're just going to bring these people in and nothing's going to happen to them?" And that in turn undermines public confidence, because everyone sees that, and then they, as you just said, don't bother reporting it.

So a simple thing we can do that is completely within the governor's control is stop and reverse the prison closure program, which is what I've committed to doing — to increase prison capacity in California. That means that you can relieve the pressure on county jails, but also that means that you can use the prisons for what they should be doing — not just bringing accountability (you commit a crime, you should be punished), but also rehabilitation.

We've got one of the worst recidivism rates in the whole country. One of the best states is Virginia — they're less than half what we have. That would massively reduce crime if you could just get — you've got to take seriously the rehabilitation part. A huge proportion of prisoners in these jails — they can't read properly. Many have dyslexia. You've got to have a really serious view on it. And they just don't. They have an ideological view. I think that is the problem with so many of these issues. It's ideology. In this case, it's decarceration. Can't have people in prison. Prison is racist. Criminal justice reform. All this ideology instead of just practical things to keep people safe.

主持人：Newsom shut down four or five of the California state prisons. You're absolutely correct — according to my notes. And then it peaked in 2006: California had 165,000 people in state prisons. Now 93,000 people. So it is definitely a trend. And I think a lot of folks who are living here, or who were living in California where I used to live, are not in favor of that.

无家可归问题：执法 + 戒毒 + 心理健康

主持人：Looking at homelessness, is it intractable in California?

Hilton：One thing I'll just point out — if people are interested in digging in further to some of the things I've been saying, there's a couple of places you can go for real depth on this. The last three years, I've been traveling the state and learning about this stuff and developing solutions. I had a policy organization for that called Golden Together — goldentogether.com. You can find policy reports on many of these areas we've discussed and more, including one on homelessness called "Ending Homelessness."

Actually, my real partner in developing that was someone called Michele Steeb, who's done a lot of work on this. She actually ran homeless shelters and really at the street level of this for many, many years. Also someone called Tom Wolf, who's given me a lot of great advice. He's in San Francisco — a recovered addict, who's just fantastic.

主持人：He's very vocal on Twitter and very common-sense approach.

Hilton：Exactly. Okay, so very simply, I'll try and capture it simply. It's three points.

第一：执行已有的法律

Number one, it actually already is illegal to live on the streets, and the homeless encampments already are illegal. They've always been illegal. It's another example of where we just got to enforce the law. For years, local politicians in California hid behind a court ruling called the Boise ruling from many years ago. The Ninth Circuit ruling applies to the western states, which stated: you can't remove people from the street unless you have sufficient shelter available locally. They used this to say, "We can't remove people because we don't have enough shelter."

It didn't define what shelter was. They defined it as these permanent supportive housing units costing $900,000 a door — but it could have been a camp with cots. There's no reason. But even that excuse has been lifted, because there's a Supreme Court case called Grants Pass versus Oregon in 2024 that overturned that. So there's no excuse.

These people running local governments — they have the power and the legal authority to remove every single homeless encampment, and they should. My argument is: I'll give them, once I'm elected, a certain amount of time, and if they haven't done it, then I'll use state law enforcement resources to take people off the streets.

第二：强制戒毒

Then you get to point two and three of the plan, which is: what do you — you got to give people help in a compassionate way, help them get their lives back on track. Over 80% of people who are homeless have drug or alcohol problems, severe addiction, or severe mental health problems. So you got to deal with that.

The second part is drug and alcohol recovery. You got to get people into recovery. That used to be the rule in California: rehab or jail. And we got to get back to that. It can't be an option. You got plenty of service providers who can do it. You got to require it.

Last year, even — going back to our point about the legislature — even the Democrat legislature passed a bill called the Sober Housing Act, which would have taken a certain proportion of homeless spending and allocated it to shelter where you had a requirement of sobriety. Newsom vetoed that bill. It's unbelievable. So we got to have 100% sober requirement for any kind of state services on homelessness.

第三：心理健康设施 + IMD 豁免

The third part is mental health, where, honestly, going back to the jails conversation, you talk to sheriffs around the state — the number varies, but they say 50% (I've heard as high as 70%) of the people in their jails have severe mental health problems. The fact that we are actually treating people with mental health problems either on the street or in jail is totally barbaric.

One of the reasons is that — when you're talking about the homeless population, obviously low-income people, so it's very much entwined when you talk about mental health care with Medicaid, with the federal system. There's a rule in Medicaid that was set up right at the beginning when it was founded in the mid-60s called the IMD rule — Institutions of Mental Disease. This was a time when they didn't want large mental asylums. The idea was you have small facilities in the community.

So the rule is: there is no Medicaid reimbursement to the states for any mental health care provided in a facility with more than 16 beds. It's a 16-bed rule. Of course, that makes the whole thing incredibly uneconomic and inefficient. Imagine if hospitals could only be 16 beds — how inefficient that would be.

The first Trump administration created a waiver, the IMD waiver, that states could apply for, so you could get around the rule. A lot of other states have taken that up. California hasn't.

There's plenty of money in the system. Like we've been saying, the budgets are there. They've just been diverted into the wrong places. So the third part of the plan is to take the money that's currently going into the homeless industrial complex — these ridiculous apartment units for people who should be either getting mental health care or recovery treatment — take that money and put it into modern, large-scale mental health facilities. And then we can treat people.

主持人：That's broad work. That's a great place for you to put a big magnifying glass, because that's where there's massive amounts of corruption. People cannot believe how much we spend in California on homeless. And if you pay for something, you will get more of it, and they're getting a lot more of it.

胜选路径：数学、选民身份证与多种族劳工阶级联盟

Chamath：Steve, as we wrap up, give us the quarterback view of your path to victory. Walk us through the sequence of events, the key moments leading up to the primary vote, and then from primary to election day. What has to happen for you to get to Sacramento?

Hilton：We have the top two system. For those who — another crazy California thing where you end up with two candidates going through to the general election regardless of party. The idea of this was to have more moderate politics. Ever since it was introduced, the state's gone further and further to the left.

You've got various scenarios that are possible. Right now, I'm leading in all of the polls. On the Republican side, there's one other candidate. With President Trump's endorsement of my campaign, I think we can expect — I'm pretty confident that we can make it into the top two. It's not certain. We've got to fight very hard over the next month or so. The ballots go out next week, early May. But I think that we're going to have a top two with myself and one other Democrat. And right now it looks as if it's going to be one of Tom Steyer, Katie Porter, or Xavier Becerra. And all of those three represent either no change from what we have now, or a move even further to the left in the wrong direction.

So I think broadly the argument is going to be very straightforward: are you happy with the way things are going in California? Do you want more of it? If you do, you vote Democrat. Or do you think we need a change? It's a classic change vs. more-of-the-same election.

数学：选票其实是够的

Getting into the numbers — I know a lot of people look at California and say it's impossible for a Republican to win. Jason was pointing out we've had Republicans in the past, but that was a long time ago. You could say special circumstances, because Arnold Schwarzenegger was elected in a recall election and so on.

主持人：And he was a celebrity who was highly loved in Los Angeles, half the state.

Hilton：Exactly. All of those things are true. I've always said from the beginning of this that it's not going to be easy to win — going to be very difficult because of the structural factors in California. But it's not impossible. And given the seriousness of our predicament and how much I think the whole country depends on a successful, growing, thriving, leading California, then we should go for it. Getting things back in a common-sense direction is just a really important thing. I always say California means to America what America means to the world. And so this matters.

If you look at the numbers, some people look at the voter registration numbers and they say Democrats outnumber Republicans 2-to-1, and that is true. But when you look at actual voting, the gap is a little bit closer. Over the last 20 years where you haven't had Republicans elected, pretty much the average Republican vote has been just over 40%. So it's been like a 60-40 split. Obviously that's not close, but the gap is perhaps not as wide as some people might think.

But then you look at a couple of factors that I really think are different this year.

First of all, there's a dissatisfaction with the way things are going that wasn't there before. If you look at that basic number — is the state on the right track or wrong track? — even four years ago in the last governor's race, the wrong-track number was kind of mid- to high-40s. Now it's mid- to high-50s. So there's a majority for change in California, just put it that way, which is a good environment to be going into as a candidate representing change.

Second point: if you look at the actual votes you're going to need to win — this is a midterm election, 2026. If you try and get some kind of sense of how many votes will be cast in the midterm election this year, take the average of the last two, 2018 and 2022, you get a total of 11.7 million total votes as an estimate. So to win, you're going to need just over half of that — call it 5.9 million.

Now, when people say there aren't enough Republicans in California to win — in 2024, in the presidential race, President Trump in California, without even campaigning here or spending money on ads or anything, wasn't a targeted state, got 6.1 million votes. In other words, there's more than enough people who just voted Republican for President Trump. Now, of course, you're not going to get 100% of a presidential year turnout in a midterm election, but the reason I make that point is that the votes are there actually, even with just Republicans.

I don't think we're going to get there just with Republican votes, but that's the starting point — a strong campaign to turn out Republican votes. A big driver for that this year, that again is a unique feature this year, is the fact that in November we're going to have voter ID on the ballot. That just qualified for the ballot. Republicans particularly are enthusiastic about voter ID. It's going to help us get a big turnout.

多种族劳工阶级联盟："Californable"

And then in terms of the coalition for victory, I think you've got a real opportunity to put together the kind of multi-racial working-class coalition that President Trump put together. Because, going right back to where we started, it's working-class people who are really, really struggling and being hammered the most by these policies. They get to vote directly for no taxes —

主持人：No state income taxes.

Hilton：Exactly. Because that's my tax plan. I just put this out there just the other day, which is no — and no tax on tips. That's the other part, which has been implemented at the federal level, but California won't do it at the state level.

My whole plan is geared toward $3 gas. I call it "Californable." $3 gas. Cut your electric bills in half. Your first 100 grand tax-free. A home you can afford to buy. Really simple, practical, common-sense things that particularly help the people who've been hurt the most over the last few years. And I think that's how we pull this off.

收尾

主持人：Steve, on behalf of all, I just want to say thank you for being so incredibly candid and open with us. We're wishing you the best of luck. Thank you for joining us. And just from my seat, if you want —

主持人：Moving back?

主持人：No, I mean, if you want it just — I left for a reason, and part of it was the dysfunction of the state. And if you want things to continue, I think having an unbalanced government that's all in one party is a way to do that. You got to try to find some balance here, and I think — why not give it a shot? If you're in California, you have nothing to lose. The state is in a massively dysfunctional situation. So I wish you great luck, Steve Hilton.

Hilton：Thank you, guys. Great to be with you.

主持人：All right. Cheers. Now, I'm going all in.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的发现：加州的"环保叙事"正在亚马逊雨林砍伐森林
整段访谈中最令人脊背发凉的一句话不是税收数据，而是 Hilton 顺口提到的一个细节：**加州为了"climate dogma"限制本土钻油，结果重炼油厂被迫匹配"重质原油"的进口源——伊拉克和南美。为了喂饱加州的炼油厂，亚马逊雨林正在被扩张性钻探。**
这是典型的"道德外包"（moral outsourcing）现象——和时尚业把污染外包给孟加拉、科技业把锂矿外包给刚果是同一个结构性病灶。加州人享受着"我们很环保"的心理舒适，是因为污染和碳排放被推到了12海里之外（加州空气资源委员会真的只从海岸12英里外开始计算碳足迹）。这是会计学上的"碳殖民主义"。
更深的讽刺：**油轮烧的是bunker fuel，是地球上最脏的燃料形式之一。**所以加州的"climate policy"实际净效果可能是负的——它没有减排，只是把排放转移到了无法投票反对它的人头上。
## 二、跨领域连接：加州正在重演1970年代英国"被工会绞杀"的剧本——但这次没有撒切尔的解药
Hilton 的个人轨迹本身就是一个隐藏的预测模型。他亲历

item.title: [Mercatus Center] Violent Saviors: A Conversation With Bill Easterly
item.dc:creator: Mercatus Center
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、被忽视的真正"发展悖论"：暴力本身就是反证

最让我震撼的，是 Easterly 转述 Smith 的那个逻辑刀锋——"如果你带给他们的真是好东西，为什么需要用暴力强迫他们接受？" 这是一个被现代发展经济学几乎完全遗忘的"显示偏好"悖论。

经济学家擅长用 revealed preference 来分析消费者，却从不把它应用到被殖民者、被强制迁移的农民、被奴役的劳工身上。一旦我们把 Samuelson 的方法论严肃地外推到政治经济学，殖民者声称的"benevolence"就立刻在自己的逻辑下崩塌。经济学最锋利的武器其实早就握在手里，只是从不对准自己的客户。这是一种学科性的方法论失明。

二、跨领域连接：发展经济学是一种"医疗化的暴力"

听这段对话时，我突然想到一个意外的平行结构——现代发展经济学和 19 世纪精神病学惊人地同构。

在 Foucault 笔下的疯人院里，专家以"为你好"的名义剥夺患者的同意权，把强制约束重新命名为"治疗"；今天的发展话语里，专家以 GDP per capita 之名剥夺被援助国的同意权，把强制再分配重新命名为"援助"。两者的核心修辞都是："你不知道什么对自己好，所以你的拒绝不算数。"

更深一层的连接：Easterly 提到现代发展圈对"benevolent autocrat"的追捧——Kagame、Meles、习近平时代的某种叙事——这其实和精神病学史上对"道德疗法"院长的崇拜是同一种心理结构：人类似乎天然渴望相信存在一个"既强大又仁慈"的家长。这种渴望既是殖民主义的心理燃料，也是 ESG、科技乌托邦、家长式自由主义（libertarian paternalism）共同的暗流。

三、一个让人不安的观察：自由主义的失败可能是结构性的，而非偶然的

Easterly 几乎是带着自嘲承认：Bauer、Friedman、Sen 三代巨人都没能让发展圈接受"自由本身是目的"。他归因于左右两派都更想"换一批专家"而非"取消专家"。

但我想抛出一个更尖锐的解释——自由主义在发展辩论中的结构性弱势，源于它无法提供"叙事性英雄"。社会主义提供革命者，殖民主义提供文明使者，民族主义提供救国者；而古典自由主义提供的是……一个"我也不知道该怎么办，但请不要打人"的谦卑姿态。

这正是 Easterly 在最后那段几乎是无意中说出的最深刻洞见：他说自由主义者也许只能扮演"明知必败仍要战斗的电影英雄"。这个比喻看似浪漫，实则揭示了一个残酷事实：在所有政治经济学派中，自由主义是唯一一个其美学上的吸引力低于其智识上的正确性的学派。它在真理市场上滞销，不是因为错，而是因为不够好看。

这也解释了一个跨领域的现象：为什么硅谷的"effective altruism"、ESG 投资、全球卫生干预，所有这些当代版本的"violent saviors"都比 Hayek 式的克制更有传播力——因为人类购买的从来不是政策，而是故事。自由主义需要的不是更好的论证，而是更好的剧作家。

📝 转录文本

节目开场

You're listening to the Hayek program podcast. This podcast includes audio from lectures, interviews, and discussions from scholars and visitors of the FA Hayek program for advanced study in philosophy, politics, and economics at the Mercatus Center at George Mason University. To learn more about the Hayek program, visit hayek.mercatus.org. To learn about graduate student fellowship opportunities with the Mercatus Center at George Mason University for students at Mason as well as at universities across the globe, please visit students.mercatus.org.

Today is Monday, February 2nd, 2026. I'm here with Professor Bill Easterly of New York University.

— Bill, thanks for joining me. — Pleasure to be here.

Yeah, this is the last month of the 2025 season. We had the great pleasure of having Bill come and give a talk on his new book, Violent Saviors, which was a great way for our PPE workshop to end the year. And today I have a great opportunity to delve a little deeper into the book with Bill.

写作缘起：对"仁慈独裁者"叙事的不满

— So Bill, why don't we just start by telling us how it is that you came to write Violent Saviors?

Yeah, I think it came out of a lot of frustration that has been building for a long time watching the development world. I was really struck by how any sort of regime that led to rapid economic growth as measured by GDP per capita growth was presumed to be benevolent. You know, it could be China, could be Rwanda, could be Ethiopia. And those rulers were called benevolent autocrats.

It's like all the weight in measuring well-being is put on GDP per capita and nothing on whether people are oppressed, whether they're denied freedom. So there's sort of like zero weight on freedom and 100% weight on GDP per capita, and that didn't seem quite right to me. There's been a lot of debate on this and I think the other side's been winning that debate. So I thought, you know, maybe I could look for help to some guys who had written about this before, beginning with a person named Adam Smith two centuries ago — 250 years ago.

And it struck me that the classical liberals very much analyzed this problem of when there is material increases in well-being but a denial of freedom — how do we think about that?

— Yeah.

And they specifically commented on another kind of benevolent autocrat, which were the colonial autocrats, who often promised that they were bringing material benefits to the people that they were colonizing. The classical liberals responded to that, and that's what really inspired this whole book. The colonizers were saying, "We're going to conquer you for your own good." That's kind of the essence of what I call the development right of conquest. As long as we bring material development, we have a right to conquer you, and it's actually for your own good. So we conquer you for your own good.

Smith 与 Condorcet 之争

— So in the beginning of the book, you contrast Condorcet and Smith in this. I think you might have actually been even able to use Mill, though it's of a later period, but Mill argued that liberty was maybe not fit for everyone if you were a backward culture or something like that. But nevertheless, the Smith–Condorcet discussion — could you give some examples of what Condorcet was on one side and Smith on the other side?

Yeah. So Condorcet is very much of the modern development mindset. He actually envisioned kind of a graph where there'd be a strong upward trend. He didn't know the concept of GDP per capita, but he definitely had some idea of material well-being brought by the conquerors. And so he is very much in the "we conquer you for your own good" school. And even more dramatic, he analyzed different groups that were being conquered and thought some of them would actually disappear as a result of the conquest — and yet somehow it was still for their own good.

Adam Smith — what's interesting about Adam Smith is he does not choose to argue with Condorcet or to take another debate. He doesn't argue with slave owners when they claim that they bring material benefits to their slaves. We'll come back to that later. And so the debate between Smith and Condorcet is not about the empirical scientific proposition of which is better for raising GDP per capita, you know, colonialism or leaving them alone. That's not what the debate is about.

It's interesting that Smith actually thought that the American colonies were like the ideal situation for rapid material growth — lots of land available, relative freedom for the colonial subjects of European origin. And yet, despite that, he described — here's a quote that I like from Smith — he described what he called "the savage injustice of the Europeans" and said that this had been "ruinous and destructive to several of these unfortunate colonies."

And since he already said he thought material GDP was increasing rapidly, what he meant by "ruinous and destructive" was about their freedom, about the justice of what had happened to them. And so Smith saw this very much as what we would today call a normative debate, or maybe to put it in easier language, just a moral debate between whether the conquest was just. Condorcet said justice follows material GDP — as long as the conquerors bring more material GDP, it was just. And Smith said, "Oh no no no, not quite. If you bring material GDP but you force your increases in well-being on the people you're conquering, then they haven't actually consented to this supposed increase in their well-being." And that for Smith is determinative — if you don't consent, then you're not better off, and it's unjust if it's forced on you.

道德愤怒为何反复倒向"另一种规划者"

— One of the things that I found very appealing in your book, as you might suspect, is that a lot of people who are critics of let's call it this emphasis on GDP and development that's led through the market or whatever — so a philosopher like Thomas Pogge, who complains about the obsession of economists with growth and therefore missing things, or the activist criticisms of the Washington Consensus and whatnot — is that they in some sense are just… their moral outrage turns into a demand for a planner on the other side.

— Yeah.

— Right? As opposed to this point that you're making at all. And I was wondering, you've been dealing with this for a long time, the tyranny of experts — it's a quite fascinating aspect about how you thread the needle between both being a critic of expertise, a critic of this obsession with growth, but not falling onto the other side of wanting to have expert reformers, but from the other moral side of things. How did you negotiate that in your own mind?

That's the other big debate between Condorcet and Smith — it's the role of experts. Condorcet is saying, you know, that's another way to assure well-being is just put the experts in charge — should say, put us experts in charge. It was part of the French physiocrats who very much saw themselves as experts bringing economic development to places that they were conquering or places they were advising at home.

And Smith was saying, "No, no, no." First of all, he didn't think material development even happened as a result of experts. But again, that's not the core debate. The core debate was that the experts are forcing their regime on those that they are supposedly benefiting. Smith disputes that. He says, "No, you're not making them better off. You're forcing them to take it."

And we can see here the origins of really kind of liberal skepticism, which I think is the key. Smith is saying, if you experts are advising those with unlimited power to impose your expert views, that's not a great situation. He's skeptical that you can trust those in power and their expert advisers to actually be benevolent — to act in someone else's interest rather than what Smith thought more obvious: they would act in their own interest, not someone else's interest.

So he saw the colonizing of India, of North and South America, as the colonizers engaging in just plunder, despite the expert veneer — the veneer of expert advice that all this was happening with.

So I think there's kind of almost a convergence between the right and the left on wanting the experts to be in charge. They just want different experts to be in charge. Where there's like a third alternative that Smith is presenting, that you don't have the experts in charge. You don't have unlimited power for the experts.

Smith 的核心怀疑："如果真好，何须强迫？"

— Yeah. In your discussion of these ideas, there's a couple things that really stand out. One of them is what you said already, which is that the determining factor in Smith's judgment was not whether or not material progress happened or not. To most economists, the material progress is the point. And to Smith, even if you could have material progress, but it came at the expense of the freedom and dignity of the individuals, then it wasn't to be deemed as better.

So I think this is another kind of important liberal skepticism that was brought by Smith and his successors like John Stuart Mill and Hutchison. Smith's skepticism was: if what you're doing to these people is really so good for them, why do you have to force them to take it?

— Yeah. Why is force necessary to get them to take your benevolence?

— Yeah. That again goes to his enshrining of consent as a determinative principle: if they don't consent, and you have to force them through extreme violence of conquest and slavery to take it, why do you think that violence is necessary if it's so good for them?

This also was the way in which Smith and later Mill entered the slavery debate. Slave owners claimed to bring material benefits to the slaves, and Smith said, "Oh yeah, that could have been possible compared to the people in Africa that were not enslaved, or compared to the British working class." It's possible that there could have been material increases in income compared to those control groups. But again, for Smith, that's not the determinative question. The obvious use of force is again — why, if you're so good for your slaves, why do you have to enslave them? Why do you have to force them to take the benevolence?

And for that matter, if slavery is so great, why don't you want it for yourself? Your slave owners show by your own choices in slavery is not good.

国家能力理论的兴起与古典自由主义的怀疑

— Yeah. The second part that I was going to raise there is that in this discussion you also talk about — you mentioned it earlier — that Smith doesn't even see the emergence of the institutions of natural liberty as arising from experts. They arise as, as he says, "not due to any human wisdom" or overarching design.
