二、commencement address：射出那一箭

Absolutely incredible story. Maybe my favorite version of this answer that I've ever had. First time I've ever done at the beginning of the episode, which makes it fun, too. What was your message in the commencement speech that you ultimately gave? What did you decide was the message to leave them with?

So I got up there and I looked on the dais and I said, "How many of you — there's like 20 people over 50 or 60 — how many of you remember your college commencement address?" None of them. Not one of them could remember.

I love to hunt and fish. So I go through and I talk about just the normal challenges they're going to face in life. And I also talked about how going through this process, I wanted to make sure I was going to say something that was memorable. So right there at the end, I said, "So whatever you do," — and I pulled out my bow and I knocked an arrow — "so whatever you do, aim high and shoot straight." When I went like this, the whole crowd goes "Woo! Aim high and shoot straight." And I had an apple on a table over here and smashed it.

Knocked it.

Yeah. So I don't know whether they still remember or not, but I know when everybody was ducking, I thought, "Oh, they're going to remember that." I told the president of the university, I said, "Listen, you don't have to tackle me or anything like that, okay? This is part of it. I'm just trying to let these people have a night that they'll remember for some time."

三、交易员的诞生：Bunker Hunt 与白银的崩塌

And it's an excuse for me to ask my other question about this difference between investing and trading. So I'd love you to explain the life of a trader in contrast to that of an investor.

If I just think about when I started, I started in 1976. Inflation was raging. I started on the floor of the commodity pits. Stuff was literally going up and doubling and cutting in half every year. The volatility was insane.

To give you an example of how crazy things got — Bunker Hunt was squeezing silver at the time and he bought about 200 million ounces at an average price of about $3.12. And between 1976 and 1980, we had crazy monetary policy. Inflation started ripping and silver went literally through the roof. Ultimately, I started out in the floor of the COT exchange. I bought a membership on COMEX, which was the metals exchange at that time. I got in the pit and was executing some orders for them sometimes along the way.

And the bottom line was, it was just the most amazing bull run. I want to say by like '79, silver was around $30 an ounce. And all of a sudden he was worth about five or six billion, which made him worth three times the next closest person in the world. Bunker says, "Well, I think this silver here is the most valuable asset and resource on earth." Someone asked him, "What are you going to do with all that silver?" And he goes, "I'm going to bury it. I'm going to bury it. In fact, what I'm going to do is I'm going to buy 20 million more ounces and I'm going to bury that too." So he buys 20 million ounces at $35. And when that happened, it just roofed. Well, it goes to $50. So at $50 now, he's worth about $11 billion and he's got a multiple of five or six on the next closest guy. I just couldn't even believe what I'd seen and how much money this guy had made.

Anyway, so that was when COMEX decided — the commercials had been just eradicated because they're sitting there holding physicals and having to put up this margin every day and their bankers going, "Yeah, I can't do it." So anyway, they made it liquidation only. Silver collapsed. It went from $50 to under $10 in the space of about eight weeks. And that had a searing impact on me — to see him go from the richest guy to virtually bankrupt in the short space of six or seven weeks. Right then and there, I would never own anything for the [long run]... or trust anything for the rest of my life.

祖父的格言与流动性 DNA

My grandfather, when I was really young, said, "Son, you're only worth what you can write a check for tomorrow." That also had a really deep impression on me. So liquidity has always been something — it was in my DNA as a child, because that was my grandfather's favorite saying all the time. And then also just my early trading days — my early trading days, I had a $10,000 account. I'd run that thing to a hundred, run it back down to zero. And also living in my early 20s, I had this friend, he's such a character, he could take $2,500 or $5 grand and run it to $2 million. Of course, the market moves were crazy like no one I'd ever seen. We were brokers at that time at EF Hutton. And we called him a mortician cuz he'd get an account with $10,000, churn about $100,000 commissions, take it to a million bucks, and then have it in deficit.

So you learned that liquidity was really important because the volatility was so huge and we're all living on the edge. The idea of owning something for the long run was laughable, because look how much money you could make by trading in the short run, because the volatility profile was off the chart.

50 年讲台上的 #1 bullet：ride a trend for the longest time

One of my mentors who got me into trading was teaching a class on investments in Virginia and asked me to come be a guest lecturer. I started in '82 and I've been doing it every semester since. I remember one of my favorite stories to tell that class — I would go through how all the greatest fortunes in the world were made. I'd always ask them, "Who's the richest guy in the world?" And back then it was Bill Gates and Warren Buffett. How did they get there? And my point to them would be: they got there by riding a trend for the long run. And that would be my big lesson to them. I'd always have two or three bullet points. My number one bullet point is: you're going to make your money by riding a trend for the very, very longest time.

I said, "There are many different ways to achieve it. You can own a company like Bill Gates or Steve Jobs, whatever. Or you can be like Warren Buffett. You can be one of those value investors who won't spend a nickel." And I used to just sit there and rail on Warren Buffett year after year after year. And I would self-congratulate myself for trashing him, going, "He just happened to be in the right place at the right time and caught this bull market. If that guy had been in Japan, forget it. Never would have happened. If it started 1989 in the Nikkei, forget it. Never would have happened. Different story. Right place, right time. Bull market genius."

Alpha vs. Beta：BBI 基金 40 年的 -0.12 相关

Oh yeah, I guess this will be my 50th year. And the difference between my trading and investing — I just take our fund, BVI fund, for 40 years it has a minus .12 correlation with the S&P 500. So you can see the difference between investing and trading. So 100% of our returns are alpha — 100%.

And I was just thinking, "Oh my god, why couldn't I be Warren Buffett? Just believe in America." And just went, "When you're down 50%, who cares? Cuz America's going to bring you through." And not that he doesn't — he works as hard as any human being — but it's that belief system that's so fantastic. As opposed to: I feel like I'm a right guard, and I've been a right guard in the NFL for 50 years where I'm just every day fighting in the freaking trenches every day. And every time someone says to me, "Gee, I want to go into trading," I said, "Go long-short, go on the equity side, go do [that]." I've always envied that belief system. It's such a great belief system and it's worked so well for so long. But I would have huge difficulty to have been in Warren's shoes in 2008-9, have a 50% drawdown. It would have had a really material impact on me. I don't think I have his calm and patience and fortitude to be able to do what he does.

9 岁的 Buffett 与"What I Realize Now"

And so anyway, I was listening to the Acquired podcast on Berkshire Hathaway and for the first time I learned that at 9 years old — 9 years old — he understood the power of compound interest. And actually when I listened to that podcast, I just went, "Oh my god, this guy is a genius and I have been the biggest fool." I've always wanted to write a book called What I Realize Now, which will go through the successive mistakes of beliefs that I've had my lifetime. What I realize now is what an idiot I was. That guy is a flipping genius cuz he understood the power of compound interest, which I somehow managed brilliantly to avoid my entire career. He understood it at nine. He went out at 17, went to Columbia, and sought out Benjamin Graham. What a genius.

Now the great thing from that podcast is that he was also so smart that he partnered with Charlie Munger. Charlie was clearly a tremendous genius in his own right because whereas Warren would buy 50-cent dollars, right, Charlie understood the power of compounding for companies that were growing all the time. And I think the two of them together — man, what a combination. But anyway, if Warren, if you happen to hear this, I'm deeply apologetic. You are the OG of compound interest and I wish I was one-tenth as smart as you are.

四、AI 与风险管理：尾部事件的 Build-Break-Iterate

Didn't you talk to him about what he thought about AI soon thereafter when we first talked about — what was that conversation like?

You cannot be a trader, investor — whatever term we use — and not be a really good risk manager. Anyone that's really succeeded investing or trading is first and foremost a great risk manager.

I went to this conference about 18 months ago and I just was so alarmed by what I heard, and then I mentioned on CNBC — and he watches CNBC every day — and sent me a note and said, "I agree with you 100%, but the genie's out of the bottle. I don't know if we can get it back in." I think he's completely on board with the belief that we have some real threats from AI.

The problem with AI is — if you think about the way AI is being deployed, practiced and deployed right now is the build-break-iterate model. Build it, break it, fix it, and iterate. If you think about it, that's been the invention model since the beginning of man. Build, break, iterate. But we've never been in a situation where the tail event — the break — can do so much damage. It could be hundreds of millions if not billions of lives.

What's so scary — when I was at this conference, which is only about 35 or 40 people with one modeler from the four biggest model companies — and when I was able to ask them pointedly, "How do you think that AI safety gets resolved?" the pretty much consensus answer is, "I think we'll finally do something about it when 50 or 100 million people die in an accident." I mean it's crazy.

My big problems with AI are: one, there's no plebiscite on this. There's no vote. There's no way for the public to say yay or nay on the pace at which we're going. And that's the way that most innovation occurs. Again, this one has a tail event on it that's so large.

I go back to when the nuclear bomb was dropped — 18 months later, our Congress and our administration was smart enough and forward-thinking to create the Atomic Energy Commission to begin to try to regulate something that had huge tails. Here we are, we're three years in and "regulate" — what are you talking about?

If there ever was a leadership position that needs to be taken by any president, it's on AI regulation right now — not just the United States, but convening with China, with all the other purveyors, to make sure that we don't do something that has catastrophic consequences. And that's just on the safety side, not even what's going to happen to social disruption. Matt Schumer came out with a very lengthy essay on how the two new models that were released six days ago are going to be so unbelievably disruptive to the workforce.

So the news in my mind keeps getting more alarming. If this was anything to do inside Tudor — this thing would have been so contained so long ago. I mean, that's what a good risk manager does. And yet there's zero risk management here.

水印、深度伪造与脑机接口

Your point about great investors and traders are great risk managers — in the face of AI, how do you think about this exogenous variable lurking?

The one thing that I think should be part of this next election — the simplest, most important thing that we can do — is we can demand that all AI is watermarked. That's the single most transformative thing that we could possibly do for the country, for the world. Make it a felony if someone knowingly violates that three times and put them in jail.

I want to know what's authentically human and what's not. And when that happens — talk about restoring trust in the country, which I think is one of the biggest problems. I would say two times since this year began, I've had very serious people call and say, "Did you see XYZ?" And of course, it turned out to be a deep fake. I think just so we can get back to some truth and honor and normal discourse, I think we've got to do that.

And the reason why it's so important is, if I go back to that conference 18 months ago, I would say a significant portion of the scientists there envision a future where humanity is going to have a chip in our brain that's going to allow us to access so much knowledge, power, etc. So I'm just kind of looking down the road and thinking — okay, we really need to know what we're reading, what we're looking at. Because again, that group, without having any feedback from the rest of America, thinks that a blended human with a machine is perfectly acceptable, is the future, should have inalienable rights. For me, I don't think that's the case. I'd vote no. I think most humans would vote no.

五、Eli Tullis 与"棉花地里的微笑"

So coming back to some of these themes of investor versus trader, risk management, etc., I know you learned a lot from this guy, Eli Tullis. And I love these lessons that were formative for you, for what became the trading legend that you now are. What did he teach you? And was there any specific episode from your time with him that taught you the biggest or most important lesson?

God, he was so strong. He was really good at executing at the maximum apogee of fear as well as greed. He was so good at smelling that, because he traded hardly anything but cotton. He would just sit there and focus, and he could just — he was just so good at waiting for exactly when there was just too much elation or when there was too much fear.

And then I would say the number one thing I learned from him was — oh, we were so long cotton one weekend, and there was a great drought, spectacular drought. And of course it rained all through the belt over the weekend. Walk in — market was limit down. He'd gotten absolutely smashed. I thought, "Oh my god, it's over."

That day for lunch, his wife brought in four of her friends. He had the most beautiful office I've ever seen in my life. And he came out and just — oh my god, he had a smile on his face. "Oh, ladies!" — and flirting with the wives. I mean, I was sitting there going, "Are you kidding me? This guy's just broke and he's acting like he's Rock Hudson or something."

Wow. Boy, I tell you what, I'll never forget that one to this day. When the going gets tough, the tough get going. And you wear it here, and you have that confidence you're going to come back. Hugely important.

六、交易是什么：拳击比喻、催化时刻、日元与比特币

Feels like the right time to ask you just what trading means to you. You've said before that the whole world is just this interconnected series of capital flows and part of the job is being on top of that and taking positions based on what's going on in the world. But mostly I interview investors who buy stakes in companies. I very rarely interview someone that is moving and putting on major positions in any sort of asset class or trading instrument. And so I would love to just hear you describe — like, what to you trading is, what is the literal act that you are doing every day with such excellence for decades.

拳击比喻

There's a couple of metaphors that come to mind. Well, let's start with boxing, because you have an opponent. In this case, it's the market. You're going into the ring with this opponent who's after you the whole time. And I'm thinking more of a classic one as opposed to a Mike Tyson one. But you're kind of pairing, jabbing, feeling each other out, looking for an opening. And then every now and then you'll have a great opening and you take a big shot and you may land one.

If I think about big shots — Bitcoin 2020, a knockout. Two-year rates 2022, knockout. You have these incredible opportunities at times. If you just sit and wait the whole time in the interim — some of it you're just gathering information, looking for those openings. You're always trying to make progress. You're always trying to win the round. But there'll be those few opportunities where you can do something really material.

催化时刻：Dollar/Yen

I love the boxing analogy and the examples you gave. Bitcoin, two-year rates, precious metals, so many things you've been through. What is going on in one of those? Like, maybe pick one and I'm literally curious what you are doing in the moments, what you are looking at, what you are studying, what supply-demand imbalances you see. Like what is the literal nature of one of these windows that opens every so often?

If you think about all the really big moves, it's probably because the market has gotten too carried away, or there's been some imbalance that's gone on for too long, or a central bank does something that they shouldn't be doing, or a government does something they shouldn't be doing. So that's I think the genesis of most of the big moves. Typically, it's going to be inspired by central bank or central government.

A good one right now in the making that's going to be interesting, I think, is dollar-yen. The yen's grossly undervalued. Has been for some time. What's the catalytic moment? That's really the point you've got to ask. What's the catalytic moment? And that catalytic moment was this new prime minister that was just elected. She has all the characteristics of a Ronald Reagan or a Margaret Thatcher or Donald Trump when he got elected the second time. And if you look at what those local currencies did, they all appreciated pretty quickly 10% off the bat.

I want to say Japan has $4.5 trillion — a $4.5 trillion net international investment position to the good with the rest of the world. Probably 60% of that's in the US and most of that is unhedged. So they just have this massive dollar liability. And all of a sudden, we've now got the most dynamic leader in certainly half a century in Japan, who's "Japan first," is going to have a very entrepreneurial way that she is going to remake that economy.

So you're looking for something that's underowned, undervalued, way out of whack. People have gotten complacent on it, and you're looking for that catalytic moment.

2022 两年期国债 & 2020 比特币

In the case of the two-year rates in '22, we had way too much fiscal stimulus. And then Powell — he overstayed being easy way too long because he wanted Biden to reappoint him. So as soon as Biden reappointed him, it was go time to get short two-year notes because you knew the Fed was going to normalize.

And then in 2020, when you saw again all the interventions, both the central bank and the Treasury, you just knew that the inflation trades were going to take off. And of all of them, what was the best one at that point in time? It was Bitcoin. Bitcoin is unequivocally the best inflation hedge that there is — more than gold, because Bitcoin is finite. There's only so much Bitcoin that can be mined.

The problem with it as an inflation hedge is — if you got into kinetic exchange, there's clearly going to be cyber warfare, and anything that you have to deal with electronically is going down, including Bitcoin. So strike one. And then secondly, quantum computing. Who knows if and when, with AI advancing as fast as it is, that we may actually have quantum computing. Now, quantum computing — someone can come in and can hack any bank and hack anything they want to. So in terms of it being a great inflation hedge — gold increases supply every year by a couple of percent; Bitcoin, there's a finite amount that can be mined, it's decentralized. And so in that sense it has the greatest scarcity value of anything.

七、泡沫诊断：1987 / 1998 / 2000 与今天

If you think about the big ones that you've lived through — '87, the global financial crisis, COVID, etc., various asset bubbles through history — maybe say a little bit about those general experiences. You're very famous for what happened in 1987, of course. And I'm curious about those ones because I'm curious what you think about today's environment. "Are we in a bubble?" is a very popular question, mostly answered by investors, less so by traders. And so I would love your perspective on the big ones you've experienced historically and how those lessons apply to today.

共同的火药：杠杆与衍生品

If I think of some really really big accidents, most of them have the same underlying reason, same underlying foundation, which is too much leverage somewhere. Most of the leverage, most of the time — if I think of the big ones — were derivative-inspired, either futures or options.

1987, that crash was 100% portfolio insurance — 100%. Had they had limits, which they didn't, it would have been a 10%, maybe 15% max. But that was 100% derivatives.

If I think about 1998, Long-Term Capital — big derivatives, they had a huge balance sheet with a lot of derivatives they were off-sides on.

2000 was a little different. 2000 was the easiest bear market I've ever seen in my whole life. It's got so many similarities to right now in the sense that the bear market of 2001 and 2002 was a consequence of all the IPOs in '99-2000. And then as they unlocked, you just had this never-ending cascade of selling.

当下的 IPO + Unlock + Buyback 反转

cascade of selling — that's a great way of putting it.

And we're getting ready... I want to say that the contemplated IPOs in the next year are going to be 5–6% of market cap. So why are we where we are right now? Because we've been retiring 2 or 3% of market cap — probably a little less than 2% market cap every year without fail for the past 10 years.

Through buybacks and things like this.

Through buybacks. And so now all of a sudden, you're going to completely reverse that math. I don't think it necessarily happens instantaneously with the IPOs, but then there'll be the unlocks. So you could see a situation where, okay, maybe go through some kind of rolling top, and then 18 months from now, six months — we'll have to look at the buyback, the unlock schedule. But you're going to want to watch those because that'll just be adding equity supply. And you're already going to be diminishing the buybacks because of all the commitment to capex from the hyperscalers. They're already going to be eating into their cash flow. So I think that's why tech has dogged it and why it will continue to dog it, because much of the funding for these IPOs is going to come out of existing tech stocks.

主权债泡沫 & 252% Market Cap / GDP

When you say "are we in a bubble" — I don't know if we're necessarily in a bubble. We're clearly so leveraged in equities in this country. We're so dependent upon firm equity prices at this point in time. And when I say leveraged — we're 252% of stock market cap to GDP. So 1929, we were I think at the top, we were 65%. And then in '87 we got to about 85 or 90%. Then 2000 we got to 170%, and now we're at 252%.

So you can just imagine — if you think about the periodicity of significant bear markets since 1970, we get kind of a mean reversion on average every 10 years. When I say mean reversion, let's say mean revert to the past 25 or 30 year P/E. So if we did that here — and again these are elevated P/Es way elevated, beyond the 20th century — that would be, say, a 30-35% decline. Well, 35% on 250% of GDP is 80-90% of GDP — the reverse wealth effect. Oh my gosh, 10% of our tax revenues are capital gains; they go to zero. So you can see the budget deficit blowing up, you see the bond market getting smoked, you can see this kind of negative self-reinforcing effect. And so it's troubling. It's troubling.

So are we in a bubble? We're clearly in a sovereign debt bubble. In the stock market, we're over-equitized as a country, have the highest individual equity weightings in the history of the country. And then the real problem is that — if you look at private equity in 2007-2008, that was about 7% of institutional portfolios. Now it's about 16% of institutional portfolios. Real estate's gone up. Infrastructure bets have gone up. We're so much less liquid than we were in 2008. So you have to be cognizant of that fact when you think about how you have your money deployed.

给"长期投资者"的反驳：22 倍 P/E 之后的 10 年

I had a friend ask me — he was a wealth manager and he hates hedge funds because of the fees. He just says, "We just need to put money in the S&P 500." He said, "If you were investing for 20 years, what would you tell somebody?" And because he knows that I'm supposed to say, "You just buy the S&P, close your eyes" — well, the problem is that if you buy the S&P at this current valuation, the 10-year return is negative when you buy with the S&P P/E of 22. That's what history shows.

So yes, the S&P is a spectacular long-term [investment] if you have a 100-year view. But that's because that's an average of 100 years, including times when the S&P 500 P/E was six and seven and eight, or one-third of what it is right now. So valuation matters a lot, and the stock market's really high, and it's going to be really hard to make money from here, I think, with any kind of long-term view.

八、一天的样子：6:15 起床、信息过载、exquisite execution

If I came and shadowed you on a given day today, how does it go? What are you doing? What does a day look like today? I know you have a system that you can be heard by all times by your execution traders. I know it's intense, but just walk us through a day in the life.

I get up at, say, 6:15. I work till 7 o'clock. I go work out till 7:45. I try to do 45 minutes of hard cardio every day. I get in front of the screens for the opening. I typically won't have any meetings till 10:00, and then I'll probably do meetings till about 12:00. Typically have lunch with someone, have a meeting after lunch, try to make sure that I've got an hour before the close and an hour after the close to be able to map out what I want to do the next day — because, again, I want to have a plan — as well as think what's going to happen that night in Tokyo, Hong Kong.

I'll go home around 5. Walk with my wife for an hour. Go up, work for an hour, come back down and eat. I'll normally watch the news and the most mindless entertainment that I possibly [can]. I used to read a book and a half a week, and then once the internet came, forget it. I'm so tired of reading by nighttime. I've read one book in the last year, which is going to be — by the way, I recommend everyone read it. It's David Wood. He's kind of a newsletter writer, but he has written what's going to be a spectacular bestseller on globalization markets. It's fantastic, and I'll bet you it gets made into a Netflix series. I bet you it's going to be a banger.

Anyway, I'll try to watch something on Netflix. Then I'll work from say 9:30 to 10:15 and then go to bed. Then I'll wake up at 6:15 and do it [again].

No, no, no.

I'll wake up at probably 2:30 or 3:00 and I'll work for half an hour, watch London open for 45 minutes, half an hour. I'll do a lot of analytical work. Then it's a quiet time. It's good. Then I'll go back to sleep and wake up at 6:15.

Wow. And you've been doing that for 50 years?

I've been doing that — yeah, I've been doing that certainly since the '80s. I feel like I work so much harder than I did 40 years ago, 30 years ago because there's more information.

800,000 封邮件与 Exquisite Execution

Oh my god, I get 800,000 emails a day. If I think about when I was a pit trader — I think about even the '80s when there was so much less information, I could spend more of my time intently focusing on what the highs and lows were going to be in the day, which are really important for execution. Doing what my boss Eli did — just waiting, paying attention, focusing. Are we at a point of maximum pain? Is there so much fear right now, which is a great time to buy? Is — does it look like it's going to go up forever, a great time to sell? You have to be intentional to be able to pick those points in a day. And when you're trading 25 different instruments, instrument by instrument by instrument — a lot of times they'll be correlated, sometimes they're not — you have to be very intentional about that.

So if while you're doing that there's 48 emails coming in at the same time, all of which could be actionable information... I think today, for me at least, it's a lot harder, I think, because the information overload distracts me from exquisite execution.

What does that mean, "exquisite execution"?

Am I buying when there's blood on the ground? And am I selling when there's complete elation? Let's take last Friday, where we had the largest down day in the history of gold and silver. So you've got to be paying attention every minute. Because when you have a 33% move in silver in one day, you just have to be so focused on that day's activity, what you're going to do on the opening, what you're going to do if the price moves through a certain price that you weren't anticipating that day.

It gets back to what I learned in Bed-Stuy. You better have a plan. You better have a plan ahead of time and it better be self-executing and you better have thought through that. And I hear it from my other friends who are macro traders. They go, "God darn it, I just feel like I'm two hours late, three hours late." And last Friday was a great example. Can be so material.

九、激情、概率与"用进废退"

If I think about what's required to do that day in day out, there's got to be just an enormous amount of passion for markets and what you do. Can you just talk about the notion of finding, nurturing, the importance of passion in a life's work?

70% 是天生的：概率论与游戏狂

It's so funny when we talk about traders, great traders — and again, I'm talking about people who are alpha generators, not investing — we had a dinner about, I guess it was a year ago Christmas, and I had four or five of my best risk-takers at dinner, and we had the debate: is a great trader born or made? I'd say it was unanimous agreement at the table that 70% of it is nature.

And when I say "born with it" — by the time I was 21, I had played [everything]. I loved games. I love chess, backgammon, Parcheesi, Monopoly, battle, gin rummy — you name it, I played it. Then I started gambling in college. I had a degree in probability theory and had never taken a math course on it, but I knew it. I was such a game fanatic and I loved it so much.

If I had to pick out the number one traits — Type A personality, incredibly curious and inquisitive, and loves competition, loves games. Because, again, our whole business is just another form of probability theory. And I still love today — I play bridge with my friends all the time. I can't get enough of that. I love any kind of game of chance. And I love trading.

棕榈滩 GP 的箴言："You retire, you die"

The other reason that I love trading — I'll never forget, my wife's Australian and we first got married back in 1989. She said, "Well, I know you guys live in New York, but I came from the beach. So when our last child graduates from college, you're taking me to the beach." So sure enough, my son turns 18, my fourth child, 2014, we're going. So we ended up down here in Palm Beach. She gives me a GP — a general practitioner. So she makes me go in. I see the guy and this guy's 83 years old. And I say to him, "You're living here in the land of the walking dead. Everyone here is basically fossilized. What is the secret to longevity?" And he goes, "It's real simple. You retire, you die."

And that had a really profound impact on me, because I realized as I get older — and maybe when you get older, you will too — boy, you better [keep moving]. If you don't use it, you're going to lose it. And that's why I try to work out two hours a day. And it's why another reason that I enjoy trading is I want to keep my mind sharp because there's too many things that I want to do when I'm — my father lived to be 100 — when I'm in my 90s that I want to do. So I think trading is great therapy for me too.

The other reason that I really enjoy trading is I'd like to make an absolute pot of money so I can give it away. I got so many causes that I want to give it to that I actually feel like this is the pursuit of nobility. So I enjoy [it] — I feel it's a privilege to wake up and I just hope I freaking kill it so I can give it away.

【广告插段】Vanta / Ridgeline

As your business scales up, everything gets more complex, especially your compliance and security needs. With so many tools offering band-aids and patches, it's unfortunately far too easy for something to slip through the cracks. Fortunately, Vanta is a powerful tool designed to simplify and automate your security work and deliver a single source of truth for compliance and risk. There's a reason that Ramp, Cursor, and Snowflake all use Vanta. It frees them to focus on building amazing differentiated products, knowing that compliance and security are under control. Learn more at vanta.com/invest.

I know firsthand how complex the tech stack is for asset management firms. And seemingly every new tool and data source makes the problem even worse — adding more complexity, more headcount, and more risk. Ridgeline offers a better way forward. One unified platform that automates away that complexity across portfolio accounting, reconciliation, reporting, trading, compliance, and more — all at scale. Ridgeline is revolutionizing investment management, helping ambitious firms scale faster, operate smarter, and stay ahead of the curve. See what Ridgeline can unlock for your firm. Schedule a demo at ridgeline.ai.

十、Robin Hood 的诞生：1987 之后的"最糟宏观判断"

Can you tell us the story of starting Robin Hood and what you talked to us earlier about the charter school work and everything in Bed-Stuy. Robin Hood started the year after. It's a really important part of your life and legacy.

Robin Hood happened the day after the crash. And maybe the worst macro call of my life was thinking we were going to go into depression. I thought for sure — I'd been looking at the parallels in 1929 for a year. All of a sudden it happens. I think, "Oh my god, this is a perfect replay, a perfect analog." So I called up my friends and we began it.

It's just been the most marvelous journey. I can't recommend anything more highly than getting involved with some cause that you believe in. The best part of philanthropy and charitable giving is the people you meet. Oh my gosh, I've met the greatest, most wonderful, most giving [people]. It just brightens every aspect of your life to be associated with the best people who embody the best ideals.

Anyway, we said we got to do something. So the only alternative — at that there really weren't any charities focused on fighting poverty, which I thought was going to be rampant at the time. So I've always believed: if you want to do something, you're better off doing it yourself. So we started out very small and we just applied basic business principles to finding the most efficacious way of helping people in need. And again, along the way, I'm learning. I'm learning by doing. I'm making all these mistakes along the way. And I'm learning so much, and then I realized, "Boy, this is a science." And then we just started hiring the very best people that we could as our staff. And then recruiting — it was so easy to recruit people from the financial world to help. The '90s were a special time. Everyone wanted to participate and give back and help.

I don't know when it was, but my gosh, we created so many fantastic philanthropists in that era. In the '80s it was completely different. Everyone wanted to be associated with the Philharmonic and these are all great, but they wanted to be with the named social charities. I'd say after the crash, everyone found their significance by helping others. Maybe it was just because that was when you really started to accumulate these big fortunes, individual fortunes for people, and they were just really wonderful about wanting to give back. And the whole financial community supported Robin Hood in such a spectacular way. The whole hedge fund community in New York — it was, and it still is, a joy.

十一、对未来最乐观与最脆弱的地方：无工作世界、新闻写作、人生主成分

What if you think about the whole broad world feels the healthiest to you today about the system that is the world, where that makes you the most optimistic, and where does it feel the most fragile?

无工作世界中的"意义"

I'm trying to fast forward to the workless world — to where AI does so much for us that we don't have to work. And I used to have a really negative outlook on what that would be like, cuz if you think about it, so many of us define our significance through our work. Imagine a world where now the significance — that hugely important component of human happiness — is now taken away from us because of the fact that we don't have to work.

I used to really despair about that, because as someone who works all the time and finds my significance [in it] — what am I going to do? But I've become more optimistic recently because, as I think through how athletes find significance in the sports they do, how I play bridge with my friends and find so much significance in competing with them — I think maybe humans are adaptable enough that we'll find a different way to find significance. Maybe it's going to be in finding that intentional simple act of kindness every day. I'm not sure what it is, but I do think that as a race, we're so unbelievably adaptable, so smart, that we might be able to find a way to find happiness. I think that's going to be the biggest challenge potentially in four or five years — is what do we do when so many of our jobs have been replaced by AI?

新闻写作 = 主成分分析

So many young people are trying to figure out what to do, what their careers are going to be. You've talked and written a lot about the power of communication, like great communication. It's such an essential skill. Journalism is an interesting way of learning that. Can you talk about that power as a message to young people trying to figure it out — why they should maybe focus on that?

I have long maintained that better than a business school degree, Journalism 101 should be a mandatory subject in every college, which is newspaper writing in particular. It was so important to my development.

So my father had a really small trade finance legal paper in Memphis — 2,500-person subscription. I would be the copy editor, the front-page editor, and then I would write for it and took journalism classes. And what newspaper writing does is it teaches you you have to write where the conclusion comes first. So unlike writing something and then at the end we get the story — here, the end comes first. And you have to write it with this incredibly rigid discipline so that every succeeding thing that you write — the most important part is in the first sentence of the first paragraph. The first paragraph is not more than two sentences. Who, what, where, when, why, and how. That's the first one. Then the next most important thing — that's the second paragraph, no more than two sentences. The next one, same thing.

What is that really? It's a principal component analysis of taking whatever event may have transpired and putting it in this cogent way where the most important stuff starts the beginning and you work your way down through it. Particularly where today's world the attention spans are this short, and time clearly is money. You want to be able to communicate in the quickest and most concise fashion you can, where you get your entire point across in the shortest amount of time. There's that old saying: if you can't tell your story in 15 seconds or less, no one's going to listen. And it's never been more true than right now. Because again, if you're getting a thousand emails a day, I want to know in the first paragraph whether I want to keep reading or not.

As a macro thinker, man, it helped me so much frame every potential trading decision, every single action. I could just, again, very quickly — because I had learned how to do that principal component analysis and hierarchialize the most important things. And in trading, there's so many different variables that come into play in making a trading decision. Let's say that there's 10 really important things. Every one of those will have its day. It'll rotate through in terms of importance. Again, the yen's a great example. It's been completely undervalued for the past 24 months, way off-sides. It's so ripe to rally sharply, but it needed a catalytic moment. And that catalytic moment was this new prime minister that was just elected. So if you take valuation, which everyone's ignored now for the past two years — in the end, all of a sudden this moment takes it from here and puts it at the very top.

So newspaper style — hugely important in developing any logic framework. What's the most important thing that's actionable at this second in that particular instrument, and what's my checklist, and how do I hierarchialize? And that's literally what trading is all about.

人生的主成分：God, Family, Friends, Fun, Service

If I were to ask you to do that on the topic of the principal components of a great life, how would that read?

God, family, friends. When I think of friends, I think of fun. So God, family, friends, and fun. Service.

I mean, my significance is not going to be from being a trader. I don't think that's where my significance is going to be. My significance is going to be — first and foremost, going to be my family. Every now and then — like, I actually get joy out of thinking about my funeral cuz I'm so excited about the songs that I've chosen that'll be sung. I almost wish I was going to be alive for that, cuz it's going to be such a great time. I know my family and friends will enjoy it.

I'm thinking of family because, again, if I think about the end of my life and I look back, I'm not going to be thinking about the '87 crash or Bitcoin. I'm going to be thinking about who I loved and who loved me. That's what I'm going to be thinking about — and what kind of relationships we had and what kind of times we had.

I think the professional aspects are these great tools that allow you to do more meaningful things — and the things that count, which is: what have you done with your family? What have you done with your friends? How have you served others? What have you done to leave a legacy of happiness and betterment and goodness to the people that you've been privileged enough to come in contact with? And when I say legacy, I don't mean words. I mean deeds. So what have you done that have allowed other people to enjoy and better their station in life, their happiness in life? For me, that's the most significant thing by far and away.

信仰、生活准则与"上天堂"

Do you always believe in God?

I have trouble at times. I mean, yes, I do. But I — you know, my faith is tested like I think anybody's faith is tested. I wish I could say with 100% certainty I know I'm going to heaven. I certainly pray every night.

The most important aspect — and the reason why I think it's so important for us all to have God in our lives — is you've got to have some set, some code by which you live. I think Christianity, Judaism, a variety of these have these wonderful doctrinary ways of bringing stability and order and goodness in people's lives. They give you something that you can lead a productive life with, in a way that's very sustainable and allows you to engage with everyone around you with great happiness and joy.

春之巅、秋之巅：神在那一刻

What about Africa?

I love Africa so much because I love the outdoors. Can I just say this? It's taken me 70 years to get to this point. My new thing is I love to find peak spring and peak fall. It doesn't have to be Africa. You can find that day in a neighborhood. And in peak spring, when the colors are vibrant and when the fragrance is just overwhelming, you can find it to the minute in the right place. And I've never felt so alive in my life.

I now travel the United States for that moment, because I know when peak spring is in Georgia. I know where peak spring is in Tennessee. And I know when it is in New York City and I know when it is north of New York City — because New York City's normally a week behind this place I go to in upstate.

Same thing in fall. There'll be a day where you get the intersection — and the Northeast is the best because it has the greatest biodiversity, where you see the greatest color. And you can see those color changes by day, by tree. And you find that day with that intersection of color change. I'm telling you, if you find that day, you can feel the energy. I've never felt so alive. You can feel God at those moments. It's such a wondrous moment, as you go through how life is changing so fast — in the case of spring, obviously, it's blooming; in the case of fall, it's transitioning into a more dormant period — but they are so energetic and so exciting. And I highly recommend people try to, in their neighborhood, find that day. Boy, make sure you're walking that afternoon before sunset. Oh my gosh, it's a joy.

十二、收尾：Kill them with kindness

Boy, you've lived so much life in the years that you've been with us. And I never asked the question of general advice — I think it's kind of a lame question — but I'm tempted to close the conversation there. With what would you just tell people listening? This audience is passionate, they're curious, they're searching for their thing, or they're trying to get to be the greatest at their thing, they're competitive. What would you tell them based on the sum total of all your amazing life and experience?

My mother used to always say — and I think this is what this country really needs right now — you've just got to kill them with kindness. So you're going to wake up someday, it's going to be terrible. You're going to be in a bad mood. There's going to be something on TV that's going to make you angry. And particularly in these times right now, my gosh, when everyone wants to demonize the opposition — I think we've got to realize it doesn't have to be that way.

We can humbly devote ourselves to finding the kindness within ourselves and the goodness within ourselves and transmit that to somebody else during that day. I think that's the secret to happiness. You don't have to worry about yourself. You have to worry about: how am I going to brighten someone else's day? And with that attitude, you'll always be happy. "I'm just going to spend this one day doing this one outward act." And if you repeat it enough — again, everything is about the reps, right? — pretty soon, you take "I should" and they will become "I ams." So if you take that mentality that "I want to do this wonderful act of kindness for someone else," pretty soon you become an incredibly kind person. It becomes natural. It becomes instinctive and organic, and it's going to brighten your day, and you'll have just such a positive outlook on life. And I think that's what we need to intentionally begin to do — every single person, for sure. It'll be a much better world.

Paul, thank you so much for your time.

Thank you.

【广告尾段】Ramp / Vanta / Ridgeline / Rogo / WorkOS

Your finance team isn't losing money on big mistakes. It's leaking through a thousand tiny decisions nobody's watching. Ramp puts guardrails on spending before it happens — real-time limits, automatic rules, zero firefighting. Try it at ramp.com/invest.

As your business grows, Vanta scales with you, automating compliance and giving you a single source of truth for security and risk. Learn more at vanta.com/invest.

Ridgeline is redefining asset management technology as a true partner, not just a software vendor. They've helped firms 5x and scale, enabling faster growth, smarter operations, and a competitive edge. Visit ridgelineapps.com to see what they can unlock for your firm.

Every investment firm is unique, and generic AI doesn't understand your process. Rogo does. It's an AI platform built specifically for Wall Street, connected to your data, understanding your process, and producing real outputs. Check them out at rogo.ai/invest.

The best AI and software companies — from OpenAI to Cursor to Perplexity — use WorkOS to become enterprise ready overnight, not in months. Visit workos.com to skip the unglamorous infrastructure work and focus on your product.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的发现：他的"交易天才"其实是一种**童年创伤的代偿性延续**
Paul 反复强调"流动性是我的 DNA"，归功于祖父那句"你只值你明天能开出的支票"，以及亲眼看到 Bunker Hunt 在六周内从世界首富沦为破产。但更深层的线索藏在他 2 岁半在菜市场走丢的故事里——那是他**第一个童年记忆**。一个让他"恐惧到极点"的失控瞬间，被一位陌生黑人老人的善意"接住"。
注意这里的隐藏对称： - **童年的恐惧 → 被一个外人解救 → 形成 4000 次的祈祷复读** - **交易中的恐惧 → 被流动性解救 → 形成 50 年的"绝不持有"信条**
他自称是 NFL 里的"右护卫，每天在战壕里搏斗"，而把 Buffett 描绘成"信仰美国就好"的人——这不是单纯的策略偏好，这是**一个永远等不到母亲的孩子，无法允许自己进入"信任并等待"的状态**。Buffett 的 50% 回撤"会对他造成实质性影响"——他承认了。这是他自己说的，但他可能没意识到这句话的深度。
**最意外的洞察**：他用慈善（Robin Hood、特许学校）疯狂"复制"那位陌生老人的善意，用交

item.title: [Dwarkesh Clips] What this Florence bridge reveals about tyranny – Ada Palmer
item.dc:creator: Dwarkesh Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、塔楼的几何学：权力的"绕道"是文明最精确的体温计

我们习惯用宏大叙事衡量自由——宪法、革命、宣言。但 Palmer 指出的真相要刁钻得多：自由的真实尺度，藏在建筑的"不规则处"。瓦萨里走廊那个绕开 Minnelli 塔楼的笨拙拐角，比任何成文法都更精确地度量了佛罗伦萨公爵的权力边界。

这里有一个反直觉的洞见：建筑越光滑、越对称、越"完美"，越是暴政的征兆。费拉拉的埃斯特公爵能裸体持剑游街，是因为他的城市没有"绕道"——没有任何东西会让他的意志拐弯。而美第奇的走廊有一个"咯噔"，那个咯噔，就是公民社会还活着的脉搏。

跨领域的连接：这与软件工程中的"Chesterton 的篱笆"惊人相似——一段看似多余、绕弯的代码往往承载着系统中最古老、最不可替代的契约。删除它的人，几乎总是新晋掌权者。走廊的拐角和遗留代码的丑陋分支，本质上是同一种现象：权力对历史的让步。

二、"祖先的时间"作为唯一能挡住暴君的货币

让我打破一个常规观察：Minnelli 家族能拒绝公爵，不是因为他们"有钱"或"有兵"——他们没有。他们拥有的是一种更稀有的资本——深时间（deep time）。"这座塔在美第奇还不是姓氏之前就属于我们"——这句话不是法律论据，是形而上学论据。

意外之处在于：现代人以为产权是被法律创造的，但 Palmer 暗示的恰恰相反——产权是被"超过任何在世者记忆的连续性"创造的，法律只是事后追认。这就是为什么新富不能复制旧贵族的免疫力，为什么硅谷的"老钱"焦虑、为什么宪法越年轻的国家越容易滑向威权。

跨领域连接：生物学中有个概念叫"进化稳定策略"——一个策略之所以稳定，不是因为它最优，而是因为它存在得足够久，以至于任何偏离都会触发整个系统的免疫反应。Minnelli 塔楼是社会层面的进化稳定策略。它告诉我们：抵抗暴政最深的护城河，不是制度设计，而是"让某些东西活得比任何当权者都久"这件事本身。

三、最危险的悖论："失败的抵抗才是真正胜利的抵抗"——但前提是抵抗者不知道这一点

Palmer 给出的金句是"resistance loses, resistance wins"。但这里藏着一个更黑暗、更精微的层次，她和 Dwarkesh 都没说透：

这种"失败式胜利"只有在抵抗者真心相信自己在追求完全胜利时才会发生。 一旦人们意识到"哎，反正就算输了也能赢一点"，这种半隐性的妥协就会立刻失效。因为暴君也会读历史——他知道你只想要"部分"，于是他只让你拿走"无关紧要的部分"。

这就是 Dwarkesh 那句被低估的话的真正含义："让美国抵抗变弱的唯一原因，是人们把部分胜利当作失败。"——但反过来同样致命：把部分胜利当作完全胜利，抵抗会更快地萎缩。

跨领域连接：这与量子测量有诡异的同构——一旦你测量"我们到底赢了多少"，叠加态就坍缩了，那种使费拉拉公爵不敢裸奔的"模糊的、未结算的、还在燃烧的市民意志"就消失了。自由不是一个被赢得的状态，而是一个永远不能被结算的进行时。佛罗伦萨之所以比费拉拉自由，不是因为它的共和派胜利了，而是因为它的共和派从未承认自己失败——那座塔楼直到今天还在那里，公爵的走廊仍然要绕着它走。

意外的总结：真正的暴政从来不是从镇压抵抗开始的，而是从允许抵抗者写下自己的失败史开始的。

📝 转录文本

佛罗伦萨的瓦萨里走廊：一座弱势公爵的纪念碑

Uh there's a really cool building that I love um in Florence. It's, if you've been there, there's the famous bridge, the Ponte Vecchio, which has the little jeweler shops all along it. And when you get to the end of it, there's this funny uh over-the-head corridor, the Vasari corridor as we call it, which was built by the Dukes of Florence to connect the old city palace — where the Senate used to meet, where they had to have their seat of power — to their new palace across the river, which was much bigger, where they could have grand balls and things that dukes need to have.

And because they're so terrified of being assassinated by their own people, they built this overhead walkway that goes from one end of the city to the other so that they could walk in safety without being assassinated. Right? This is a sign of a weak Duke.

走廊的暴力施工，与一座不肯让步的塔楼

But also when he was building it, it's going across the roofs and sometimes blasting off the second stories of different people's houses. And most people, when his grace the Duke says "I'm gonna blast the top story off your house," would say "yes your grace, please continue," because there are literally severed heads of people who resisted still rotting on spikes in front of the palaces.

Uh, but they get to this one point where there's an old tower — a very old tower, a 500-year-old tower — and this belongs to, I think it's the Minnelli family, who are descended from peers of Julius Caesar and can trace their genealogy all the way back to an old Roman gent. And when the Duke says, "We want to knock the top off your tower," they say, "No. This is our tower. This tower has been ours since before the Medici existed as a named family. You may not knock the top off."

And the Duke does not knock the top off, and the corridor goes around in this awkward square uh around that tower — because he knows that if he violates something as traditional and core to the civilization as the property rights of somebody who has owned something for a long time, there will be rebellion. There will be civil war. There will be dissent. There will be resistance.

These are monarchs who know that they are weak, and are therefore careful — and therefore more rights, like property rights, exist.

对照组：费拉拉的埃斯特公爵与真正的暴君

Meanwhile, across the river in Ferrara — which I already mentioned as being a place where Dukes worked very, very hard to gain power — uh, Duke Alfonso d'Este of Ferrara used to wander around Ferrara buck naked, uh, with a sword in one hand and his dick in the other, to show off that nobody would ever possibly try to harm a Duke d'Este.

And he and his siblings used to do things like: if they liked a musician, kidnap them and lock them in a tower so that nobody else could hear them. Or if they wanted each other's musician, send goons to kidnap each other's musicians. They also used to recreationally murder each other's servants when the siblings were tiffing with each other.

That is what you do when you don't fear your people and when you feel confident in power. And so they are much closer to tyrants than the Medici are ever able to be, even after the republic falls.

核心洞见："抵抗失败之时，抵抗仍胜"

And that's what's so neat, right? Because the resistance failed. If we're looking at it in black and white, the republic fell. Uh, there wasn't a republic anymore. Uh, there was a Duke. He took over. The old system was gone.

But because the republic fought so hard, and because the people really believed in it, the people had a lot more rights, and the tyrant was a lot less tyrannical, because there had been that fight.

It's a great example of how even when resistance loses, resistance wins.

现代回响：美国会变成普京式国家吗？

Yeah. I I I think there's actually an interesting parallel to today, where — not to be too on the nose — but like sometimes people debate, like, what is the odds that America becomes a sort of a Putinist kind of country within a couple of decades. And I think the odds are actually quite low, just because even though constitutionally, or at least in precedent, the president is very powerful, it just — the expectation of, uh —

Yeah, the republic expectation is so strong that — that the amount of resistance that is faced, even when you successfully do something, right —

demotivates the next escalation. The only thing that makes resistance weak in the US is when people feel as if partial victory is failure.

If you enjoyed this clip, you can watch the full episode here and subscribe for more clips. Thanks.

观看原视频 →
item.description:

我们习惯用宏大叙事衡量自由——宪法、革命、宣言。但 Palmer 指出的真相要刁钻得多：**自由的真实尺度，藏在建筑的"不规则处"**。瓦萨里走廊那个绕开 Minnelli 塔楼的笨拙拐角，比任何成文法都更精确地度量了佛罗伦萨公爵的权力边界。
这里有一个反直觉的洞见：**建筑越光滑、越对称、越"完美"，越是暴政的征兆**。费拉拉的埃斯特公爵能裸体持剑游街，是因为他的城市没有"绕道"——没有任何东西会让他的意志拐弯。而美第奇的走廊有一个"咯噔"，那个咯噔，就是公民社会还活着的脉搏。
跨领域的连接：这与**软件工程中的"Chesterton 的篱笆"**惊人相似——一段看似多余、绕弯的代码往往承载着系统中最古老、最不可替代的契约。删除它的人，几乎总是新晋掌权者。**走廊的拐角和遗留代码的丑陋分支，本质上是同一种现象：权力对历史的让步。**
## 二、"祖先的时间"作为唯一能挡住暴君的货币

item.title: [投资TALK君] ✨【投资TALK君1426期】大空头做空芯片！如何应对本周大波动！陶氏看通胀影响✨20260427#CPI #nvda #美股 #投资 #英伟达 #ai #特斯拉
item.dc:creator: 投资TALK君
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、价格新高与估值未破新高的"剪刀差"——市场的隐秘叙事正在切换

最让人意外的观察：指数连续破新高，但芯片板块的forward P/E反而从去年10月的28倍降到了现在的23.4倍。这个看似矛盾的现象，其实揭示了一个被绝大多数散户忽略的真相——当下的上涨不是"估值扩张"驱动的，而是"分母在追赶分子"。

换句话说，市场不是变得更狂热了，而是华尔街对未来一年EPS的预期在悄悄上修。这与Michael Burry做空芯片的逻辑恰好相反——Burry赌的是"贵"，但贵是价格层面的；估值层面其实在变便宜。这就像一个看似涨得很猛的胖子，其实身高在以更快的速度增长，BMI反而下降了。

跨领域连接：这种"价格新高+估值收缩"的现象，在生物学里有个对应概念叫异速生长（allometric scaling）——器官增长的速度超过身体本身。当一个系统的"承载能力"（EPS）扩张速度超过"表观体积"（股价）时，系统其实在变得更稳健，而非更脆弱。Burry读的是表观体积，市场定价的是承载能力——这是两套完全不同的语言。

二、阿尔法市场的"反向公地悲剧"——指数掩盖了机会，也掩盖了风险

最隐藏的系统性真相：当下市场的"参与度"和"指数"出现了背离——指数在涨，但参与的个股在减少。罗素2000的参与度从90%降到现在的水平，但指数还在涨。这意味着什么？指数本身正在变成一个"幸存者偏差产品"。

这里有一个反直觉的洞见：油价上涨12.6%、黄金跟随宏观走、JEPI还在被战争压制，但标普却在涨——这不是宏观逻辑失效，而是整个市场进入了"板块脱钩"状态。芯片一个板块的50% YTD涨幅，正在伪装成整个市场的健康。

跨领域连接：这个现象在生态学里叫关键种效应（keystone species effect）——海星消失，整个潮间带生态崩溃。当下美股的"关键种"是芯片板块。如果Burry的做空真的演化成芯片回调，受伤的不会只是芯片，而是被它"伪装的健康"所掩盖的整个市场。指数的强势本身是脆弱性的伪装。

三、陶氏财报里藏着的"时间炸弹"——一个被市场严重低估的传导链条

最让人意外的发现：陶氏财报告诉你，化工原料的成本上涨还没有传导到任何下游公司的财报里。市场现在交易的是"战争结束=油价回落=正常化"，但陶氏管理层明确说，这次冲击的影响将持续12-18个月——也就是说，就算明天战争结束，传导链条仍在路上。

这是一个典型的"延迟引信"现象：包装公司、食品饮料、汽车OEM、医疗器材、建筑——全都还没在财报里反映成本上升。市场在为还没爆炸的东西定价"已经过去了"。

跨领域连接：这让我想到地震学里的走滑断层应力累积——能量在地表看不到的地方持续积累，直到某个临界点突然释放。这次的"释放"不会在Q1财报里，可能在Q2、Q3甚至Q4——而那时市场早已为"通胀回落"做完了定价。陶氏的财报其实是在告诉你一个跨越多个板块的成本-定价权传导测试即将开始。

真正的投资信号：与其去赌芯片是否回调，不如建立一个"定价权地图"——哪些公司能把成本传递给消费者（如苹果、Costco、JEPI），哪些公司只能内部消化（多数中游制造、包装、OEM）。这是一个用财报会议管理层语言去筛选公司的好机会——下一个季度，凡是CEO提到"我们在监控原材料成本"的公司，都值得重新审视。

最后一个反直觉点：小陶反复强调"满仓不动"——这看似保守，其实是基于一个深刻洞察：在阿尔法市场里，最大的风险不是回调，而是因为恐惧切换仓位而错过下一波结构性机会。这与赌场里的"随机漫步谬误"完全相反——他不是相信运气，而是相信认知边界。真正的稀缺资源不是仓位，而是不被市场情绪绑架的判断力。

📝 转录文本

开场白

各位观众大家好，欢迎来到一个不单只聊新闻的专业美股频道，我是市场奉闲小陶，欢迎大家来到周日的更新。

在上周五的时候，大空头Michael Burry宣布了它做空芯片板块。那今天我带大家去看一下芯片板块的一个估值，看完之后可能会让大家非常的意外。虽然指数在最近这段时间连续破新高，且超出了前高已经非常远的这个距离，但是估值其实并没有上年10月份要高，甚至已比上年10月份还低了不少。说明什么问题？稍后会详细的展开和大家聊一下。

然后4月份的反弹因为非常的厉害，大家可能认为整个市场里面也没有什么机会了，那稍后和大家分享一个标的，是我2025年的第一大职仓，大家可能已经猜到了——GE。

然后本周标普500里面42%的公司发布财报，再加上大科技全部集中在一天，周三又有美联储会议，感觉这一周将会非常的热闹。然后战争这边的事件也有一些更新等等。现在的感觉好像是手里有仓位的那些人感觉非常的恐慌，会不会出现sell the news；手里没有仓位的人现在感觉我是不是要追，我又怕财报出来之后继续出现上涨。那到底应该以什么样的一个心态去迎接呢？然后我们聊一下。

然后最后上周五其实有一家公司的财报是非常值得去关注的——陶氏。陶氏YTD到现在为止走的非常的好，就是因为受到战争的这个利好。通过陶氏的财报和大家分享一下，接下去油下的这个上涨，特别是化工原料这个上涨，对于不同板块的一些影响。

好吗，那我就废话不多说了。在开始之前，拜托大家记得点赞、留言加转发，让更多的人可以看到我们这么专业的美股频道，大家说是不是？

一、过去一周资产价格回顾

先来看一下，回顾一下过去一周资产价格的走势。

美元美债与股指

美元美债市场这一块，美元出现了小幅上涨，美债收率也出现了小幅上涨。理论上来说，美元指数上涨、美债收入上涨，标普应该出现下跌，但我们并没有看到。标普外在上一周上涨0.5，那只是1.5，道指出现了下跌。

所以其实上一周市场起码看标普吧，标普的这个上涨主要是因为纳指的一个强势，而纳指的强势主要是因为科技股的强势和芯片板块的强势。

阿尔法市场的特征

我记得我在一周前做展望的时候，我说过去的那一周——就一周前做上一周展望的时候——我说因为3、4月份以来的反弹，前三周的反弹非常的厉害，那接下去的一周可能会出现阿尔法的一个市场，一部分的一些个股可能走出来，从指数这个角度去看稍微要贴一些。

为什么？因为我记得一周前我们在做、在看市场参与度的时候，当时市场的参与度非常的高。那我记得当时罗素2000的参与度是去到90%了，然后标普在82%，然后纳指在一个相对70%多的一个位置。那经过过去这一周的一个横盘，其实参与度是下来了，虽然指数继续上涨，参与度下来。

说明什么问题？说明一些个股出现了回调，但是指数仍然上涨，是因为一些板块的个股非常的强势所带领。那么这种市场就是我们说非常标准的一个阿尔法的一个市场。

什么叫做阿尔法的一个市场？就是你可以看到指数在上涨，但是指数上涨是因为一些个股的一个上涨，如果自己手里没有指数或者没有这些个股的话，可能就会跑出这个市场，这就叫做阿尔法的一个市场，个股的一个市场。

板块分歧

同时我们看到有很多人没办法理解嘛——那为什么就黄金还是走一个宏观的一个走势，原油、美元指数上涨，然后黄金下跌；原油上涨了12.6%，为什么标普500还能上涨，为什么科技股还能上涨？那稍后我们看一下整个芯片板块的一个估值，我觉得是似乎里面有一个结论在里边。

IGV上涨了0.1，罗素2000也是小幅的一个上涨。板块这边可以看到分歧非常的明显，那虽然最终指数是上涨，但是板块分歧非常的明显：国防板块下跌了7%，也就是和GE一相关的这个板块下跌的幅度比较大，GE在上一周也出现了比较大的幅度的下跌；医疗板块下跌3.1，公共事业1.5，看很多的防守性的板块上周都出现了下跌，等权指数下跌0.6。

那为什么指数上涨？芯片板块直接反弹了11%。

二、芯片板块估值深度剖析

短期热度与历史涨幅

YTD到现在为止，我今天特地回过头去看了一下，大家我建议大家也可以有时间去看一下，3月30日我在那期做直播的时候，芯片板块YTD上涨2%，2%啊。然后就是仅仅一个月不到的时间，整个芯片板块上涨了50%，并且把过去一年到现在的一个走势已经上涨了150%，翻了一倍半。

这种短期之内市场情绪面非常高涨的一种涨幅，让大空头Michael Burry在上周五的话宣布自己做空芯片板块。当然他做空芯片板块的同时，他又在做多Adobe，然后他又在做多微软。感觉它是和现在的市场是反着去做：现在市场是做多硬件、做空软件，它现在在做多软件、做空硬件。

估值视角的客观分析

那现在芯片板块是不是真的非常贵的呢？我肯定是认可，我也没办法不承认现在短期之内芯片板块的热度非常的高，就大家情绪面非常的高。但是我们来看一下估值，可能有一个比较客观的一个视角去出发。

白色这条线是整个芯片板块forward PE向前看的这个PE。我们可以看到24年当时向前看的PE来到了30倍以上，甚至于有3次去到了30倍。然后上年10月份的时候，这个点就是上年差不多就是上年10月28日、10月29日，那一天芯片板块forward PE差不多是在28倍左右。然后从上年的10月份，经过4个月的一个回调，特别是3月份下跌的幅度非常的大，一度下跌到了16倍，16倍、17倍左右。

当时这张图我记得我拿出来和大家分享过，芯片板块的估值已经回到了上年4月份的水平。如果去看纳指的话，已经低于上年4月份的这个水平。但是当时我在和大家分享的时候，大家整个环境就沉浸在战争到底会往哪个方向演变下去，特别是非常多的一些观点认为指数并没有出现非常大幅度的下跌，现在并不是一个好的一个进场点。

但是我和大家分享的是，我说更客观的一个视角应该是从估值这个角度去出发。包括我记得当时有人问我有没有可能先杀估值，然后再下EPS，那我说取决于这一次所持续的这个时间，包括取决于市场这个情绪面。

上年的4月份是一个非常典型的——就是2025年的4月份，是一个非常典型的杀估值之后完全没有影响到EPS增速，然后估值马上就出现一个V型的一个反弹。那这一次到现在为止，我认为出现了类似非常相同的情况，非常相同的一个情况。

估值未破新高的深层含义

那怎么去理解上年的10月份估值在28倍，现在估值只有23.4倍？我这边画了一条红线23.4倍，其实差不多是过去三年的一个中位数，没有到非常高的位置，也没有在非常低的位置。

为什么上年的10月份估值在28倍，现在只有23.4倍、23.5倍？估值没有破新高，但是指数却连续破新高，这就说明背后市场对于EPS这个增速非常的乐观。也就是说整个芯片板块的一个反弹并不是因为宏观事件的这个风险没有了，也不是说市场不担心，而是市场对于EPS这个增速、对于财报本身非常的乐观。

所以如果现在有任何的人或者现在我们认为"我现在要选择去做空芯片板块，因为芯片板块非常的贵"，绝对得不出这个结论。除非你认为整个未来——因为forward PE嘛，看的是未来一年这些芯片板块的一个盈利——除非我们认为未来一年这些芯片板块赚不到这个钱，或者现在华尔街是过于预期了、过于乐观了，那我们可以去做这样一个结论。

你单从价格这个角度去看，现在价格突破了新高，所以价格非常的贵，所以我现在去做空？不应该得出这样的一个结论。因为现在从估值这个角度去看，根本就没有去破上年10月份的一个新高，不用谈2024年的一个新高。

YTD视角下的阿尔法市场

其实回过头去看YTD到现在为止的走势，包括大科技其实不强的。大科技到现在为止，我前面看到了大科技YTD只上涨了2%都不到，而标普500你去看上涨了5点几，包括纳指的话上涨了6点几，将近7%不到。

这说明什么？就说明其实YTD到现在为止，仍然是一个板块之间阿尔法的一个选择、阿尔法的一个市场。板块之间芯片板块走很好，上涨了50%，所以把整个纳指走拉的非常的好，然后把标普也拉的非常的好。

即使大科技里边，前期大科技股走势分歧也非常的大。那么在上一周的话，那包括现在盘后的一个走势，英伟达突破新高了。前段时间我说英伟达非常的便宜，还记不记得？英伟达非常的便宜、Meta非常的便宜、微软非常的便宜，然后博通非常的便宜。现在博通也破新高，然后英伟达也破新高，为什么我认为非常的便宜？就是从估值这个角度去出发。

那么所以回到大科技这边，大科技也是一个阿尔法的一个市场。特斯拉YTD仍然是下跌的，微软YTD仍然是下跌的，虽然前段时间已经去到了一个非常便宜的一个位置，但是YTD仍然是下跌的。上一周只有两家YTD只有两家前期大科技股突破了新高，一个是亚马逊，一个就是英伟达。

所以在一个阿尔法的一个市场里边，如果我们拿指数拎出来去做分析的话，就并不是一个非常好的一个视角。这就是为什么在3月份指数只下跌了10%都不到，很多人认为市场根本就不便宜，一个非常重要的一个理由。但其实市场已经非常便宜了，很多人只是拿指数下跌这个幅度去衡量市场这一波的一个DF到底有没有到位。

三、GE：阿尔法市场中的机会

那么这一波反弹了那么多，是不是完全没有机会呢？其实像我前面说，如果是一个阿尔法的一个市场，一定有机会的。因为阿尔法的市场就说明并不是所有的个股都出现了反弹。

油价与GE的负相关

比如说GE就没有出现反弹。为什么油价还在高？这也是很多人不理解的——明明油价还在高，为什么指数能够反弹？因为它是阿尔法的市场，因为整个芯片板块非常的强，所以把科技股带回来，所以把标普也带回来。

我们看到这张图，蓝色这条线是油价，所以油价现在还在一个相对的高位嘛。白色这条线是GE的一个走势。从2月28日战争那一天开始，我特地去看了，非常仔细的我去看了一下，几乎每一天，GE的一个走势和油价的一个走势全部是负相关，只要油价上涨，GE就下跌，只要油价下跌，GE就上涨。

GE基本面与时间延后

那在上一周我分析GE财报的时候，大家聊过他们这一次财报会议了。如果说管理层说，如果不是因为这一次战争的一个事件，我们在第一季度财报的时候就会上调全年的指引，但是因为这个战争的事件，所以我们对于下半年做了一个非常保守的一个指引。

那在这种情况下，所以我们坚持认为我们并不改变我们原本的这个guidance，我们还是target 我们的目标还是上一次给出这个guidance一个上区间。但是在服务这一块的一个需求，它并不会因为比如说下半年在航天这一块——就航空这一块——如果增速放缓，服务这一块的需求放缓，这服务这块的需求放缓，它不是需求消失了，只是需求往后延，有点类似于像疫情也好、2025年的4月份也好。

那么再说的直白一点，就是GE这一块的一个营收和盈利，它并不是赚不到这个钱了，他只是这个赚钱的一个时间点稍微往后延了一些。所以只要我们认为那么这一次战争的一个事件，肯定未来某一个时间点会结束，那油价未来也不会长时间保持在一个非常高的位置。如果我们持有这个观点的话，那只要油价下来，我认为GE的价格会出现反弹。

且更重要的一点是从基本面这个角度去看，从一个比较长期的一个角度去看，GE的一个基本面并不会因为这一次战争事件而改变。

GE的估值定位

从估值这个角度去看，那截止到上周的收盘，GE的forward PE是在36.1倍。36.1倍确实和其他那些个股去做对比的话，并不便宜了，而且算上GE的一个EPS增速的话，它是并不便宜的。

但是我认为GE它在处在一个比较垄断的一个位置，所以它的一个基本面有点类似于像沃尔玛或者Costco，在整个行业里面，它有绝对的一个垄断，所以对于这类的标的，市场愿意给出是一个估值上的一个溢价。那这是我个人的一个看法。

所以从估值这个角度去看，36.1倍我认为现在处在一个相对比较中心的这个位置，那没有前段时间那么高了。下区间我认为绝对的一个底座，现在我认为已经是一个非常好的一个进场点。下区间那个底就是上年2025年4月份的一个30倍。

四、本周财报季：四大科技股集中冲击

OK，然后我们就来看一下本周将要发布财报的公司。42%的公司发布财报，所以本周非常的繁忙。

财报日程

  • 周二盘前：UPS、可口可乐、Corning
  • 周二盘后：Robinhood、Blue Energy（BE，这家公司YTD做的也非常的好）
  • 周三：重头戏——四大科技股，很少以前几乎很少会看到四大科技股全部放在一天去发布财报，都在周三的盘后。然后周三还有周三下午还有美联储的会议。所以还有高通。

高通与苹果

高通也是从上周开始出现反弹。然后市场有一条新闻说了，高通有可能就有可能OpenAI要进入手机行业，他们要做自己的一个手机。所以市场有可能担心，看到苹果其实走的不是非常的好，市场有可能担心OpenAI会对于苹果这一块造成一定程度的一个破坏。

那我个人并不是非常的担心，因为我自己手里持有苹果。我个人并不是非常的担心，因为手机这一块如果真的有这么好做的话，为什么其他的大科技并没有进入？硬件并不是那么简单的。而且随着未来AI的这个落地，对于芯片的这个需求又非常的大，而苹果的自研芯片其实早就为未来AI的这个落地已经有一个铺路了。

应对财报季的心态建议

那关于怎么去应对这一次财报季度，其实很简单。或者你们问我我会怎么做？我现在是满仓的情况下，你们我在上周最后一个仓位，然后加入了苹果之后，我就完全没有动了。你如果问我的话，我完全不会动，完全不会去做任何的操作。因为对于我来说，我满仓的状态下要不就加杠杆，要不就是减仓，但我两者我并不会去做选择。

第一点，有几点我想和大家分享：

第一点：放弃对于财报之后价格的一个走势，放弃对于去赌财报。你要赌我不知道，赌财报，说白了你就是赌——你无论是赌它上涨还是赌它下跌，它和财报本身没有太大的关系。财报好可以上涨，财报好也可以下跌；财报不好可以上涨，也可以下跌，一样的一个道理。

虽然大多数的、上次我和大家聊过，大多数的情况下每一个季度的财报，标普500里边70%以上的公司都是好于市场预期的。那是不是标普500里边70%的公司都一定要出现上涨？当然不是了。所以没有必要去赌财报。

第二点（关于满仓恐慌者）：然后我知道手里现在满仓的人可能比较的恐慌。如果你真的恐慌——因为有人问我，有人问我说我现在手里大科技、满满多的大科技，然后周三全部发布财报，我非常的慌。一旦说出来非常的慌，有这种有这个疑问的话，对于这一类的观众来说，我建议的都是你减一些仓位。

因为有这个问题出来，其实心里已经有自己的答案了，已经有想法了。而且我不希望大家带着非常恐慌的心情去进入这个财报季、去进入任何一家公司的财报。因为一旦有非常恐慌的这个心态，其实代表了其背后就代表就有且仅有一个原因，就是自己的仓位和对于这家公司的一个了解并不匹配。

那在这种情况下你减仓一定是一个正确的决定。如果盘后出现了下跌，那非常好，你已经减仓了。如果盘后出现上涨，你减仓完之后，那你减掉的这一部分仓位，它所导致未来有可能这个盈利，就你减点已经卖飞了嘛，你卖飞这一部分的仓位也不在你的能力范围。这不是我们能力范围能够赚到的，那靠运气，即使赚了这个钱，以后也会在其他标的身上亏回去，一样的一个道理。

核心原则：我们在这个市场里边，无论是交易也好、投资也好，一定只能够赚到自己认知范围里面的钱。靠自己运气赚来的钱，最终一定会靠自己的实力给亏回去，这是一定的。

对四大科技股财报的预判

那回到财报这边，我对于这4家公司的财报我丝毫不担心。

微软：其实对于微软这一就财报，你看一下微软在过去一个季度里面呢，他们的CEO Satya出来的在X上所出现的这个频率非常的高，一直在和市场说我们推出了哪些新品、推出了哪些新品、推出了哪些新功能。你哪时候看到Satya在这段时间里边抛头露面或者上媒体的这个时间那么的频快，那么的勤快？为什么？是因为试图要去解决市场对于软件公司这个担心，特别是对于微软的这个担心。

亚马逊：亚马逊就更不用谈了，Andy是在一个月之前、几周之前嘛，写了一篇致投资者信。那一封信出来之后，亚马逊是一路飙升。但我并不认为这封信就能解决公司的一个基本面，对嘛？这封信只是解决市场的一个情绪面而已。

财报才是答卷：最终无论是软件板块能不能走出来，还是这些大科技的资本开支能不能证明它的资本开支未来是有回报，一定只能反映在财报里边。财报就是答案，财报就是答卷。市场再怎么去乐观，你最后财报不好的话，你仍然是不过关的，仍然该下跌的还是下跌。

我在今年的年初我就说过，我说今年对于这些大科技资本开支非常大的这些大科技，就是要用财报去证明自己。这一年因为在今年的这个财报反映的是25年的资本开支，那为25年的资本开支也不小，所以今年的财报里面都能够看到。

我的期待是这些所有的大科技，他们在25年、24年这个资本开支都应该使得他们在26年EPS增速上调，营收这一块的增速开始出现一个再加速。

  • 微软：微软的Azure，可能我认为这一个季度会转到40%甚至40%以上。那这是也这应该也是市场的一个期待，因为上一个季度是39%，市场不满意嘛。
  • 亚马逊：亚马逊也是一样的，亚马逊这一个季度AWS这个增速我认为起码要到28%以上，甚至于有可能接近30%。
  • Meta和Alphabet：那另外两家公司Meta和谷歌也是一样的，我并不认为这两家大科技也会有任何的问题。

五、陶氏财报：战争对各板块的传导链条

好吧，那最后今天要和大家讲上周五发布财报的陶氏（Dow）这家公司。我认为他们的财报是非常有意义的，因为我看他们的财报，就你如果我们整天花这个心思在地缘政治上，不如去看一下陶氏的财报。

因为陶氏这个财报是陶氏家公司本身是受到这一次战争非常大的一个利好。那你通过他们这个财报可以看到这一次战争所导致些化工品、包括原材料、包括对于油价、包括这些油价的一个上涨，对于底下的这些下游、包括中游这些科技、中游这些公司到底有什么样一些影响，或者这个影响到底会有多长。

陶氏Q1财报数据

先来看一下数据：营收高出市场预期0.6%，这一个季度就是第一季度。第一季度其实对于陶氏这个影响并不大。然后EPS这一块52%，我和大家聊过了，我说如果说它营收这一块的增速仅仅连1%都不到，EPS能够有那么大的这个正面的一个surprise，说明它是一次性的一个影响。

所以这一块就EPS这一个增速、EPS这一个超出市场预测这个幅度没有必要去过渡这个解读。你去看一下它那个EBITDA 9.21%，你就知道了。OK，营收增速超出市场0.62，那just a EBITDA 从盈利这个角度去看高出市场10%不到，合理。因为战争是从2月底开始，然后持续整个3月份，那其实这也是为什么对于陶氏影响并没有第一季度的影响并没有那么大。

二季度接下去三个季度的影响可就大了。

陶氏的行业周期

你看一下，其实陶氏在过去三年一直走的不好，它一直在走一个市场——对于这些原材料就整个原材料这边也是有个周期在那边——一直在走一个周期在变得越来越糟糕。然后因为这一次战争的一个事件，你看一下：

  • 23年：是一个非常大的一个负增长，和22年去做对比 -27、-24、-10
  • 24-25年：在走一个行业的一个周期，也是同样一个行业周期的一个负增长
  • 接下去三个季度（Q2-Q4）：20%、21%和20%
  • 27年Q1：同比增速10%
  • 27年Q2：又是一个负增长
  • 27年Q3、Q4：负增长

所以现在市场pricing了什么？现在市场pricing了——因为这次战争使得陶氏未来一年包括27年的第一季度有这种正面增长，然后到27年的第二季度、第三、第四季度又是负增长。也就是市场认为这一次战争的一个影响，对于陶氏这一类的化工品的原料的这些公司来说，将会有一年的这个利好，然后一年之后可能再次回到整个世界对于这一块经周期的一个负面的一个增长。

乙烯/聚乙烯的全球产能格局

我们知道乙烯和聚乙烯是全球很多制造业的一个底层最需要的一个原材料。那从这一次的战争对于乙烯这一块——乙烯包括聚乙烯——的一个影响是非常的大。

但是可能让市场可能让大家比较感到意外的是其实对于美国是有利好。首先我们从产能这个角度去看：

  • 欧洲产能：15%
  • 亚洲产能：45%
  • 中东产能：15%
  • 美国产能：25%

对于右边的三个地区——欧洲、中东和亚洲、亚太这一块基本上都是负面的影响，少有有一些中性的影响。对于美国来说，几乎全部是正面的影响，因为美国这边会增加他的出口。

陶氏他们认为，这一次所持续的时间，即使在战争结束了以后，也会有非常长的一个延续的时间。他们认为总共对于化工产品的一个影响的一个时长在12至18个月左右。

各板块影响传导

最后我们来看一下，那这些原材料价格的上涨、石油产品的一些上涨、石油化工产品的一些上涨，对于哪些板块有些利好，哪些板块有些利空。

直接利好板块：肯定是这些生产商、包括天然气的生产商。

中性板块：对于炼油厂这些公司来说，它是一个混合的，它是中性的。因为炼油厂你原油的价格上涨，你炼油厂取决于你能不能把这一个价格传递到你的下游去，炼油厂你的成本上升了嘛，那如果你可以把你的价格传递到下游去的话，炼油厂来说它是一个中性，你也可以获得更高的这个利润。

包装公司（中间商位置）：对于包装公司来说，也是处在一个中间商的一个位置。他们的树脂成本上升百分之将近要50%。那包装公司能不能把这个成本的一个上升转移到他们的客户身上？那包装公司是有一定的定价权在那边的，所以对于包装公司来说，短期可能需要承压，但是长期的话他们有一个传导能、价格的一个传导能力。

负面影响板块：那最后负面影响的一些板块其实很多很多，就基本上我们生活里面的点点滴滴都受到这些化工产品的影响。只要这个底层的一个原材料的一个价格上涨，几乎所有的价格全部会出现上涨。 - 食品饮料：对吧，你的瓶装水、碳酸饮料、牛奶、植物奶，这个瓶装本身这个容器本身它都是来自于这些化工原料，所以价格全部上涨。 - 汽车OEM：福特、通用、Stellantis，然后特斯拉都会受到影响。特斯拉的影响相对会较小一些，因为特斯拉的垂直整合做的相对比较好。 - 医疗器材、建筑行业：这些都会受到影响。

给投资者的思考框架

那这些东西对于我们投资来说有什么影响？我给大家分享一个思考的一个方式，就是一个切入的一个角度吧。

因为基本上对于很多公司都会影响——不看对于亚马逊、UPS、物流电商都会影响，因为你成本上升了，你单价上升了，那可能对于亚马逊这块的需求也会受到影响。

所以从我们个人角度去出发，思考一下自己手里只有这些个股有没有可能成本上升。这个成本上升，那从公司这个角度来说，能不能把这个成本的上升传递到消费者身上？如果可以的话呢，这家公司就有这个定价权在那边。

如果有定价权的话，那这一次因为我们接下去会看到这一次价格、原材料价格的这个上涨、化工材料的这个上涨，还没有传递到最终的一个消费者的手里，所以接下去我们是会看到的。

所以可能需要长一个心眼，就是我们所持有的这些公司有没有可能一个季度的财报不是非常的好，然后在财报会议里面就EPS增速有可能不是非常的好，然后在财报会里面管理层有聊到——"哦，因为在3月份的那场战争，然后持续到4月份、5月份，导致化工原材料价格的一个上涨、石油化工品的这些上涨、原材料这些上涨，导致我们现在的成本上升，然后没有办法把这个价格转移到消费者身上，所以导致我们的盈利出现了一定程度的影响"。

那么这是我们对于每一家公司都需要去思考的一个问题，因为我认为现在这个价格的一个传导还没有反映到财报里边。

六、对通胀与美联储的影响

那最后对于通胀的这个影响，那对于通胀的影响有可能会影响到美联储的决策，美联储的决策有可能影响到整体市场这个PE。

通胀的两面性

对于通胀的影响是两面性的。因为你原材料价格的这个上涨，你没办法影响到消费、没办法影响到服务通胀这一块。那现在整个美国这边的通胀，服务通胀起码还是在一个往越来越低的一个方向去走，所以是一个非常好的一个方向。

但是商品通胀这一块确实因为上一年的关税，然后持续保持了一个高位。所以美联储的一个期待是到了今年的下半年，特别是今年的9月份、10月份，关税通胀所导致一次性的这个通胀的影响将会消失。

但是不好意思，可能这一次因为战争的这个影响，会使得在今年的下半年，美联储对于商品通胀这一块的一个期待——商品通胀下跌这一块的期待——可能会失望。所以通胀这一块现在暂时还有一个比较明确的一个不确定性在那边。

对降息节奏的判断

但是我个人认为我个人现在暂时——如果有未来有更多的信息进来，那我可能会改变这个判断——但是到现在我并不改变我这个判断，我并不认为：

首先第一点：先排除美联储加息。美联储绝对不可能加息。新任美联储主席上来之后，他更不会加息。那么排除了加息的这个选项之后，那无非就是每一次的美联储会无非就是保持不变或者降息。

暂时来说，即使接下去在战争这个原材料价格的一个上涨，有可能会使得商品通胀还是保持在一个相对的高位或者要和上年同期制作对比的话没有出现非常明显的这个下跌，我的base case仍然是9月份进行第一次这个降息。

那接下去看吧，接下去随着每一组通胀数据出来，我们去看。

提醒不要追高

我们想一下，如果现在市场情绪真的非常的好，然后接下去有一组通胀数据出来出乎市场的意外非常的高，那市场该下跌还是下跌。所以市场还是会回调的，也没有必要因为最近这段时间美国的反弹非常的厉害然后自己非常的上头了FOMO。

那这是一个比较大的一个视角。从一个比较小的一个视角，从下到上这个角度去触发，那我们就只需要关注投资这家公司他的财报是不是好就可以了。

七、Seeking Alpha量化选股推荐

最后今天要和大家聊的是Seeking Alpha，他们这个Alpha Picks量化的一个选股策略。这个量化的选股策略YTD到现在为止回报将近21%，非常的好，跑出了标普的4%。那我这是上周的一个数据，到现在为止这一个应该他们的这个投资组合应该是24%、25%了，因为里面有很多和芯片相关的一些个股。

我这边挑了一些他们在4月份选的一些标的，4月份和3月份分别都是做的不错的，我不能显示他的一个标的一个名字，因为合规的一个原因。然后我这边还挑了一些他们在上年年底之后选的一些标的，其中包括了美光，他们就是在上年的10月15日所选入的，那到现在为止这个上涨的幅度就不止140%了，140%应该要到将近了200了。

那么大家如果感兴趣的话，那现在Alpha Picks他们的这个量化选股策略正在打折，现在有一个优惠： - 原价是499美金一年，现在是10% off。 - 然后同时他们还有一个Seeking Alpha的一个Premium这一档会员和订阅，那档会员和订阅现在也是10%的折扣，并且还有7天的一个免费试用。 - 然后如果Premium这一档包括Alpha Picks量化选股都喜欢的话，还会有一个combo的一个价格，这个折扣的优惠会更大一些。

那我把这三个订阅的连接都会放在置顶的留言里面，大家感兴趣的话可以在置顶的留言里面找到这个连接。

好吧，那今天这期内容就到这边，我们下期见。
