But what are the most interesting bottlenecks to you on the supply side?

Dylan： Supply chains are usually very fast to react. One unique thing is that our supply chains now are more complex than ever, and the things we're building are more complex than ever, and therefore the lead times are longer. It's not like we haven't seen 18-month-long lead times in other industries. It's just building incremental supply didn't take years.

瓶颈 1：Memory（DRAM / NAND）

This is the case with memory. Memory can only grow capacity low double-digit percentages a year — 20s, 30% a year. Even less for NAND, a little bit higher for DRAM. Even though the demand signal was very strong at the end of 2025, the memory companies immediately sort of started reacting. None of that incremental capacity really gets here until the second — that they've decided to do in addition to the typical 20 to 30%. They can stretch a little bit, but really the true incremental supply doesn't come till '28, which is a very unique thing. Even if they wanted to build as fast as possible, it doesn't come till '28 — early late '27 at best.

The result is memory prices have gone through the roof. And guess what? They're going to double and triple again. At least on DRAM especially, people are like, "Oh, the memory story is overplayed. Everyone gets it." And it's like, "No, no, no. You don't get it. DRAM will double or triple from here still." Because that's how much capacity is required, and they have to steal capacity from somewhere else. And the only way to steal capacity from somewhere else in a capitalist economy is demand destruction via higher pricing. We're not rationing stuff here. So ultimately, that's what's going to happen. And so margins continue to go up.

瓶颈 2：Logic (TSMC)

I think Logic also has humongous capacity problems. TSMC just had their earnings. They keep upping capex. Ultimately, it takes them quite some time to build fabs. They're trying to do everything they can to squeeze every little output out of every fab that they have. But ultimately, they're not raising prices fast because they're good people. It seems like single-digit price increases instead of triple-digit price increases like the memory guys have had.

So ultimately you have this market where, yeah, TSMC is a great company, but are they actually going to extract all the value?

瓶颈 3：WFE 半导体设备

I mentioned things like copper foil, glass fibers for PCBs, lasers. These are things that are well-understood and niche supply chains, but they're very, very tight. Ultimately, upstream, the semiconductor wafer fabrication equipment supply chain is one that — I still think is — it's gone up a lot, but it's still very underappreciated.

TSMC capex this year they say $56. We've had $57.4 billion since January. We may up it slightly more just because we see some ways that they can get incremental capex. But what people aren't focusing on is: what does that mean next year, and what does that mean the year after? It turns out 3 years from now TSMC is going to spend $100 billion on capex. Maybe two years from now, right? Maybe '28. Sincerely, they may spend $100 billion on capex in 2028. And people just can't fathom that.

But what does that mean for their downstream supply chains? Companies like Lam Research or Applied Materials or ASML, or their further downstream supply chains like MKSI and all these other companies — the tail whip just gets whipped harder and harder and harder. Ultimately, that's a shortage. If TSMC wants to spend $100 billion in 2028, which is a real possibility — I think people would think that's insane, but that's a real real possibility.

十三、GPU 之外：CPU 与 FPGA 的隐形需求

主持人： What about other parts of the chip ecosystem where GPUs have been completely dominant? What about like CPUs or ASICs, or things that start to pop out as both opportunities and bottlenecks beyond just Nvidia's GPU dominance?

Dylan： ASICs are obviously taking off, but I'll sort of pivot away from AI chips to talk about these other things. There's a project we did on FPGAs, and there turns out there's 120 FPGAs per next-generation AI rack. And then all the FPGA names —

CPU-wise, all these reinforcement learning environments, plus all the slop code you and I are generating that is now running on some Vercel instance, or some AWS instance, or some bucket that we've spun up — all of that requires CPU. And so CPUs are completely sold out and demand is skyrocketing there.

主持人： Help people understand the role that CPU plays in everything.

Dylan： Yeah. So there's two main reasons why you need tons of CPUs.

原因 1：RL 环境运行在 CPU 上

One is when you're doing reinforcement learning, the CPU is very critical to that. Before, you would throw all the internet's data into the model, train it, it spits some stuff out. Now you train — you put all the internet data into the model, then you put it in this environment. This environment is like, "Hey model, try this out," and it tries stuff out. It tries a bunch of different things, and in the end there is an environment which scores whether or not what it tried out is successful, and it grades it.

These environments can be anything. It can be "check if the text was outputted in the right way" — structured outputs. Very simple stuff. Very complex stuff. People are starting to get into very complex things: "Hey, I want you to open this file, change it, edit it, update it, submit it to this website. I want you to open up this physics simulation from Siemens and edit this CAD model." The environments can get more and more complex, and those environments run on CPUs. They don't run on GPUs. They don't run on ASICs.

The ASICs run the model that takes the input data from the environment, runs it through the model. The model creates outputs of various different trajectories — ways that it thinks it could solve it in different instances. Those trajectories are graded/scored, and the ones that are successful you train on, and you update, and you reiterate and iterate and iterate. So CPUs are very useful for that.

原因 2：生产环境部署

Once you have these great models and you're deploying them, those models are generating code, generating useful output. That useful output doesn't go from a GPU straight to the human brain — it goes from a GPU or an ASIC through to a deployed app that you're deploying somewhere that actually just runs on CPUs. So that's another area where there's a lot of demand, and things are sold out in a large way.

十四、测量的盲区：Tokonomics 还无法量化

主持人： As you continue to assess and try to be the world's best-informed person on both the trajectory of supply and demand, what are things that you wish you knew to make that understanding that you don't know?

Dylan： I think the hardest area for us — and for everyone — is understanding tokonomics — economics of tokens. I think we have a tremendously good insight into how much it costs to run infrastructure, what the cost of tokens are, what the cost of models are, what the margins of these labs are. But the usage and adoption is what's really difficult to model continuously.

We had crazy estimates for January. For February, Anthropic smashed them. How do we calibrate this model? What are the data sources for this? February, we had crazy assumptions for March, and then they smashed them. And everyone sees the number of $10 billion and they're like, "What the — how do they add $10 billion in revenue? Who is using all these tokens? Why are they using them? What are they building with them?"

And then more importantly, with what they're building with these tokens, how is that actually diffusing into the economy? And what value is that generating? Because it's not really something that you can capture in any GDP statistic. All of the value of the tokens that I use get transformed into better information, which I then sell at a discount to what people used to sell information for. And therefore that information is now making its way throughout the economy, and people are making better investment decisions, or better competitive decisions if they're a semi company or data center company or hyperscaler.

How much — what is the value of this, and what has that done to the economy? It's clearly by every subjective metric amazing. But where is the phantom GDP? What is the phantom GDP? How do we track the real economic impact? Because the GDP metrics are not accurate. If you were to say "what is the GDP that Dylan Patel is making" — it's tiny compared to what the value that I think is being created.

Ultimately, what is the value being created by these tokens? Not on a basis of just simple — what is the knock-on effect of all the things that these things are doing? I think that's the real question and challenge that's hard to measure. I think we've got a tremendous reading on the supply side of things. I think we've got a tremendous reading on even a lot of the demand side signals. But it's: what is the value these tokens are generating? That's hard to quantify and measure.

十五、三个月后的预测：AI 将引发大规模抗议

主持人： I hope we get a chance to do this every 3 months, because this changes so quickly. What do you think is going to happen next? When I come back 3 months from now and we're in San Francisco together again, what do you expect?

Dylan： Large-scale protests.

主持人： Really?

Dylan： Yeah. I think there will be a large-scale protest against Anthropic and OpenAI.

主持人： Expand on that a little more.

Dylan： People hate AI. AI is less popular than ICE, less popular than politicians. Confused how Pew surveyed this, but apparently AI is less popular than politicians. With Anthropic adding so much revenue, that's going to start causing business changes downstream. People are going to get more and more scared of AI. They'll start blaming more and more of their own problems — things that are global, have been deep-seated problems for a long time. Those will bubble up and be blamed on AI.

Probably some politician or some social media influencer will be able to start taking and weaponizing AI against people. You look at the comments of news articles where Sam Altman had a Molotov cocktail thrown in his house twice in like two weeks — they're like, people are cheering it on. And this is just the beginning. So I think we'll see large-scale protests against AI in three months.

对策：品牌与叙事的重构

主持人： What is the counterweight to that? How should the AI industry head that off?

Dylan： 1. First of all, Sam Altman and Dario have to stop getting on interviews. They're so uncharismatic. I don't know what they're doing. Every interview they do is like, "Wow, normal people are going to hate you even more." Sam being on Tucker Carlson probably made all Republicans hate OpenAI. And same with Dario. They just have no charisma. 2. They need to start showing uplifting things that can be done with AI. 3. They need to stop talking about how the capabilities are going to change the whole world constantly. Because then people are going to get fear of that capability, because they have no connection.

主持人： They don't know how to use it.

Dylan： Yeah. There's no connection to it either. The average person doesn't know an Anthropic employee. The average person doesn't know an OpenAI employee. The average person doesn't know who these people are, what their goals are. They just view them as this sneaky cabal of 5,000 people at this company that are going to change the world and automate all the jobs and destroy society. That's what they view it as. And as people who are funding the building of all these data centers and power plants that are going to pollute the world. They don't quite understand what's happening.

They have to stop talking about the future thing that's going to happen and only talk about present — how uplifting AI is. I think it's a huge reorg and rebranding that needs to be done.

主持人： I love doing this with you. Thanks for your time.

Dylan： Awesome. Thanks.

[结尾广告：Ramp / Vanta / Ridgeline / Rogo / WorkOS]

Your finance team isn't losing money on big mistakes... Try it at ramp.com/invest.

As your business grows, Vanta scales with you... Learn more at vanta.com/invest.

Ridgeline is redefining asset management technology as a true partner... Visit ridgelineapps.com to see what they can unlock for your firm.

Every investment firm is unique, and generic AI doesn't understand your process. Rogo does... Check them out at rogo.ai/invest.

The best AI and software companies from OpenAI to Cursor to Perplexity use WorkOS to become enterprise ready overnight, not in months. Visit workos.com to skip the unglamorous infrastructure work and focus on your product.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一个让人意外的观察：Dylan 不是在描述 AI 革命，他是在描述一场"token 封建主义"的复辟
表面上这是关于供需的技术访谈，但如果你仔细听，会发现一个惊人的结构性真相正在浮现：**我们正在从"软件的民主化时代"退回到"计算的贵族制时代"**。
过去 20 年互联网的故事是"边际成本趋近于零"——软件一旦写出来，所有人平等地使用。而 Dylan 描述的世界恰恰相反： - Mythos 只给"愿意下跪求情"的人 - Ken Griffin 可以用 $10B 买断首发 token 供应 - "你必须有一个 Anthropic 的销售代表"才能拿到真正的 rate limit - 模型将"越来越少地广泛部署"
这不是 SaaS。这是**中世纪的行会制度**——技术的访问权重新与社会关系、资本和"被恩赐"的身份绑定。Dylan 自己在访谈里无意识地用了一个极具启示性的词："**permanent underclass（永久下层阶级）**"。这不是科技词汇，这是阶级分析的词汇。
## 跨领域连接：这其实是"石油诅咒"在数字世界的镜像，但反过来了
看一个意想不到的类比——**

item.title: [No Priors: AI, Machine Learning, Tech, & Startups] SAP: Bringing the ‘Operating System’ of a Company into the AI Era with CTO Philipp Herzig
item.dc:creator: No Priors: AI, Machine Learning, Tech, & Startups
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的发现：SAP 正在把"测试驱动开发"从坟墓里挖出来——但驱动者不是程序员，而是 AI

Philipp 半开玩笑地提到那个谷歌工程师"5 点下班是因为先写测试"的旧故事，然后承认"包括我在内，没人真的那么干"。这是一个被业界默默埋葬了 20 年的实践。

但真正的洞察在反面：TDD 当年失败不是因为它错了，而是因为人类的大脑本能地讨厌"先描述边界，再创造内容"——我们更喜欢边写边发现。而 LLM 恰好相反：它最讨厌的就是"自己摸索意图"，最擅长的就是"按已知边界生成内容"。所以 agentic coding 不是让 TDD 复活，而是第一次让 TDD 成为生产力的最优解——人类终于被迫做了那个一直该做却做不到的事。

这意味着一个更深的预言：未来"高级工程师"的核心技能将不是"会写代码"，而是"会写 eval"。代码生成会被压成像编译一样的基础设施层；真正昂贵的、需要品味的、需要资深判断的——是定义"什么算对"。

二、跨领域连接：SAP 的"知识图谱 + RPT-1"≈ 物流业的"霍尔木兹海峡焦虑"≈ 蛋白质折叠

Philipp 不经意地把"霍尔木兹海峡封锁"作为一个供应链查询的例子抛出，而后又花了大段时间解释为什么 LLM 永远做不好预测——必须用他们自研的 RPT-1（关系型预训练 Transformer）。

把这两件事连起来你会看到一个隐藏命题：企业的核心数据问题，本质上和 AlphaFold 解决的是同一类问题。

  • 蛋白质折叠：从一维序列预测三维结构（关系预测）
  • 表格预测：从离散字段预测业务结果（关系预测）
  • 供应链重规划：从已知节点预测扰动传播（关系预测）

LLM 在文本上的胜利让所有人误以为"通用智能 = 序列建模"。但 SAP 用 10 万张表训练出的发现恰恰相反：真正的企业智能是关系建模，不是序列建模。这也是为什么 OpenAI/Anthropic 进入企业核心系统会比想象中艰难——他们的架构假设就是错的。

惊喜推论：未来最值钱的 AI 公司可能不是基础模型公司，而是"领域关系图谱 + 小模型"的公司——SAP 的知识图谱事实上已经是一个全球最大的、被严重低估的"商业 DNA 数据库"。

三、隐藏真相：从"软件即服务"到"服务即软件"——这不是修辞，这是一次商业物理的相变

Philipp 用一句话带过：定价模型正在从 seat-based → consumption-based → outcome-based 迁移。听起来只是商务条款的变化，但背后是一场物理性质的相变。

过去 现在 内核差异
软件 = 杠杆放大人的智能 软件 = 自己有智能 用户从"操作者"变成"监工"
价格 ∝ 用户数 价格 ∝ 产出量 软件第一次承担经营风险
UI 教人怎么做事 UI 由 AI 即时生成 界面消失，意图浮现

最反常的洞察：当软件按"成果"计费时，软件公司就变成了某种意义上的金融衍生品发行方——SAP 在为客户的业务结果"做市"。这要求他们必须比客户自己更懂客户的业务波动率。

这也解释了一个看似不相关的细节：为什么 SAP 要花两年时间研究 RPT-1 做表格预测？因为没有概率预测能力的公司不可能定价 outcome-based 合同。这是同一个战略的两面：要卖结果，先得能算出结果的概率分布。

更进一步：这是企业软件历史上第一次，技术能力和商业模式之间的相变是耦合的——不是技术先行、商业跟进，而是必须同步翻越同一座山。这是为什么 Philipp 说很多创业者"低估了规模的挑战"——10 个文档的 RAG 谁都能做，但要让一个德国员工和美国员工的差旅政策回答都正确，你需要同时跨过"工程规模"和"商业模式重构"两座山。

📝 转录文本

开场：一个时代的终结

"There's a lot of exciting opportunities and new things you can build that we only dreamed of in the last—I don't know—20 years, at least since I'm a developer. We are serving some of the largest customers. They have a lot of heritage. They have a lot of complex landscape. They can reduce like 30% of their efforts right to get to the outcome faster, which of course then directly reduces the cost.

The time is clearly over where you design software that requires the intelligence to sit in front of the computer. If you look at classical software, what did you do? You design a user interface to teach a human how they get their tasks done by clicking through the UI. Essentially, this is over."

主持人引入

Host: Hey listeners, welcome back to No Priors. Today I'm here with Philip Herzig, the CTO of SAP, the enterprise juggernaut. We talk about their AI strategy, why SAP has endured and thrived through several technology transitions, why entrepreneurs are underestimating the challenges of scale, why AI is a business model transition—not just a technology transition—why he thinks that LLMs are not enough for predictive analytics, and even about the traveling salesman problem in the real world and the Strait of Hormuz.

Welcome, Philip.

Philip: Yeah, it's a pleasure to be here. Thank you.

一、SAP 是什么：企业的"操作系统"

Host: Everybody knows the name SAP, but I do think that for lots of engineers or people who aren't close to the system in a larger enterprise, they don't really know the breadth and function of the platform. Can you just describe what you guys do for customers?

Philip: Oh, absolutely. SAP is the market leader in enterprise software applications and platforms—400,000 enterprise customers. Usually I describe it as running their finance and HR, supply chain, manufacturing execution, logistics, warehouse management, and then of course everything on the customer side: sales, services, commerce, procurement, you name it—end to end.

We always say we have the broadest portfolio in terms of running the business end to end. This is where SAP started: giving real-time insight. I really describe this as: it's not just software in itself, it's kind of the operating system of a company. From order-to-cash, from source-to-pay, end to end—managed for companies around the entire globe.

二、为什么 SAP 能穿越多个技术周期？

Host: I definitely want to talk about AI, LLMs, some of the stuff that you guys are doing internally and then around predictive models as well. But just because the macro backdrop is on everyone's mind, both from a technology and an economic perspective—I want to talk about SAP's position in the market a little bit.

SAP has stood the test of time through multiple technology and market cycles. As an early-stage venture capitalist, I'm kind of on the other side of this, where the narrative is: when you have internet and cloud and mobile and AI and social, you have an opportunity for new players. SAP, even today, is, I believe, the largest market-cap enterprise software vendor versus the last generation of the new guard, like the Salesforces of the world.

How did you do it? What makes it so durable?

Philip: What makes it so durable? It's happening a little bit the same way also when we talk about the SaaS-debt narrative or the SaaS apocalypse. I have the feeling like in this market last year AI was in a big bubble and everybody was kind of doing that—"no, it's not"—and "SaaS is dead," and so on and so forth.

Look, the reality is now, of course, with the costs of building being so low, specifically with agentic coding and all these latest powerful models—something has always prevailed over the years. Even when SAP was founded in 1972, a long time ago—why was it started? Because actually in the 70s, when the founders of SAP worked still at IBM, what did they do? They went to each customer and they implemented the finance system again and again and again and again. And then they said: "Hey, this makes no sense, because the economics—it doesn't scale. You can do this, but you can only add so much value in any given time. And by the way, we are basically programming the system very similar—of course there's always a little bit that is specific then to the customer."

This was the idea where the notion of standard software was born essentially. And then of course that stood the test of time, because there are simply things and companies that need to get managed end to end. That has also transformed throughout the years—you've mentioned that—first from the mainframe to client-server, then to the internet, then mobile, and now of course AI. The software has changed and evolved all along with these technologies.

What hasn't changed is what customers are seeking for: which is outcomes—outcomes and return on their investment in order to get the things done. AI is an amazing technology that helps to get more things done in the enterprise. That is actually what SAP is standing for.

What we are really doing—given the breadth of the portfolio and the customers—is to help customers achieve more by deeply embedding AI agents and transforming the user interface, and so on and so forth, to help them get more done in whichever industry that they are working in. We believe that still can continue, because this is exactly what we're also seeing right now. There's still tremendous progress, but we also see that AI adoption in the enterprise is still not where we want to see it.

There's this Gartner curve where you say there's this AI innovation race and then there's this AI outcome race, and the gap almost increases versus getting narrow. That is what we are really focused on: that customers get the outcome from AI to achieve more, given the foundation we have. But simultaneously, we are kind of re-engineering the entire system with the help of AI in a totally new way.

三、CTO 的优先级：三层重构

Host: As CTO of SAP, you have a very broad purview. It also includes the AI strategy piece of it, internal and for your customers. What do you think of as your own top priorities for the organization, and where is SAP on this re-engineering or reimagination journey?

Philip: We are in the meantime all-in on AI. Everybody in the company is using agentic coding because that's an amazing productivity boost that our developers have, no matter in which programming language they are building the software for the customers.

But of course it's also really focusing on customer outcomes. We've seen this for example early in the early days now with consulting—we built this thing called Joule for Consulting, which is phenomenal because it was one of our fastest-growing AI products. What this actually helps is to help the consultants in an SAP project or in a complex landscape. We are serving some of the largest customers; they have a lot of heritage, a lot of complex landscape. To help them actually to move into the cloud, to adopt the latest AI capabilities. With Joule for Consultant, they can reduce like 30% of their efforts to get to the outcome faster, which of course then directly reduces the cost—not just the time, but also the costs that are necessary in order to get to the latest software.

We've seen this also with Concur, where now our travel-booking agents or expense agents are alive. There are many of these outcomes that we are designing.

三层重构：UI、流程、数据

But from a CTO perspective, in my mind it's three things that are really massively changing. The metaphor is a little bit like when we moved from on-prem to the cloud. Originally everybody thought, "Hey, we just take the on-prem software we already built, we put it on the internet, call it cloud." But then over a certain period of time, people realized: "Oh, what does CI/CD actually mean? You can deploy daily or multiple times per day." And then you realize: "Hmm, we always had multi-tenancy in the on-prem software already, but in the cloud you have to learn how to scale it up and down." All of a sudden the software kind of got re-engineered to really build real SaaS software with all the concepts in cloud computing. With AI, the same is happening—on three levels.

Level 1：UI 层——从静态界面到生成式 UI

It happens on the UI side. The time is clearly over where you design dumb software that requires the intelligence to sit in front of the computer. If you look at classical software, what did you do? You designed a user interface, trying—hopefully you did some user research—to figure out the easiest or most intuitive way to teach a human how they get their task done by clicking through the UI. Essentially, this is over.

We now call this generative UI. The UIs get dynamically generated. If you have analytical questions, for example, or if you want to do your deep research—not just the deep research you find on Perplexity or the usual chatbots, but deeply rooted: let's say tariffs are being introduced, or new taxes, or the Strait of Hormuz—what does this mean for my supply chain? You can analyze this in conjunction with your SAP data.

There's a lot of exciting opportunities and new things you can build that we only dreamed of in the last 20 years, at least since I'm a developer. The system becomes much more multimodal, much more proactive—because it can run overnight, and only when you wake up in the morning it tells you, "Hey Sarah, have you looked at this? Here's a problem on the sales side. Maybe the order entry is going down. You should do something. Here are some recommendations already for this." Or "Here's a problem in the supply chain"—because if you're an oil and gas customer, obviously you want to know what are my options, you need to replan. All these things become super important for customers, and that changes the UI.

Level 2：流程层——结构化与非结构化的融合

Then the second one is of course the business processes. Like an order-to-cash—in the past, of course, it has variances, but it was a rather rigid process, like the standard operating procedure of a company. But now with these agents, we can blend the structured and the unstructured world more seamlessly to actually get more work done.

So this whole move from software-as-a-service to—some call it—service-as-a-software or outcome-as-a-service: that is what these agents are building for us.

Level 3：数据层——全球统一的语义视图

And then below that you have the whole data layer. SAP has a lot of super-valuable data for a company—all your general ledger and your invoices and your warehouse and inventory information, etc. But of course you now want to combine this with the plethora of other data, in order to build this one harmonized semantical view. Because we always say: AI is only as powerful as the data is. That is exactly what we are also doing and transforming on the data side—to help our customers benefit from a globally harmonized data model to fuel the AI.

四、最大的工程挑战：规模

Host: What is the biggest engineering or technical challenge for you guys when you look at these three bodies of work, or anything else that you're doing at SAP?

Philip: The biggest challenge, quite frankly, is actually not the AI so much—but it's actually teaching the AI to do the right thing at scale.

Two years ago, everybody built a RAG service. You could easily blow off everybody's—the CEO's—socks: "Look how easy it is to build a chatbot on 10 documents." But SAP and these large customers always have a problem of scale. What do you do now with 100 documents? Well, becomes a little harder. Thousand documents? Becomes an engineering challenge.

And now if you go into Joule, you are Sarah, you're maybe an SAP US employee. If you ask a question, of course, for a travel policy, you expect a very different answer than me as a German employee would get. So you now need to connect this actually with your master data: where are you located, in which country are you under, which payroll, which taxes apply to you, and so on. All of a sudden, it becomes a very, very tricky problem.

Same with MCP. Last year, oh, everybody could build an MCP server—super simple to hook up your MCP server and do amazing things with it. But for 10 APIs, not an issue. 100? You get already context bloat and all these challenges. But we have 20,000 APIs! It becomes this problem of scale—and doing this really end-to-end for the customer. Because what we also built is really an integrated experience across: you can ask your finance question, your HR question, your supply chain, and you can correlate that. This is the biggest challenge: to bring that together and design it for the right outcome.

五、Eval 的回归：测试驱动开发的复活

Host: And another interesting thing from my perspective—you had recently this other podcast, I think with Andrej. The most important thing from a development perspective is actually that people start writing their evals.

Philip: I was on this tour for a very long time. The problem is: why does agentic coding work so well, Sarah? Because you can verify by the outcome. You can either say, "Hey, is the program compiling?" or "These are your unit tests—does it work?" Combined with a little bit of taste and a lot of hard engineering work, Anthropic and OpenAI built these phenomenal code-generation models.

The problem is, if you now want to build a reliable outcome in finance and so on, you need the data that says: "Hey, with this input, that's the output," in order so that the coding agent can validate that and assert that against the reliable outcome. That's something where there's a mindset shift in terms of how you describe the right boundary conditions to your coding agent—the harness. All the boundary conditions need to be true from a security perspective and from the data privacy perspective, all the code qualities, because you also still want to maintain that code on day two and day three and day four—not just get the first version vibe-coded.

And then of course these evals tell you: "Hey, this agent is actually doing what it's supposed to be doing in a variety of ways."

TDD 的回归

Do you still—and you sometimes need to laugh—do you still remember when I was a computer-science student, where the Google guys came in in a lecture and they said: "Hey, I can go home at 5 p.m. because I wrote my tests"? And of course this was—you remember that? Like test-first or test-driven development.

Host: Yeah, it's coming back.

Philip: It's coming back. Exactly. The reality is nobody did it. At least I never did it, because (a) it was so much more fun to write the code first, and (b) usually the product manager gave you a very messy requirement; it was very hard for you to write the test actually first. So while you wrote the code, you kind of iteratively discovered how the system would actually behave.

Right now, the behavior and the writing of the code is so much automated—because now you can write almost software completely on its own. But of course, now you need to describe the right outcome, what you want from this thing. So that changes very much how the developers also now need to work, specifically now that the models have stepped change since December last year.

六、企业 Agent 是否能像代码生成那样指数级提升？

Host: It's really hard, or I think it's not obvious, how to picture if there's a version of agents—and models powering those agents—in enterprise systems like SAP getting better in a compounding way the way they have in generic code generation. Do you think it's possible in terms of verifiability or the ability to go understand and evaluate against that intent? Because it's not obviously verifiable, as you pointed out. Do you think it can be?

Philip: That's exactly the point. The starting condition is great. I think in terms of two lanes:

Lane 1：System of Record——天然可验证

The first lane is, of course, you have the system of record today. You know exactly in the system: "Hey, given this or that instruction, what is the outcome?" Because you can see it in the database. And then you can construct: "Hey, if the order-to-cash process runs like this, then you need to expect that the cash, the accounts receivable, needs to come in this way, with the following taxes," and so on and so forth. So that gives you verifiability.

Lane 2：部落知识与 Agent Mining

Now the challenge, of course, is that is never enough. If you just look into the system of record today, that data is insufficient for this grand vision that everybody has—that it becomes this autonomous enterprise, or that the agency of these agents is increasing over time.

At the beginning, the agents come back to you—some people call this "human-in-the-loop"—they need to come back to you, also still with Claude Code or Codex, and still ask you some clarifying questions: "Hey, I could go this way or that way." And what you want to design for is that you start to capture more of that context. I always call this the tribal knowledge—the stuff that is not in the system, stored somewhere, that just lives in people's heads, or maybe in Slack channels, or Teams channels, or maybe it was just a discussion on the phone. So how can you drive a decision from that?

The agent needs to come back, ask you for input. Now you want to store that. In the past we called this process mining; now we call it agent mining. Because you record all these decision traces, these contexts of what the users are entering into the system. And then you can either use it to say: "Hey, wait a minute, this is actually an anomaly. Folks in—I don't know—UK from our company, or the folks in Australia, shouldn't do this, because the standard operating procedure is this." Or you say: "Oh, that's actually a very good improvement." And then you can elevate this to be the new standard operating procedure—maybe not just for Australia, but maybe for the rest of the world or more countries—to run your company more efficiently. Because now you learn something about how the organization behaves.

It can go two ways: it could be either good, or it could be a bad thing, and then you maybe want to streamline the process so people then conduct the process in a different way.

数据飞轮

That then leads to this kind of—I call this the data flywheel. With every trace, every input a user gives you, with all the observability that an agent writes, you have new data sources that can then lead to new evals, where somebody says "yes, that's a verified output that I want." And then of course you can optimize the system more towards that outcome, depending on which data you gathered.

七、Computer Use vs. Tool Calling

Host: Do you have a strong point of view today as to whether agents operating against these business processes within SAP—or otherwise in enterprise software—do you think it's going to be computer use? Do you think it is all code and tool use on APIs?

Philip: It's an interesting question. I have not a finite answer yet to this. Given how clunky UIs are and so on, and knowing the challenges also from UI automation from the past—I mean, it's phenomenal what they can do already today, quite frankly. They're still a little bit slow, and so on. But I still believe for the most part it will be that the majority will live with tool calling and agents running in the background. Because you also don't maybe want to have the browser open all the time. Okay, you can do this with headless browsers and so on, but if you can do this with a more structured approach from an integration point of view, I think that will be the preferred method. But then of course there will always be kind of things where an API is maybe not available, or you have a legacy system for a time being, and then these computer-use approaches will nicely tie in as well.

八、Agent 最先在哪些领域产生效果？

Host: If we zoom out a little bit and just think about agents and automated business processes, in what domains do you hope customers will see that be most effective first?

Philip: Well, we need to be clear: it has been for the most part very productive in what I call the unstructured world. Let's face it, large language models are very good in the unstructured world—text and images and stuff like that. So everything where unstructured data is concerned, for the most part: services and support, and maybe sales. And then anything related to knowledge work, that deals a lot with documents. This is where we see, like Joule for Consultant—it's a lot of unstructured information; this is where it was the easiest to get quickly to the return on investment.

It was harder to combine—you mentioned tool use—the models had to learn first of all, and got better, on how to use the tools. And then you need to build orchestrators and disambiguate: "Oh, what does an order actually mean? Do you mean a maintenance order, sales order, purchase order?" "Order" is a very overloaded term—very ambiguous. That orchestration logic is a hard thing to build.

But now that it has gotten better, you can do things like chat with your data. Instead of going to your data analyst or business analyst that curates you some dashboards—and in 80% of the cases that might be a good-enough dashboard, but for all the other 20% of the questions you always need to go back to your IT department—now you can just converse in natural language with the system. It pulls the data, natural-language to SQL or whatever, pulls that data; you converse with it until you have that point of view of the data that you want. And then you just pin it and say: "Okay, that's actually my problem. Now I want to manage that problem for the next, I don't know, two-three weeks until the problem maybe has disappeared." And then you move on, maybe you delete that tile.

This combination of the structured and the unstructured world is required if you want to go into the tabular world. Because lots of data in finance is stored in tables, and sales, and in the supply chain. Unlocking that took a little bit of time, but now we are seeing—through, for example, the SAP Knowledge Graph that we've built—which is kind of the glue between natural language and the structured data in the system to really bring this together.

九、为什么 LLM 不够：表格预测与 RPT-1

Host: That actually leads to one of your—I don't know if it's unconventional, but it's certainly not the dominant narrative in AI right now—which is your interest in predictive and tabular models. Can you talk about why LLMs aren't the be-all end-all here? Why we can't just use tools and calculation external to the model, in combination with LLM, to achieve what you want to achieve?

Philip: First of all, from a business motivation: it's a great question. LLMs—unstructured world—that's all good. But most of the time, if you want to plan forward, if you want to make good decisions in a company, you need predictions. You need predictions in terms of: "Oh, what's my demand?" Depending on the seasonality effects, what's my demand forecast for my products in the retail store? Or what's my demand for my product so I can plan accordingly my manufacturing? Or you want to predict your cash flow. And that has a bunch of input variables—like, what are actually my days sales outstanding? That is determined based on: are customers paying yes or no? That's a classification question. And if you then say "okay, if a customer is not paying within the payment terms, what's the payment delay?"—a classical regression question. And so on.

LLM 不擅长预测

Now, the problem is, of course, still today—if we look at these predictive questions and you want to maybe do a what-if analysis from it: if you want to do these predictions, quite frankly, the challenge is large language models are not made for this. The way they generate just one token after another, essentially in a sequence-to-sequence modeling—they're language models. They do this phenomenally well. But if you still want to do these predictions, you have to go back to these classical machine-learning approaches: XGBoost or AutoGluon and many of these AutoML approaches.

The problem is, it doesn't scale. We haven't seen in the predictive space the same level of democratization. You still need to hire a very good talented data scientist. And then if you, for example, are a large company—we did this for example at a pharmaceutical company. If you just want to solve the payment-delay prediction problem I've mentioned: they are running in 90 countries around the world, and they need these two models. So you end up with 180 models you need to train. You need to create the data, train the models, figure out the right model, do feature engineering—the classical machine-learning approach.

RPT-1：关系型预训练 Transformer

What we said all the time is: "Okay, look, we have all this data stored in these tables—hundreds of thousands of tables where all this information is stored. Can we not apply the same idea that large language models or multi-modal models did for the unstructured world, actually for the structured, in order to start predicting things?" So you can just basically provide a little bit of context, a small amount of data—not a large amount of data, because that was always a problem—and then start making highly accurate predictions in that domain.

That led to—this was two years of research—we published that also at NeurIPS and a bunch of other conferences. We call this RPT-1: Relational Pre-trained Transformer. It's still based on the transformer architecture, but with a very different architecture. We released this and we see meanwhile some very, very good results from that in various domains where—as I said—classification and regression, sometimes time series, are concerned. We believe this will be huge, because it obviously will allow way more people, from a business-impact perspective, to make these predictions, which large language models have a really hard time with.

十、Innovation Race vs. Outcome Race：企业采纳的真实瓶颈

Host: When you think about the gap in—I don't know, I think you described it as hype versus adoption within enterprise customers—the innovation race there versus the outcome race. The change is happening very quickly; that's hard for companies to absorb. Where do you see challenges for the enterprise in adoption today, and where are customers making the most progress with you, or where are they most excited?

Philip: It's a good question. Usually I say the primary problem, as I said, is the problem of data. Most of the time, the data is, of course, very disaggregated in a company. For a variety of reasons: either because you made certain decisions on how you purchased solutions in the past, or you did an M&A, so you acquired a company—and naturally, of course, they bring a very different IT system landscape as well. So you have this segregated information, and the problem is, of course, that limits the potential of what you can actually do with AI.

The next question is: how do you integrate this safely? What I see is clearly customers who did that kind of homework. Now of course, it's not a new topic. We're discussing this for 10, 15, maybe more years. The ones that did their homework, they of course have a much easier life to then also reap the benefits of AI.

The second one, as I mentioned, is the problem of scale. The bigger and more complex the landscape, then bringing this together in a unified experience is a challenge.

And then finally, of course, everything around security. Because there's always this gap between: "Oh, there's an amazing innovation"—take open Claude, for example—I mean, amazing what this has brought to the world in terms of further ideas. But from a security perspective, that's a problem. You don't want to run this just like it is there on GitHub and deploy this in your organization. Nobody would ever do this. So then of course you need to bring this, make it secure.

We have seen with LiteLLM—how long is this now ago, two weeks or something? You probably saw it—with this vulnerability that all of a sudden steals all your keys and credentials and so on. That you don't want, right? If you're the chief information security officer in the company, you don't have a job anymore. That's another big challenge from an adoption perspective as well.

十一、几年后，财务/HR/供应链团队的工作会变成什么样？

Host: What do you think the function of a finance or an HR or a supply-chain team that would have been operating out of SAP in their day-to-day work, you know, a year ago—what do you think that looks like a few years from now if they're successful, if you and your customers are successful with the AI transformation?

Philip: First of all, it's very simple. They will get rid of a lot of the mundane work—like collecting information and preparing PowerPoints for decision-making. So what we are going to see is a much, much faster way of making decisions, making better decisions, and automating the mundane work.

What people will do is they will run more scenarios. They will get better, deeper insights in a much faster way, in order to then really think about—we always call it—this more strategic thinking.

In a way, Sarah, if you will—for me—this is the same way like everybody who works today maybe in the finance shared-service center: it's, for me, the equivalent of a junior developer today with Claude Code. They got one level higher. They're not so much anymore tasked with writing a lot of the code, with Codex or with Claude Code, but they actually then start supervising the code, give feedback, capture the essence of what the code should look like, do much more review, and then rather think about what to build next—think about the next requirement and how is that actually differentiating.

Every role, every level, will kind of get up-leveled, so to speak—because the work that's being done today will be pushed down to these agents. Therefore I believe in general what we will see is that people will just achieve so much more, because there is a lot of intelligence baked into the system that gets rid of many of the things that we're doing today and that are actually—well, at least in many cases—not a lot of fun.

十二、定价模型的迁移：从席位到消费到结果

Host: I must admit my ignorance here. I'm thinking about this and I want to talk a little bit about the impact on the business if you're right as well. I don't actually know how SAP prices broadly today, but the question would be: how do you price? And if you are delivering more outcomes for customers, or serving them software in a different way, do you think that changes the business model for SAP?

Philip: It does, absolutely. There's no question, and we have prepared for this already.

For me it was always very clear. For the most part, SAP software is seat-based licensed today, with a few exceptions like Concur, for example, or Fieldglass, or the Business Network. But very clearly with AI, it was very clear for us that step by step it will go towards this consumptive world—first consumptive, and then maybe in the next step, once we have more verifiability in the system, then also towards maybe an outcome-based license model—what Sierra is doing, and so on and so forth.

But the reality is also: today for us it's a hybrid model. It's consumptive, but it still has a certain element of seats in there. Because it's a joint journey with the customer. The customer is saying they are not yet ready, in many cases, for a purely consumptive model, because they need predictability. Then they are also not yet fully everywhere trusting the outcome—they know the value is already there, but then they are afraid that the costs may explode from a consumptive perspective, etc.

So what we have designed is a hybrid that is basically ready for this consumptive world, but actually meets the customers where they are today—knowing that they demand still a lot of predictability in the enterprise space in order to cost-control the whole thing for themselves as well.

Host: That makes sense. It's unclear—I also believe that transition is going to happen; it's unclear how quickly it will.

Philip: Exactly. Nobody knows this. At first you see customers that are more—you have a wide range of opinions—and of course some customers are a bit more forward-leaning already, and then others are more still asking or demanding a classical model. So it's a journey.

十三、五年后：谁是赢家？

Host: When you look forward and think about SAP's position five years from now, and you compare it to the broad market pivot away from SaaS and software in terms of just how investors are valuing these businesses and their enthusiasm about their durability—my own opinion is the challenge is real, and yet it will affect the incumbent software companies very differently. There will be winners and losers, versus universally everybody's market cap coming down. What do you think is going to be characteristic of a winner—or why does SAP get to endure again?

Philip: I think at the end of the day, it's all about adoption and the outcome you bring to the customer. The reality is, for most companies, the technology doesn't matter. I always tell to my developers all the time: our job at SAP is to make the technology disappear. We need to get the outcome in front of the customer.

And of course not just the value itself—you also be able to produce and price it in a way so it's a win-win situation for the customer and the vendor at the end of the day. This is also why, from an architecture perspective, we are so flexible. We said: "Don't overindex on a specific LLM." We have partnerships with all of them. And really only invest in the things that are actually differentiating for our customers, versus the things that anyway will likely get commoditized in the tech stack. And then try to make sure that we bake the enterprise qualities in, and the integration is there, and the customers can turn these capabilities on almost instantaneously in order to benefit from it.

Why is this important? Because if you take a lot of time to reap the value, then your return on investment is essentially gone, or the business case becomes harder. Therefore what we are really focusing on is to deliver these outcomes for the customers. I think that will differentiate the winners from the losers at the end of the day: to really focus on the business outcomes for the customer.

十四、快问快答：CTO 的一天

Host: As we wrap up, I want to ask you a few quickfire questions, including a little bit more personal one. Our listeners always want to know: what do you do all day as the CTO of SAP? Can you just describe how you spend your time?

Philip: I spend most of the time reviewing the progress with the teams, and thinking along, from all the layers with the teams—from the database to the models, to the UI. Review the progress, give guidance, feedback, learn something new, study what happens outside, do a lot of prototypes. While we speak here, I have a bunch of command-line interface instances running here, prototyping a bunch of things, trying things out—seeing what works, what doesn't work. And then giving this also as kind of inspiration back to the team.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的发现：SAP 正在把"测试驱动开发"从坟墓里挖出来——但驱动者不是程序员，而是 AI
Philipp 半开玩笑地提到那个谷歌工程师"5 点下班是因为先写测试"的旧故事，然后承认"包括我在内，没人真的那么干"。这是一个被业界默默埋葬了 20 年的实践。
**但真正的洞察在反面**：TDD 当年失败不是因为它错了，而是因为人类的大脑本能地讨厌"先描述边界，再创造内容"——我们更喜欢边写边发现。而 LLM **恰好相反**：它最讨厌的就是"自己摸索意图"，最擅长的就是"按已知边界生成内容"。所以 agentic coding 不是让 TDD 复活，而是**第一次让 TDD 成为生产力的最优解**——人类终于被迫做了那个一直该做却做不到的事。
这意味着一个更深的预言：**未来"高级工程师"的核心技能将不是"会写代码"，而是"会写 eval"**。代码生成会被压成像编译一样的基础设施层；真正昂贵的、需要品味的、需要资深判断的——是定义"什么算对"。
## 二、跨领域连接：SAP 的"知识图谱 + RPT-1"≈ 物流业的"霍尔木兹海峡焦虑"≈ 蛋白质折叠
Phili

item.title: [Bits + Bips] DeFi's Security Ceiling: Why Lending Can't Be Like Uniswap
item.dc:creator: Bits + Bips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、意外观察：DeFi 借贷协议其实在售卖"流动性"，而非"技术"

最让人意外的真相是 Michael 那句被轻描淡写带过的话——"people think it's about the technology. It's really not. What really large lending protocols are selling is liquidity."

这句话颠覆了整个 DeFi 叙事的自我认知。过去七年我们以为自己在构建"去中心化金融基础设施"，实际上 Aave 和 Compound 的核心商业模式和 1970 年代的货币市场基金（MMF）几乎一模一样——把流动性池化、把期限错配、把风险社会化。代码只是包装纸，池化才是产品。

这就解释了为什么点对点（peer-to-peer）模型虽然更安全却总是失败：用户不是为"安全"付费的，他们为"随时进出的幻觉"付费。这和传统银行挤兑的心理学完全同构——只要大家相信能随时取出，就没人真的想取。

二、隐藏模式：DeFi 正在重演 1907 年的美国银行业危机

你们谈话中一个没被点破的深层联系是：DeFi 现在走的每一步，华尔街在 1907–1933 年已经完整走过一遍。

  • 1907 年银行恐慌 → 对应当前 48 小时内 TVL 从 995 亿掉到 863 亿的"链上挤兑"
  • J.P. Morgan 个人救市 → 对应 Tether 与 Drift 的合作（私人资本承担最后贷款人角色）
  • 1913 年联储法案 → 对应你们在讨论的"延迟队列+社区审查"机制（本质是央行的"窗口贴现"机制）
  • 1933 年 Glass-Steagall → 将来必然出现的"隔离池强制令"

跨领域连接：这不是金融史第一次。航空业在 1950s 经历"彗星号事件"后也面临同样的困境——工程师想要完全自动化的飞行系统，但每次空难后都被迫加入一层"人类在回路"的冗余。DeFi 现在正在经历它的"彗星号时刻"：代码层的完美主义遭遇物理世界的非线性崩溃。

三、被忽视的系统性真相："不可能三角"的结构性来源

Austin 和 Michael 在讨论中接近但没有说破一个结构性定理：借贷协议面对的"外部性输入"（价格、流动性、波动率）使其本质上不可能像 Uniswap 那样"自洽"。

这里有个惊人的类比——这和哥德尔不完备定理的拓扑结构一模一样。一个系统要么是自洽但不完备（像 Uniswap，功能有限但内部一致），要么是完备但不自洽（像 Aave，功能丰富但必须依赖外部预言机，形成无法闭合的信任环）。

更深层的洞见：Michael 说"我已经放弃了（resigned）"——这不是失败主义，这是系统成熟的标志。真正成熟的领域都会达到这种"认知谦卑"： - 密码学承认"没有绝对安全"，于是发明了概率安全 - 统计学承认"没有无偏估计"，于是发明了可接受风险 - 航空业承认"零事故不可能"，于是发明了可接受伤亡率

DeFi 缺的不是更好的代码，而是"可接受损失率"这个概念。 一旦把"每年 x% 的协议损失"视为系统成本而非灾难，整个架构哲学就会从"防御性编程"转向"保险与再保险层"——这恰恰是 Lloyd's of London 在 17 世纪解决海运问题的方法。三百多年前的海盗问题和今天的链上漏洞，在系统论意义上是同构的。

留给你的一个反直觉推论：DeFi 的下一个百亿美元机会可能不在"更安全的借贷协议"，而在"链上劳合社"——为协议漏洞承保的去中心化再保险层。攻击者不会消失，就像海盗没有消失，但风险可以被定价、分散、证券化。当"被黑"从灾难变成成本线上的一个数字时，这个行业才真正长大。

📝 转录文本

开场：一连串攻击后的根本拷问

Austin（主持人）： We just had Kelp DAO here. We've had like attack after attack after attack. And to me it raises the question — and I'm curious, you know, for the rest of the group — do we have to change our default thought model of "transactions are legitimate"?

嘉宾 A： It's a good question, but you risk giving up concepts like immutability and the concept of non-recourse and finality.

Austin： Okay. So I will grant you that on finality — yes, because if we slow down transactions processing, agreed. But immutability is just that we can't change the history of a chain. If there's like a $280 million exploit and it goes into a queue and everybody looks at it during the delay period and says, "Oh, that's not correct," and so a transaction spits out that then reverses it, you still have an immutable chain where the history is transparent, known, and unalterable. It's just a question of under what terms do we let them sort of transact down the chain, right?

中心化的引力：风险总是流向被信任的一方

嘉宾 A： Yeah, it's interesting. I mean, you're asking all the right questions. I was nodding my head there, and you start to see how things naturally get centralized over time. So over time, people flow risk to trusted parties. They're trusted because they're regulated and capitalized and have all sorts of oversight and auditing. We have to find a way to keep this going with code and technology, not bring it back into the system. You know, one would think that there's a way to transfer the risk of each transaction. Maybe it's not at the pool level, maybe it's at a transaction level. There's more work to be done.

Austin： It's a major setback. Like this puts DeFi back a couple of years.

构建者的困境：按下地毯下的气泡

Michael（构建者）： Yeah, you can try to build these mechanisms — I've thought deeply about this kind of stuff as a sort of mechanism builder myself. We used to try to think about how we could introduce these kinds of mechanisms into our protocol, and it becomes quite difficult because there are all sorts of nuances here and sort of edge cases. When you think you fix one thing, it's like pushing a bubble under the carpet. You just sort of move it somewhere else, but the bubble's still there.

If you want to do rate limiting, for instance, on protocols, you need to know what you're actually limiting. What's the actual unit of limit there? Is it the underlying asset? Well, the amount of asset in circulation can change, so that needs to sort of adapt over time. Its value could certainly change a lot, so then you need to start to bring in oracles and third parties, and that introduces new dependencies.

So adding these mechanisms in principle sounds like a great idea, and people often ask builders like me why aren't there more of these — and it does come down to just being very, very difficult to do it without adding new trust assumptions into the mix, and that carries a cost ultimately.

Austin： So I strongly agree that it carries a cost, but let's look at the counterfactual — which is that in 48 hours DeFi TVL has gone from what, $99.5 to $86.3 billion. I think the market may be speaking that the cost of what is currently there is just rapidly becoming unusable.

嘉宾 A： Too high.

Austin： Totally agree.

两条路径：简化 vs. 接受中心化

Austin： So Michael, I would ask you as a builder yourself — and I know some others that have been posing this question to people — I kind of see two paths here.

Path One is increasing call it slowness, checkpoints, multiple redundancy in the system one way or another, because Chris has said the latency problem is real. And if we're going to be optimistic about transactions with zero latency, I don't see a pathway to interdicting these things — caveat, if we continue to build relatively complicated things.

Because as a counterpoint, let's look at a model that's largely been successful and stood the test of time, which is Uniswap, right? If all of our LP pools are segregated and unalterable, and to upgrade we've got to withdraw from V2 and go to V3, and withdraw from V3 and go to V4, and all of these could sort of blow up in their own self-contained box — yes, Uniswap is a very simple thing. They are not building complex layers upon layers upon layers.

But is the future, I guess I would ask you as a builder, more likely to be: actually we need to simplify a lot of these things and remove the attack surface area — go back to basics in DeFi? Or is it: if we want to retain this complexity, we need to think much harder about security, which may, as you noted, lead to certain forms of centralization?

借贷 ≠ AMM：为什么借贷协议无法像 Uniswap 一样"自洽"

Michael： Yeah, this came up a lot through my time building a lending protocol. Ultimately, an AMM or an exchange is very self-contained. It really doesn't care about the outside world. It just looks at what's inside, and it has to try to balance a single invariant in the pool.

If you look at a credit market or lending market, there are all sorts of dependencies, and credit markets really care about the outside environment that they sit in. So they care about the volatility of the assets. They need to know the prices of those. The prices aren't intrinsic to the assets, so then you have to have some kind of third party report on those prices.

Liquidity is a massive, massive issue. A DEX doesn't care what the liquid environment is outside of the DEX, right? It can operate with $10,000 or $10 billion — it doesn't matter. But lending protocols, as we've seen with the liquidity crisis we're in now, are very, very heavily dependent on what else is going on.

And I think that's what makes it so difficult to build a fully, truly immutable Uniswap-like lending protocol. We've tried to get as close to that as possible. You know, Morpho also building with a similar sort of mindset — trying to keep as much of that as you can while still offering a viable, capital-efficient lending market.

At this point I'm resigned to say that it's just not possible for credit, really. We've seen many teams come up with all sorts of interesting ideas on how to do this, and I just don't think it's possible. So then we have to accept this is the state of the world now. This is how it has to be. How do we — what's the minimum amount of centralizing force that we can add back to these systems to make them not collapse so spectacularly when they do, whilst maintaining the core responsibility and disintermediation, composability, and all the rest of it?

资源问题，还是不可解的问题？

Chris： We saw Drift partner up with Tether, and I don't know — is it a centralization force that's going to restore security, or is it just the resourcing that you need to have to go through an inordinate amount of audits, bring in the AI? Clearly for this industry to move forward, we need to provide confidence in whatever protocol you design. So I guess, is centralization the only option? Or, you know, like you're seeing this partnership between Tether and Drift — don't know all the details yet — but is it more of a resourcing issue? It's really hard for a startup to compete.

Michael： I really don't think it is. We talked earlier about Mythos, right? Finding bugs and flaws in 26-year-old operating systems — the amount of man-hours spent on that is just more than anybody could afford to ever spend building a DeFi protocol, and yet there were still bugs that could be teased out there.

So I don't think it's purely a resource issue. Everybody's really cognizant of the importance of security, and they really are buying in. It's just a very, very hard problem. I sort of think it's unsolvable at the code level. So we need other fail-safes. We need other mechanisms that are introduced, I think, at this point, to help safeguard things a little bit more than just relying purely on code alone.

两个方向的制度设计：限速器 vs. 双边报价

Austin（"商学院教授帽"）： So working backwards — this is me putting on my business school professor hat — but looking at some of the lessons from history, I feel like there are two paths we can go down here.

Path A（引入传统金融的安全阀）： One is exactly what you said, which is looking at tradfi lending markets and starting to build in a lot of the fail-safes in modern markets. Great example: if you have call it $200 million of supply and somebody deposits $500 million, not making all of that available for borrow immediately. Allowing that only to increase by call it 10% per day or something like that gives you a very long runway to interdict these things.

Yes, it obviously makes that protocol somewhat less efficient from "I can't take a $300 million flash loan or immediately deposit something in size." But it might also be fair to say maybe that's a problem, not a feature. And "why are you so intent on doing this literally right now at 3:00 a.m. on a Sunday?" becomes a valid question, along with many of the other redundancies you build in.

Path B（像 AMM 一样结构化双边借贷）： The other one though, to go the other way on a lending protocol — I know this makes it less liquid, but there's no reason you can't structure it like an AMM where all of these are essentially bilateral offers for lend and borrow, and then you don't have this contagion risk, right?

Like: "I will lend $100,000 of USDT at this price, and I will accept the following things as collateral" — and that's that. You could have these prepackaged boxes where each person can do their lending and terms. The problem you run into there, which I think you've spoken to in your earlier answer, is that's less efficient than it might otherwise be. If I recall, I think there was an early version of like SushiSwap's lending protocol that was similar to that way back in the day.

流动性才是产品：为什么点对点借贷永远打不过资金池

Michael： Exactly. Yeah. It just didn't attract enough users. It was so clunky, sort of almost peer-to-peer-like, and when you're competing against a pool model which is "I can just deposit and withdraw whenever I like" and there's so much liquidity — you know, people think it's about the technology. It's really not.

What really large lending protocols are selling is liquidity. It's that ability to go in and take out those huge loans and repay them whenever you like. That's the main product. When you've got liquidity, that's king above all else.

And so when you have these isolated, almost peer-to-peer-like lending systems — much more secure, as you said, you don't need all these external dependencies and all the rest of it, you just need to trust that the code (a very simple piece of code) works for that particular loan — but then it's just: how do people find each other? That's the problem that I think Compound originally solved, and that Aave, heavily modeled on the Compound system, followed on with. And it worked so tremendously well that it scaled up to billions and billions of dollars.

But with it has come this other challenge, which is: when things go wrong, they tend to cause contagion in a way that those isolated models don't. So we've experimented a lot in DeFi at this point, and I'm sort of resigned to the fact that maybe it's just not possible to get the best of all worlds, and we're going to have to sacrifice something somewhere along the line.

结语：鱼与熊掌不可兼得

Austin： I will say as a closing remark here, as we are hitting time, that I have resigned myself to the belief that I think you are probably correct about that — that we are not going to be able to have our cake and eat it too. And we're going to have to figure out which — by the way, there are multiple paths to tradeoffs — but we're going to have to tolerate some of them, because one thing I think all of us agree on is the current paradigm is not sustainable.

节目推广

If you like this segment, please like, subscribe, and tune in every Monday at 4:30 p.m. Eastern time. I'm Austin Campbell, the host of Bits and Bips, along with my friends Ram Ahluwalia and Chris Perkins, and our slate of exceptional guests. Every week we're going to discuss macro, crypto, and the collision of worlds, covering topics that move markets and shape the financial landscape.

If you've been loving Bits and Bips, don't forget that the show is transitioning to its own feeds on X, YouTube, and your favorite podcast player. If you're not already subscribed to Bits and Bips on its own channels, go there now and hit that subscribe button so you can keep up with our twice-weekly live streams and macro-meets-crypto breakdowns. Bits and Bips will only be on the Unchained feed for a few more weeks, so subscribe today to be ready for launch. You can get all the links at unchainedcrypto.com/bitsandbips.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、意外观察：DeFi 借贷协议其实在售卖"流动性"，而非"技术"
最让人意外的真相是 Michael 那句被轻描淡写带过的话——**"people think it's about the technology. It's really not. What really large lending protocols are selling is liquidity."**
这句话颠覆了整个 DeFi 叙事的自我认知。过去七年我们以为自己在构建"去中心化金融基础设施"，实际上 Aave 和 Compound 的核心商业模式和 1970 年代的货币市场基金（MMF）几乎一模一样——**把流动性池化、把期限错配、把风险社会化**。代码只是包装纸，池化才是产品。
这就解释了为什么**点对点（peer-to-peer）模型虽然更安全却总是失败**：用户不是为"安全"付费的，他们为"随时进出的幻觉"付费。这和传统银行挤兑的心理学完全同构——只要大家相信能随时取出，就没人真的想取。
---
## 二、隐藏模式：DeFi 正在重演 1907 年的美国银行业危机
你们谈话中一个没被

item.title: [Alan Chen加州生活号] 大盘新高，下周潜在雷点，时候取利了吗？有什么板块值得关注的？
item.dc:creator: Alan Chen加州生活号
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、那个被忽略的"暗池信号反转"——市场结构正在悄悄换头

视频中一闪而过的细节其实是整段最锋利的洞察：周四周五暗池里的看跌put flow突然消失，几乎所有人都"埋好了保护"。这不是看涨信号，这是博弈论意义上的"纳什均衡前夜"——当所有人都已经买好了保险，保险本身就变成了下一轮被收割的筹码。

这里有个反直觉的连接：保险密度越高，黑天鹅的杀伤力反而越低，但白天鹅的杀伤力会变得更大。因为如果突发利好，那些"埋好的put"会集体归零（视频里也提到了"上周大量保护归零"），强制性的delta对冲会反向推高大盘——这就是为什么主持人直觉地说"如果保护被打穿，大盘还得继续涨"。这是一种反向的索罗斯反身性：恐惧本身在喂养上涨。

二、跨领域连接：The RealReal、二手豪车与"K型通胀的金融化"

主持人对The RealReal二手奢侈品平台异军突起感到困惑，只能猜测"高收入家庭加税后收入还涨了15%"。但真正的解释藏在汽车二手市场的微观结构里——他自己提到的"GT3要加价买二手"。

这其实是经济学里Akerlof柠檬市场理论的逆转版：当新品产能受限（关税、供应链、品牌饥饿营销），二手市场反而出现"反向溢价"，二手品的金融属性盖过了使用属性。The RealReal的上涨和MSTR、CIFR这些比特币矿股同时启动绝非偶然——它们本质上是同一种资产：抗通胀的稀缺性载体。一个是数字稀缺，一个是物理稀缺，但都在做同一件事——把"无法被印钞稀释"卖给焦虑的资金。

让人意外的观察是：Simon Property（线下mall）和Lending Club（次级贷款）同时起涨，这看似矛盾，其实是K型分化的镜像——上层去RealReal买二手爱马仕，下层去Lending Club借钱付下个月房租，资本同时押注两端，唯独不押注中产。这是2026年最值得警觉的资金流向密码。

三、隐藏的系统性真相：这不是"V反转"，是"叙事换轨"

视频里反复纠结"为什么大盘新高但Mag7没回到高点"，主持人归因为"领导板块换了"。但更深层的真相是：市场正在完成一次从"GPU叙事"到"GPU基础设施叙事"的悄然换轨——TT（空调/冷却）、Eaton（电力切换）、DLR（数据中心地产）在领涨，而Nvidia本身在补涨而非领涨。

这里的跨领域连接非常有趣：这和19世纪末美国铁路泡沫破灭后的资本流向几乎一模一样。当年铁路股见顶后，资金没有离开，而是流向了"铁路周边"——钢铁、煤炭、电报、车厢制造。真正赚到钱的不是修铁路的，是给修铁路的人卖铲子和卖电力的人。今天的TT、Eaton就是新时代的卡内基钢铁。

最让人意外的反向洞见是：主持人提到"Forward PE反而比之前便宜了"——这句话被他轻描淡写带过，但这是整段视频最重要的一句话。股价新高+估值更便宜=盈利预期上修速度>股价上涨速度。这意味着市场不是在做多股价，而是在做多"未来盈利能力本身"——这是牛市最强的形态，也是最容易让看空者血本无归的形态。当所有人都在讨论"该不该止盈"时，真正的玩家在问"分子涨得快还是分母涨得快"。

📝 转录文本

大盘新高，下周潜在雷点，时候取利了吗？有什么板块值得关注的？

开场：13连涨的历史性反弹

Hello everyone, today is April 19th. We continue to review the US stock market.

The US stock market has been on the rise for 13 consecutive trading days. It has created more than 20 or 30 years of historical records. All the indexes are innovative, except for the Dow Jones Industrial Index. And then everyone said this is a very shocking rebound.

Our community has been increasing constantly in the past week or two, and the revenue is good. Today, we will review some of our operations and some of our views on the future.

踏空者的心态与节奏

So many people hate it because many people have been left behind. If you look at social media, many people were washed away before they left the country. But to be honest, no one will know before they leave the country, right? Because the news of the fire was on the plate. If you are not in front of the computer, you can't catch it. Right? Yes.

And this information is not being used for a day. And now the weekend information says that the sea house is closed again. There are still quite a lot of variables. So everyone is not afraid or cautious. There is not much mistake.

Have you noticed that Buffett has a lot of cash in his pocket? I remember you mentioned this in your previous video. People said that Buffett has a lot of cash in his pocket, so why should I invest in him? So many people are being stalled. The most common problem is that it's not like it's going to go up or down.

And now it seems that everyone is very sad. The mentality is very sad. You say, "It's going to fall, it's going to fall." Everyone hopes it will fall. But if you say it's going to fall, it's actually from the data of Gao Sheng.

资金博弈：Hedge Fund 被挤压与暗池信号

Some data from Trade Desk last week said that this wave of growth is a very large scale. Hedge funds are being squeezed out. Hedge Funds are the funds of CTA and Long Only. What do these participants mean? We won't talk about it here. In our course, we specifically talk about the different participants of the whole market. For example, Long Only Fund, CTA, and Platform. We will not talk about them here.

So, in this wave of growth, we see that the transaction volume is particularly large. 主要还是因为双方各方的看法都不一样，主要还是因为双方各方的看法都不一样。有些是被击空然后不断的现在又加空。

上个星期前一段时间我们还看到挺多在暗池里面，因为我们会追踪暗池，暗池里面挺多做空大盘的 put flow，我们当时也在社群里面说了。但是星期四星期五就见到很少，似乎大家都已经埋好保护了。

But I don't know, I have a feeling that if those treasures are pierced and then they are broken, then the big game has to continue to go up. Yes, including last week's full-time strike. Many of the protection was returned to zero.

下周的潜在雷点：财报、听证会与FOMC

So the market will continue to judge the market trend based on the financial statements of the next two or three weeks. Personally, I think most of the big heads may have finished eating. In this position, it may not be appropriate to do more aggressive warehouse management. More may need to wait for the financial report or the market will digest the news for the next period of time.

Including the US-China policy, next week there will be a hearing, including the FOMC. These things may affect the development of the future.

The hearing is very important. Everyone actually hopes that he will listen to Trump to calm down. But what he said at the hearing — will it bring a big thunder to the market again? No one knows. I think it's not very certain this week. Especially this hearing, what did he say. If he said something wrong, the market may say he is not independent. Or a very British person comes and starts to QT directly, and then QT starts from the back, not QE. The market will fall again.

So I think this week's hole is quite big. Yes, but it's easy to predict. He basically doesn't follow Trump. So he should still — it's better to let everyone guess what direction it's going to go in. It's not going in any direction. The most stable direction is that it's not going in any direction. It's always about looking at the data.

Yes. It's a stable direction, but the new president of the United Nations may be a person with a direction. So the direction it is going to show is likely to be a big hole.

技术面：Mag7 极度超买与 50 EMA 偏离

But you just talked about a lot of stocks, which have actually grown a lot. If we look at the technical side, everyone must be very interested in the question of when we will take the example.

Some of the Max7 stocks have actually grown a lot, especially those that are under control, like Microsoft. They are very far from 50 EMA, 50 days of military line. Very extended.

If we know a concept called Average Trading Rate, which is the average fluctuation period, the fluctuation range, you will see that many of them are already three to four to five times the fluctuation range of 50 EMA. Under this situation, what I saw today, there is Microsoft, Nvidia, AVGEO, Broadcom, and even Meta, they are all far from… Look at the growth, it's 90 degrees up. You can't do anything about it.

If you get out of the air, don't be too sad. Think about this 90-degree-up-and-down-pull-out-of-the-air picture. There are not many people who can catch it. If you catch it, you have to catch it at the first time. And you can catch a few people in our community.

I won't say that I haven't caught people in a whole section. When our community started to add more people, when the news of the nuclear talks came out, it took advantage of all the oil stocks. Then our M1 long-term account immediately added all the major and small technology stocks, banks, and added a little bit of — Right.

进场机会与极端 V 形反转

家庭对人生对自己账户负责任的进场的机会是完全没有找到。所以这一波被踏空的人也不要太伤心。这个没什么好伤心。这种就是不是猛一进去的，就开始那一下没抓住，就是没有太多的机会了。

然后回到说那些非常 extended 的股票，比如说大家讲的 Microsoft、Nvidia、AVG、Meta 那些。Look at which stocks are far away from the 50-day military line. There are two possibilities for this stock. Either it will shake horizontally and wait for the military line to catch up. Or, as soon as there is a news of a storm, a relatively large trend will appear.

I'm talking about the stormy mine. Maybe it's this week's hearing. At that time, because it has been very extended, many people know that this stock has been seriously overbought. I will definitely lock the profit before I talk about super full.

Look at QQQ and SPY — their RSI has also reached 80, it's also super full. Yes. Then we also shared a data in the community: 80% more than 80% of the value stocks are above the 20-day military line. This is already close to super full. So in fact, the entire technology is 100 yuan is very super full.

Yes, this is a… Some people call this super full, and some people call this breath thrust. That is to say, suddenly there are so many stocks that rebound together. This should be a start like a bull. Anyway, from a technical analysis, we can pull it back so quickly. Then the next recovery is theoretically able to continue to do more.

Don't say it's because it's high. It will go into a counter-clockwise trend change may take a while to say this place. Maybe it's really up or something. But this trend is going to really go from a multi-head to an empty head — it still takes a while, especially from a weekly perspective. We do have a weekly dead end, but you can see that the past period of time has taken a few weeks to shake and organize to digest it. This time we have a very extreme vertical V-back. This is really rare in history.

M1 长期账户表现与领导板块切换

So don't feel that it's a pity that it didn't catch up. Our M1 is a large long-term account. It is also the overall performance of the home storage during this period. You can see that the profit rate is 15% this year, right? I didn't post it last Friday, but today before we started broadcasting, we checked it with others and it was around 14.95%. It was around 15%.

But let's take a look at DaPan this year. It's only 1-2% higher than the year-end. So in general, our long-term accounts are not bad. The main thing is that we have a large part of it — Midcap's technology stocks. And then we also caught a few very important ones. For example, we have several different stocks. We caught a lot of semiconductor stocks. And then some small chip stocks. And then an ANET. Although this is a software stock, but it's used for data center.

We also specially issued the financial report to award Inet. This stock — this is also an all-time high. So we actually — and we actually added two more tanks in the previous year when it fell. When we were fighting, we added one more 200-day military line and then another 200-day military line in the next day.

In fact, the performance of the MAX 7 this year, you can see that the MAX 7 has not yet returned to the new height at the beginning of the year. It just returned to the new height, but it did not return to the historical new height. So this year, the Mac7 and the big market are a little bit of a disadvantage or a weakness. The big market has already created a new record, right?

So now the leading market has started to quietly change. Maybe after the financial report, they will take back the relay stick again. Because they are still the most profitable company. So I think the financial report will give a lot of guidance.

数据中心生态：从 GPU 到周边受益股

We also found that the data semiconductor — you will find that second-class chips, even some capital-spent devices, are now more popular than pure GPUs. You will find that a lot of money is left in that place.

There is also the stock around the data center. Many people have not seen the air conditioner, such as the Train called TT. We have TT stock in M1. And Eaton, which specializes in the exchange of electronic devices. DLR is also in M1. This is the landlord. They bought land and rented it to the data center. Then they paid the rent and distributed the money.

These surrounding stocks are all extending to the new high. So the entire half-billion dollar market is now leading. But we must pay attention to the small stocks other than GPUs. Because it has a trend of expansion now.

资金扩散：加密币、奢侈品、二手平台、地产、贷款

Speaking of expansion, we found that last Friday, funds went to other directions. For example, the currency of encrypted currency. I don't know if you have encountered it. I am very familiar with the currency of encrypted currency. But have you seen MSTR, CIFR, and CLSK? They are all very beautiful breakthrough technology. Yes.

And we also saw that the funds were going to travel hotels. And the clothes, especially the ones with the high price. We also saw that — I found a new stock today. It's called The RealReal. It should be called TRR or RR. The name is The RealReal. It's real. I was laughing today. Do you see any fake real? Then I saw this stock. This is a second-hand product selling platform. There is such a thing.

This is also a lot of funds in the fifth week. I think this may be a message that was sent out last week. The high-income families that were taxed in 2025, their income grew by 15%. Their tax-raising income grew by 15%. I don't know if this has something to do with it. I can't find any other reason. Either it's the stop-war or other things. It's not directly related to the luxury goods.

The only thing I can think of is that the American families — if you are really rich, you should buy a new one. I feel that second-hand is prosperous. You have to buy the goods. You can't lose goods. I bought GT3. I can't buy it. I can't get the price. I just add money to the second-hand market. The second-hand market also adds money to buy. It's higher than MSRP. So sometimes those luxury products — I haven't been to this website either, I just want to guess like this.

But there are also retail stores that are growing a lot. For example, Simon Property SPG, it should be SPG, SNGP. It's a mall. Look at the sales volume of the market. It's growing in volume, right? And then there's a Kite Real Estate. Kite, it should be KITE Real Estate. That is also a real estate in the shopping center. Okay. The last week is also a volume.

Then CRBE is the kind of commercial real estate. It looks like it's all moving. But we haven't figured out what the reason behind it is. Then there are some loan companies. The last week is also in the top. For example, Lending Club. Lending Club. This is still not out of date. Interesting. Yes, it's obvious that it's a risk.

多空两种解读与霍尔木兹海峡变量

But you have two opinions. If it's a pessimistic person, the people who are pessimistic will say that the funds for those companies that are empty are now empty. They want to switch to making big companies and big technologies. Or they want to go to the flat. They want to release these things. This is pessimism. People who want big companies to fall will think like this.

But we started from… we can talk about this weekend for another 5 minutes. This weekend, when the market closed, the Hormuz sea house was shut down again. Iran said that the 7 ships that Trump is talking about — um, the market is going to sell the news or is it just a gradual rebound? The market may become a bit numb. Do you have any opinions on this? I'm just curious.

操作原则：守住军线，承认错误，结构未破不做空

If my personal approach, but I also know that my approach will affect a lot of people. So we also need to be more cautious. But my personal view is that as long as the big companies can hold the military line for 50 days or even more than 200 days, — Because these things are very simple.
