It doesn't matter if you're wrong.

For example, we often make mistakes. I posted a classic news article on social media. After talking about oil, I don't know if you remember, after talking about oil, it's their turn to make a profit. So at that time, I was talking about chicken. It's because everyone has no money. If you have no money, you can't afford beef. Because beef is very expensive now. So you have to turn to eat chicken. But who knows, I found that everyone is still very rich. This idea is completely wrong. But it doesn't matter if it's wrong. No one is god, right? They are all wrong.

So as long as the structure of the whole big plate is still bullish, I think it is to stick to one principle, which is to buy a chance to dip. But if it is the structure changes and the DAA is lost — when the 200-day or 50-day military line is lost, you don't try to buy a store. Just wait for a big plate to appear. It's a combination of many aspects. It's just the technical side and the flow of the institution. Plus the red line. When something like this changes, you can go in.

So we've been waiting for a long time. We've been waiting and waiting and waiting. So this is the situation. I think both sides don't mean to continue. So he came out like this, but instead gave some people who were left empty a chance.

Yes, so I just said that this trend now, even if you think you have a lot of money, don't go, it's not recommended to go empty. Because one is the risk of being limited. Another is that as long as the structure can be maintained in these military lines, we are now going to —

估值修正：Forward PE 反而更便宜

I was wrong last time. I have to correct it. I said it before, the value of many technology stocks is price for perfection, beyond perfection. But today, when I checked their forward PE, it's not expensive at all. Look at a lot of big technologies, it's even lower than QQQ's comprehensive forward PE. Yes, yes. So it's not expensive at all.

Yes, that's right. The financial report is going to be a blast. I'm sure we'll see. That means this financial report is quite interesting. From a valuation perspective, it's not very expensive. Unlike the financial reports in the first quarter, which are basically all low. No matter how bright your financial report is, one is that their opening capital is very large. One is that the valuation at that time was not very certain.

It's interesting. The valuation has been a bit lower. Even though it has rebounded quite a bit, their valuation and profit capacity are also growing. So, the position is still quite subtle. Whether it's Nvidia, Tesla, Meta, Amazon, Microsoft, they are all in a tricky position. It's interesting.

Forward PE 概念解释

Can you explain to the viewers why many of its stocks have returned to their historical high points but its valuation is cheaper than before?

Sure, forward PE — actually, everyone, if you just joined the stock market, we can explain it very simply. Look at 4PE, the price is the expectation of your future wealth. It's a mathematical equation. The price above is your current price, and the expectation below is your future. When your expectation is getting higher, when your share price is getting higher, your entire PE ratio will be getting lower and lower. So even if your current price has returned to the original high point, but your — That's right. That's it for today. You can go back and… Yeah.

Netflix 与收尾

Yes, it has fallen quite a lot. But the second day, when I tried to fill the gap, the transaction volume was quite large. Yes, this is a… I don't think the CEO's move has had much impact. Because Netflix is a cash cow after all. When it enters the market, it has a very critical one.

OK, if you think this kind of analysis or discussion is helpful to you, you can leave a comment in the comments section. And you can tell us in the comments section what topics you are interested in, including the 4-P and valuation that we explained today. And next week, if there are no more surprises, we can continue to talk about this week's hearing and the financial news.

And thank you for your time. Good luck to everyone this week. Thank you, everyone. Thank you, everyone. Bye-bye.

item.title: [No Priors: AI, Machine Learning, Tech, & Startups] Scaling Global Organizations in the Age of AI with ServiceNow Chairman and CEO Bill McDermott
item.dc:creator: No Priors: AI, Machine Learning, Tech, & Startups
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、关于"删错容忍度"的反常识洞察

McDermott 在访谈中抛出一个表面平实、内里却极具颠覆性的判断："人们会原谅人犯错，但永远不会原谅软件犯错。" 这句话表面是为 ServiceNow 的确定性工作流辩护，但它实际上揭示了 AI 时代企业软件的一个隐秘悖论——LLM 越像人，企业用户对它的容错度反而越低。

为什么？因为人类犯错时携带"上下文与责任"，软件犯错时只携带"故障"。这意味着 LLM 的"拟人化"是一把双刃剑：交互越自然，用户越倾向于以"机器标准"而非"人类标准"评判它。这与心理学中的"恐怖谷效应"形成了跨领域的精妙呼应——机器人越像人，我们越对它的不像人之处感到不安。在企业软件领域，这就形成了"信任谷"：模型能力越接近人类决策，每一次幻觉的伤害反而越大。

这解释了一个被市场低估的事实：SAS apocalypse 的真正护城河不是代码量，而是"可被起诉的责任结构"。

二、跨领域连接：从"街角小店"到"AI 控制塔"的拓扑同构

McDermott 全场都在讲熟食店、500 个顾客、Xerox 接待员，看似是怀旧叙事。但如果你把他对 ServiceNow 的描述抽象化，会发现一件有意思的事：他经营 ServiceNow 的方式，与他 16 岁经营熟食店的方式，是同一个拓扑结构。

熟食店的核心不是三明治，而是"让蓝领、老人和孩子三类完全不互通的客户都觉得这里就是为我而生"。ServiceNow 的核心也不是工作流软件，而是"让 hyperscaler、LLM 厂商、systems of record 这三类彼此竞争的玩家都觉得 ServiceNow 是中立可信的连接层"。

这就是他反复说的 "control tower"——在熟食店里，他自己就是 control tower；在 ServiceNow，平台是 control tower。两者的商业模式都是"中立化连接 + 排他性关系"。这种模式与古代丝绸之路上的中介城邦（撒马尔罕、威尼斯）异常相似：自身不生产丝绸或香料，却因为是唯一可信的中转节点而获得超额利润。McDermott 不只是在做企业软件，他在做 21 世纪的"数字撒马尔罕"。

三、令人意外的观察：AI 时代真正被替代的不是程序员，而是"COVID 期间的招聘动作"

最让我意外的，是他几乎是顺口提到、却几乎没人接住的一句话：

"2020 到 2024 年，公司在远程环境下大量招聘，没人真正近距离看过这些人……现在 AI 让大家意识到要变得更精瘦。"

这句话隐含了一个没人公开讨论的命题：AI 不是在替代员工，AI 是在"算账"——为 COVID 期间因虚拟招聘而无法精确评估的劳动力做事后审计。

换句话说，过去三年所谓的"AI 裁员"其实在做一件诡异的事：用 2024 年的 AI 算法，去修正 2021 年 Zoom 招聘的判断误差。这不是技术替代人的故事，而是技术替代了"管理者在视频会议里看不清的那部分判断力"。

这又联通到一个更深的系统性真相：AI 在企业里的第一波真实价值，不是创造新能力，而是暴露旧组织里被高估的部分。它像退潮——McDermott 自己在结尾说"潮水退去时你要穿好衣服"——但他没说出口的另一半是：潮水退去时，先看到的不是裸泳的人，而是 2021 年偷偷穿了三层衣服的人。

四、一个被忽略的战略信号

注意他提到 "20 天集成 Move Works 和 Armis"。在传统 M&A 中，这不可能。这意味着 ServiceNow 内部已经把"被收购公司的工作流"本身做成了 ServiceNow 平台上的一个工作流——他们在用自己的产品并购自己的并购。这是一种递归式的组织自指（self-reference），在数学上对应哥德尔的不动点定理：当一个系统能描述自己时，它就获得了一种不可被外部模仿的复杂度。这是任何 LLM "重写企业软件"叙事都触及不到的护城河层级。

📝 转录文本

开场金句

The cost to replace an enterprise platform in this SAS apocalypse that people talk about is an extraordinary expense. Let's take that cost and then let's take the cost associated with the human capital doing that instead of something else, cuz the platform was doing the work for you. And then let's add up the cost of the GPU factory and the tokens that will materially affect their business model. And so for a simple application on our platform, it would be 10 times greater in cost to try to replicate it with a language model. People that run businesses understand that people make mistakes. They never will forgive software for making a mistake.

主持人介绍

Hi listeners, welcome back to No Priors. Today I'm here with Bill McDermott, CEO of Service Now, and the closest thing we have to a rockstar in enterprise technology. We talk about leadership in the age of AI, what enterprise customers actually want, the difference between SAS platforms and the SAS apocalypse theory, and the next 10 years of Service Now. Welcome.

Thank you so much for being here.

Thank you for having me, Sarah. Thank you.

一、从熟食店开始的故事

So, um, I want to talk about your career and leadership and what's going on with Service Now, but I want to start at the beginning. Um, I read Winner's Dream last week, and it's an amazing book. You talk about the deli you bought when you were 16. Uh, first of all, that's a very strange thing to do. Um, but it's such an amazing story. Can you tell me a little bit first about your thought process, and then we can talk about how you managed it?

Yeah, I don't, um, I don't deserve too much credit for, um, you know, doing a strange thing buying the deli, because I was really trading in multiple part-time jobs. Uh, at the time I was stocking shelves, pumping gas, and busting tables. And so I had a chance to consolidate all of that into the delicatessen and have one job where I could spend all of my hours. And for a kid going to high school, that kind of made a little bit more sense.

So I got lucky. I bought the business for 5,500 — notes, 7,000 with interest. If I make the payments, I keep it. If I miss a payment, they take everything away from me. So, it was a pretty cut and dry moment.

客户即一切

And I think the biggest thing that I've learned in my life and especially there is: it's all about the customer. In the end, the customer and the customer alone determines whether you win or lose. And it's a very simple equation. If you keep them coming back, you got a good chance. And if you don't, you lose.

And, you know, back then, one of the most interesting parts about that store was knowing your customer and knowing your base. And I really had, you know, three main customers. One was the blue collar worker like my dad — as I said in the book — was rich on Friday and dead broke by Sunday morning. They were the senior citizens, and this was the early days of Door Dash, I guess, because they never wanted to leave their house and we delivered, and no one else did. And the hard part was getting the kids to walk a block and a half past 7-Eleven to come to my store. And these were the early days of video games and Asteroids and Pac-Man, and you know you tell that to people today, most of them never even heard of these games, but kids would stand there for hours plucking their parents' quarters into those games, and that's how I got them to walk a block and a half past 7-Eleven and come to my store at the end of a long day.

One of the young people said to me, "You know, when we want to have good food, be treated with dignity and respect, and play video games, we come to your store. And when we want to steal stuff, we go to 7-Eleven."

So, if you take care of your customer, you know who your customer is, and you give them what they want just the way they want it, no matter what business you're in, you've got a really good chance. And that's all I did. And it put me through school, including college, and it got me my start. And I have to tell you, the EQ that comes with dealing with 500 customers a day is worth a lot. And I have a feeling the world could use a little more EQ.

I, uh, learned a great deal not managing, um, a deli, but, uh, at least being a hostess at an Outback Steakhouse.

See? Yeah. You can relate, right?

Uh, well, you just, you know, the repeats of dealing with a lot of different types of people is useful.

怎么买下了那家店

Um, so it's still amazing and strange to me. You talk about this set of customers that you understood and tried to serve. Like, were you just sitting there in the deli observing them before you bought the thing? Like, how did you even figure out that these were people?

I worked there first, you know, and uh, what happened was the owner, uh, was going through some personal divorce circumstances and wanted out. And I was the guy that was always there anyway. So he tried to sell it. It was only a one-year lease. You never owned the land or the building. So he was selling a one-year lease. The lease was owned by Sunmark Industries, which is Sunoco Gas Station. And they could throw you out anytime they wanted. So only a kid who didn't know any better would pay anything for it.

So eventually that's how the deal got put together. I said, "I don't have any money, but I'll, you know, take a note from you and pay you back with interest. If I miss a payment, you get everything back."

What was really interesting about the story is I didn't have any money. Um, but what I did have, because I worked there, is relationships with all the suppliers. So, I got them to give me the first order on consignment. I said, "I will always pay you back. This is not a favor. This is a chance for you to get your shelves filled up with your stuff that I'm going to sell for you. But in the end, I'll always owe you that first delivery."

And probably they charged me too much on the margin, but I didn't care. I was in business and that's all I wanted was a shot. And that's all I've ever wanted is a shot. And I'm so honored that in my life I've had the shot. And when you have a chance, you can really bring the winner within you out. And all people should ever want is an opportunity, because an opportunity is a gift. And it's something that you should really cherish. And every time you get one, make the most of it.

That resonates. I, um, one of the things I love about being in venture is you're in the business of giving people shots.

Exactly. And, uh, entrepreneurs like yourself, especially when they get to be very successful, they value it for a really long time. Well, I deeply admire what you do, and, I mean, can you imagine being the guy that came from the corner store that went to the corner office? And I think about your great job that you do in the venture world and how unbelievably creative you have to be and the benefit of seeing all these young entrepreneurial companies. It's something I've always had a great deal of interest in. So, like, I should really be doing the interview of you right now, Sarah.

Absolutely not.

二、领导力：韧性、野心与执行

Um, when you, uh, um, okay, so it starts with your part-time jobs in the deli, and then this amazing career in sales and leadership at SAP and Service Now. Um, one more piece on the leadership front before we go to talking about the company happening in the tech landscape. So in the book you talk about resilience and ambition and disciplined execution. When did those ideas crystallize in your head as the way you would lead? And then, do you think that changes today given how dynamic the landscape is? I was just talking to the leader of a, you know, tens-of-billions-of-dollars AI company and they're like, "We have to update our strategy every month, like we have no idea what's going on." Um, and so, uh, how do you think about operating in today's environment?

I had the great pleasure last night of, uh, meeting with, uh, about 800 business people that were in town from Brazil. And we were talking about leadership, and they took me back to my book Winner's Dream. A lot of them listened to it. Um, which is fun, because I actually read it, uh, kind of straight through in between business trips, and I'm glad I did because it added the authenticity to the book I had hoped for.

Um, but it was wonderful being with them and they really wanted to talk about leadership. And I think leadership is the greatest profession in the world. And in this environment we're in today, which is changing and it's dynamic like never before and it's fast like never before, I tell them: it is fast, but it'll never move this slow again. So this is the way it's going to be.

That's kind of stressful.

Yeah, it is. And it is in one way. Um, but it's inspiring in another. You know, we have to learn to get real inspiration from the challenges that are in front of us, because if we're able to cope with them, and especially if we're able to overcome them, we get superpower.

在 Xerox 拿到第一份工作

But I go back to the original job of being a leader and, you know, getting my first break with getting my job at Xerox. And, you know, Xerox back then was the Google of its era. And it was just like such an honor.

And, you know, I go back to that original interview a lot in my mind, and I think about the opportunity of getting that shot. And, you know, I'm getting geared up to leave Long Island. My dad drops me off at the railroad tracks, and you know I'm just turned 21 years old, and I guaranteed my dad I was coming home with my employee badge in my pocket. And there's something important about that, because my dad said, "Bill, you're a good guy. Don't put all that pressure on yourself."

And I said, "No, no, no. I guarantee it."

And I went into New York that day, and I was reading the annual report on how that CEO, David Kearns was his name, was reinventing this corporation under the quality management principles given to the world by Deming. And at that time, as you know, most of the most valuable companies in the world were from Japan. Um, and they were actually selling a very high-quality product for a lesser price than Xerox was — building a lesser-quality product.

It's hard to do, right?

And so this is exactly the time I'm joining the company. But I was so inspired by this guy who wanted to transform that company that by the time I got into New York, I wanted to be the next David Kearns. But I had to go knock on cold doors for a living to get my shot.

战斗求生的面试

So on my last interview, I'm so psyched up because I'm meeting with the big boss at 9 West 57th overlooking Central Park. And I sit on a bench next to another friend that's my buddy today who's waiting for an interview. And he's been waiting for a long time. I said, "What are you doing waiting?" I go over to Joanne Siciliano, who's a receptionist, and I said, "Look, I had all day. I'm not going anywhere. Please let Mr. Fullwood know I'm waiting and I really look forward to our meeting."

I was instantly invited into his office.

And this is the essence of it all. As I looked over his shoulder at Central Park on the 38th floor in 9 West 57th, I realized then, as I realize now, it was not an interview. It was a battle for survival. Because if I get that job, I'm in control of my own destiny. It's my shot, and it's up to me to do something with it.

So, at the end of an interview, he said to me, "Bill, it's very interesting interview. You're an interesting young guy. The HR department's going to get in touch with you in the next couple of weeks."

And I said to him, "You know, Mr. Fullwood, I don't think you completely understand the situation, sir."

And he looks at me with the tilted head like, "What's this kid up to?"

And I said, "Um, I haven't broken a promise to my father in 21 years, and I guaranteed him I'm coming home tonight with my employee badge in my pocket."

And he really, like, kind of tilts his head now, like, "What's this guy up to?" And, uh, you know, he goes, "You know, Bill McDermott, as long as you haven't committed any crimes, you're hired."

Now, I hadn't committed any crimes, of course. I told him that, and I double-checked: "You really mean it? I'm hired?" And he goes, "Yeah."

So, I go over there, and, you know, he's kind of sitting the way you are. I pick him up, hug him a little bit, put him safely back in his chair, and I run past the receptionist Joanne and get down to, um, Fifth Avenue and head over to 6th Avenue to a Bonwit Teller — to a pay phone. I call up Mom and Dad and I say, you know, "Break out the Cor-bel, got the job."

Now, people don't often know, especially in Silicon Valley, what Cor-bel is.

I'll admit I do not.

It is, like, the cheapest, worst champagne you've ever had in your life. But in Long Island, where I come from, that's Cristal.

25 年后的回响

So the point of the whole thing — 25 years later, Emerson Fullwood asked me to give a speech at the Rochester Institute of Technology to students. And in introducing me, he said the only time that he had ever broken policy at the Xerox Corporation was the day he hired me. I didn't know that. And what I found remarkable about it is that he remembered that, and he knew what he was doing when he did it, and thought it was worth it.

So we have to put tremendous care into people, tremendous attention to detail when it comes to, you know, uniqueness in human beings and what they are capable of doing. And without him, I wouldn't have gotten my shot, and I never forgot that. And today when I have CEO meetings, he still comes to them, and he's my friend for life.

So I think somehow in the mixture of things with AI and today's society, we're losing a little bit of the human power that we all have to connect with each other and do magical things together. And just make sure we remember: AI is to serve people and to make the human ambition greater, not to take it away from us. And so that's, uh, my feeling on, you know, just how things have changed, but in many ways the essence of leadership, human connection — people are just more important now than ever.

三、能动性可以教吗？

I know I said I was going to then ask you about Service Now, but I have to ask you a little bit more about this. Um, do you think it's possible to coach or to teach people that type of agency that you have?

Yeah, I do. Um, I think that, you know, someone asked me last night a similar question, and I said, you know, I'm a person like a lot of other people. I was a very shy, quiet, laid-back kid and really came into my own through work. And, you know, those part-time jobs I was telling you about, and the desire to be somebody in this world, and know that my only way out — I wasn't going to make the NBA. I love basketball, but I wasn't going to make the NBA.

Yeah. Me neither.

Right. So I said, you know what? What can I control? I can control how hard I work. I can control desire to do something and make a difference. I can control that. And so I got a lot of confidence from work. And, you know, other people might get confidence from something else, and that's fine. But I think it's so important to be connected to work, especially work that ties to people.

'Cause when you know how to deal with people — and in the deli, I had 500 of them a day — you start to get a good feeling and a good understanding that if you really listen, if you really care, and you're really there present and not just going through the motions, there's people that want to connect with that kind of person.

And so I really do believe that you can teach that, but you do have to practice, you know, those ideas. You have to be a part of the process, and I think so much is done today on the beautiful mobile phone in your hand that it's replaced, you know, the human side of the connection. I, like you, I've got one in each pocket and I value them because they make me more productive and happier because I can do those things. But, um, we really do have to train people. Um, we have to make them do it. Um, where they actually have to simulate and be trained and be certified and understand and be coached well so they have the confidence to get out there and be in front of other people where they can lead and make a difference. And I'm convinced that they can do it. Yes, there are differences. Some people are more skilled at it naturally, innately, no question. But, um, more people are capable of more things than they realize.

四、AI 时代的领导力：把变化当作机会

That's very encouraging. Uh, one thing that also struck me as soon as I asked you the question about, like, leadership in the age of AI was your immediate reaction. I said, you know, something that's a very common sentiment — it's the pace of change is scary. And your immediate reaction was like, "Well, it's also inspiring," right? It's a very positive attitude, um, especially for, you know, someone running an amazing, incredibly durable platform company where you're also facing more questions than you have previously. Um, how do you think about, like, you know, in getting everybody at your company or encouraging other people to have that type of reaction to change — that it's all an opportunity?

Well, for one, I agree with you that a lot of people are frightened of change to begin with. The era of AI has disrupted every industry, every business, every person, and everybody needs to really rethink everything. And it seems to be coming all at once.

Agentic Business 蓝图白皮书

So, my approach to that was to lean into it. And one of the ways we did that here is put together a white paper on the blueprint for agentic business, which I'll happily give you a copy of today. Um, and train them on, you know, what is the difference between a language model and an enterprise platform. Not saying better or worse — they're both important. They're both extremely important. And one actually makes the other stronger. But if you don't know where one begins and one ends, it really is kind of confusing for people. And that was a big unlock.

I literally had CEOs tell me that that white paper actually let them see the world totally different — that there were many things in it that they didn't know or didn't realize.

例子一：Aisle 7 的薪酬问题

One such example would be, you know, um, let's take an example that's real. Every company today has a compensation issue. So, okay, we have a compensation issue on Aisle 7 in XYZ Corporation. The language model will do a great job of telling you, in a compensation situation like this, "Please consider step one, two, and three." Excellent. Does it in milliseconds. Fantastic. Only problem is — it doesn't actually close the case.

So now in Aisle 7, we got to have the data, the context, and we'll have to go in and out of several different iterations of departments — 'cause let's assume it's a salesperson in Aisle 7, and oh by the way that's connected to the HR department, and because it might be a problem, probably finance is involved in it, or legal, or compliance, or risk. And now we have data and a workflow that's gone through many different iterations, databases, and functions to come back and remediate the issue and close the case.

That's what a workflow platform like Service Now does. And it was also important to have AI think, but workflow act. That's an unlock for a lot of people.

例子二：银行 CEO 与 60 天的董事会会议

Another case example would be: "Hey, you know, I'm a bank. I've got 15,000 of my own engineers. My CEO is under pressure from the board to come up with AI use cases. The board meeting is coming up in 60 days. And I got to look like I got all my stories straight on what we're doing to infuse AI." You agree that that's a common thing?

Very common. Yes.

Very common thing. Okay. So one thing would be, AI — and some of the AI code out there is fantastic — and it can write new code very beautifully and set up the engineer for success and get things done at a speed never before done in the world.

It's also true that that net new code comes at a cost, because the cost to replace an enterprise platform in this SAS apocalypse that people talk about is an extraordinary expense to try to match what some of these really important enterprise platforms do, because you'd have to rebuild it. You're getting a model. "I'm going to write code now." So, I'm rebuilding your model.

Let's take that cost, and then let's take the cost associated with the human capital doing that instead of something else, 'cause the platform was doing the work for you. And then let's add up the cost of the GPU factory, the business model associated with the language model company, and the tokens that will materially affect their business model. We've actually done the math on this.

And so for a simple application on our platform, it would be 10 times greater in cost to try to replicate it with a language model.

软件犯错与人犯错

Now the other thing about that is: if I give you the language model and it makes a mistake, you call me up and you say, "Hey, Bill, the language model made a mistake." And I say to you, "The language model works." And you say, "Yeah, it works, but it made a mistake." Well, it's probably right, but it's not deterministic. And incidentally, it doesn't have the context of all the data that has materialized in the company and our relationship for a couple of decades now.

And so what we're learning too is: people that run businesses understand that people make mistakes. They never will forgive software for making a mistake.

So we're kind of in a situation where, at a very small fraction of an IT budget, a premier platform is a bargain. And to combine that with AI and the things that AI can do, you get the best of both worlds. You get more innovation, better integration, and a better outcome. Try to build it yourself at a 10x higher price, and then you have a problem. And I just tell you that the model works, because I just give you the model. You don't have someone to call to fix things.

I think a lot of the SAS apocalypse discussion ignores the basic fact that I don't think most businesses want to build workflow software for everything they do internally. Um, and I do think they want somebody to be responsible for it. And I think it takes a lot of energy to go gather that context today. It is not an automated thing, and maybe it'll become more automated, but, uh, that all resonates for me.

五、长期 Agent、计算机使用与控制塔战略

I will have to ask you, you know, um, so LLMs — they can, in the traditional interaction mode, they give you an answer when you ask a question. Right now, you have long-horizon agents, maybe you have computer use, people can use applications with these models. Does that change your thinking at all?

No, I think we lean into it. You know, our perspective is these language model companies are fantastic and they're very important, and they're going to do fabulously. Um, we said the same thing with the hyperscalers. You know, it wasn't that long ago that people were like, "Hey, you know, why wouldn't the hyperscalers just eat software, because everything is going to go to a workload in these great hyperscaler companies?" And they are great companies. Yeah, they're fabulous companies.

AI 控制塔

And so our perspective was: we have to be the AI control tower for business reinvention that integrates with all the hyperscalers, with all the language models, and all the systems of record, because they too are important companies and the data that resides in them is very important. So if we can be that fabric that connects all those nodes, now we can help you run a true agentic business.

进军安全

And on top of that, we made the move into security. Not because we're going to do all things in security, but because US is the world's largest economy, China is number two, and cyber crime is number three. It's a $1 trillion-a-month problem.

And so we feel that being able to integrate to all the good security platforms but manage it, and OT — which is the operating technology — with Armis now becoming a part of Service Now, we're going to give corporations the ability to have a full purview of the whole landscape.

And so for example, in operating technology, you know, it could be networks with devices attached to it. It could be manufacturing with various tools that are or aren't on a network where AI can or may not even see them. It could actually be medical devices in a healthcare situation. Um, it could be a world's prestigious bank managing critical infrastructure. Um, which includes the people, places, things, but also the building and the things in it. To have all of that in one automated AI platform for this reinvention of your business — it's pretty compelling.

So, it's all there now. And I really see such a future and such growth in all of these businesses, our core and what we've extended it to, because we're a native AI company. Even the platform itself now is autonomous. And, you know, you've seen what we did with Move Works, the agentic front door to the enterprise, and Veza, which is human and non-human identity. And now the security move with Armis on top of an unbelievable core. By the way, we integrated these businesses in 20 days. 20 days. So, a lot of companies don't have the engineering power to say they can do hard things quickly. Um, but, you know, we're blessed with some real talent here.

It's very impressive. I have, uh, sold companies to, you know, large enterprise software vendors where it takes like a year and a half of rebuild to get some value out of it.

Totally.

六、谁在 AI 时代脆弱？

Uh, I think it's a really good historical reminder that people forget that there was this fear about the hyperscalers as well, which, as you mentioned, have become very important to all of our businesses, and yet, um, have not replaced the need for, you know, workflow software and platforms.

Yeah, as you said, you know, actually, are very good partners, you know, and on the back of a Service Now franchise get billions in workloads into those clouds. And they're all such great companies, but they all tend to do different things super well. So for the customer to have choice is also a great idea, just like they'll want choice on the language models, and us not staying in the way of that and giving choice but acting as that control tower is a huge competitive advantage — or differentiation, whichever your perspective might be.

Do you think there's any segment in enterprise software that is at risk from generated code and agents?

I think that it's true that there will be companies that will be at risk. It's also true that they theoretically should be more valuable because of the data and the context around that data. But if there was a risk, I believe it's more departmental companies.

So if you think about companies that do something that span multiple departments or are a serious system of record that is very hard to replicate, they're safe and they'll be a part of the future. If you're serving one function and what you do is not tremendously high value-add, and it might not even be high up on the priority list of a CEO, um, I think that that would be the chance where the vulnerability equation really rises quickly.

Service Now 的护城河

Um, we're fortunate in the sense that we go end-to-end on a horizontal and north-to-south on a vertical — up and down the stack and across the enterprise. And I think that's a pretty huge moat. And, uh, right now I believe the last time I looked at it, was more than 85 billion workflows are in flight as we're having this podcast, and we got 7 trillion transactions happening on that platform. Um, and it's representing the most important brands in the world. And that's, uh, not only something we're proud of, but it's also a serious responsibility, 'cause those companies can't miss, and that system always has to be there for them. And fortunately, it is.

七、什么是平台业务？

You uniquely have run two companies that are just clearly platform businesses, SAP and Service Now. Um, do you have a definition in your mind of what makes something a platform business? Because, uh, I grew up as an investor in the age of SAS, and, you know, "rise the cloud" and everything. Uh, and I think it was wonderful for innovation that you could build, like, sort of features that turned into companies, right? Software workflows that were, um, sort of tangential to a system of record, for example.

Um, uh, Service Now originally began with, like, IT service desk as the workflow. But how do you think about how the positioning is — and the actual work — is very different now, and then, like, you know, how you got there?

Yeah, I think that had we only, um, done what you rightfully point out, which had been an IT service company as an example, that might have been a system of record for the IT department. But I think when CEOs look at a corporation and they say, "What are the needle movers?" — those are the companies that, since you mentioned the two that I work for — um, you know, one is running the financial system of global corporations of mass scale in multiple currencies, geographies, industries, um, and it's really kind of thorny and complicated to even think about removing something, especially something that's probably been mass-customized for the unique attributes of that business.

Um, so the investment scale was great, the switching costs are very high. I think you would consider that a platform.

Service Now 作为平台

And I would say the same is true in a certain sense for a company like Service Now. Um, but for a different reason. Yes, it's a system of record for all the things IT and the services that it provides, but it's even bigger than that in the sense that all of the other systems of record integrate with it.

So now you have roughly 800 of the more substantial systems of record that integrate into it, and because of our in-memory open-source data platform — so it's built on the open-source technologies, but its speed, its scale, its ability to ingest data — we could take as much as you give us.

But similarly, you might say, "Okay, you know, I'll give you your core IT and OT and I get the holistic end-to-end process, but, you know, I have data lakes, I have hyperscalers, I have language models — what about, in the flow of work or a business process, what can you do for me if I don't want to put it in Service Now?" Oh, we make a zero copy and then the transaction is complete, done and dusted. Nothing moved, there was no risk associated with it, and it works.

So I think that is a mission-critical platform in the execution of work and the basics of getting things done. What's unique about it is you can change the business process instantaneously using the AI tools that are resident in the platform. So if you want to build a net new application, you can do so. If you want to see how the business process would change in a knowledge graph, you can do so. And you can see how that change impacted the processes and the way work flows and all the individuals impacted in a corporation in real time. Uh, so I think the motion of that is very, very hard to replicate, both on the history of it, the bandwidth of it, and also what we have designed in terms of the systems' capabilities. It's very unique.

八、AI 加速了部署

One of the things that makes workflow platforms so sticky is, um, the organization, beginning with IT, learns how to deploy workflows on it, and as you said, maybe they can do it even more instantaneously now.

Yeah.

Um, the cost to implement new business processes, integrate, customize has always been very high for enterprises. Um, is that decreasing with AI? Um, is that, like, an advantage for you, a concern for you?

It's a great question. It's actually a big advantage because we were always quick to implement. You know, nobody has time for multi-year projects. You know this. And, uh, the customer wants to get to value really fast. And with autonomous platform like we have now, um, we have major customers, big ones, that are going live in less than 30 days. Big ones.

And so think about it: you know, if you go back, as you mentioned, you know, think about the financial crisis era and the scale even then of SAS applications. If they could have been implemented in less than 30 days, everybody would be doing a rain dance with happiness. Um, it took months. And to think that you can do things — big things, complex things — in less than 30 days, and in some cases even faster. Um, and it's all modular. So you can get your quick wins and just keep going.

Um, you know, some people thought that the SIs, you know, would be hurt by this, but the difference is they actually have many more projects now that they can do much more quickly.

Oh, that's what you're saying.

Yeah, I'm saying much more quickly. Everything is happening much faster. The customer's ROI is better. I'm a big, you know, proponent of a native AI company, and everything we do is about being a native AI company. Every change we make is because of AI.

客服的重塑

And some of those things, you know, they change business models, they change, um, the way humans actually work in the company. For example, you know, 90% of our customer service cases now are managed by agents. And, you know, that means, like, only 10% are actually involving people. Um, and so there's a lifting and a shifting and a changing of the guard in terms of what people do for the critical thinking and the judgment calls that they have to make instead of the tactical, you know, work of just grinding out details. And I think that makes people happier in the work, but it also does change the work.

九、五年后的员工与组织

Um, if you were to fast-forward five years, like, what do you think is different about the employee base at Service Now or even, like, the work being done?

I think, in terms of the employee base, I think you're going to see that the net new added headcount will be dramatically reduced, and that's because the company will be far more productive. The agents are real and they will take on a tremendous workload.

So whereas to keep up with growth in a growth company like Service Now you'd have to hire thousands of people in finance and HR and the supporting functions and services just to keep up with it all — and now the agents are going to be able to do a lot of that.

So you're going to invest in things that really matter, like humans that engineer great innovations, and humans that actually manage the relationship and the importance of human-to-human connection, building trust, making promises and keeping them, and enduring a relationship and the net present value of loyalty in that relationship — that's all going to be human.

Um, but I can see a company where, you know, you don't have to really increase headcount to achieve that goal. And I do think that the bar got raised in terms of differentiating your skill set and making sure what you're capable of doing can't be easily replicated by an agent. Um, not just at Service Now, but in all businesses — if an agent can do it as good or better, that's an easy economic decision to make. Which is why, you know, there'll be 2.2 billion of these agents entering the workforce in the next couple of years. And so, you know, there's going to be a lot more agents than there will be people.

十、衡量 AI 是否在奏效

When you think about — you're just describing, you know, Service Now is growing about 20% a year, $13+ billion in revenue trailing. So, a massive business growing at scale. I think one of the things that's so interesting about this, um, SAS apocalypse narrative is, uh, you know, many of the companies, as described, are actually, like, doing really well on fundamentals. Um, what are the signals you look for in your own business besides, you know, what customers tell you they want to do with you, right? Um, like, quantitatively or otherwise — let's say, like, what we're doing at AI is working.

Well, the most important thing is we've expanded the boundaries of the platform. So we have a company that's operating globally. We operate across multiple industries. And the companies that we serve, we serve everything from IT to the employee experience to our fastest-growing TAM, customer relationship management, obviously all the work that the creators do, and security, as I talked about.

衡量信号

So what I'm looking for is the adoption of the AI control tower for business reinvention, and: are we the agentic front door to the enterprise? Are we managing the humans and the non-human identities? Are we connecting in the workflow data fabric all of the nodes that are operating in an enterprise? Um, whether it's a data lake, a hyperscaler, a language model, a system of record — all those things should go through the central nervous system, which is Service Now.

And obviously when I think about IT, we know the people, we know the places, we know the things. When I think about OT, we actually know the networks, the devices that reside on them, the critical infrastructure, the shadow IT, the disconnected controllers in the case of a manufacturing company as an example. So this amazing real-time insight and visibility into how that company is running and how we can make that company run better.

That's the ethos of everything in terms of the customer relationship: that we know more, that we care more, and that we can do more for that customer than any other enterprise company in the world. That's the game plan.

And, you know, for my money — what else do I look for? I look for the AI and the assists that the agents are doing to help the humans in the companies we serve. And I want to see that the trend line of those assists and the consumption of that innovation is really going, uh, right and up, to the north side of town, because by doing that you've solidified your position as super important to the way the company actually runs. And not to mention the fact that the shareholder value creation takes on a whole new meaning once you kick in that consumption model on top of everything else.

十一、企业当下的 AI 状态

When you, um, talk to, like, this amazing enterprise customer base that is deeply embedded with you and then, as you said, importantly, trusts you and trusts you to do more all the time, um, what is the state of play in terms of, you know, you describe a very forward-looking vision for what AI can do to transform enterprises — like, where are people today? What are the first questions they ask you? Do they believe it?

Yeah, I think that's a great point, because most people today know that they have to do something with AI. But, like, in the case of Brazil as an example — only 11% of the companies, since that was last night's meeting, have actually gotten past the experiment phase, where they're doing experiments.

And so we're seeing now that companies are going past the experiment phase into mainstream on building the agentic business. "I'm building the agents to complement the humans, and I'm driving business model innovation and efficiency that is now picking up steam."

But you have to remember it's a very diverse set out there. I mean, in public sector, they're just basically now trying to consolidate complexity and get one department to speak to another department so we don't have billions of fraud getting through the cracks. Um, other industries like healthcare as an example, we're now moving into the modernization phase, so the patient experience isn't so miserable, because the professionals that work there are great, but the systems have been holding them back.

But if you look at a financial services firm, for example, um, you know, they're moving full speed on, "What can I do with AI to fundamentally rethink how I run my company, to manage my headcount better, to get more done with a lot less?"

So it's kind of based on industry, based on geo. Different geos have, um, adopted it more quickly. The US is the leader in terms of consumption and adoption of AI, and they're moving faster. I give you a flavor of some of the industries, but I would say that it's more about modernization as a standard, and the first movers now are moving out with AI, and they're aggressive, and they're going for it.

"Going for it" 意味着什么

When you think about, like, what going for it means, are there a set of use cases that are universal across companies, or are there different things that matter to the different segments?

I think that they're looking at their business model. And what can AI do to change my business model? The ones that are first movers or early movers. Um, and headcount is definitely on the table.

Um, obviously, if you believe in the agent world, and you do increase headcount, it's because you can see that the heads are supporting the digital transformation and the inclusion of the agents. And there's more revenue to be gotten by adding them.

Yeah. There's ROI on it.

Exactly. And they're not going to add them anymore — meaning people — without a clear ROI case.

COVID 招聘的回声

And in many cases, a lot of these companies, especially if you look at the years 2020 to 2024, they hired a lot through COVID. Not a lot of people talk about this. I haven't actually heard anyone talk about it. But when they did the hiring, and everybody had a hand in this, a lot of it was virtual. A lot of it then was, "You know, keep the people. We need the people. Um, you know, there's a crisis out there."

And now they've realized they've layered up the companies. Um, some of the people maybe didn't get as close a look as they should have, because, you know, Zoom was the way to meet them, greet them, and hire them. And now with the inclusion of AI in the business model and the innovation of the companies, it's kind of like, "How do we get leaner? How do we get smarter?" And if AI does make us better, we should see that in the ultimate profitability of the company, the revenue per employee of the company. And the company should have expanding margins, expanding or re-accelerating revenue, and should be in a more exciting growth story than it was 2, 3, 4, 5 years ago. So there's high expectations for what AI can do.

十二、客户对话过去一年的变化

What is the biggest thing that's changed in customer conversations for you about AI over the last year right now?

I think it's just: tell me what I need to know. Um, it's not like, "Let me come in and discover your problems," or, "Let me bring you a solution 'cause I have one in my pocket and I can't wait to share it with you." Um, it's like, "You know my business. If you don't know my business, there's no conversation. But let's assume you know the business. Be very prescriptive. Tell me very specifically at the macro level — depending on the level you're talking to, how do I transform the company in keeping with the CEO vision or the management team's vision? At a departmental level, you really understand — whether it's sales, it's human capital, it's engineering, or it's technology, or CTO and its orientation — you really understand the landscape, and you have very quick solutions that can get up and running and deliver the value in a highly predictable manner. And it's AI-driven."

Like, they have to have AI in there, because they know the AI is the force multiplier. And so I would say the dance, um, has gotten brief. The execution is where they want to spend their time. "Get me there fast."

Uh, pretty impatient set of customers, but they sound like they're pretty decisive, given the parameters.

Real decisive, and real fair. I mean, you know, very open-minded. "If you can help me, do it, because I need the help." Everyone will tell you they need the help. They're not telling you they have all the answers. So, it's our job again to know more about this than they do. Otherwise, why do they need us in the room?

And then you have to have a position that you take that brings something unique and variable to the equation. And I always tell people: if two people are in the same room at the same time with the same opinion, one of them is redundant.

十三、好奇心与一日生活

Uh, I want to, um, well, I want to close up with, like, two, um, maybe more personal questions. Uh, you have a lot to cover with Service Now, but you're also just an interested guy. What inspires you or makes you curious in AI or elsewhere in technology, um, outside of what you're doing with Service Now?

Well, I'm fascinated by so many things. Um, what really interests me in technology is how it will truly improve the world, the human condition. Um, it could be an environmental conversation. It could be what we're seeing happening in outer space. It could be, uh, a global corporation trying to do unique things in a world in new places that they've yet to conquer, but they can do so with technology now and AI. And they can simulate a presence that might not actually be physical, but it could be unique, virtual, and new. There's new routes to market. There's new channels. There's so many new things that could be done.

So I think that this is the era where everything really does evolve, and possibilities exist where there just wasn't possible 3, 4, 5 years ago. Um, so I find that, you know, it goes back to the quote that I put in the book, um, since you mentioned Winner's Dream — when, uh, the great Robert Kennedy once said, you know, "Some men see things as they are and say why. I dream things that never were and say why not." So I always try to look at things that didn't exist or don't exist with the application of technology, or just a great dream. What's possible?

一日生活

Uh, and then, in terms of just the day-to-day in between your dreaming, um, what is the day in the life of Bill McDermott as Service Now CEO look like?

Well, it depends on where you're physically at at the time, um, based on what you're doing, because the time zones are all different. But if you're in, um, California, which we are today together, um, you know, you start your day off, um, really easy to understand this — with Europe. You migrate yourself then to the United States, and you wrap up with Asia. Um, so you always have to keep the tempo around the time zones and what's happening in the global economy. I just try to be a global person and be a leader that's involved in all dimensions of the company in any time zone in the world. So that's one aspect of it.

And you're talking to your teams there, hearing what's going with customers? What's going on with —

Today? Today I've been in two out of the three of the regions of the world, and I'll finish up with the third. And absolutely, I want to hear what's going on with them, what's going on with the customer, what's going on with the business. Uh, what's going well, what's not going well. Um, based on, you know, where they're at at any one moment in time, I can see, um, and I feel things very deeply, and I'll offer my suggestions. I try not to make the decision for everybody, but I'll offer my suggestions just on observations. And then, you know, we have an open, honest, riffing relationship on ideating on something, and then ultimately we get someplace quick. You know, there's no time to screw around. We got to get to the point and move on.

客户与员工

Um, a lot of time with customers. You know, I feel that I really get grounded with the customers and understanding what they're trying to accomplish and how I can help them. And, you know, a lot of people talk about, you know, sales and the idea of running corporations — it's all based on the customer and the customer alone. They determine whether you win or lose. And if you never lose that in your heart, you're closer to success than failure.

Um, the other thing is, I listen to the employees, you know. Um, for example, yesterday I started my day with 17 conversations with 17 quota-carrying reps.

Wow. That's a lot. Okay.

  1. And I got 72 on my calendar this month. 72.

Okay.

And it's inspiring, because anytime you need to feel good, talk to the people that are closer to the customer, and you realize just how great your company is, how great the people are, how smart they are, and, you know, they have so many good ideas, and they're doing so many good things. You know, one woman said to me yesterday, you know, she was most proud of the health check of her customer, and she told me all about the customer. The health check is our heuristics around customer satisfaction, the performance of the system, and so forth. And it just inspired me.

And then, you know, always try to do, you know, one-to-one, one-to-few, and one-to-many. And last night, I, you know, wrapped up my day with, um, 800 executives from Brazil, and it was, like, amazing.

领导力的当下时刻

And they wanted to, you know, talk about leadership, and many of the things that, you know, you want to talk about. And I think everybody is in, uh, in the mode right now where they want to learn, they want to experience what you think, and they want to grow, and they are uncertain, and to some extent, you know, they don't have all the answers, and sometimes they're even afraid.

And I explained to them, you know, don't look for the escape hatch. You know, this is the moment when leaders really matter, because as the waters get choppy, you know, we see who's tough and who's not. And when the tide goes out, you want to be fully dressed. And even if things don't look so good, you'll figure it out if you're fully dressed and you're ready for the battle.

And I think that this is bringing out the best in real leaders, and I live for this moment.

You seem like a leader for that moment. And I love the confidence that you have that other leaders want to meet that change, too.

Thank you, Sarah.

Awesome. Thanks so much, Bill.

It was an honor meeting you, and, uh, spending this time with you. Thank you very much.

节目尾声

Find us on Twitter at @NoPriorsPod. Subscribe to our YouTube channel if you want to see our faces. Follow the show on Apple Podcasts, Spotify, or wherever you listen. That way, you get a new episode every week. And sign up for emails or find transcripts for every episode at no-priors.com.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、关于"删错容忍度"的反常识洞察
McDermott 在访谈中抛出一个表面平实、内里却极具颠覆性的判断：**"人们会原谅人犯错，但永远不会原谅软件犯错。"** 这句话表面是为 ServiceNow 的确定性工作流辩护，但它实际上揭示了 AI 时代企业软件的一个隐秘悖论——**LLM 越像人，企业用户对它的容错度反而越低**。
为什么？因为人类犯错时携带"上下文与责任"，软件犯错时只携带"故障"。这意味着 LLM 的"拟人化"是一把双刃剑：交互越自然，用户越倾向于以"机器标准"而非"人类标准"评判它。这与心理学中的"恐怖谷效应"形成了跨领域的精妙呼应——**机器人越像人，我们越对它的不像人之处感到不安**。在企业软件领域，这就形成了"信任谷"：模型能力越接近人类决策，每一次幻觉的伤害反而越大。
这解释了一个被市场低估的事实：**SAS apocalypse 的真正护城河不是代码量，而是"可被起诉的责任结构"**。
## 二、跨领域连接：从"街角小店"到"AI 控制塔"的拓扑同构
McDermott 全场都在讲熟食店、500 个顾客、Xerox 接待员，看似是怀旧叙事。但

item.title: [WhynotTV] 翁家翌：OpenAI，GPT，强化学习，Infra，后训练，天授，tuixue，开源，CMU，清华｜WhynotTV Podcast #4
item.dc:creator: WhynotTV
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的观察：他不是"叛逆者"，而是把"反叛"本身做成了系统

读这篇访谈最容易产生的误读，是把翁家翌看成一个"反GPA、反PhD、反学术体系"的反叛者。但仔细看会发现一件极其有意思的事：他从来没有真正"反"过任何系统——他做的事，是把自己抽离出旧评估系统的同时，立刻给自己装一个新评估系统。

  • 中学：用"未来投资回报率"代替"当下成绩"
  • 大学：用"GitHub 三位数 stars + 论文 + 竞赛"代替 GPA
  • OpenAI：用"模型 release 上署名次数"代替职级与论文数
  • 人生终局：用"死时多少人记得我的名字"代替财富/地位

注意，他不是"无目标地自由"，而是永远活在一个被自己设计的奖励函数里。这其实是 RL 工程师最深的人格烙印——他对待自己人生用的方法，和他对待 PPO 训练用的方法是同一套：定义一个 reward signal，去最大化它，避免 reward hacking，并且有意识地审视这个 reward 是不是被外部 saturate 了。

换句话说，他真正的天赋不是数学，不是工程，而是给自己设计训练目标的能力——这件事大多数人一辈子做不出来。

二、跨领域连接：组织管理 = 代码库维护 = 知识树搭建

访谈里有一句被埋得很深的话："管理一家公司和管理一个 codebase 其实非常相似，都是 consistency。" 这句话如果只读字面意思会浪费它。

把他散落在访谈各处的话连起来看，会发现他在用同一个本体论解释三件完全不同的事：

领域 核心隐喻 衰败的根因
学习数学 知识树（root → branches），用 shortcut 跳过中间层 树不完整就强行用顶层结论
维护 Tianshou / OpenAI infra 代码一致性，"改一个地方只能改这里" 多人协作 context 不传递，复制粘贴导致腐烂
管理 OpenAI 这种 3000 人组织 信息流动不失真，自上而下、自下而上对齐 人多了 context 不一致，infra 臃肿，组织也跟着臃肿

这是一个被严重低估的洞见：他实际上认为，人类一切系统的失败模式只有一种——一致性丢失，而所有解决方案的本质也只有一种——让一个有完整 context 的"主语"贯穿始终。所以他在访谈最后说"也许未来每家公司都让一个无限 context 的 agent 当 CEO"——这不是科幻畅想，这是他用过去十年所有工程经验得出的逻辑必然结论：人脑的 context 长度，是一切大型组织衰败的根因。

三、最值得停下来想的悖论：决定论者为什么还要"投资未来"

这是访谈里最锋利、也最容易滑过去的部分。他坚信世界是决定论的（"我下一个词说什么，宇宙大爆炸那一刻就定了"），但他同时最擅长投资未来——七年级学高中数学，大三建 Tianshou，疫情期间写 tuixue.online，每一次都是为了一个"还没到来的自己"。

这构成一个看似无解的悖论：如果一切已被写定，投资未来的努力本身也是被写定的，那"投资"还有意义吗？

但他给出了一个比哲学家更精彩的解法——他用"未来的我给过去的我发消息"来重新定义因果。在四维时间里，因果不必是单向的：可能是未来已经成功的他，把信号塞回过去那个高三晚上的他，让他突然冒出"impact 才是终极指标"这个念头。在这个框架里，"投资未来"和"被未来召唤"是同一件事。

这其实和他做 RL 的方法论暗合得让人发凉——RL 的本质就是用未来的 reward 反向塑造现在的 policy。一个用 reward signal 反向训练自己人生的人，最后形成了一种"被未来训练"的世界观，几乎是必然的。换句话说，他的形而上学，是他职业的副产品。

额外的意外角度

最后留一个让人后背发凉的小观察：他在访谈中无意间说了三次几乎相同的话—— - "ideas are cheap"（在谈研究员可被替代时） - "tuixue.online 技术上几乎不算系统，几行 PHP 就够了"（在谈创业时） - "如果方向对，bug 修完了，算法可能根本不用改"（在谈 infra 时）

这三句话，本质是同一个信念：真正稀缺的从来不是"想法"或"技术"，而是"把正确的事持续做下去的执行力 × 时间"。 这个信念解释了他从竞赛、开源、到 OpenAI infra 的所有选择。也解释了为什么他对"AGI 还差什么"的回答如此朴素——不是新算法，是 bug-fixing throughput。在这个时代，这句话本身就是一记重锤：当所有人都在追逐范式革命时，他在告诉你，决定胜负的依然是"谁的代码 bug 更少"。

📝 转录文本

翁家翌：OpenAI、GPT、强化学习、Infra、后训练｜WhynotTV Podcast #4

开场金句

A lot of OpenAI model releases have my name on them. That's because inside OpenAI, I built the entire post-training RL infrastructure.

So across the whole post-training RL stack, you were the key contributor.

Yes. Because I think information asymmetry should be eliminated. Information asymmetry is very useful if you're trying to get by at Tsinghua. It's a huge advantage. But I think everyone should have equal access to that information.

Even if I knew then what I know now, I still couldn't have done it.

No, it's not that I didn't want to publish papers. I just felt publishing papers was completely meaningless.

Back then, High-Flyer said they wanted to start an AI lab, which later became DeepSeek. Right, and I got an offer.

If you want to go into industry, then doing a PhD is a waste of your life.

Teaching a researcher to do good engineering is harder than teaching an engineer to do good research by a lot.

Every company's infrastructure has bugs to some degree. And whoever fixes bugs, the more bugs they fix, the better their models train.

主持人引言

Hello everyone. Today's guest is Jiayi Weng. He joined OpenAI in 2022 and has been a core contributor behind a series of OpenAI models. From ChatGPT to GPT-4o to GPT-5, behind every model leap you've seen, he was there. And his core contributions can be summed up in three keywords: reinforcement learning, post-training, and infrastructure.

But to me, Jiayi Weng isn't just someone who makes models stronger. Before joining OpenAI, he had already influenced countless people through open source and code. From open-sourcing assignments and materials at Tsinghua to try to break information asymmetry, to open-sourcing Tianshou, the open-source RL library, and building a free visa lookup system, he sees code and tools as a form of charity.

In this episode, we'll start with Jiayi Weng's childhood, then his path through Tsinghua and CMU, and then his firsthand experience after joining OpenAI in 2022. At the center of the global AI storm, what has he seen? This is WhynotTV Podcast. Now come with me into Jiayi Weng's world.

一、童年：奥数、System 1 与"投资未来"

Hi Jiayi. Welcome to WhynotTV Podcast.

Hello hello.

One funny thing about this episode is when I was preparing the outline, I used GPT-5 Deep Research. So in a way, the product and model you helped build as a core developer were doing Deep Research themselves to come up with questions and interview you. You're the person behind them, their developer. I think that's a pretty surreal loop.

We'll cover a lot today, from your childhood, to your undergraduate years at Tsinghua, to grad school at CMU, and then joining OpenAI in 2022, and all the core work you've done since. I want to start at the very beginning, with your childhood. Can you tell me what kind of kid you think you were?

I really loved Olympiad math as a kid. Olympiad math. I started studying it in first grade. At first, my parents had me sit in on an Olympiad math class at an after-school program and a Chinese class. I had zero interest in the Chinese class, but I was really into Olympiad math. So I signed up. I kept taking it from first grade through sixth grade, and in middle school too. Then I realized I could do math problems faster than anyone else.

Back in elementary school, yeah, back then, for example, when other kids were doing mental math, like in second grade, they might not even be finished yet, maybe only halfway through, and I'd already be done. It was the kind of thing I didn't even have to think about. In today's terms, you'd call it System 1. Right, it was all at the surface level, I'd look once and the answer would pop out. And that gave me a huge sense of accomplishment in math.

Because I think I'm someone who, compared with other people, learns relatively slowly. How should I put it? When I learn something new, I often need two or three times as long as other people. Honestly, it's still like that now. For example, when I read a piece of code, I need a lot more time than other people to understand it — all of it, the whole thing, the full context. But once I understand it, I can use it very quickly.

This isn't a perfect analogy, but, for example, when I was a kid, we often had to memorize texts, whole passages. Before going to bed, I'd try every possible way just to stumble through the whole thing once. Even with lots of pauses, as long as I could remember it, I'd sleep on it, and wake up the next day knowing it cold.

My thinking back then was, since I needed more time, I had to start learning earlier than others. So back then, I started high school math in middle school, and I'd go straight to my middle school math teacher with all kinds of high school math questions. He was always happy to answer, and I was very grateful. By eighth grade, I'd already finished the high school material, and in ninth grade I started calculus.

In math, were you naturally just faster at it, faster than other people, while at the same time feeling you learned things more slowly?

I think it was more that I needed longer to build my knowledge tree. Normally, a knowledge tree has a root, then it branches upward, then branches upward again, and again, layer after layer. Say you take a top-level conclusion and apply it to a problem, you have to run through the whole chain and slowly figure it out. But I might just build a shortcut, go straight there, and skip that whole process.

So from early on, you realized you might be smarter than other people?

No. I never felt that.

So you're saying you just found it interesting. Then where did the core motivation to learn math early come from?

I wanted to invest in the future. I wanted to invest in my own future.

So in eighth grade, you already realized that learning high school math early was an investment in the future.

Right. It would help with my future studies. Rather than wasting time grinding through exercises now, I'd rather learn something useful for the future, where the payoff later might be bigger.

Was that something your parents told you?

No. My parents didn't really care. They just didn't pay much attention to how I studied.

But I'm still really curious. Why was it, in middle school, that you already had this idea of investing in the future? Because, I'm sure you had room to spare then, right? Why not just go play? Why not basketball or video games? Why were you thinking I should study high school math and all that?

First, because I was interested in it. I just felt elementary math was too boring, and, how should I put it, slightly more interesting math was a lot more engaging.

Then where did that interest come from? Was it innate, your interest in math?

I guess you could say that.

Or was it that, back in elementary school, you got a sense of achievement from it, and that got you hooked?

You could say that. There was an initial sense of achievement, an initial reward. Then slowly, you start building out your math skill tree, unlocking it bit by bit, because each point gives you positive feedback. Then you unlock another point, get more positive feedback, and you just keep going down that path.

But with something like basketball, I tried it, but I didn't get much positive feedback. I kept getting destroyed by my roommate. And as for sports, I practiced taekwondo. Taekwondo was okay, but once it came to sparring, I'd just get beaten up.

So instead, it was that kind of positive feedback: you were good at it, and you didn't mind it, so gradually it became an intrinsic interest.

Right.

二、初识编程：七年级与一段计算机课

So it wasn't really your family environment, it was something you developed on your own. That's the math side. You started learning high school math early in middle school. So when you got to high school, why did you become interested in programming?

I started programming in seventh grade. Yeah, in seventh grade. That was because when I was moving up to middle school, I went to Shidai Middle School. It was a private school, and since it was private, they had a programming class. So I just signed up for fun.

That makes sense. There are informatics competitions in high school, but programming in middle school didn't help much with admissions, right?

Right right. Not much.

Was that class something you found yourself, or did your parents find it for you?

My parents thought I might be interested in it. So I gave it a try. And I thought it was pretty good.

三、高中：OI、省队与那枚铜牌

All right, let's talk about high school. In high school, what I'm actually most interested in is your experience with OI provincial selection and NOI. Could you tell us about that? Why did you get into competitions in the first place?

Because there was college admissions pressure. If you didn't do competitions, then for someone outside Beijing, getting into Tsinghua or Peking University was extremely hard. Basically impossible.

So you did OI because of admissions pressure?

Yes yes. Mostly because of admissions pressure. I'd already been doing math competitions and OI before that. Then in tenth grade, I spent some time on math competitions. I got some decent results. But if I wanted to go further, to the provincial selection in math competitions, our school didn't have the foundation for that, and it was hard to self-study. So I gave it up. And beyond that, for math problems above the provincial-selection level, I really struggled with them. Because I hadn't started that early, and studying them felt exhausting.

You mean even starting high school math in middle school still wasn't early enough?

Starting competition math in middle school might have been early enough. I just didn't start that early. So after weighing it up, I gave it up.

So you went into OI, the informatics competition track, and focused on that. Could you walk us through how admissions through informatics competitions usually work, what steps are involved, starting from provincials?

First there's NOIP, uh, not the beginner division, the NOIP Advanced Group, which is the provincial qualifier. It's usually around October. After that, each province has its own selection criteria for choosing a provincial team for nationals. As for the provincial selection, Fujian had two rounds. One during winter break, and another in April. After the selection, in May or June, there was usually a Tsinghua or Peking University summer camp. You could choose one. You could get some admissions concessions there. At least that was how it worked then. Then in July or August came the NOI nationals.

So tell us, through that process, what was it like for you? The first round, second round, third round?

NOIP was okay, right? NOIP itself was okay, because I'd been doing it since middle school, so it was fine.

And provincials?

As for provincials... In tenth grade I didn't even get past provincials. I basically couldn't solve anything. Hardly anything. Then in eleventh grade I could do a little, mostly by hacking things together. And I used some heuristics. For example, there was a problem called Minimum Two-Circle Cover. And I got 70 points on it. At first I think I had the top score overall, because I solved that problem, and that got me onto the provincial team.

But after making the provincial team, the next step was Tsinghua's summer camp. That went pretty well. I got a 60-point reduction offer. An unconditional 60-point reduction. And a conditional top-tier cutoff offer.

How does that work?

It basically adds 60 points to your Gaokao score if you didn't get direct admission. Then, if that adjusted score cleared Tsinghua's cutoff that year, Tsinghua would admit you. And the conditional top-tier offer meant that if, at NOI, you placed in the top 150, which I think was the silver-medal cutoff, then you'd get the top-tier offer. As long as your Gaokao score cleared the top-tier cutoff, you'd be admitted unconditionally. But if you won gold, you didn't even need that. You'd get direct admission.

Did you get that conditional top-tier offer at NOI?

Unfortunately no. Because this is another embarrassing story. I was the lowest-ranked person on Fujian's provincial team. Fujian had only one bronze medal that year. That was me. Everyone else got silver or above.

Did that setback affect your college plans at the time?

It did, quite a bit. Because at that point, in the second half of eleventh grade, I hadn't been studying my regular school subjects, and I wasn't sure about my future either. I had no idea what I'd score on the Gaokao. There were earlier examples. Some older students, for instance, ended up needing all 60 bonus points, with not a single point to spare. I thought, what if that happened to me too? That felt pretty risky. I was really afraid of that uncertainty.

But after thinking it through, I felt like I probably wasn't that bad. So I gritted my teeth and chose Tsinghua's 60-point reduction.

So your other option was something safer?

The safer option was right there at NOI, signing a top-tier-cutoff offer from Shanghai Jiao Tong.

So was that a really hard decision?

Not too bad. Mainly, on the mental side, I was pretty upset at the time. And the uncertainty about the future really scared me. But with my family's encouragement, I still chose it. I went with Tsinghua's 60-point reduction.

高三：iPad 上敲代码的训练

So after OI was over, after the competitions ended, by senior year you stopped touching any of this and went all-in on the Gaokao?

Not really. I'd still sneak back to it. Like, for example, I was really into code optimization. I had unusually high standards for it. I would even get to the point where on an iPad, since Safari doesn't have a compiler, I'd just use the iPad keyboard and type the code straight in. Then I'd submit it directly. No compiling.

And that whole stretch really trained my thinking. Because it required a complete grasp of the whole problem, and if something went wrong, you had to locate it fast. It really sharpens your reactions. I felt that was great training for me.

But for you, you didn't really need that kind of training then.

Right right. It was just something I was genuinely interested in. For example, in OI there's this thing called constant-factor optimization. Like, with a given algorithm, even if the time complexity is still O(n^2), in practice there's still a constant factor in front. And in OI, the judging reflects that. For example, submissions are ranked by runtime on the test cases. The fastest one comes first. And if two are equally fast? Then they go by code length. So I'd optimize both metrics. Grinding that was really fun. Not that it was useful, but it was fun.

You were built for this. You really liked it, right? It actually made you happy.

Yeah.

四、清华：开源作业、信息差与导师的指引

Let's talk about Tsinghua. If I asked you about starting undergrad at Tsinghua in 2016, what would you say stands out now as something especially memorable from your first year or two? What's the first thing that comes to mind? If there is one.

I open-sourced all my homework. People had different opinions about that. For example, some older students were pretty against it. But I felt I should do it. What I did was take everything I'd collected, all those ancient homework sets, and old course materials, and open-source it all on GitHub. I left out anything with copyright issues, but everything else went public.

Why?

Because I thought we should break that information gap. That information gap if you're trying to survive at Tsinghua, is incredibly useful. But I think everyone should have equal access to that information. Even now, if you go back and grab any CS undergrad and ask them, do you know who donated the new CS building? Even if his name is on the building, do you know who he is? No. Do you know Jiayi Weng? Probably. After all, everyone survived off my homework. [Laughs]

That's more useful than donating a building. [Laughs] This is where the comments should be flying by: "+1 if I've used or seen your homework." People should be posting that right now. But why? Why did you open-source your homework? Why is that fundamentally about information gaps?

I wanted to close that information gap. I didn't want the people after us to keep scrambling like that. A lot of people just aren't great at gathering information, but they're actually very capable. And really, if you can give those people a fair shot at equal access to information, they can do a lot better at Tsinghua.

So by equal access to information, you mean knowing things like previous years' assignments, or what upperclassmen say the key exam topics used to be, that kind of information, and homework, and assignments the professor never said couldn't be shared.

Otherwise, you can easily spend ten or twenty hours on something, and still not dare ask the TA, so you just don't know what to do. Then you end up spending huge chunks of time just going down rabbit holes, and it actually doesn't help your learning much. The payoff is tiny. So I felt that back then, if I open-sourced my own homework, I could probably help people like that and help a lot more younger students later on have more time of their own to do what they actually wanted, instead of, you know, doing all this kind of life-wasting stuff.

So you really pulled it off. Your homework has basically become a widely circulated GitHub repo at Tsinghua by now.

Yeah.

进入科研：朱军老师的实验室与一次"误打误撞"选了 RL

Other than that, I also noticed you probably started sometime in your first few years getting into research and all that. When was that?

Probably sophomore year. After that, I asked some older students which labs they recommended. At the time, an older student gave me three names: Professor Jun Zhu, Professor Jie Tang, and Professor Peng Cui. I just assumed they were ranked by how impressive they were. Maybe that wasn't actually true, but that's how I took it then.

So you thought the order meant something?

Yeah, I thought the order mattered. Then I applied for a CS Academic Rising Stars program, and I think all of them accepted me. So I chose Professor Jun Zhu.

And in sophomore year, I first met with Professor Jun Zhu and asked what directions there were. There were three: Bayesian methods, GANs, and reinforcement learning. Those were the three options. At the time, I actually wanted to work on GANs. I thought image generation was really interesting. But I didn't know which one was GAN. So I picked RL.

What do you mean you didn't know which one was GAN?

I really knew nothing back then. I was a total beginner. I just stumbled into RL by accident. There was nothing special about it. I just thought RL was the thing for images. That was it.

So it was a pretty random choice.

Yeah exactly. But once I got into RL, I realized it was this game-playing thing. Mm. I thought it was pretty interesting. So I kept going.

But actually, my original thinking was that there were three big directions I really liked. One was artificial intelligence, or AI. Another was graphics, and the third was cybersecurity.

黑客精神：从校园网漏洞到 16K 渲染

Cybersecurity. I thought hackers were really cool. So when I was in college, in my spare time, I also did a lot of cybersecurity stuff. I even fixed quite a few bugs in the campus network. For example, there was this thing I fixed: a loophole in transcript downloads, this one-cent transcript thing. A transcript normally cost 10 yuan. A senior and I found a bug. And then you could get one for one cent, or even download it for free. Later on, after trying it a few times, we fixed the bug. Yeah. We reported it directly to the school's academic affairs office.

So you really enjoyed the hacking process. Cybersecurity is one kind of hacking. What about the others?

As for graphics, I felt that was hacking too, just hacking reality. From your own perspective, you can build the scene in your head, and it makes you feel like God. Like you're the creator. Back then, graphics was what interested me most. Because when I was in middle school, I saw a movie called TRON: Legacy. It was a sci-fi movie, and the visual effects were incredible. It completely blew me away. And I thought, if one day I could make effects like that, or build a virtual world like TRON, a world of my own, then that would be it for me. I'd feel complete. So I felt graphics was a way to make that happen.

So, I became really interested in graphics. Then in my sophomore year I took graphics, and because I liked it so much, I ended up spending a huge amount of time on the final project. And I think I got one of the only two A+'s in the class. I even came up with a new algorithm that could reduce the number of iterations to converge. And I used a ton of compute to render an ultra-high-resolution image, I think it was 16K. And before that, no one had rendered a 16K image before. And it had zero noise.

You were so into graphics then. Did you keep going with it later?

No. Because I felt that if you want to do research, it's better to stay focused. You can't split your focus.

第一次做 RL：VizDoom 与"调参炼丹"

Because by then you'd already joined Professor Jun Zhu's group?
