I'd already joined Professor Jun Zhu's group. There was a project on VizDoom. It's basically Doom, a game from the '90s. There was a competition then to use a neural network on a fixed map to beat the game. There was a spawn point and an end point. You had to kill monsters on the map, pick up medkits, avoid obstacles, and finally reach the end and clear the level.

So this was basically your first RL project?

Yes. We won first place.

So back then, you really enjoyed doing RL research?

No.

Why?

I really didn't enjoy it. Because in that setup, first, the task and the environment were far too narrow. And you had to overfit like crazy. And then basically, you had to use all kinds of tricks to keep training from collapsing. And even if training didn't collapse, you still had no idea how to tune it right. It really was alchemy. Compared with CV, the tuning was maybe 10 or 100 times harder. And it was all black magic. You had to rely on very ad hoc heuristics to avoid corner cases.

So that was when you realized RL research back then was mostly heuristics and tuning.

Yeah exactly. Actually changing the algorithm wasn't the core issue. And because the tasks back then were too narrow, and most of the time, they just didn't work. The tasks back then, the first one was of course VizDoom. And after that, was it things like Atari, DeepMind Control Suite, and MuJoCo? I think MuJoCo and Atari were both easier than VizDoom. Yeah, VizDoom was one of the harder ones. But in a case like that, for humans, it's a simple task, but for RL, it's an extremely hard task. Because it requires a lot of prior knowledge. You need things like knowing what counts as an obstacle. For humans, that takes no thought. But for RL, well, or for AI more broadly, that's a completely different level of difficulty.

Right. So even back then, you already had a clear sense that the environments were too narrow, and the algorithm wasn't the bottleneck.

That's what you realize once you do it: there are just too many challenges. Right, so I didn't really enjoy the research process. So I deliberately shifted my focus to how to help this kind of research move more smoothly on the process side. So I by my senior year, was thinking about building a set of RL infrastructure or a small RL library to make it easier for people who wanted to grind in this field. That way, I felt this was my strength. I was very good at this more software-engineering side of things. I could refactor code, make it easier to use, and improve the user experience. But when it came to how to tune these things, I had a visceral aversion to it.

It wasn't that you were bad at it. You just didn't like it and resisted doing it.

Exactly.

大三 Mila：一次"被错配"的 NLP 暑研

And that's why you built Tianshou in your senior year. We'll come back to Tianshou in a bit. Right. So you started doing research in your sophomore year, working on VizDoom, and by junior year, you were applying for summer research positions?

Yes.

What was that period like for you?

Applying for summer research was mainly for my senior-year applications. And then I sent a ton of cold emails, but nothing much came of them. In the end, my advisor helped connect me with Yoshua Bengio, and then I went there. Yeah. Actually, that summer research stint wasn't all that successful either.

That was at Mila, right?

Yeah, it was at Mila. That was in 2018. No 2019. The summer of 2019. In the summer of 2019, first of all, in 2019, Yoshua won the Turing Award in April or May, I think. And I had applied before that, so that was fortunate.

And later, Yoshua gave us — the task he gave me and the postdoc supervising me was to build something like an MoE. There was no MoE yet back then. First, it had nothing to do with RL. Second, it was pure language modeling, just Transformers. And there was no MoE back then. We didn't know what MoE was or how it worked. But he had this MoE-like idea: there'd be a router that could choose different paths, and he asked me to implement it.

So he hired an RL person to do NLP.

Yeah. I thought that was weird. And I had to spend — I'm not quick at learning new things, so it took me a long time to get up to speed on Transformers and NLP. Then I tried to cobble something together. I did get something working, but it didn't work very well. Looking back now, for something like that to work, first, you need compute. You need strong engineering. And you need to scale it up. Back then one person had only a few GPUs. You couldn't scale something like that. So there was no way it would work. Even if the direction was right, you still couldn't make it work.

But don't you feel like somehow fate was pushing you toward the technical path OpenAI is on now? You started with RL, then Yoshua had you do NLP. Those two pieces, somehow, before OpenAI, you'd already experienced both.

If you really want to put it that way, sure, you can force that interpretation. Ha ha. But that's just hindsight.

But back then you obviously couldn't see the future, so what was your view of NLP after that summer?

I felt NLP tasks were too scattered. And back then a lot of people were thinking about how to apply RL to language model training, because that problem wasn't solved yet. Because with Transformers, if you ran them directly in RL, they would collapse very easily. And nobody had figured out how to stop that from happening. Later it turned out your environment had to be pure, and your reward had to be a very strong reward signal.

What do you mean by a pure environment?

For example, text-only. Pure text. So the less pure setups people used before, you mean things like using Transformers for Atari decisions, for example by turning images into embeddings, or taking the Atari game's internal state and representing it structurally, then feeding that into a Transformer and running it. And the Transformer was randomly initialized.

At the time, first, I didn't have enough context. I didn't understand the field well enough. So I really struggled with it. I think even if I could go back now and help my past self, it would probably still be the same.

You mean back then you were in a situation where there was just no way to make it work?

Right right. There wasn't enough compute, and back then our understanding of MoE wasn't enough either. And put it this way: even if we'd had what we know now back then, we still couldn't have done it. The engineering wasn't there. And the compute wasn't there either.

申请季：只拿到 master offer

Then after the summer research ended, you went back to Tsinghua to prepare applications. What state were you in then? Was the pressure intense?

I really wasn't in a good place. Because the summer project hadn't worked out. And classmates around me, like people who went to Stanford or did summer research at CMU, had all gotten a lot done — and publish papers. They all had first-author papers. Usually, you needed a first-author paper. Otherwise you'd get screened out. It was really cutthroat. There was no way around it. So, after my summer research, I actually only got one recommendation letter, and I did not even know what it said. So, I really was not sure.

How did the applications turn out?

I applied for PhD programs, but I only got master's offers.

Because at the time I was only a sophomore. When you were applying, I think I was just a freshman or sophomore. And every year on Zhihu there were posts like, how did your 19 Fall or 20 Fall applications go? That was when I saw your answer. It really stuck with me. Back then, when I looked at your profile, it mentioned a strong rec from a Turing Award winner and all that. But then I saw your results were not great, and you did not get into a PhD. I remember thinking, wow, the competition is that intense.

For you personally, going through that application cycle, did it feel like a setback?

A little, maybe. But looking back, it was fine. I mean, first, in that environment, I really was heavily influenced by that immediate environment. A PhD was seen as better than a master's. That was basically the atmosphere at Tsinghua, that whole status hierarchy.

Yes exactly. If you are inside it, it is hard to escape that influence. And even abroad, it was treated the same as in China: a PhD was better than a master's. Because people have this ingrained belief that the higher the degree, the better, and the better it is for your future. But a lot of the time, that is not true. It really depends on what you have actually done. And back then, I was definitely a little disappointed. And it took me a while to readjust. Later on, though, I think I had always wanted to do things that made me stand out.

评价体系：GPA 不是唯一指标

So at the time, you were probably trying to break out of that fixed evaluation system.

Yes exactly. Yes. I have always felt that GPA is not the only metric. In college, you need to find a standard that fits you, and work toward that standard. Make yourself happy. Even if you rank first in GPA, you can compare yourself with past cohorts, with other departments, or with other schools. There is always someone else. GPA does not really tell you how good you are. I think this is the better way to see it.

From the demand side, for example, if your end goal is to get a job, what will employers care about? They will care more about relevant experience. They will not care that much about your GPA. It does not mean much to them. If you have experience that closely matches the role, that alone can outweigh several years of work experience, even if you are a new grad. Because the point of hiring is to bring in someone who can do the work.

So you realized that as an undergrad?

I realized it later, during my master's.

But as an undergrad, you were still under that system.

As an undergrad, I tried to break out of it. But only partly. I had not fully broken free.

What part was that?

I realized that I needed to create my own evaluation system instead of using one handed to me by others. I adopted an unofficial one, one my advisor, Professor Hang Su, suggested. Basically, I still remember this line: for evaluating a CS student, in his view, there were three metrics. One was papers. One was competitions. And one was GitHub stars, in the triple digits.

And I really think that mattered because it gave me a completely different perspective: in a different arena, I could actually do things in open source. And that let me — it gave me something that set me apart. That was better for my long-term growth. Instead of just spending a lot of time chasing GPA, just grinding for GPA.

I feel the same way. I think when I was an undergrad, I also realized very early on that GPA, especially undergrad GPA, was one of those things that three or four years later, you wouldn't even put on your resume. But during those three or four years, undergrads still have to put 80 or 90 percent of their energy into it, because whether you're looking for a job, or applying abroad, or trying to get into grad school, it's all about GPA. How did you deal with that contradiction, between something that's hugely important for three years and then, three years later, completely unimportant?

I'd put in the bare minimum. By that I mean, I'd spend the minimum amount of time to get it where I needed it, to hit that bar. Anything beyond that, I wouldn't put in even one extra minute.

Right, not one extra minute. Good enough is enough.

Good enough is enough. I mean, it's actually pretty simple. You just do the math. Like, before finals, figure out how many points you already have, and then you can decide whether that exam is worth studying for.

So you're saying if your GPA is already high enough, you'd rather just get a 60 in that class and not care?

Yeah. That's how I am. I've always been like that. Right, and then, maybe 60 isn't quite enough. Like, an 87. An 87 is a B+. And I'd already be very satisfied with that.

五、出国与疫情：Tianshou、tuixue.online 与"做公益"的执念

Do you think you broke free of that, because a choice like going abroad, was that driven by that evaluation system, or by that whole atmosphere?

No. At the time, the atmosphere was already basically that studying in China was better than studying abroad. Around 2018 or 2019, by 2019 that was already true. At Tsinghua, roughly only about 20 percent went abroad. But our class had even fewer, because of COVID, only about 5 percent went abroad.

You were in the December 2019 application cycle, right? So you were probably still waiting on offers when COVID hit. And then, say, once you got an offer, the U.S. consulates shut down. And you still chose to go abroad. That was a hard choice, because back then there was COVID, the pandemic, and international politics were also very turbulent, and your own future was tightly bound up with both. With that much uncertainty, what state of mind were you in?

I think I was probably more focused on what was right in front of me, for example, working on Tianshou at home, building tuixue.online at home, building that visa status checker. Basically, I was really just redirecting my attention so I wouldn't spend every day fixating on grand international narratives, and would focus on what was right in front of me. That probably helped me feel calmer.

Tianshou：两周写出第一版的 RL 框架

Okay. Then let's talk about two projects from that period. The first is Tianshou, and the second is tuixue.online. Let's start with Tianshou. You actually already touched on it, Tianshou. You said the motivation was that the RL scene back then was too overfit, and innovation in RL algorithms wasn't that important. Instead, what mattered was infrastructure, or just solid implementation. Tell us why you built Tianshou.

The reason was first of all, back in December 2019, I already felt, deep down, a strong urge. I thought I'd already written a lot of RL experiment code, so why not pull it all together and make my own experiments run better? That was the starting point. Then in February, in February 2020, after the January break, I took a look at RLlib's code, the RLlib under Ray. At first, I was thinking about using RLlib and seeing if I could tweak it to support my own experiments. I spent a month on it. It was too complicated. Far too much abstraction. And RLlib alone was already pushing a few hundred thousand lines. That was completely unacceptable. I had no idea how to make the changes I wanted. So I decided to forget it. I'd just reimplement it from scratch, tear it down and start over.

Right, start over. I'm really curious: when you were doing this, was there any practical calculation behind it? Because a lot of people then wanted to make some "blah blah policy optimization" NeurIPS paper.

No, I didn't want to publish papers. I thought publishing papers was completely meaningless.

Tell us why. How had you already broken out of that whole system?

First, I already had papers. I felt that one more or one less made no difference to me. And I already had enough for applications. And I had competition results too. I guess I did have a repo with a few hundred stars, like the course repo I built, but that wasn't really a serious one. I still wanted a real one in the hundreds.

Right. Okay, so with Tianshou, after looking at RLlib, you felt it wasn't usable, and you weren't interested in papers.

Right. But you were very interested in open source, in a solid, useful project that could help move the field forward. Very interested in that.

How hard was it to build Tianshou back then?

Very easy. Two weeks.

Just two weeks?

For the first version.

What algorithms did the first version have?

A lot of algorithms. You just take the paper and implement it. If your abstractions are right, then implementing an algorithm is basically under 20 lines. Less than 20 lines per algorithm.

Then how could you build a first version in two weeks when RLlib ran to hundreds of thousands of lines?

I think maybe its initial design had some problems. And then maybe once more people got involved, everyone kept contributing code, and in the end the whole project gradually decayed.

So you built it all yourself?

Yes.

So this kind of efficient solo iteration, where you get the top-level design right and then execute, you think can actually be more powerful?

Yes. I think code, or really any project, gets most of its value from consistency. If a project is consistent from start to finish, then it's a good project. A lot of project decay, comes from inconsistency. Say you have two people, or ten people. With ten people, everyone writes a bit of code, but nobody really knows what the others actually wrote. And some assumptions just don't get passed along in time. That leads to things like the same code getting copied and pasted everywhere, or other bad patterns, so the codebase just keeps bloating and starts to rot.

Tianshou kind of took off right away back then. You could say it was pretty popular. What do you think Tianshou got right?

I think it captured what users needed. Researchers, or really the people doing hands-on research work back then, all had the same need: was there something easy to use and modify, an RL framework they could use right away? And Tianshou's codebase is compact, and the abstractions are pretty decent. At least if you dig into it, you'll find that if you modify this part, it'll work correctly, and it's the only place you need to touch. And also, if you want to support your feature, this is the only place you need to change.

In other words, if someone wants to change something, the design already makes it so they can only change this spot.

Mm-hmm exactly. And for something like this, the usability is pretty good too. It has basically every algorithm you'd want to run.

So back then, for the PyTorch version, did you write the first version by yourself? And later did it gradually become more of a team project? Or did it just become open source, with the lab maintaining it?

It just became open source. There really wasn't anyone in the lab maintaining it. Right, we just open-sourced it directly.

Then how did you keep Tianshou from running into what you just described, where once too many people work on it, it stops being consistent and starts to decay?

Early on, I had enough time, so I could handle everything myself. That obviously kept it consistent. Though looking back, that wasn't ideal, not for long-term growth, but it was enough back then. Later on, my focus shifted, because once I started working, I didn't really have time anymore. So I basically handed it over and transferred maintenance to people in the community. And if they thought something made sense, they'd do it. As long as there's one person making the call, it should stay consistent.

Looking at it now, five years into Tianshou as a community effort, do you think it's decayed?

A little bit, definitely a little. Because the context I was working from and what my successor had weren't exactly the same, there was some mismatch. So he ended up rewriting some of my code. The two of us weren't actually that consistent. So there was still some decay. But I think in the long run, that's acceptable.

tuixue.online：因为我自己需要

Yeah. The second project, Ha, I used it too. tuixue.online. Why did you — Weren't you back then...

I was a sophomore. But later, during my summer research, right, I also had to book a visa appointment. And I needed the earliest available slot.

So, why did I build something like that? Because I needed it myself. It was basically the same as with Tianshou. I felt like my need — I looked all around, and I didn't know of anything that could meet it. So I hacked together a visa appointment tracker. Otherwise I had no way to do it.

Because back then you were probably in that period where one consulate would close, then another one would close.

Yeah exactly. You needed to know in real time where visa slots were available. And I figured if I needed that, like, at the beginning, I wanted to collect some ancient upperclassman's old homework. I figured a lot of people probably needed that too, so I put it out there. Then when I needed to check visa appointments, I just wrote a scraper for visa slots and open-sourced it. and let everyone use it for free.

That got really popular. I checked last night, and it had over a million total visits. It was already over a million back then. So by now it must be over ten million. Probably over ten million, right?

But it shut down a long time ago. Once COVID passed, there just wasn't as much demand anymore. So and the U.S. consulates upgraded the website, so the old scraper stopped working. And I didn't have time to rewrite it. Right. So it served its purpose.

对"impact"的执念

In a way, neither of those projects was very strategic. Right. The first one wasn't even for applications. You built it after you'd already applied. You easily could have and even though you couldn't travel during COVID, you could've spent that time doing something more relaxing. And tuixue.online was the same. You could've just stopped after making it for yourself. So you still have this really deep, very strong urge to create something you see as useful, something you yourself need, and then share it with everyone.

Yeah. I want to do things that truly have impact. Even if they lose money. Like, doing public-good work. I think projects like Tianshou and tuixue.online are public-good projects. Yeah. Completely nonprofit. Working on projects like that makes me feel deeply fulfilled.

So compared with money, impact fulfills you more.

Yeah.

How long have you had this drive for impact?

In high school.

Why did it become about impact in high school?

This may sound a little strange, but when I was in high school, one day out of nowhere, in my senior year, an idea suddenly popped into my head. Maybe it was this: sometime in the future, future me sent a message to past me, and that made me realize something. Like, if life is a game, then your final score is how many people remember your name. That's it.

That's it. What does that mean?

It means more people need to know your name.

So one day in high school, you realized that this really mattered.

I wanted that after I die, at the moment I die, as many people as possible would remember my name.

Even today, you still use that as your metric?

Pretty much.

Then I want to go a bit deeper. Why does that matter?

I didn't think it was that important back then, but I tried it a few times and realized it really is what I want. If what I make can benefit other people, then first I feel good about what I made, and second, other people affirm it too, and I get positive feedback from them.

Can you equate that directly with fame?

Not really. Because some kinds of fame or prestige like landing some position, do make you well-known, but that kind of prestige in other people's eyes may not benefit them that much. It could even be negative. What I want is more like this: Of course, I don't think you can be good to everyone. That's very hard. But I can at least try, to be good to the people around me and do things that matter to people.

So the metric is doing things that matter to people and having more people remember you.

Right.

If I were to challenge that view, I'd say, why not just live happily yourself? Why do you need other people to remember you? This urge to be remembered, to have lots of people use something useful you've built, where does it come from? Are you afraid of being forgotten?

No. I think life can also be seen as an experience. Since you're already here in this world, why not make the most of it instead of wasting the journey?

Yeah, but why does making the most of the journey require other people to remember you? I find that tension really interesting. Because the first thing you said was you wanted to break free of external standards, like GPA, like the gaokao, or whatever else. People saying a PhD is better than a master's. But on the other hand, the internal standard you've set for yourself, that intrinsic standard, is still recognition from other people.

But it's not official recognition. Not recognition from an existing system.

Yeah. It's consensus. So what you want to escape is the existing system and its rules of evaluation, but what you really want is heartfelt approval from each person. Yeah. So that kind of validation can show up as GitHub stars, or page views on tuixue.online. It can also be your Google Scholar citation count, or your OpenAI model's daily inference volume.

Right.

Do you worry that your standard could become a new orthodoxy? Maybe the older generation judged young people by GPA, and when you become the older generation, you'll judge the next generation by impact.

That's just the standard I hold myself to. I wouldn't hold others to it.

So you don't think your standard is universally applicable.

Right.

So you don't like being pushed by external standards.

Right.

Then is there a chance your own standard could start pushing you?

Not so far. If that ever happens, you can change it. You can change your own standard.

So even though you have your standards, you're not a slave to them. You have your own impact metric, but you're not trapped by it.

For example, there was actually a long period when I didn't have any open-source project. and I didn't feel that it really bothered me. I don't worry about that. I think OpenAI models are the best form of impact.

六、CMU、求职与 OpenAI 的选择

This was at Tsinghua. And then during that rough stretch of the pandemic, you got into CMU and were supposed to start in September 2020. By then the first COVID semester had passed, and classes were online, so you just stayed home and took classes remotely. For a full year, right? But even then, did you have to think about jobs from day one?

Yes.

What was that process like?

It went like this. I was pretty careless at first, and I applied to I think 18 companies. In the end I only got offers from Google and OctoML, Professor Tianqi Chen's company. And at the time I thought, if those were my only two offers, I'd definitely choose OctoML. I didn't want to go to Google.

Because it didn't seem that interesting. What do you mean by "interesting"?

You'd be a cog in big tech, doing work you don't really like, like frontend or backend work. Later I thought it through more clearly, and kept interviewing. Then I got an offer from High-Flyer. High-Flyer said they were starting an AI Lab, which later became DeepSeek. Right, I got the offer then. Right, and then but I didn't go.

You didn't go. Then you went to OpenAI. So looking back now, with perfect foresight, the choice in front of you was DeepSeek versus OpenAI.

Yes.

So even the High-Flyer offer back then wasn't for quant, it was for AI.

The High-Flyer role was RL infra. Right. If I hadn't had other offers, I would have chosen High-Flyer.

By other offers, you mean besides OctoML?

No. I mean things like OpenAI and NVIDIA too, I think. NVIDIA was also an RL systems role. FAIR was too, actually. But FAIR ultimately, for process reasons, rejected me.

OK, so your options were High-Flyer, OpenAI, NVIDIA, and TikTok. And maybe FAIR too. And among those options, you ultimately chose OpenAI. How did you make that choice?

I think... What I want to emphasize is this was before ChatGPT.

Right right. So at the time, you really didn't know ChatGPT was coming. So how did you make that choice?

First, OpenAI had long been doing RL. OpenAI and DeepMind were basically, in RL back then, the two best research labs. And I felt that if I could get in, that would already be huge. Actually, back then, I didn't really think I'd get in. So just getting in already felt amazing. And I also felt that I'd have a chance to see how the world's most cutting-edge research is actually done. Instead of being in some little workshop, like at a university, where a few PhD students cobble something together, without any real methodology for running the project. That just felt wrong to me. So I wanted to learn what a real methodology looks like for doing research in industry.

被 John Schulman 招进来

So when you joined OpenAI, was it straight into John Schulman's group?

Yes, John Schulman hired me.

He interviewed you?

Yes, and I'm very grateful to him. I mean, I'm very grateful he gave me that opportunity. Even after he left, on the day he left, I was sad the whole afternoon. I shut my laptop and did nothing.

So he clearly thought highly of you. Why? Did you ever ask him?

Yes. He felt that, first, my GitHub looked very strong. He also agreed with that evaluation logic. And for him, that was one signal in his hiring framework. Nobody had really noticed that before. Also, bringing in someone with strong engineering skills helps any project.

Any interesting interview stories? How did you impress him?

Maybe in the final round, he gave me a problem. It was a very end-to-end one, and very open-ended. He gave me three hours, and I finished it in two. I built the whole thing from scratch, and got it done. Then I showed it to him and walked him through it. A bug did come up during the demo, but I fixed it on the spot, so it was fine. Anyway, maybe he thought I had solid engineering skills and at the same time, I was pretty capable overall. So he hired me.

Also, he only used that interview question on two people. One was me, and I think the other was Andrey Mishchenko, the guy working on Codex now.

And you both passed?

Yes, both of us did. So the pass rate was 100%.

为什么不读 PhD

Alright. We'll get to OpenAI in a second, but before OpenAI, I'm really curious: did you consider doing a PhD when you were job hunting?

No.

Why not?

Once you talk to people in industry, you realize that doing a PhD, if you want to go into industry, is basically wasting your life. A master's can absolutely be your springboard, and still let you meet the bar for industry roles. For example, in your master's, or even as an undergrad, you can build up enough citations, and do some standout projects so you can compete directly with PhD candidates. Then figure out what else you can do to make them choose you with a master's instead of another PhD. I think knowing what makes you different is crucial.

So you figured out pretty early that your future had to be in industry.

Yeah. Because I felt that academia, whether teaching or being a professor, was too cutthroat and not what I wanted. And then I'd have to go raise funding for projects. And on top of that, I'd rather not do that. It just felt way too restrictive.

For example, let's say we're a company that's hiring. We have candidates with master's degrees and PhDs. Do you think those two paths train very different abilities? Because a PhD is more about developing academic skills, right? How to write a good paper, tell a complete story, make the figures look good, and present it well. Do you think that matters for a company? It probably helps in some ways, but compared with someone like you, with really strong engineering skills, how would you compare those two skill sets in today's AI era? Which one is more valuable?

In today's world, better engineering is clearly more valuable. But at that point in time, it really wasn't so clear. So what I did back then was try to cover both.

I mean, you'd published papers too, and your open-source infrastructure work was popular too. And your engineering was strong. So why do you think it's so obvious now that engineering comes first?

Let me quote a colleague of mine. Basically, he was an RL PhD, and he built a famous RL framework. What he said was: Teaching a researcher to do good engineering is much harder than teaching an engineer to do good research.

Here's why: For the kind of frontier exploration research labs are doing right now, what really matters is whether the infrastructure is correct. If the infrastructure is right, then it comes down to how many iterations you can do per unit time. Because with ideas, you can always get them by talking to people, and something useful will come out. Then you validate it. As long as you can validate well, that basically becomes your research work. You don't need to do that much original thinking yourself.

The deeper thinking is often done by people who've been in the field a long time, like Alec. He's been in this field since the GPT-1 days. So he probably has very strong research instincts. So when he thinks through a problem, it's more useful than an average PhD doing the same. So you just talk it through with him. Ideas are cheap. What matters is within a given amount of time, how many good ideas you can validate. So you need correct infrastructure, correct results, and fast iteration.

Exactly. And current PhDs often don't have that ability, or at least it isn't their focus. Because for them, it just isn't that important. I think the current academic training system is about finding a good research direction. But people in companies can have that too. If you've worked in this field for a long enough time, you develop research intuition. Then you start to see what's good, what isn't, what to do, and what not to do. So ideas are cheap. But strong engineering skills, building the infrastructure correctly, and iterating fast, once you have that, even agents can probably do the validation.

Yeah. Mm. From what I've seen, every company's infrastructure has bugs to some degree, and the teams that fix bugs, the ones that fix more of them, end up training better models. So maybe Llama can't match GPT because Llama has too many bugs. Maybe. I don't know. But that's the kind of thing I'd guess.

So you knew pretty early on, and pretty clearly, what the real issue was. For the whole pipeline to work, the key isn't algorithmic innovation but the right hyperparameters, the right infra, and a good system for fast iteration

Yeah, and then I wasn't that interested in research around hyperparameter tuning It had no appeal to me at all. I preferred selling shovels.

"卖铲子的人"：让我的名字出现在每一次模型发布上

You like having a playground where you lay the foundation and let other people play let them publish the papers and not worry about it yourself and maybe get your name on the paper too

And then you realize that my name is on a lot of OpenAI model releases and that's because inside OpenAI I built the entire post-training RL infra

So for the whole post-training RL infra, you were the core contributor

Yes. And then because before that, everyone used that whole post-training RL infra to train RLHF models so every time there was a major release, every time a major model shipped, my name had to go on it

So you could say there's a bit of you behind every OpenAI model because you like selling shovels

Right and I was the shovel seller closest to the customer because RL sits at the very top of the infra stack It's a high-visibility spot. Right, because if you're too low-level, then your name won't get as much exposure If you build a data loader or storage layer, it doesn't have the same visibility but RL is something everyone wants

Exactly. That's smart.

I thought about that too before, how I wanted my career to develop And at the time I set another metric for myself I wanted to maximize how often my name appeared on the OpenAI blog

You're really good at designing your own reward function.

Right so once you define that reward, what do you need to do? First, you focus on infra because if you do individual research, it doesn't scale But if you do infra, everyone uses your work so that's how you scale up

Right. And RL infra was something I was good at. So it was a perfect fit So basically once you got into Tsinghua, RL infra became your main track Right.

给后来人的建议：早点进工业界

Would you still recommend that today? You've already made that choice, but a lot of our podcast audience might still be undergrads or master's students and they're still deciding between industry and academia How would you help them think through that decision, especially in 2025, under the current landscape? In the long run, I still think academia, in its current form, needs to be restructured. But right now, for ambitious young people who want to make an impact like you have, should they go get a PhD or enter industry as early as possible?

I still think entering industry early is better. Because if you do a PhD, you have no idea what the world will look like when you finish By the time you graduate, the paradigm shift may already have happened and then you'll realize what you've been working on may not matter much If your objective is to join an AI lab, then the first thing you need to figure out is what kind of people AI labs actually need If they need infra people more, then do more infra work even if you don't have a PhD because what matters more is whether your experience matches what they need and whether it's actually useful

So what kind of people do AI labs need most right now?

I think it's still infra. Right. Infra is a bottomless pit. I mean, research-wise, there are only so many people with real research intuition since ChatGPT came out, there are only a handful of people in this industry who've been at it for, say, more than three years And so the bottleneck right now is still infra can you scale up, and by how much? meaning, in a given amount of time, how many iterations you can run. And that directly determines your productivity.

That sounds like not a very PhD-friendly environment.

Yes. This also points to a gap, and I think it's something both of us have felt deeply. Because RL research in academia mostly just overfits to Atari and a few MuJoCo tasks, tuning them back and forth, just to see who scores higher at 100K. Right. But industry doesn't care about that at all. What industry does is use RL to solve real problems.

Exactly. Once I realized that, then, in August 2022, I started winding down Tianshou. Because Tianshou was still aimed at those toy benchmarks. And so I felt I should spend more time on something more meaningful, like, inside OpenAI, getting the RL infra right. So most of my effort went into maintaining it or developing the RL infra.

Honestly, that view of yours was quite ahead of its time in 2022. It definitely wasn't the consensus. Right. Why didn't you close that information gap then and write a blog post telling me to stop?

I was worried that if I said it out loud, OpenAI might call it a leak or something, just in case. I mean, it wasn't that confidential, but you know. Haha okay.

七、OpenAI 内部：从 ChatGPT 之前到 GPT-5

Let's talk about OpenAI. I'm really curious. We've already touched on it a lot, but at OpenAI, you're one of the very few people in the world who worked on ChatGPT, GPT-3.5, GPT-4, GPT-4V, GPT-4o, GPT-4.5, and GPT-5 as a core contributor on all of them. Some people contributed to the first half, others to the second half, but you were there the whole time. Your main contributions, I'd sum up in three words: reinforcement learning, post-training, and infra.

Right.

We'll get into those technologies and the stories behind them in a bit. But I want to start with the first question. It might be easy to answer, or very hard to answer: What is reinforcement learning?

If there is feedback, if you can model an environment and get feedback from it, that loop is reinforcement learning. Using that feedback to keep improving.

Exactly. Then the second keyword is post-training. What is post-training for a large language model?

Back then, post-training wasn't even a thing yet. At the time, the team was just called RL, and there was no real split between pre-training and post-training.

So when you first joined OpenAI, that wasn't a thing yet?

Right. But when you first joined OpenAI, was ChatGPT already the main focus? No. When I first joined, there was just John Schulman's RL team, working on a follow-up to WebGPT. WebGPT used a GPT-3.5 model for browsing. But for that browsing setup, if you used 3.5 directly, the results were predictably bad. Because browsing required tool calls. So we took a step back and first focused on getting the user experience right. The one thing we really had to solve was chat. And chat could be handled through instruction following, using RLHF.

So when you joined, GPT-3.5 already existed internally?

Yes. But the PPO pipeline back then was very hard to use. So what we used most at the time was GPT-3.5 SFT, and we iterated on that several times. GPT-4 only came later. And later, for GPT-4, Greg wrote dedicated infrastructure to make GPT-4's training infrastructure support RL training.

So you joined in July 2022? Right. Was all of OpenAI already all-in on GPT-3.5 then? It was still a few months before launch, right?

Right.

Internally, could you imagine ChatGPT becoming this massively successful?

No.

At the time, were you able to — I mean, you could test the model internally, right? Yes. Did you realize this was going to be game-changing?

No. I didn't feel that way then either. Because I felt like I could see a lot of obvious shortcomings. I mean, the first time I used it, I thought, okay, it's a talking model. That's about it. Then after using it a few more times, I realized it could help with some coding problems, but only to a point. It still couldn't solve that much for me. So I used it a few more times, and I didn't really want to keep using it. The problems it could solve for me were limited.

Because you already knew about it beforehand, and you saw it improve gradually, so it didn't feel that sudden to you. But for example, when I later showed it after it launched, to people around me, they felt it came out of nowhere. And that was what I didn't expect.

Yeah right. So back then, across OpenAI as a whole, was everyone all-in on ChatGPT in July 2022?

No. Only our group was working on it. You can look at the ChatGPT blog post and scroll down. There's a contributors section. John Schulman should be listed first, and then the next twelve names, ending with Shengjia Zhao, that whole stretch, starting with Barrett right after John Schulman, from Barrett to Shengjia Zhao, was everyone in our group.

So when was it that you realized your work at OpenAI had really made this blow up?

Probably when ChatGPT launched. I was at NeurIPS at the time. It launched on November 30. And in December, just a few days later, I realized that the people around me were all talking about ChatGPT. And they found it really useful. It even crashed OpenAI's servers several times.

It was kind of like what happened with tuixue.online. The tuixue.online servers crashed several times too. That created this kind of organic demand and word-of-mouth effect. Basically, everyone became an organic promoter. They'd promote the product on their own. And that's definitely something worth investing in, something worth putting effort into.

It actually sounds like from the time you joined OpenAI to do this, even inside OpenAI, getting something like ChatGPT out wasn't really something anyone planned.

Right. It was probably a series of half-accidental, half-inevitable factors reacting together.

Yes, and when we launched ChatGPT, it was really just to see whether we could collect some real-world user data. And after that, we might shut it down five days later if nobody used it. I mean, we expected maybe ten or twenty thousand users at first, and then it would taper off and that would be the end of it. But the actual curve was exponential.

加入 OpenAI 的第一印象 & Google 风格的"productivity playbook"

Right. All right, that was ChatGPT's sudden breakout success. I'm also really curious: what was it like when you first joined OpenAI? What was your first impression of the company?

It felt like a bigger research lab. It actually wasn't as process-driven as I expected. But there were a lot of people there with very strong research instincts, who could point out the direction, and then you just executed.

But after Barrett, Luke, and Liam came in, the three of them from Google, and joined John Schulman's RL team, our group changed. We started bringing in Google's productivity playbook and started iterating. Google really is exceptional. There was a chart showing iterations per unit time and success rate, and the two were directly proportional. So the more iterations you can do per unit time, the more your success rate rises linearly.

Right, that's basically an RL curve too. Because RL is constant trial and error.

Right, it's just you keep trying, and after enough attempts, you eventually reach your goal. A lot of cases in life are basically RL.

So back then, the productivity playbook you brought in was really a philosophy. Yes. Meaning, let's not start by chasing brilliant ideas or brilliant algorithms. Let's build solid infra first. Let infra take us from 30 iterations a week to 300 iterations a week.

Yeah roughly.

谈 OpenAI 文化：人才密度、组织结构、信息流

I saw an interview where someone asked Sam Altman what made OpenAI successful. He said OpenAI can produce major breakthroughs because in a small team with extremely high talent density, mediocre performance isn't tolerated. Do you agree with that?

I do. Because when talent density goes up, then unexpected things can emerge spontaneously. But in a different environment, say everyone is average, people just stay in their own lane and finish their own piece. And then things start dragging on.

When you joined, OpenAI was only a few hundred people then, right?

I was number 280.

You were number 280. Now OpenAI must be in the thousands.

Over 3,000 now.

Over 3,000, so ten times bigger. In three years. Do you think OpenAI can still keep that small, elite team style of making hard-core innovations?

I think the odds are lower, but not dramatically lower. You can always carve out a small team to focus on specific research.

What has OpenAI's leadership done that's been interesting to avoid that problem? Or preserve small teams, high talent density, and hard-core innovation? For example, simplifying the org structure, and canceling unnecessary meetings altogether?

Well, basically, I think org structure matters more.

What counts as an efficient org structure for hard-core innovation?

Smooth information flow.

How do you mean?

For example, if a decision gets made today, it reaches the people below without distortion. And if there's new progress below, that reaches the top without distortion too. Otherwise, the people making decisions at the top and the people doing the work below may be thinking in completely different directions, and not pulling in the same direction.

How does OpenAI achieve that kind of information flow?

First, people like Sam and Greg, they had dedicated people for that. For Sam, he used to have a dedicated research assistant to help him stay on top of the latest internal research progress. Greg, of course, on the infra side, across the whole infra stack, he had been involved in almost all of it.

Right so they're both extremely familiar with the tech.

And what matters is staying technically sharp. At minimum, they need to know how far the current work has gotten, what's new, and what it's useful for.

So this has to be the top one or two leaders themselves, who are willing to dive in all the way into the details and understand every capillary of the company.

I think managing a company and managing a codebase are actually very similar. It's about consistency. If you're not consistent, if things aren't aligned, then you end up like a person who... For example, a brick-built person whose torso moves but the feet don't, that looks very strange.

八、Post-Training 实战：GPT-3.5、GPT-4 与 RLHF

Okay, let's talk about post-training. Let's start with GPT-3.5.

But actually, the PPO for GPT-3.5 wasn't that dialed in. I got GPT-4 working first. Because GPT-3.5 was using the old infra then, and the new infra had just landed in August 2022. So I first used the new infra to get the first version of PPO working, and I ran it on GPT-4. That should have been around September 2022.

Wow, that's really interesting. So when GPT-3.5 came out, GPT-4 already existed internally. And RLHF was first made to work on GPT-4 before it worked on GPT-3.5.

Yes. But in practice, other teams had already helped us avoid a lot of pitfalls. So we reused some existing pipelines wherever we could. But for the main pieces, the core stuff, like how to train the reward model, we did that ourselves. We also collected the data ourselves. And when it came to infra issues, we handled those ourselves too.

So back then, to get RLHF working, what were the key challenges and breakthroughs?

I think the main thing was figuring out how to measure what the performance actually looked like. Because nobody knew what good performance was supposed to look like.

You mean after training a lot of checkpoints, you couldn't tell if it was actually better?

Right, because a single reward can lead to reward hacking. The reward might just saturate, and turn into a flat line. It slowly creeps upward and then flattens out. But the real situation is, if you looked at human reward, it might go up first and then slowly come back down. That's reward hacking. So you had no way to know which checkpoint was actually better than the others. So choosing checkpoints was a skill in itself.

But we didn't spend that much time on it. We just built a bunch of sampling-based evals to see what each benchmark actually looked like. But really, a benchmark was just a number. And if it crossed some threshold, that was enough. But even then, you couldn't really call it good, or call it bad, because every time you ran a model, the eval variance was huge. There was a lot of noise.

So how did you solve that in the end?

In the end, you had to inspect it yourself and interact with it a few times, and then see, for example, what your own experience felt like. Then get a few more people to look at it and have everyone vote.

So evaluating RLHF still comes down to human feedback?

Yeah exactly. That's the only way. There's no other way. Back then, the technology just wasn't there.

Toy infra vs. production infra

So this was basically your first time building production-scale infra. Right. How do you think this kind of large-scale RL infra for large models compares with what you'd built before, like single-task RL systems such as Tianshou or toy-task RL infra?

I think the difference is huge. Because for toy tasks, the bottleneck is the environment. The model is very simple, so whether you're training or sampling actions, it's all very cheap. But with large-model RL infra, the model is huge, while the environment is very simple. The environment is just a prompt. But when you're sampling from the model, you have to think about how to do it efficiently. And when you're training, you have to think about efficiency too, because just feeding in the prompt as the environment might take only a few microseconds, but running one pass of inference or training could take hundreds of seconds or even thousands. Especially if you don't have many GPUs.

So for future models, compared with GPT-3.5... Compared with GPT-4, what new challenges does that bring for RL infrastructure specifically?

I think it's still mainly about performance, or scaling, how to scale it up. How do you use more GPUs, and get higher throughput?

Right exactly. So that involves more than just RL. There's also model inference, so it's more end-to-end, and you have to go deep into implementation details to do end-to-end optimization. So what you do is really an intersectional role. There's the RL side, you need to understand RL, and you need to understand ML systems, and also how language model inference works. You need to understand all that, right?

Yeah. You probably won't be in that kind of role, to be honest, when you're just a student in school.

进急诊室的那段日子

Yeah and that role really sharpens you, but it's also exhausting.

And later on, there was a period when I was really worn out by the work. I even ended up in the ER. I was overworking myself so hard, my head was hurting, and I couldn't take it, so I went to the ER to get checked. But the ER doctor said it was nothing serious.

How intense was the workload then?

I'd wake up and start debugging, or dealing with issues, all the way until I went to sleep. Seven days a week. Maybe six days a week on average. Right? But after a while, you realize that's not sustainable. So first of all, your health comes first. You have to make sure your body stays healthy. So now I've built a habit of running 3,000 meters twice a week. But back at Tsinghua, I used to fail the 3,000-meter run in PE, and I really couldn't run 3,000 meters. But now I do it on my own, because I've realized how important it is.

I think I'm really envious of what you've done at OpenAI over the past two years, because what you get to explore is something that 99.99% of people out there, including researchers and infra engineers in this field, aren't even qualified to touch. You have this incredible advantage: you get to work on the most advanced models and optimize them, and every day you're exploring the unknown. And you know that whatever you're exploring, you're probably the first person in human history to find that answer.

But I think what I do is pretty trivial. I feel like my work is mostly day-to-day maintenance, and it doesn't really require that much raw brainpower.

Yeah, it's more like you just need to do the work well and do it right. And direction matters a lot. As long as you're headed in the right direction, just do what you believe is right. Do you think RL for large models still needs a breakthrough? Does it still need a major breakthrough, or is the paradigm already there, and we just need to scale up the infrastructure?

I think there still are. Again, you can't use the current state to predict what happens next. From here, anything could happen. There could be a new paradigm, maybe a new RL paradigm, or maybe a new pre-training paradigm. Any of that is possible. So every day you have to face unknown challenges. But having said that, I also think I'm just lucky to be in this position. If you replaced me with anyone else, as long as they had the same context I do, they could probably do the job just fine. So yeah, I don't think this is something only I could do. It's just that if you swapped in any normal person, they could probably do it too.

You're too modest. Looking ahead five to ten years, what do you think is still left to explore? Where will the biggest challenges be? What's the bottleneck in current model capability, and what kind of breakthrough do we need to see?

I think at the current stage, we still haven't fully scaled it up. Let's first wait for it to hill-climb gradually, as large-scale RL experiments play out, and see how far the performance can go. Then we can see what still needs to be done.

So what you mean is, not scaling up isn't because compute is lacking, it's because you haven't exhausted the current approach yet. First, you push the current methods and the available compute to the limit, then see how far that gets you. And there are still a lot of infra bugs. Even in your current infra, you're pretty sure there are bugs.

Right. You can't say it's 100% bug-free. I mean, it was built by humans, after all, and anyone can make mistakes. Also, you still have to fix that stuff. For example, once the team gets bigger, the context becomes inconsistent, and everyone writes weird things.

So for future large models, plus reinforcement learning, in the post-training pipeline, where do you think the biggest bottleneck will be?

I think the bottleneck is bug-fixing throughput in infra. Throughput. How many bugs can you fix per unit time, and how many iterations can you do per unit time, how many of those iterations are actually correct. Everything else is secondary. This unlocks everything else. Yeah. Whether it's the algorithms or the environment, if you fix all the bugs, you might not need to change the algorithm at all. It will just work.

重构 OpenAI 下一代 infra

So how do you improve infra efficiency? What kind of architecture do you need, what kind of people do you need, and what kind of resources do you need?

We're still figuring that out. Right. What I'm working on now is, I'm actually no longer in the very core role, but I think I should be doing something more important. So, my team is refactoring OpenAI's internal infra and building the next generation of it.

So with each generation of infra, do you tear it down and rebuild, based on what you know now, with a better top-level architecture, and then build it again like you once built Tianshou, or is it more small patches and fixes?

Right now it's a full rebuild, because the previous generation is over three years old, and it's accumulated a lot of problems. So we're hoping to use a new infra stack to clean up a lot of the old technical debt, and within a given amount of time, give researchers better iteration speed.

So researchers wouldn't be involved in the infra-building process?

They might just provide requirements. Right. But actually writing the code and doing distributed training, that isn't their job. Exactly. They might just flip a flag and be done.

That sounds like OpenAI researchers might be the first kind of job AI replaces.

Yeah. [laughs] I think so. Basically, it comes down to how many experiments you can run per unit time. Researchers mostly just come up with ideas. Right ideas. And ideas are cheap to generate. How many can one person sitting there come up with? [laughs]

And even idea generation itself can be modeled by AI. Right. And next, I think it replaces researchers first, and then infra engineers, and then everyone's replaced. But sales probably won't be replaced as easily, because sales is still about persuading someone to pay, and the person on the other side is human. AI may not be able to persuade people that well. That probably still depends on person-to-person communication, which matters more there.

Agentic RL 与 AGI 的定义

Okay, we just talked about text-only GPT-3.5, and then multimodal models. What about agentic RL post-training? How different do you think that is?

Not in any essential way. There's no fundamental difference. It's really the same thing. You just add a few tool calls in the middle, and maybe a bit more environment interaction.

Right, the environment. That's where the change is. So compared with standard LLM RL post-training, agentic RL post-training doesn't really introduce new challenges?

Right. Because fundamentally it's the same thing.

What's your personal definition of AGI? Do you think we've reached AGI yet? If not, or if we're close, what do you think we're still missing? Can pre-training plus RL post-training get us to the AGI you have in mind?

There's a joke inside OpenAI: Ask 15 people, and you'll probably get 20 definitions of AGI. Right right.

And then, the definition I used before was: if it can complete 80 to 90 percent of the tasks I personally find meaningful, then it's probably AGI. As of now, I don't think we've reached it yet. Because right now, personally, at least from my perspective, I still wouldn't trust it to directly modify my infra code. Because that's very out of distribution. AI infra accounts for almost none of its training data. And with AI infra, the verification feedback loop is too long and too expensive. So right now it still can't really touch that area.

Good for you. Sounds like AI won't replace you anytime soon.

But anyway, before Strawberry came out, internally we'd already been using Strawberry for a while. Back then, everyone thought, "My job is going to be replaced," or "let's just write a bunch of spaghetti code," and later on, Strawberry will clean it up for us once it's trained. Looking back now, maybe a year or two has passed, and it's still the same. The spaghetti code is still there. It doesn't really change anything. Everyone overreacts, thinking once a new technology appears, oh, now we have to do this and that, but it doesn't actually work like that. It's a very slow, gradual process.

九、Open vs. Closed：OpenAI 的"开放"与公司生死

You're at OpenAI now, and before that you were at Tsinghua and CMU in academia, and probably in research labs too. But now a lot of people criticize OpenAI for not being very open anymore. And you, used to care a lot about open source and reducing information asymmetry. Do you think that conflicts with your personal goals and preferences?

I think it's a trade-off. I still really love open source. If OpenAI has open-source work, I'd probably try to contribute. But I think I should do what I believe matters more. And open source, for OpenAI, is a trade-off. I mean, you can't directly open-source your best model because the company has to survive. If the company can't survive, then you probably won't be able to keep raising money, keep running experiments, or make breakthrough progress. Those are all very practical issues. So I accept that trade-off.

But when OpenAI was first founded, its structure was actually unusual. Right. It wasn't originally set up as a for-profit company. At the beginning, at least publicly, the slogan was to ensure AGI benefits all of humanity equally. Right. Do you think being closed-source has brought that goal closer or pushed it farther away?

First, this idea of AGI benefiting all of humanity has always been split into two parts. The first is achieving AGI. The second is benefiting all of humanity.

As for AGI itself, that part is straightforward: you just keep scaling RL, or pre-training, add more compute, and scale up.

As for benefiting all of humanity, the current approach is to build products and make them as cheap as possible. For example, free ChatGPT users can access it directly, which makes the technology more accessible. For example, free users can probably use voice mode too and just try it out. That may do more to benefit all of humanity than simply open-sourcing it. If you just throw out bare model weights, most people won't know how to use them.

So what you're saying is, OpenAI's "open" isn't openness toward other big model companies, it's openness for ordinary people.

Yes. But with a strategy like that, if I think this is the final mile, that we're in a 100-meter sprint, and AGI is almost here, then that makes sense. I agree. But if AGI is still I wouldn't call it a marathon, but also not something we'll solve next year either, would greater technical openness and transparency also make OpenAI itself more likely to reach AGI sooner? Or do you think that OpenAI actually doesn't really need to disclose its technical details, get community feedback, or any help from the community at all? That OpenAI can already achieve AGI on its own?

Let me think. I think there is a path where you can open-source things, accept community feedback, and still do a better job of reaching AGI. That can be done. In theory, yes, but in practice it's hard to execute. Take everyone else, for example. You're ahead right now. If you open-source it, everyone else immediately catches up, then they train on top of it, and keep their own version closed. And that means not everyone is working toward the same goal. Then you end up in a world where, for example, OpenAI, in the current environment, might not be able to raise funding, and no one keeps backing it. It becomes a bit of a game theory problem.

Even if I want AGI to benefit all humanity, other people may not think that way. Some people just want to make money. So to guard against that, OpenAI has to stay closed-source. At least that's how I see it.

So that's really a company survival issue, right? If OpenAI had unlimited resources right now, and never had to worry about going under, then you'd be happy to open-source the last two or three years of your RL infra? Would you be happy to do that?

Of course I'd be happy. John Schulman actually asked me about this, whether we should open-source it. At the time, I didn't think it was a good idea, mostly because of company considerations. But he really did ask me about it.

So did DeepSeek, at least with its open weights, trigger another round of internal evaluation at OpenAI?

Yes yes.

十、OpenAI 的执行力、董事会风波与人才流失

You just said OpenAI's mission has two parts: first, achieve AGI, and then benefit all humanity. To go a bit deeper, if we're trying to understand that mission, what do you think the biggest opportunity and challenge are in actually carrying it out?

Execution, and executing in the right direction. As long as we can keep executing. We just can't have something like November 2023 happen again, when the company almost collapsed, when Sam Altman was fired.

So you want the organization to be as stable as possible so you can move fast. From the inside, then, when Sam was fired, what kind of information did you all receive? Because from the outside, everything we heard sounded pretty surreal, like what exactly did Ilya see?

No, it was all just rumors, people grasping at straws, and a lot of people making things up.

Right. So what was the internal view?

The internal view was just that it was basically a trust issue. Ilya and some other board members didn't trust Sam, so they voted him out. And then... But those of us doing the work were very surprised, completely shocked, and had no idea what had happened. Because the board had always been pretty opaque to the rest of us. And we didn't know how that decision had been made.

This lack of trust, what exactly didn't they trust about him?

They didn't trust him as a person. Because you can go look at the official public investigation report.

Right, that matches. But in the end, Ilya was the one who left. Right. How did that shift happen? Sam had already been fired, and Ilya didn't trust him, but in the end Sam seemed to have more support.

Because a lot of employees felt that if it were led by someone purely technical, it might not have as much vision. AGI won't happen through technology alone. It's not enough to just solve the tech. There are still a lot of business factors involved. For example, you need to raise money, you need compute, and you need to convince people to invest in you. That's a crucial part of it too. And if all you have is strong research experience, that still may not be enough to carry you that far. So in the long run, you still need someone like Sam.

You still need someone like Sam. So Sam may actually be the hardest person for AI to replace. Because he has to navigate business, even geopolitics, and resources.

Basically, you can think of Sam as a persona, as an identity, and in the short term, if you try to replace that with AI, then people's buy-in to that identity would disappear. So that part can't be replaced.

You just said when John Schulman left, you were down for the whole afternoon. But actually, John Schulman wasn't the only one to leave OpenAI. After OpenAI became hugely successful, countless team members left. Do you think, for an extremely successful organization like OpenAI, a major talent exodus is inevitable in the end?

In a healthy organization, everyone should be replaceable. As long as you can keep developing new people, and keep renewing the talent pipeline, and keep the organization running, that's enough. Even if a lot of people leave, you can still spend some time and effort training the next wave, and keep renewing the pipeline, like stem cells.

So right now, nobody at OpenAI is irreplaceable.

Right.

Does that also mean what OpenAI is doing isn't some impossible thing for outsiders to replicate?

You could say that. Because really, it's about doing the simple things well. There's no black magic.

十一、外部竞争、迭代速度与组织规模的代价

Right now, the world may be in the middle of the fiercest tech race in human history. OpenAI is the company that lit the fuse. I'm curious: how intense is it internally right now? Are you under a lot of pressure?

It depends on the team, the deadline, and the project timeline. For example, in post-training, the pressure is pretty high right now. For other teams, like ours, where we're rebuilding infra, there is pressure, but not as much as post-training. They have very concrete deadlines, while we can slip a bit, because we need to optimize for the longer term. We need to get it right.

So does the fierce external competition, whether from xAI, Anthropic, or Chinese foundation model companies, spill over into your company's day-to-day development?

Not really. Except for the DeepSeek wave. Because they were claiming on Twitter that their iteration speed was very fast. That definitely put a lot of people on alert. Because internally, our iteration speed is actually a bit slower than others'. That's why we need to rebuild our infra.

So for a company like OpenAI, a foundation model company, the make-or-break metric is infra cycle time.

Right. Other things, like data, or algorithms, or how many researchers you have, you can just throw people at those. That's labor. It scales linearly with headcount.

Isn't AI infra labor too?

Good question. But AI infra needs much more context. If you're just doing some data ablations, like running a few experiments, that doesn't require nearly as much context. You can just come in and write a for loop, set up the data config, and run it. Right, all of that can be automated.

So what really caught your attention was realizing DeepSeek's internal infra is really good, and that they iterate very fast. That's what made you take notice. You don't actually care which model sits where on a leaderboard, or how much higher it is than GPT. That's not what you care about at all.

I mean, for a long time now, we haven't specifically done anything just to game LMsys. What we really care about is, over a given period, iteration speed and success rate.

Right. Do you think OpenAI is number one in the world on that metric right now?

No. Definitely not. This has a lot to do with org structure. For example, if you spin out a team and turn it into a startup, then their iteration curve would definitely be much steeper than OpenAI's. First, the codebase is smaller. Communication costs are lower. And you only need to focus on a few use cases. That lets you move much faster. But at OpenAI, they have to think about many use cases at once and make trade-offs on all sides. That's just what happens when an organization gets big.

Then if, on that make-or-break metric, OpenAI is no longer number one, can it still lead in AI models going forward?

Every company has this problem.

You mean once a company gets big and successful, its pace slows down?

Yes. Then it just comes down to everyone being pretty bad, and seeing which one is the least bad. And that's only bad relative to startups. A startup may do better on this metric, but on other metrics it may struggle to compete with OpenAI. Like gathering user feedback. So it's all trade-offs.

This is just what human organizations inevitably face at this scale. You can't avoid it.

It's not even that it's hard to maintain high talent density. It's that it's hard to keep context sharing consistent across the org. That first leads to inconsistent infra. Then the infra gets bloated. Then the org structure gets bloated too. That's just what happens when you have a lot of people.

So context sharing, in theory, should be handled by an agent with effectively infinite context instead. That sounds like a must-have.

Yes. Because it would solve the problem bloated organizations have always faced, whether it's writing code or doing anything else.

Right, because the human brain's context is limited. You can't hold that much context at once. But AI can. Maybe in the future every company will have an agent with effectively infinite context and just make it the CEO. It handles all the information sharing and all the decisions.

Right. There may be nothing better suited to be the decision-maker.

十二、决定论、未来的我，与"投资未来"的悖论

Right. Looking further ahead, if you wanted AI to solve one world-scale problem, what would it be?

Predicting the future.

And when you say the future, you definitely don't mean something like how a cup will fall, you mean whole lives, the world order, all of that.

Yeah.

Why is that the thing you most want to do?

Personally, the idea of building your own world still feels very appealing. It's something worth pursuing. But if you look at it from a higher-dimensional space, you need a script generated ahead of time.

My understanding is, you think our fate can be predicted.

Yes. The world is deterministic.

So we live inside a deterministic Markov process.

Yes. Taking that further, you think people have no free will.

Yes. What I'm thinking right now, what my next word will be, what my next question will be, all of it was fixed at the moment of the Big Bang.

Yes. I've verified this countless times.

To me, that sounds like a pretty pessimistic worldview.

Yeah. Because it might actually be true, but deep down I don't want to accept it.

Neither do I. It's like I'm just an atom being simulated.

Yeah.

Why do you think that?

Some personal experiences, I guess. Not something I can really talk about. But I'm sure it's true. Everything is predictable. So in theory AI could solve it.

But if you had a machine that could predict the future, it would be a disaster for the individual. I think it would collapse every value system. Yeah. If an AI model ever did that, then for human society, the best choice might be to destroy it and make sure it never comes out.

Right. But some people would really want to build that kind of model. Because otherwise, they'd just be trapped by fatalism.

You mean someone would want a model like that because they want to break out? So you mean once you have a model like that, I'd do something different from what it predicted. And you'd call that escape?

No. Just to understand the rules behind how this world works, or why it has to be fixed, why it's deterministic.

So you think God doesn't play dice.

Right. Not at the macro level. At the micro level, yes.

Does that hold up?

If we're talking about quantum measurement, then yes. Because behind the scenes you can alter some world lines. You can take what I'm saying as nonsense, and that's fine.

I don't think it's nonsense. I'm trying to reason it out. I've thought about this for a long time too. Like, is this world deterministic, do we have a script, are we just puppets under this world's dynamics, playing out a random process? I think it is. It's not random at all. It's deterministic. I think so too. You have no doubt about that?

I have a lot of doubts. But I've tried to disprove it and couldn't. I really want it to be disproved.

I wonder about this too. I'm really interested in it. I think I even brought this up to my parents when I was six or seven. I was like, how do you know what I just said wasn't predetermined? How do you know your reaction isn't predetermined? I was thinking about this as a kid. But later I realized that if that's true, I don't even feel human. Or rather, the best thing is to forget all of it and pretend you don't know. and just experience whatever is happening now.

Have you done that already? I've always done that. I don't have a choice.

Another explanation is that the world doesn't have a linear timeline. It can jump around. So one theory is that my future self helps my past self make certain decisions.

You mentioned a moment earlier when you were in elementary school, or maybe middle or high school, and your future self suddenly told your past self what you wanted: To make an impact.

Right, it was just a sentence that popped into my head. But I don't know if my future self told me that.

Then why do you think the timeline isn't continuous?

Maybe things from the future move back and forth through it. I think three-dimensional beings all have limits. From a 3D perspective, time is linear and only flows one way. But in four-dimensional time, it may not flow in just one direction. It could jump around freely. That's the most reasonable explanation I've found so far.

So you really think there were countless moments in your past there's a future version of you like Murphy in Interstellar, pushing that pillar from behind.

Right, that's one possibility.

Because you can't falsify it, and pushing that pillar would then lead future you to go back and push it again. Right. So your best solution now is to forget about it. Right. And keep believing Sisyphus is happy. Right. We could spend another half hour on this. Let's move on for now.

十三、未来：创业的种子、十年后的自己

I think everyone in AI today, especially people at OpenAI, probably has an entrepreneurial seed in them. Have you thought about that?

Maybe a little. But right now, I haven't seen a really good idea yet, and for me, OpenAI is still a pretty good place to be.

What kind of idea would you like? More product-oriented, or what?

I think I'd prefer product. Like Tianshou, or tuixue.online. I mean, when I do research infra, that work also has users. Because your users are researchers, and you can use their feedback to iterate in real time.

So you'd want something like that, something people really need, where there's real demand, and where your strengths can really be put to use, and the exact tech doesn't matter.

For example, take tuixue.online. It's just a very simple notification tool. Honestly, it barely counts as a system. It's only a few lines of code. A little PHP is enough. You don't even need PHP. You could even just use some bare-bones setup. Even if every day, the first version of tuixue.online just updated the time once during the day and once at night, I did it manually. But even so, there was a lot of demand. The tech doesn't matter. What matters is capturing the demand.

What would you want Jiayi Weng to be like in ten years? Where would he be, and what would he be doing?

I'd hope he's doing whatever he wants then. And that he has enough resources and enough ability to do what he wants.

So you wouldn't try to dictate what he wants then?

Right, because what you want changes. Maybe what you want now doesn't matter anyway. Things will unfold deterministically anyway. So what you hope for now isn't that important.

So all I can do now is invest in that future version of me, or invest in the future, so he has the freedom to choose. Then why invest at all? You'll get there either way.

But I still think it's probably better to invest a bit. What if you don't invest? Isn't that just fooling yourself?

Investing won't change anything. Things unfold deterministically. You could sleep at home every day and still end up there. Investing in the future might be deterministic too. So whether you invest or not isn't really your free will either.

Yes.

Before, your way of investing in the future was learning high school math early, or things like that. Now, besides technical stuff, besides AI, what else do you want to invest in for the future?

Retire early. And have enough capital to do whatever I want.

If you had unlimited money, what would you retire to do right now?

Spend some time figuring out what I want to do.

So you're saying what you're doing now isn't really what you most want to do. You're doing it more for a living, to save enough to retire, and then go find that thing.

What I'm doing right now, like RL infra, was what I wanted to do for a long time. But as time goes on, say, once things gradually stabilize, or after certain things happen, people's priorities change. I once thought I'd figured out what I wanted, but now I'm not so sure.

And now you're not sure again.

Yeah. I think that's actually pretty normal.

So you're in a period of uncertainty right now.
